An ancient Greek interpreter has, "like one mourning for a brother born of the same mother." I felt the affliction of my enemies and sympathized with them, endeavouring to alleviate their distress as much as possible.
Such was the conduct of David, the figure of the Messias; so that the expressions which seem too strong, must not be taken for imprecations.
Christ was the good Samaritan who relieved the wounded man, Luke 10.
St. Paul admonishes us to weep with those who weep, Romans 12:15.

34:15.text: Against me. Hebrew, "in my distress," (Houbigant) or "infirmity."
Thus was my kind attention repaid.
My enemies became only the more insolent, and stood beside me, to shew their contempt.
Scourges. Hebrew also, "vile men," (Berthier) necim, people stricken, gathered themselves upon me, and I knew not."
The Roman psalters have "and they were ignorant," ignoraverunt.
David could not think that he had given any offence: much less had our Saviour.
He knew no just cause why the Jews persecuted him, as they were actuated by malice.
David knew not personally those who afflicted him.

34:16.text: Separated, and could not agree in their testimonies. This may be one meaning of charak, as it has several. (Berthier) -- "They did tear me, and ceased not, (16) with hypocritical mockers in feasts, they gnashed," etc.
Many of their attempts have been frustrated by thy Providence, yet they do not enter into themselves.

34:17.text: One. Septuagint, "daughter." Aquila, "solitary," monachen. Protestants, "darling," or my soul which is so desolate, Psalm 21:21.
So Christ said, Why hast thou abandoned me? (Matthew xxvii.) not affording me such consolation as other saints enjoy in their agony.

34:18.text: Strong, gravi. Hebrew hatsum, numerous, (1 Machabees 1:1.; Menochius) and "weighty," (Haydock) which is the consequence of great numbers, (Berthier) and of virtue. St. Augustine understands the Church, which is not carried away like chaff before the wind. Amama dislikes this.
The resurrection is foretold, (ver. 17.) and here the Catholic Church is signified.

34:19.text: Wrongfully. Hebrew, "liars," (St. Jerome; Haydock) alluding to Saul, etc. (Calmet) Christ explains this of himself, John xv.
"Neither let them wink with the eye who." (Protestants) This sign might indicate friendship, or evil machinations, Proverbs 6:13., and 10:10.
"They pretended by their looks what they did not entertain in their hearts."

34:20.text: Spoke. Hebrew adds lo, "not." But it may be better explained as an interrogation. "Have they not spoken?" etc., as the enemies used deceit.
Earth. This word is omitted in the Roman Septuagint, St. Augustine, etc. But Theodoret reads it, and it is in all the other Greek interpreters, and in the Arabic and Syriac.
Hebrew, "they devise deceitful matters against them that are quiet (righe, divided, etc.) in the land." (Protestants) or "in the plunder of the earth they devise deceit."
They appear friendly, but when alone they talk to the earth, like people in deep study and full of passion.
This terrestrial anger is the source of much evil.
The Jews and Herodians said, Master, etc., designing to inveigle our Saviour, Matthew xxii. The priests and Pharisees also accused him boldly, and instigated the people to demand his crucifixion.
They were split into parties, and invaded those who were the reverse of passionate.

34:21.text: This term occurs ten times in Scripture, and here denotes the joy felt in the destruction of an enemy. Thus the Jews insulted over Christ expiring on the cross.
Seen. No farther reserve is necessary: he is fallen, or we have accused him of nothing but what we have seen.

34:22.text: Silent. I have chosen thee for my judge and protector.
Pronounce sentence if they be in the right; and if not, rescue me from their fury.

34:23.text: Attentive. Hebrew, "watch over." He implores present assistance.

34:24.text: Thy. Many copies read "my," with St. Augustine, Euthymius, etc. But thy agrees with the Roman (Calmet) and Alexandrian Septuagint and Hebrew.

34:25.text: It is well, occurs only once in Hebrew, which may be imperfect, as many versions supply the second.
Mind, as we could have desired. Protestants, "Oh, (marginal note adds ah) so would we have it."
Things go on well according to our mind.

34:26.text: At the day of judgment the wicked will be condemned.
If this judgment were not to take place, religion would be a fable: as impiety is not always punished in this world.

34:27.text: Them. The blessed in eternal glory.
Justice. Or wish that sentence may be pronounced in my favour.
But the Greek interpreters read as we do.

34:28.text: Meditate. Hebrew word is used to signify speaking with reflection.

35:1.text: Himself. Psalm is understood. It is expressed in St. Ambrose and St. Jerome, (Calmet) and in the Alexandrian Septuagint.

Eusebius improperly assigns the cause of the omission to the piece being of a moral nature. Many suppose it refers to Saul, who had promised that he would give ear no more to the detractors of David, when the latter restored to him his spear and cup, 1 Kings xxvi.
But it seems rather to express the sentiments of the captives at Babylon, like the Psalms x., xi., xiii., and lii.
David gloried in the title of servant of the Lord, though he bore the sceptre, Psalm xvii., (Berthier) and Psalm 115:16.
He applies this instruction to himself, and to all in the lowest stations.

35:2.text: Himself. Hebrew libbi, "in my heart." But this is visibly incorrect, and we should substitute lobu, as St. Jerome, Chaldean, Syriac, etc., have done.
Yet Symmachus translates, "concerning the disorder of the impious within, my heart has said, there," etc. Hebrew may also signify, "the transgression of the wicked saith within my heart."
I am inwardly convinced how great the malice of the wicked may be. It touches me to the very heart. Both senses are good. The wicked are bent on evil, and this fills the virtuous with grief.
Eyes. They sin publicly, (Psalm 13:1.; Calmet) and on purpose, preferring vice before virtue, (Worthington) and constantly bent on doing evil, so that they become odious to all.

35:3.text: Unto hatred. That is, hateful to God (Challoner) and man; (Haydock) or that he may be able to hurt, as Hebrew also may insinuate.
Septuagint, "to find and hate his iniquity." But he acts not with sincerity. He wishes to defend his evil ways.
He still flatters himself with impunity, Psalm 9:25., or 10:11. To find, often means to punish, Genesis 44:16., etc.
God frequently abandons those who sin through malice.

35:4.text: Well, to those in distress, Psalm 40:2. Though wise enough in worldly concerns, he seemed quite ignorant when any virtuous actions were proposed.

Sometimes ignorance is excusable when a person does his best to obtain knowledge. But when he is negligent, the ignorance is gross, and sinful in proportion to the importance of the thing. If one desire to be ignorant to prevent remorse, this only increases the guilt, and God often leaves such destitute of the ordinary graces which he gives to others; so that they fall into a reprobate sense, and into more horrible sins.

35:6.src: St. Clement of Alexandria, strom. 5., etc.
35:6.text: Clouds. The mercy of God is great, and his fidelity indisputable. Some think these were concealed till the coming of the Messias; (Psalm 84:11.; St. Bernard) and many of the Fathers accuse Aristotle of confining Providence to the regions above the moon, by perverting this text.
But Gesner has produced 30 passages from that author which prove both a general and particular Providence.

God does not leave the most wilful sinner without some good motions, and sufficient grace, that they may repent if they do not harden their own hearts. He has promised such helps, and is most faithful and desirous to receive again the penitent sinner.

35:7.text: Of God. A title which is often given to things of superior excellence. So divina condimenta of Plautus, (Pseudol.) denote ragouts or sauces of the best quality; (Haydock) and sacra fames of Virgil, means great hunger. See Jonas 3:3., Canticle of Canticles 8:6.
Deep. After praising the mercy of God, the psalmist expresses his admiration of his inscrutable justice, Romans 11:33.
Preserve, salvabis. The latter are designed only for man's benefit, and will end with time.
But man is destined for eternal happiness, ver. 9.

God wishes the salvation of both the learned and of the stupid, (St. Jerome; Worthington) of the Jew and Gentile, (Arnob.; 1 Timothy 4:10.) of good and bad. He makes his sun to shine on both, Matthew 5:45.

35:8.text: O how. So the Hebrew and Septuagint read; quemadmodum may also (Berthier) signify "as." God has given such proofs of his great mercies to all.
Of men. People must lay aside their stupidity and resemblance with brutes, to obtain the eternal joys which are prepared for men.

35:9.text: House. In the temple, (Calmet) or in the Church of God.
The pleasures enjoyed by this communion of saints, (Haydock) is but a foretaste of what may be expected in heaven.

35:10.text: With thee, Lord, is the fountain of life, Jesus Christ.
See light, or the Holy Ghost.
We shall see thee, Father of light, in thy Son.
The saints behold in the light of God all that they can desire to know; and of course they will not be unacquainted with our wants and petitions, though they have not the asses' ears of Calvin.
Light and life denote all happiness.
The psalmist might have a sublime idea of these pleasures.
But none will presently understand their excellence till they are put in possession of them.

35:11.text: Mercy and justice, are here of the same import.
Deliver us from captivity, and extend thy mercies to all thy people.
Heart. Many who have sufficient learning, are destitute of this better quality. The right of heart are always more knowing than those who are only learned in speculation, and puffed up with pride.

35:12.text: Sinner. Hebrew and Septuagint, "sinners," who are always striving to supplant the just by pride and evil example.
Let me not listen to their wicked advice.
Let not the enemy invade our country any more.
The just may pray that no bad example or pride may place an obstacle to his salvation.

35:13.text: There. The devil fell by pride, and man by his persuasion. Neither could escape punishment.
There, in heaven, (St. Jerome) and in paradise, pride proved fatal; (Calmet) while it will be punished in hell.
Pride and injustice will entail destruction upon our persecutors. Babylon shall shortly fall a prey to Cyrus.
Stand. Hebrew kum, "rise again." The proud are seldom converted, (Berthier) and the rebel angels had no redress.

36:1.text: Himself. Hebrew has simply, "for David," (Calmet) as well as the Greek of the Vatican. "It is a mistake in Bellanger to say in general that the Greek adds "a psalm," since this is true only with respect to the edition of Aldus and Complutensian, says Berthier. But he is not quite accurate, as Erasmus inserts "a psalm" in his edition of St. Jerome's Septuagint; and the Alexandrian copy, which is equally famous with that of the Vatican, has [unto the end, a psalm] for David. Grabe has indeed marked all but the last word as a peculiarity, or not to be found in Origen's copy. But he has published his edition with such accuracy, that we may distinguish what his manuscript contained from other interpolations. It were to be wished that the same attention had been paid to the Vatican copy. But hitherto all the editors have taken the liberty to make alterations without specifying where; so that we can have no security that we ever quote the real manuscript of the Vatican. The learned prefect, Zacagni, gives abundant proof of this in his letter to Grabe, which has been published by Kennicott, Diss. 2. Yet any of these editions may be quoted as the Greek or Septuagint, as we have yet no copy perfectly authentic: and the learned are not even agreed which standard ought to be followed. If that which presents the greatest number of Origen's corrections be preferable, the Alexandrians manuscript must bear away the psalm . If the reverse, the glory must be given to its rival in the Vatican, which approaches the nearest to the koine, or to the edition of St. Lucian. See Kennicott. These remarks may be of service, as Berthier often seems inclined to place the Vatican edition on the same level with the Latin Vulgate.
This psalm is alphabetical. The Syriac, Septuagint, etc., read, (ver. 28) the unjust, etc., avilim; a word which seems now to be deficient in the Hebrew, which has no verse beginning with a.
Some other derangement has taken place.

The verses might be so divided as to begin every second verse with a fresh letter, and so to retain 42 verses. See ver. 7., and 20. The matter is of no great importance. The prophet has comprised several duties in alphabetical order, to help the memory, (Berthier) and to excite attention.

He may predict the death of Saul, (Rabbins) or hint at the rebellion of Absalom in his old age; (ver. 25.; Ferrand) or rather he may comfort the captives at Babylon, promising them liberty, and denouncing the fall of their oppressors, above ten times. He admonishes them not to be scandalized at the distress of the just, and the prosperity of the wicked.
Emulous. Hebrew, "Fret not thyself." (Protestants) "Mingle not with;" (Berthier; Pagnin) "contend not."
Be not, therefore, seduced (Haydock) to imitate the wicked (Menochius) nor offended, that they should prosper here.

36:2.text: This admirably describes the transient glory of sinners, Isaias 40:6., and James 1:10.

36:3.text: Riches. Septuagint and Houbigant read emune, "abundance." Hebrew begins with a. The sense is much the same.
"Thou shalt feed on faith," (St. Jerome) or "incessantly."
The Jews entertained the greatest desire of the promised land.
It may here denote our soul, (Origen) the Church, (St. Augustine) the Scriptures, (St. Athanasius) or heaven.
Trust in God and be content. He will give thee what is requisite.

36:4.text: He will enable thee to repose in peace, and to taste innocent pleasures in the Lord.

36:5.text: This expresses the most unbounded confidence, Psalm 54:23., and Proverbs 16:3.
Do it. Whatever may be proper. He will display thy justice, (ver. 6.) and free thee from anxiety, (Calmet) taking care of thee, 1 Peter 5:7.

36:6.text: Day. This will appear at the last judgment.

36:7.text: Be. Hebrew dom, begins only this verse with d. The other letters occupy two verses, (Berthier) the second of which may commence with any of the letters.
"Be silent to the Lord; wait upon Him."
If he should suffer thee to be afflicted, envy not those who are in a more prosperous condition, nor give way to indignation, ver. 8.
None can be truly subjected to God, who do not comply with his laws and pray.
We must wait patiently for his aid, Lamentations 3:26., and Isaias 30:15. "Allow the gods to judge what's best for us."

36:8.text: Evil. Repining (Menochius) at the ways of Providence, etc.

Hebrew, "be not angry nevertheless (ac, a word which Houbigant deems useless) to do evil;" (Montanus) or "against the wicked," (Prin. dis.) as lehareah may be perhaps signified; though it is more usually taken for a verb, as the points decide.

36:9.text: David knew that many truly pious people would never obtain riches in the land of Chanaan, even though they might have remained there, if the nation had been faithful. He therefore comforts them with the prospect of a better land. If this were not the meaning, the Church would put these canticles in the mouth of her children to little purpose.

36:10.text: While. Till the day of judgment.
And shalt. Hebrew, "and it shall not be." (Protestants) "it, or he shall not subsist."
The state of the wicked is not therefore so enviable. The captives witnessed the fall of the great Colossus, the empire of the Babylonians.

36:11.text: Meek. Hebrew hanavim, also means "the afflicted."
Captives, ye shall be reinstated in your dear country. Our Saviour alludes to this text, (Matthew 5:4.) and the Fathers beautifully explain it of heaven.

What is now become of those who have heretofore filled the world with tumult and bloodshed to obtain dominion! They are confined to the land where everlasting horror and no order dwells. They would wish they had never existed, as our Saviour mentions; while those who passed through life unknown, or despised, but always seeking God, are now arrived at the summit of all their wishes.

O holy religion! thou explainest all these things. The just have ceased to exist: but their better part has inherited the land of the living. Yet a little while, and all will be in order, and in its proper place; though that of the wicked deserves not the name.

36:12.text: Teeth. In rage to destroy him, (Calmet) whose virtue is a continual censure of his impiety.

36:13.text: Laugh. This expression is often used to denote the triumph of divine justice, whose day will set all right: that day (2 Timothy 4:8.) which ought to be constantly before our eyes.
God cannot indeed mock at any one.
But the wicked "deserve scorn and vengeance."
Digni sunt ut irrideantur in vindicta .

The day of their judgment or condemnation is at hand, (Ezechiel 21:25., and 1 Kings 26:10.; Haydock) when they will be sought for in vain (ver. 10.; Calmet) by their foolish admirers. They will seek to hide themselves from the indignation of the Lamb.

36:14.text: Heart. Hebrew, "of way." Protestants, "such as be of upright conversation." Only those whose heart is pure, will observe the right path.

36:15.text: Broken. In the form of imprecation, he foretells the event.
Hebrew is in the future, to imply as much.

36:16.text: Wicked. Hebrew, "of many wicked," or "of the impious great ones."

What the just man hath, is preferable to the immense riches of sinners, acquired by injustice. In this sense Hesiod and Psittacus said, "half is more than all." The wicked are never satisfied, Ecclesiastes 4:6., and Proverbs 13:25. Riches are a dangerous temptation, (Calmet) and the sentence is generally true, "every rich person is either unjust or the heir of one who has been such," (Haydock) aut haeres injusti.
It is difficult for the rich to enter heaven; and the unjust are certainly excluded. Yet if we confined our views to this world, it is evident that the rich may better procure the sweets of life.

36:17.text: Arms of the body, brachia. All that they have admired perishes in death, (Calmet) while the just then possess true riches.

36:18.text: Days, or "ways," according to some copies of Septuagint. (St. Augustine, etc.) God approves the conduct of the just. He takes notice of the time of their sufferings, and comforts them during life, (Calmet) yea, for ever.

36:20.text: Because. Only this verse begins with c, as the seventh does with d.
Smoke. All their riches shall vanish, and their works be disregarded by God. But they will not be annihilated, as they would desire; otherwise the justice of God would not be executed on them.

There is a continual antithesis between the good and bad. The latter shall shortly lose all their splendour. "I fear, lest offending the gods, I may receive glory among men," said the poet Ibicus, (Calmet) conformably to our Saviour's declaration concerning the vain-glorious, they have received their reward, Matthew 6:6. Hebrew, "shall be as the fat of lambs, consumed and reduced to smoke."

St. Jerome seems to have read differently, "boasting like unicorns, they shall be consumed, as smoke, they shall be consumed." Syriac and Chaldean intimate that they shall be like victims, "fattened" for slaughter, and burnt.

36:21.text: Give. Having both the will and the power to be liberal.
"He shall lend without expecting any advantage, while the wicked falls into such misery as not to be able to pay his debts. This is not always the order of Providence.
But the just is often enabled by economy to relieve his brethren, at the same time that the libertine wastes his estate, (Berthier) or at least unjustly defers to pay his debts.

36:22.text: Bless him. The just, (ver. 21.; Prin. disc.) or rather the Lord, ver. 20.

36:23.text: With. Or by the decrees of the Lord. The Hebrew and Septuagint have, "By," para.
God gives grace to do all good, (Proverbs 16:9.; Calmet) and likes the way which He points out. The just also find the greatest consolation in virtue.

36:24.text: Him. To break the fall. Hebrew, "the Lord upholdeth him with his hand."
The just man is like a courageous wrestler, who may slip, but yields not.
His fall is not mortal, (Calmet) though he may be guilty of venial sin, Proverbs xxiv.

36:25.text: Roman and Gothic Ps.[Psalters?] read, "wanting." This does not condemn the mendicant orders.
Nothing was more unusual under the old law than the extreme distress of the just: yet Job and Lazarus were reduced to it. They were not, however, discontent.
They found the bread of life in conformity to God's will, John 4:34.
Their souls were enriched with grace, which was never wanting, as the Fathers explain this passage.

It is certain that there were poor among the Jews; (Deuteronomy 15:11.) and who would assert that they were all wicked, or the children of such? Yet the prophet had not witnessed (Calmet) any person renowned for virtue reduced to this condition, (Haydock) though he does not deny but it might be possible.
The proposition may be restrained to those who have been very charitable, and who are not often thereby reduced to want, Daniel 4:24.
But the word just is more comprehensive; and St. Paul gives several instances of persecuted saints (Hebrew xi.) which is confirmed by the parable, or rather by the history, of Lazarus.
At any rate, the Church is never deprived of the word of God.

36:26.text: Lendeth. "To receive interest," daneizei, from God, Proverbs 19:17.
He maketh know the divine word.

36:27.text: Dwell in the land of the living, rather than in that of promise, from which many just people were banished during the captivity.
He who complies with these two conditions, will inherit heaven.

36:28.text: Saints. Hebrew, "merciful ones." He will free them from captivity.

Punished. This sentence seems to be improperly omitted in Hebrew, which otherwise neglects the letter a, as the Chaldean, Syriac, and St. Jerome do as well as a few copies of the Septuagint. The Roman edition with the Arabic and Ethiopic, reads amomoi ekdikethesontai, "the innocent shall be avenged," (Calmet) or "punished;" which is inaccurately put for anomoi ekdiochthesontai, the wicked shall be punished, or "expelled;" (Berthier) though Grabe prefers the former verb.

36:31.text: Supplanted. The devil shall have no advantage over the just, (Calmet) who aim constantly at perfection.

36:32.text: Death, mortificare. Some read perdere, or occidere. The wicked are constantly laying snares for destruction, (Calmet) and to draw others into mortal sin.

36:33.text: Judged, "by him." Illi seems to be superfluous; (Berthier) or it implies that God will revise the sentence of wicked judges.
The just have nothing to fear. God will pass an equitable sentence, and the condemnation of men shall do no harm.
The mistakes of human tribunals prove the necessity of a general judgment.

36:34.text: See the truth of these maxims.
While the wicked enjoy power they often conceal their injustice, which appears as soon as the veil is removed by death, when people cease to fear them.

36:35.text: Cedars, etc. Septuagint have read differently from the present Hebrew and present a more beautiful sense.
Protestants, "and spreading himself like a green bay-tree, (marginal note: or "a green tree that groweth in his own soil,") yet he passed away, and lo," etc.
Ezrach, denotes an evergreen, (Rabbins) "a laurel covered with verdure."

36:36.text: I passed. This is better than the Hebrew, "it has passed," transivit, as a tree changes not its place, and all the ancient interpreters agree with us.
His place, is not expressed in Hebrew. But it implies that every vestige of the proud is soon lost. This might serve to curb the violence of those who disturb mankind!
The wicked may here refer to Nabuchodonosor, the devil, Judas, etc.
We may behold the riches of sinners with our bodily eyes: but if we consider them with the eyes of faith, they presently vanish.

36:37.text: Hebrew acharith, "the reward," (Pagnin) "the last end of man is peace," (Montanus; Haydock) or "the posterity (ver. 38.) of such a man shall be happy."
"There are future things for the peaceful."
The expectations of the just are not confined to this world. They have something laid up for heaven, whereas the wicked have nothing.
These lose all by death; and the thought makes them take refuge in the foolish hope of being annihilated.

36:38.text: Together. At the last day, (Haydock) or all without exception shall perish; the wicked, with their posterity and riches.

36:39.text: Salvation. This is an effect of God's grace.

37:1.text: For a remembrance, viz., of our miseries and sins; and to be sung on the sabbath-day.
This sabbath might also allude to the indolent rest which occasioned the fall of David, 2 Kings 11:1.

Hebrew mentions not the sabbath; and it is not known (Calmet) why the Septuagint made this addition. The prophet may have Christ suffering for our sins in view, (Berthier) though he probably composed this psalm, when he was afflicted with an illness after this fall.
It contains an excellent model for penitents, (Calmet) to enable them to regain peace of conscience, (St. Gregory) and paradise, from which they are banished by sin.

37:2.text: Wrath. God is incapable of passion: but man deserves to be treated with the utmost rigour; and this David deprecates, begging that God would act rather like a physician in his regard.
The same petition occurs in psalm vi.; and this ought to caution people not to make imprecations, since God's judgments are so terrible.
St. Augustine and St. Gregory explain this text of the fire of hell, and of purgatory, 1 Corinthians 3:15.
Though some be saved by the latter, "yet is that fire more grievous than whatever man can suffer in this life."
"I esteem that transitory fire more intolerable than all present tribulation."

We may therefore pray, "Here burn," etc., with the same St. Augustine who assures us, (Gen. con. Man. 2:20.) that "he who cultivates not the field of his soul, will, after this life, experience either the fire of purgatory or eternal punishment."

37:3.text: Arrows. Afflictions, (Worthington) or the word of God, which convert the sinner.
The admonition of Nathan had made the deepest impression on David. [2 Kings xii.] He was also visited by sickness, like Job 6:4., and 19:21.

37:4.text: Sins. These occasion my great affliction, when I reflect on thy justice.
St. Augustine explains all this of original sin.
Jesus bore the weight of all our sins, which are above our comprehension, ver. 5.

37:5.text: Me. They press upon me like a deluge or huge weight.
The sin of David had many aggravations.
His spirit was almost overwhelmed, as the sins which are not bewailed, bring on other transgressions.
It is not so much sin as the neglect of penance, which destroys men.
This folly is here acknowledged by David.

37:6.text: Foolishness, or sin. Shame has made me conceal the state of my soul; and hence evil has increased.
The poisoned wound has infected other parts which were sound: (Worthington) chaburoth denotes sores, or "wounds still fresh or mortifying," (Haydock) rather than scars.
All sin is properly styled foolishness, as no prudent man should commit any.

37:7.text: End. Hebrew, "exceedingly," while a person feels his state is not desperate.
Sorrowful. Hebrew, "in black," which may refer either to the mourning attire, (Calmet) or to the "countenance."
Grief will allow me to take no rest.
I dare not look up to heaven, (Calmet) nor can I walk straight, to perform any good work (Worthington) of myself.

37:8.text: Loins. Psoai, as the Alexandrian and Complutensian Septuagint read, though the Vatican has psuche, soul, (Haydock) with the Arabic, etc.

David acknowledges that the irregular motions of concupiscence were an effect of his transgression. The Jews and Greeks place these sensations in the loins, 3 Kings 8:10. Plato triplicem finxit animam, cujus principatum in capite, iram in pectore, cupiditatem subter praecordia locavit.

37:9.text: Heart. I give vent to my inward grief by loud lamentations.

37:10.text: Desire, to be restored to thy favour.

37:11.text: I was no longer endued with the spirit of prophecy, (St. Basil) till my conversion, (St. Augustine) nor an object of favour.
I was abandoned to myself, (Calmet) quitting thy light.
My eyes have been hurt by weeping (Haydock) and maladies, (Calmet) while my heart is become so corrupt, that I do not relish or discern spiritual things.
The beauty of virtue, and the enormity of vice, do not strike me.

37:12.text: Have. Hebrew, "over-against my wound (Haydock) or leprosy." (Calmet) Protestants, "stand aloof from my sore."
But the original means rather "near to." Septuagint may have read u for i, in nighi.

But Symmachus and St. Jerome translate, "stood against my leprosy, (Haydock) and my neighbour stood at a distance;" as if they had been afraid of the contagion. Job (xix. 13, 19.) makes the same complaints.
These manners of worldlings may be seen in every age, and ought not to fill us with surprise.
David was abandoned by Achitophel, and by most of the tribe of Juda, (Theodoret) as our Saviour was by his disciples.

37:13.text: Violence. Hebrew, "laid snares." This treatment might be expected from enemies, since friends proved so treacherous.
They would willingly have slain David. The will is often put for the deed.

Things. Endeavouring to engage me again in sin, being displeased because I have quitted their evil company; (Worthington) or they raise their fortune, by causing dissensions in the state. This might be well applied to the Pharisees, who persecuted Christ.

37:14.text: Mouth. I utterly renounce all sin.
David would make no reply to Semei, (Theodoret) nor our Saviour to Pilate.
Silence is often the best defence.
Eagerness to justify one's self, causes trouble and disedification.
The prophet joins the deaf and dumb; as those who have naturally the former defect, are also affected with the latter.

37:16.text: Hoped. This was the reason of his silence.
He knew that God was in a manner engaged to defend those (Calmet) who rely (Worthington) wholly on Him; and he declared such to be his disposition.
Hear me. The same term thahane means, "wilt answer" (Mont.) as a judge and advocate.
God will one day manifest the justice of his elect.

37:17.text: For. On this account I turn to thee, and entreat thee not to suffer my enemies to gain the victory.
My humble and earnest prayer is another motive of confidence.
My enemies is not expressed in Hebrew.
I decline saying any thing in my own defence, lest I should offend God.

37:18.text: St. Jerome ad plagas. Chaldean, "for calamity." (Berthier) See Psalm 34:15. I speak not through impatience, as I know that my sins deserve still more.
This was admirably verified in Jesus Christ, the victim for our sins.
Before me. I cannot forget my transgression.

37:19.text: Sin. Though Thou knowest all things, I will confess that I may be saved; and will meditate on what I have deserved.
He goes to the source of his malady.
"Be not secure after confession of thy sin, as being always ready to confess and to offend. Declare thy iniquities in such a manner as to take care on account of thy sin."
Confession without repentance is only a mockery.

37:20.text: Art thou deaf to my cries?

37:21.text: Detracted me, by insinuating that my repentance is hypocrisy. Another mode of detracting is by making known secret faults, (Worthington) as calumny imputes false ones.
Goodness. Septuagint, "justice." Many Greek and Latin copies add: "They have rejected me, the beloved, (Theodoret; Arabic) as one dead, as an abomination."

37:23.text: Attend. Hebrew, "hasten;" which is the sense of prosches, "attend."

38:1.text: Idithun was one of the four chief masters of music, called Ethan, 1 Paralipomenon 6:44., and Idithun, 1 Paralipomenon 16:41. Some think that he was the author of this psalm; but it was rather given to him by David to sing.
The title shews that the psalms were designed for the public service of the Church, and not for David alone.

This refers to the Christian Church, though some explain it of the Jews in captivity, (Worthington) with R. Salomon, while others think that it was composed during some of David's persecutions. It is connected with the preceding, and with the two next psalms.

38:2.text: Tongue. The matter is very delicate and important, James 3:2., Proverbs 18:21., Isaias 32:17., and Ecclesiasticus 22:33., and 28:28.
Me, and was treating me with injustice and calumny.
Chilo, the sage, said: "I know how to bear ill treatment," (Laert. 1.) and this is a proof of "the greatest wisdom and virtue."
Weak men seek revenge; but the wise resolve to govern their tongues, and do not stand up in their own just defence, though they be, therefore, more persecuted.

38:3.text: Renewed. I was conscious of no offence against my enemies, (Haydock) but I reflected that I had forfeited my virtue, (Eusebius) and therefore gave vent to my grief.
Being afraid of saying any thing amiss, I refrained from saying what was good. But I perceived that this was wrong.
I deprived myself of all pleasure.
The seven first verses detail the arguments used by philosophers to comfort man, which all prove of little service. We must have recourse to God, ver. 8.

38:4.text: Out. This alludes to his sorrow for his sins, (Origen) or to the fire of charity, which is enkindled by meditation on the last end, etc., (ver. 5.) or rather it means, that while he repressed his tongue, he could not but feel an inward zeal and indignation, (Calmet) in consequence of grief suppressed.

38:5.text: End, as I desire to die, like Elias, 3 Kings xix.
The just have frequently expressed such sentiments, to move God to pity, (Job 7:1., and Psalm 101:4.) though they wished to live, that they might praise God on earth, (Calmet) if it were his will.
This text may indicate the impatience (Berthier) of the mere philosopher, (Haydock) or David desires to know to what a decree of perfection he must arrive.

38:6.text: Symmachus, "a spithame, or twelve fingers' breadth," perhaps in allusion to the Greek proverb, a "spithame of life;" which denotes one very short.

The Greek copies vary: some read, (Calmet) with the Vatican palaias, "ancient;" and others of the palestra with the Alexandrian palaistas, or "contentious." I am obliged always to wrestle with my adversaries.
My days are short, and spent in conflicts.

St. Chrysostom, St. Ambrose, etc., mention both. The former word is adopted by the Arabic, Ethiopic, etc.; but the Hebrew has tephachoth, "of a palm," or four fingers' breadth; (Calmet) and St. Jerome breves, "short."
Living. Hebrew, "standing," how well soever he may seem to be established. Protestants, "at his best estate, is altogether vanity. Selah."
The wisest of men confirms this at large, Ecclesiastes 1:1.
"What is this long while which has an end?"

38:7.text: Image, "of God." (St. Gregory, etc.) Hebrew, "in a shadow or darkness," where the fall of a leaf affrights him. Life is so short and miserable, why should we strive to heap up riches?
For whom. Hebrew, "who shall gather," etc.
The term is used respecting harvest rather than money.
Hebrew has disquieted in the plural, and the rest of the words in the singular; but St. Jerome agrees with us, conturbatur....et ignorat cui dimittat ea.
The prophet still utters complaints. One step farther is necessary to ensure peace.
He acknowledges that his life is but a shadow, and that we ought not to grieve for temporal losses.

38:8.text: I can depend only on thee.

38:9.text: Thou hast. Hebrew lo, "do not;" ne, or nonne; or "hast thou not made?" etc., as the following verse intimates.
Thou hast suffered me to be reproached by the foolish, who prosper in this world.
The fool may denote the devil, (St. Jerome; Origen) and all the lovers of iniquity.

38:10.text: It. St. Augustine reads "me," conformably to some copies of the Septuagint, Arabic, etc.
He is at a loss to explain the reason of the prophet, and suggests that this perhaps ought to be referred to the following sentence, "Because thou hast made me, remove," etc.
Such is the inconvenience of having incorrect copies.
The Alexandrian and Vatican Septuagint both have me, (Haydock) which his omitted in Complut.
David knew that he was scourged by divine Providence.

38:11.text: In (thy) rebukes, belongs to the next verse in Hebrew and Septuagint, referring to man in general, unless the prophet mean himself.

38:12.text: Spider. St. Jerome, "moth." Symmachus, "thou dissolvest like corruption his desirable thing;" (Haydock) which means the soul, (Berthier) or "beauty." (Protestants) Remorse of conscience and God's judgments make a man pine away.
Disquieted is obelized in the Septuagint.
It is not found in the Alexandrian and Comp. edition (Haydock) and seems to be taken from ver. 7. It does not alter the sense.
As a spider which has consumed its moisture, so he decays.

38:13.text: Were. 1 Paralipomenon 29:15. I can expect aid from no other but thee.

38:14.text: More. In a state to do good.
Grant me relief, Ecclesiastes 9:10., and Job 7:8.

39:1.text: Psalm. Protestants intimate that this was not in the Hebrew; but we find mizmor, "canticle," which is equivalent.
David speaks of his own restoration to health as a figure of Jesus Christ, who is principally intended, Hebrews 10:7. The end of the psalm is nearly the same with the 69th.
Some arbitrarily (Berthier) explain the words with relation to the revolt of Absalom.

Others think it may refer to the captives, (Ven. Bede) to Daniel, or Jeremias, rescued from prison. See Theodoret, who explains it of men waiting for the general resurrection. It may express the sentiments of the Church, when the persecutions ceased.
Christ sometimes speaks in his own name, and sometimes in that of his members.
It is certain that David had Christ in view; and if he alludes to himself, it is only as the figure of him.

39:2.text: God has, at last, granted my request.

39:3.text: Misery. Hebrew, "confusion," (Berthier) or "noise," (St. Jerome; Haydock) from the greatest danger.
Dregs. Mud, Jeremias 38:6. St. Augustine, etc., explain this of the Christian saved by faith from the sink of his sins.

39:4.text: I was before uttering complaints, now I give thanks with joy, for my health and conversion.
Song. Hebrew, "Praise." The penitent changes his language, which is no longer understood by worldlings.

Many. St. Augustine reads, the just, who take part in the welfare of their brethren, (Psalm 31:11.) while the wicked are filled with alarm, at the ways of God; who humbles or exalts people as he pleases.

39:5.text: Vanities. Hebrew, "the proud, nor such as turn aside to lies."
All the world is vanity, (Psalm 38:6., etc.; Calmet) though idolatry may be here meant.

39:6.text: Thoughts, or designs, "over us," as Hebrew adds. No one can fathom the counsels of the Lord. It is folly, therefore, to attack his mysteries.
Like. Protestants, "and thy thoughts which are to usward, they cannot be reckoned up in order unto thee." Literally, non ordinare apud te.
But haroc means also aequiparare, which corresponds with the Vulgate.
Number. Christ and his apostles preached, so that many followed their doctrine.
David also had many witnesses of his gratitude. They crowded round him. Some would improperly make multiplicati sunt, agree with mirabilia, dialogismois, which is in the masculine.
"I find no order before thee; if I would declare and number, they are more (wonders) than can be counted."
I am at a loss how to express myself, and must be content with the interior sentiments of gratitude. See Psalm 70:15.

39:7.text: Sacrifice and oblation. Neither bloody nor unbloody sacrifices of the law will do.
Pierced ears. Septuagint and St. Paul read, a body thou hast fitted to me, Hebrews 10:5.
Nobilius mentions, that he found the reading of the Vulgate in one Greek manuscript in Eusebius, etc.
The Arabic has both. "Thou hast prepared a body for me, and opened my ears."

Both are, in effect, of divine authority. The version adopted by St. Paul, cannot be rejected, no more than the Hebrew confirmed by the Vulgate. James Pierce asserts, that the Hebrew is incorrect, oznaim being put for az zip, "then a body," as the letters are not unlike. The dissertation is ingenious: the author is, however, suspected of Socinianism. We know not the reason why the Vulgate here abandons the Septuagint. The sense is much the same; the prophet noticing the entire obedience of the Messias, (Berthier) and the apostle comprising his whole person.
His body was miraculous, (Haydock) and the incarnation the work of God.

Nothing could come up to his submission. "Thou has dug ears for me," (St. Jerome; Haydock) alluding to the custom of making slaves for ever, (Exodus 21:5.) or "thou hast fitted, (Calmet) opened, (Protestants) my ears," enabling me to hear, and to obey.
The sacrifice of Christ was never interrupted, from the first moment of his incarnation.
He was always doing the will of his Father.

This sacrifice is the most essential. God rejected all such as were destitute of this condition, or were not offered by people determined to observe the whole law, 1 Kings 15:22., Isaias 1:11., and Jeremias 7:22.

No sacrifice of the Old Testament was sufficient to satisfy God's justice for sin. Christ, by the ear of obedience, performed the redemption of man by his death, as was determined from eternity. See Hebrews 10.

And is omitted in the Latin version of St. Paul, holocautomata pro peccato, inadvertently, or rather to intimate, that he was speaking of the holocaust of expiation, Hebrews 10:6, 8., and 13:11., and Leviticus 16:27.

St. Augustine also admits only one species of sacrifice, "holocausts likewise for sin." But others distinguish them from the victims designed to expiate the sins of individuals, (Leviticus v., etc.) of which the prophet also speaks.

39:8.text: Head, or beginning, (Genesis 1., John 1., and 8:25.; St. Jerome, etc.) or at the commencement of this book of Psalms, (St. Augustine) or rather in the whole Bible.
Kephalis denotes a volume, (Suidas) or stick, on which books were formerly rolled, being written on parchment. The Jews still observe the same custom in their synagogues.
Hebrew, "In the volume of the book," means, in the book, (Amama) or the Bible, which is the book by excellence, where the incarnation and death of Christ, for man's redemption, are clearly specified.
This is the sum of the Scriptures.
They bear witness to Christ, John 5:39., and Luke 24:27.

The apostle uses the word capitulum, for the sum, Hebrews viii. Whatever sense be chosen, we should meditate on this head, or volume. But Christ signed, as it were, this solemn engagement, from all eternity. If we adopt the passage to David, we may translate, "I come, having on me the volume of thy Scriptures." See 4 Kings 11:12.

39:9.text: Heart. So the Vatican Septuagint reads; while other editions have koilias, "belly."
I love the law so much, that I would hide it in my bowels, (Calmet) or in the most secret place.

39:10.text: Thy, is not expressed in Hebrew or Greek, but understood.
Church, in the tabernacle, (Theodoret) or rather in the Catholic Church; the propagation of which, (Haydock) and the preaching of the gospel throughout the world are foretold.
The justice, or mercies of our Saviour, are every where proclaimed.

39:11.text: Thy. Some copies of the Septuagint have, my justice, as well as the Ethiopic version.
But the Vulgate is more correct.
Council. Christ conceals not his mercy and truth from the greatest and wisest congregations. He spoke boldly before Annas and Caiphas, as St. Paul did at Athens, etc.
David testifies his gratitude, and invites all to praise God with him.

But we must particularly learn from our Saviour, a horror of sin; the knowledge of his mysteries; confidence in his mercy; and a conviction, that we can never be saved but by his grace. He has announced these things, and then he finishes his career, by suffering for us, and pours forth his supplications to God.

39:12.text: Withhold not. The prophet now speaks in the name of Christ's mystical body, the Church, praying to be made a partaker of mercy, and to be delivered from evils, (Worthington) or Christ speaks as the victim for our sins.
Uphold me. This might be also rendered as a prayer, "May thy," etc., with the Hebrew and some copies of the Septuagint.

39:13.text: My iniquities. That is, the sins of all mankind, which I have taken upon me.
The sins even of those who believe, are so numerous, that they cannot be seen in particular. We may faint at the sight of so many sins committed by Christians.
Christ had all the sins of mankind laid upon him.
He did not suffer to release those who were already damned; though they had received sufficient graces, in consequence of the merits of his future death.
Christ knew the number and enormity of sin.
But he would not disclose his knowledge.

39:14.text: Be pleased. The rest is nearly transcribed, Psalm lxix.
The Church prays for her weak members.

39:15.text: Backward, as those who came to seize Christ were twice, John 18:6.
Shame might have proved very salutary to them.

39:16.text: 'Tis well. The Hebrew here is an interjection of insult and derision, like the Vah, Matthew 27:40.
As St. Jerome here expresses it, Vah, Vah. See Mark 15:29.
The Jews have now become objects of contempt, (Calmet) a just punishment (Haydock) of scoffers, who wish evil to the good.

39:17.text: Magnified. Thus may those speak, who sincerely love God.

39:18.text: Beggar. King David might assume this title, as well as all mankind.
The same may be applied to Christ, according to his human nature, as the end of this psalm, and the following, belong to him, more than to David.
He speaks in the name of penitents, whose sins he had undertaken to wash away.
Slack. The faithful prayed for the coming of our Saviour, as they still entreat him to hasten the reward of the good.

40:1.text: Himself; implying, that David composed this psalm, though the word is not expressed in Hebrew or Greek.
The same articles, however, occur, which have been thus rendered before.
Some explain this psalm of the sickness of Ezechias, (Ven. Bede) or of that of David, a little before the revolt of Absalom.
This may be described as a figure of our Saviour's sufferings.

For it would be rash not to acknowledge, that He is here the principal object in view, (Theodoret) since he has applied (ver. 10.) to the traitor's conduct, (Calmet) and all the rest may properly allude to the same events. The psalmist speaks of the Messias in the six first verses, and introduces him, in the remainder, uttering his own sentiments, (Berthier) respecting his passion and resurrection.

40:2.text: Understandeth. Believing with eagerness, (Haydock) or reflecting seriously on Jesus Christ, (Berthier) who was pleased to be poor for our sakes.

And the poor, is not in the ancient Septuagint, (Eusebius) nor Hebrew, etc. But it only expresses the same idea as the word needy, (Berthier) being added to show the extreme misery to which our Saviour was reduced.
The Fathers explain the passage in this sense, though some would suppose, that David speaks of his own conduct, (Calmet) or of those who adhered to him in his distress, while most followed Absalom.
Day of death or judgment. Happy the man, who makes the life of Christ his constant meditation, (Berthier) and endeavours to imitate his example, and divine charity!

The Church recites this psalm for the sick. Those who assist them may hope for similar treatment. But such as are not scandalized at Christ, on account of his poverty and afflictions, may be pronounced blessed, (Luke 7:23.) as He will deliver them from distress, if they place their confidence in Him.
The sick are relieved, when they think on Christ's sufferings.
Preserve. Hebrew, "will preserve....and thou wilt not deliver him unto the will of his enemies."
But St. Jerome has, "and he will not," etc.
Sixtus V reads, "into the hands of his enemy," after St. Augustine, etc. Others add, "he will purify his soul from, or on the earth."
Our Lord will give to such servants more grace in this life, and glory in the next, nor will he suffer them to yield to temptation.
He will defend them and heal them, when sick.

40:3.text: Understandeth. Believing with eagerness, (Haydock) or reflecting seriously on Jesus Christ, (Berthier) who was pleased to be poor for our sakes.

And the poor, is not in the ancient Septuagint, (Eusebius) nor Hebrew, etc. But it only expresses the same idea as the word needy, (Berthier) being added to show the extreme misery to which our Saviour was reduced.
The Fathers explain the passage in this sense, though some would suppose, that David speaks of his own conduct, (Calmet) or of those who adhered to him in his distress, while most followed Absalom.
Day of death or judgment. Happy the man, who makes the life of Christ his constant meditation, (Berthier) and endeavours to imitate his example, and divine charity!

The Church recites this psalm for the sick. Those who assist them may hope for similar treatment. But such as are not scandalized at Christ, on account of his poverty and afflictions, may be pronounced blessed, (Luke 7:23.) as He will deliver them from distress, if they place their confidence in Him.
The sick are relieved, when they think on Christ's sufferings.
Preserve. Hebrew, "will preserve....and thou wilt not deliver him unto the will of his enemies."
But St. Jerome has, "and he will not," etc.
Sixtus V reads, "into the hands of his enemy," after St. Augustine, etc. Others add, "he will purify his soul from, or on the earth."
Our Lord will give to such servants more grace in this life, and glory in the next, nor will he suffer them to yield to temptation.
He will defend them and heal them, when sick.

40:4.text: His bed. Literally, "on the bed of his sorrow." His, seems to have been formerly in Hebrew, (Houbigant) though it be now omitted, (Berthier) as it was in the time of Symmachus, "the bed of misery," (St. Jerome) of infirmity.

Thou hast. Hebrew, "thou wilt make." Protestants, "turn," (marginal note; Haydock) "change, or take away." In the east, the bed was removed entirely, (John 5:8.) and this expression may denote, (Calmet) that the sick man should be cured, and no longer be confined to his bed, (St. Chrysostom) or that God would take him by the hand, to support him, and turn his bed, like a tender mother, to make it more comfortable.
When the just are sick unto death, Christ will give them greater consolation.

He will withdraw their affections from all terrestrial things, and remove whatever has been dangerous to them. (St. Gregory, Mor. 23:15.) The ineffable name has been thrice repeated in these verses, to insinuate, that all good is wrought by the blessed Trinity.

40:5.text: Thee. Christ prays for his members, acknowledging their sins, (Worthington) which he had undertaken to expiate. The Fathers explain this of his prayer in the garden.
Have we ever reflected on sin, which reduced the Lord of all, to such poverty and distress?

40:6.text: Perish? When shall we have a change, and see Absalom on the throne? When shall we get rid of this man, who reproves our conduct? So were the Jews animated to destroy Christ.
The rest of the psalm more visibly relates to him.
For after they had put him to death, he rose again, and his name and kingdom became more glorious.

40:7.text: If he, any one among my enemies.
The Scriptures often pass from the plural to the singular, (Berthier) to comprise every one distinctly.
Yet St. Augustine, etc., read "they came," etc., omitting if, as some of the Septuagint editions do likewise: though inaccurately, according to St. Jerome and Sun.
It occurs in the Roman copy, and Grabe inserts it in a smaller type. The sense is not altered.

The conspirators affected to shew David some marks of civility, to obtain their ends. The Jews often strove to entangle Jesus, by their questions, (Matthew 19:3., and 22:17, 24., and John 8:3.) while Judas continued in his company, to gratify his own avarice, and to betray him.
Those who came maliciously to hear Christ, blamed him as an enemy to the law, or as one who cast out devils by Beelzebub.

40:8.text: To me, seems useless, though it be added conformably to the Hebrew, (Berthier) or rather it intimates, that the enemies made no secret of their plots.

40:9.text: Word of affecting the regal power, etc.

No more? Jesus Christ speaks. They have unjustly condemned me: But can I not rise again? or the words may be put in the mouth of his enemies. Shall we have any thing to fear from the dead? If we were to confine him only, he might perhaps escape.
Hebrew, "an evil disease, say they, cleaveth fast unto him: and now that he lieth, he shall rise up no more."
"The word of the devil they poured out against themselves; he who hath slept, shall rise no more."
Yet lo may be explained, an non, "shall not he," etc. Septuagint have seen this insulting interrogation of the Jews who ridiculed what Christ had said of his future resurrection.
They determined to put him to death; but they could not prevent his glorious (Worthington) appearance again on the third day.
Those who explain this of David, say, that the sleep denotes a mortal illness, or a grievous fault, for which it was expected, that the king would die.

40:10.text: Bread. This characterizes the traitor, who had recieved the holy Communion, and had been intrusted with the purse by our Saviour, yet betrayed him with the sign of peace.
To violate the laws of hospitality was greatly resented by the very pagans.
Supplanted me, or kicked like a wild colt, as Plato complained that Aristotle had done, when he set up another school. (Haydock) Emas apelaktise.
David might allude to Absalom, though the Holy Ghost speaks of Judas.

Our Saviour himself says, (Worthington) that the Scriptures may be fulfilled, he that eateth bread with me, shall lift up his heel against me: Qui manducat mecum panem levabit contra me calcaneum suum: eperen ep eme ten pternan autou, "has lifted up," etc., as the Hebrew expresses it here. Judas had attempted to betray Christ already, and would do it more effectually hereafter; so that both the present and future might agree with him. We also find the psalm translated qui edebat panes meos, etc. But the difference is very small.
To lift up the heel, is the posture of one who attempts to supplant his adversary.

40:11.text: Them. No one is ignorant of the destruction of Jerusalem, and of the miserable condition of the Jews (Menochius) throughout the world.
Christ will render every one according to his deserts.

40:12.text: Over me. Thus the divinity of Christ was proved, since he rose victorious, in spite of his enemies.

40:13.text: Innocence. Jesus was the spotless lamb incapable of sin. He effaced it by his blood, and is therefore crowned with glory, Hebrews 2:9., and Philippians 2:9.
This innocence made him a fit victim for sin.

40:14.text: So be it. Chaldean, "Amen." This word, at the beginning of a discourse, implies an affirmative oath; (Matthew 6:13.) and at the end, it is a mark of approbation, Numbers 5:22.
Here the Jews terminate the first book of the psalms, which they divide into five.
St. Jerome rejects this division, as our Saviour mentions only the psalms, and the last psalm has no such conclusion.
It has Alleluia. All the rest have Amen. See Psalms lxxii., lxxxix., and cvi.

The observations which have been made in this first part, will serve to explain many other passages, on which we shall therefore be shorter, as well as in specifying the variations from the original, which are for the most part only apparent, as the intelligent reader may be convinced, by the preceding remarks.

41:1.text: Core, who composed, (Tirinus) or sung (Menochius) most of the psalms in this second part.
Pieces of a joyful nature were generally assigned to them, according to the Jews and St. Jerome, Psalm lxxxiv.

They were descendants of the famous schismatic, the miraculous preservation of whose innocent children teaches us, that the good will not be punished with the guilty, and that we must be raised above this earth, lest hell devour us, Numbers 16:31., and 26:10.
The sentiments of the captives, (Calmet) and of every sincere Christian, are here expressed.
David may have been the author, (Calmet) as it is generally believed.

41:2.text: Waters. This was sung at the baptism of Catechumens, (St. Augustine) teaching them to thirst after heaven.
The hart being infected with poison, thirsts exceedingly, as sinners must do for pardon.

41:3.text: Strong. Most Bibles before Clement VIII read "fountain."
El signifies both God and strong.

The Levites desired earnestly to serve God in his temple; Christians must wish to appear before him in heaven, (Calmet) when they will be free from temptations. Idols may destroy, but they cannot give life.

41:4.text: Bread. Ovid imitates this: Cura dolorque animi lachrymaeque alimenta fuere.
The tears of compunction obtain the remission of sin.
God. Thus the idolaters derided those who could not point at their God.
The Babylonians had conquered all the surrounding nations, and despised their deities.
The wicked laugh at the just, who are for a time in distress, comforting themselves with weeping.
Those who saw David wandering (Haydock) in the mountains, at a distance from the tabernacle, might ask him what religion or God he followed.

41:5.text: These sarcasms fill me with grief, (Calmet) while the solemn ceremonies of religion, which, I remember, where observed in the temple, cause my heart to overflow with joy.

I shall. Protestants, "I had gone with the multitude; I went with them to the house of God with the voice of joy and praise; with a multitude that kept holiday." Yet the holidays of the Catholic Church are now ridiculed by many Protestants.
The original may have several other meanings. The tabernacle may here designate the musach of the Levites, 4 Kings 16:18.
Feasting. Some such religious feasts were prescribed, Deuteronomy 12:12.
David was not permitted to build the temple, nor to enter the tabernacle: but he speaks of heaven.

41:6.text: My countenance. Hebrew, "his," as Aquila, etc., read.
Yet as the words are repeated, (ver. 12.) there seems to be a fault in the text, (Berthier) owing to v, "his" being taken in here, instead of explaining it by and, ver. 7.
The arrangement of the letters in the Vulgate is preferable.

41:7.text: Little hill of Sion. I hope that I shall soon again behold the fertile regions along the Jordan.
But these hills of Hermon, etc., are nothing when compared with heaven: They serve only to remind us of our banishment.
The difficulties of our present abode, hemmed in on all sides, teach us to place our hopes in heaven.

41:8.text: Flood-gates. The Hebrews imagined there were immense reservoirs of water above, (Calmet) which might serve to drown the earth, as at the deluge, Genesis 7:11. Both heaven and earth seemed to be armed against the psalmist.

One affliction succeeded another, (Calmet) and God appeared to have abandoned his servants to temptations. But he enables them to come off with victory, and fills them with more joy in their trials: so that they may sing in heart, and pray.

41:9.text: Night. In affliction, as well as in prosperity, we must praise the Lord. Roman Septuagint, "in the night he will manifest it."
This is very beautiful, but not agreeable with the original.

41:11.text: Whilst. Protestants As, "with a sword in my bones, mine enemies reproach me."
Thus the martyrs were tortured and upbraided.

41:12.text: Countenance. To whom I look up with confidence.
The just are comforted with the hope of God's sight.

42:1.text: David. Septuagint add, "it has no title, in Hebrew," being composed by the same author, and on the same subject, as the preceding [psalm].
David teaches the faithful how to begin a good work; and priests how they ought to officiate at Mass.
Holy. Hebrew, "merciful." The Babylonians and their king, treated the Jews with great cruelty.
After we have proved ourselves, according to the admonition of St. Paul, (1 Corinthians xi.) before approaching to the holy Eucharist, we may beg of God to judge and to protect us.

42:2.text: Me. Without thy assistance, I can do nothing. My enemies seem too strong, while thou appearest to disregard my prayer.

42:3.text: Light, your Messias, as the Jews confess, (Jarchi) truth, the holy Spirit.
Both the titles may be applied to our Saviour.
We are in the utmost distress; be pleased to send us relief.
As thou hast sent Christ into the world to impart these graces, grant that we may know, and comply with our duties, before we approach to thy holy altar.
Holy hill, the Church, (Worthington) or tabernacle on Sion, where the Jews wished to be present.

42:4.text: Youth. St. Jerome, "the God of my joy and exultation."
Syriac and Arabic agree with us, and Gil means, (Calmet) a young man, in Arabic and Hebrew.
People in youth, shew for the effects of joy.
Accompanied with light, and a pure intention, we may offer sacrifice to God, who changeth our corruptions into newness of life.
The Levites might sing near the altar, but could not offer victims.

42:5.text: My God. This word is singular; but the former "Elohim," is plural, to intimate one God in three persons.
Harp. Hebrew cinnor, which Symmachus renders, "the psaltery." The sons of Core were chiefly door-keepers: but they also played on musical instruments.

42:6.text: God. Trust in God, whom I hope to see face to face.

43:1.text: David or the Corites composed this piece, to comfort the just under persecution.

It may allude to the situation of the Jews at Babylon, (Calmet) or under Antiochus Epiphanes, (Ven. Bede) though St. Paul, (Romans 8:36.) applies ver. 22. to the persecutions of the primitive Christians, which seems to shew, that the whole psalm refers to them, (Berthier) as the Fathers have explained it. Yet it may literally be understood of the Israelites (Calmet) also, (Haydock) as well as the Christian martyrs, since all things happened to the Israelites in figure.

43:2.text: Old, in calling Abraham, and rescuing the Hebrews for the Egyptian bondage, etc.
God formerly protected our Fathers; but how are things changed?
Gideon uses nearly the same words, Judges vi.

43:3.text: Plantedst them, thy people. See Jeremias 2:21., and 12:10., and Ezechiel 17:6.
Out, by means of insects, etc., Wisdom 12:8., and Josue 24:12.
The Hebrews were miraculously assisted, (Worthington) without any merit of their own, (Calmet) as all were wicked at first. Yet Abraham was freely chosen, that the visible Church might be preserved.

43:5.text: Saving (salutes) the manifold instances of protection.

43:6.text: This we have done in former times.
Not man's strength, but God's favour, granteth the victory.

43:9.text: We have always attributed our success to thee.

43:10.text: Now. He foretelleth divers states of the Jews, and of the Church.

43:11.text: To. Literally, "after," post. They were formerly defeated; now it is our turn.

43:12.text: Nations. Many never returned from captivity. (Calmet) See Deuteronomy 28:43.

43:13.text: Reckoning. Protestants, "thou dost not increase thy wealth by their price."
Thou art eager to get quit of them, as of the vilest slaves. (Theodoret; Grotius) (Isaias 50:2., and 52:3.) Exchange. Some copies of the Septuagint read allalagmasi, "jubilations," with the Ethiopic.
While others have more correctly allagmasi, like the Vulgate. There were none to purchase, so that thou hast given the people for nothing.

At the last siege of Jerusalem, the Jews, who had sold (Worthington) or bought Christ for thirty pieces of money, (Haydock) were themselves sold for the smallest price; thirty being given for one penny. See Josephus, Jewish Wars.

43:15.text: The Gentiles propose us as an example of a people fallen a prey to the divine indignation, 4 Kings 19:21., and Lamentations 2:15.

43:17.text: Detracteth me. St. Jerome, "blasphemeth" God, while they upbraid us, as a faithless people.

43:18.text: Covenant. Till the passion of Christ, the Jews did not wholly fall from God, and then many were chosen from among them, to found the Christian religion.
The Corites speak in the name of their faithful brethren, whom no provocation had induced to follow the superstitious practices of Babylon.

43:19.text: But the negative particle is taken (Worthington) from the first part of the verse.

Yet many adhere to the Hebrew, etc., "And thou hast turned," etc. Though it may be taken in a dangerous sense, (Calmet) yet it may only signify, (Haydock) that God had removed his people from the country where his worship was observed, and had refused them his protection; hence their paths have been unfortunate.
This agrees better with the sequel.
It is no proof that God is the author of sin, in either sense.

43:20.text: Affliction. Hebrew, or "dragons," (St. Jerome; Menochius) alluding to the deserts, to which the Jews retired, when the Assyrians invaded them. The text may be more applicable to the first Christians, who might truly say, that they had not transgressed. (Houbigant) -- To the martyrs, death was but like a shadow.
Here it denotes great darkness and misery.

43:21.text: If we. This is a Hebrew idiom, to express, we have not.

43:22.text: Slaughter. They will not suffer us to be quiet respecting our religion, striving to delude us: or, if we prove resolute, like Daniel, they expose us to torments.

This was more fully verified at the first propagation of the gospel, (Romans 8:36.) as it still continues to be among those, who cease not to persecute Catholics by artifice, as well as by open violence. Christians in every age (Haydock) are persecuted, more than the prophets (Worthington) generally were.

43:23.text: Sleepest thou, seeming not to attend to our sufferings.
End, shall we never be restored to favour?

43:25.text: Dust. We are at death's door, being oppressed with grief, as long as thou dost not assist us.
We petition in the most fervent (Haydock) and humble posture, Isaias 49:23., and Micheas 7:17.

44:1.text: For them that shall be changed, i.e., For souls happily changed, by being converted to God; (Challoner) or it may allude to the variety of speakers here introduced.
Protestants leave shoshannim, which some translate, "on the lilies," (Aquila; St. Jerome) or "instrument of six strings."
The beloved, viz., our Lord Jesus Christ.

Hebrew, "of loves;" or of the young women, friends of the bride, (yedidoth) who sung the Epithalamium, as we see in the 18th Idyl of Theocritus. The Jews formerly explained this psalm of the Messias, as well as all the Fathers after the apostles, Hebrew, 1:8. Many passages cannot refer to Solomon's marriage with the daughter of Pharao, though some might be referred to that event, as a figure of Christ's union with his Church.
The whole had better be understood of Christ, (Berthier) being intended for the instruction of all converts from paganism and schism.

44:2.text: Uttered. Hebrew rachash, "boileth," as one unable to contain himself.
Speak, or "dedicate," dico, (Haydock) though here it only means to speak.
He addresses the object of his praise, instead of invoking the muses.
Swiftly. I have not to meditate.
The Holy Ghost moves my tongue, (2 Peter 1:21.) as fast as my hands can write, Jeremias 36:18., and 4 Esdras 14:39.
High mysteries, in honour of the great king, occupy my thoughts, and to him I refer this canticle.

44:3.text: Beautiful. The corporal beauty of Christ may be problematical.
But justice is the truest beauty.
All admired his eloquence, (Luke 4:22., and John 7:46.) and innumerable converts were made, by the preaching of his word.
The young women here address the spouse.

Therefore, I say, (Rabbins) or "because" God hath chosen thee freely. Solomon was styled the beloved, (2 Kings 12:25.) and was highly favoured, Wisdom 8:20., and 3 Kings 4:29., etc. But this was only a figure of Jesus Christ, (Calmet) whose hypostatical union was an effect of gratuitous predestination, (St. Augustine, praed. xv.) though his other graces were merited.
He was most excellent in all sorts of gifts.

44:4.text: Mighty. (Potentissime) Erasmus (Apol. con. Sutor.) complains, that he could not learn, whether this was a noun or an adverb, without consulting the originals.

44:5.text: Reign. Devise, execute, and perfect the establishment of thy spiritual kingdom.
Solomon was no warrior; but he only wanted enemies to be so. The sword of Christ is his word, (Hebrews 4:12.) anger, (Apocalypse 19:15.; Calmet) or human nature.
Some translate, "and ride," because kings were mounted on chariots, and governed their people with the reins of justice, etc.
Justice. These titles are eagerly desired by monarchs; as martial prowess, clemency, and justice, (Calmet) render them objects of terror, and of love.
Christ conquered by his miracles, mildness, etc., (Calmet) propagating the truth, and punishing the rebellious.

44:6.text: Fall. This seems to be placed too soon, in order to shew the rapidity of the conquest.
"Thy arrows are sharp, shot into the hearts," etc.
Some explain in corde, (as the Hebrew, Septuagint, etc., read) of the voluntary submission of those who had formerly been enemies of the Messias.
"Thy sharp arrows, the people subject to thee, shall fall into," etc.
Men are sometimes represented as arrows, Isaias 49:2.

44:7.text: O God, O Theos. The Septuagint thus mark the vocative case, (Psalm 138:17.; Haydock) and it is clear, that the Messias is here styled God, (Hebrews 1:8.) though some of the Jews would evade this proof by saying, "God is thy throne," 1 Paralipomenon 29:23. Even Munster translates, O Deus, and the Jew Agesila, o Thee. Elohim is never addressed to any one by the prophets, but to the true God, (Berthier) and this title alludes to the judicial character of Christ, (Acts 10:42.; Calmet) of whose kingdom there shall be no end, Luke 1:33.

Calvin is very bold in asserting, that David spoke properly of Solomon, as if the apostle had applied the text to our Saviour only in the mystical sense; whereas many things cannot belong to the former, and the Chaldean and Fathers expound this psalm of Christ and his Church.
Solomon did not persevere in wisdom, and his beauty was equalled by that of Absalom, etc.

44:7.src: Origen, contra Cels. i.; Du Hamel; Haydock
44:7.text: Crellius and Grotius in vain attempted to weaken this proof of Christ's divinity, as a Jew, who disputed with Origen, did.

44:8.text: Elohim is used in both places, (Haydock) with a singular verb, as being spoken of the Deity.
"O Elohim, thy Elohim," (Haydock) which implies more than one person in God.
Many king might be preferred to Solomon; but Christ was raised above all.

Fellows. In consequence of the free gift of God, in uniting the human nature to the second person, the Messias advanced in glory; (Haydock) or rather the prophet speaks of his subsequent merits, which entitled him to the greatest felicity.
The oil of gladness, alludes to the reward of his labours. Dia touto seems to require this sense, though the Hebrew may be rendered, "because."
Either the cause or the effect may be meant.
Solomon was chosen before many of his elder brothers; but Christ was anointed by the Holy Ghost, Acts 10:38.

44:9.text: Perfume. Literally, "from thy garments, from the ivory houses, out of which they have delighted thee, (10.) the daughters of kings, in thy honour." They esteem it an honour to wait upon thee, and perfume thy robes, which are placed in chests of ivory, with odoriferous herbs.
The ancients admired such garments, Genesis 27:27., and Amos 3:15.
They had been given, together with the ivory boxes, as a present to Solomon at his marriage, and might give him delight, (Calmet) as it was then deemed unpolite to refuse a present.
Stacte. Literally, "the drop" gutta, (Haydock) distils from the myrrh, the wood of settim, (Numbers 24:6.) and may denote aloes, but not the plant.
Mortification and humility (Worthington) raise the soul on high, 2 Corinthians 2:16.
The blessed Virgin [Mary] is here styled a house of ivory. The Church, and all who observe purity, may be considered as God's temples, 1 Corinthians 3:17.
The virtues of Christ are this precious perfume.

Houses. Septuagint Bareis, a word which means "a tower." Some have not understood this, and have rendered it, gravibus, "heavy," (St. Jerome, ad Prin.) which others have improperly correct by gradibus, "steps."
Houbigant would change three words, and translate, "from ivory vessels, the vases of thy anointing."

44:10.text: The Church is spotless, Ephesians 5:26. The attendants of this glorious queen, are the nations converted, or Christian virgins. They are not the maids of Pharao's daughter, whose marriage was never commended.
House. Paganism, and the observances of the old law.
The Church, and every faithful soul, may be styled the daughter, as well as the spouse of Christ.

44:11.text: The Church is spotless, Ephesians 5:26. The attendants of this glorious queen, are the nations converted, or Christian virgins. They are not the maids of Pharao's daughter, whose marriage was never commended.
House. Paganism, and the observances of the old law.
The Church, and every faithful soul, may be styled the daughter, as well as the spouse of Christ.

44:12.text: Lord. Hebrew adonaiic, "thy master," and worship him, (Haydock) like a dutiful wife, 1 Peter 3:6., and 3 Kings 1:16.
God is not found in the Septuagint. The title belongs to Jesus Christ, the spouse, who has been twice called God before.

44:13.text: Daughters of Tyre; the city, with her dependant villages.
Tyre might send presents on this grand occasion, or might even pay tribute, 2 Paralipomenon 9:26.

44:14.text: Is within. Roman Septuagint, etc., have "of Hesebon," by mistake, for Esothen.
Queens in the East, could not appear much abroad.
When they go out, they cannot be seen. The beauty of the Church consists in virtue, and in the grace of God.
Borders. Charity influences the exterior works of piety, (Tirinus) and gives beauty to the ceremonies (St. Basil) and decorations used by the Church, with such magnificence.

44:15.text: Neighbours. The Jews, as well as the Gentiles, shall embrace the faith.
Virginity became honourable only after the coming of Christ.

44:16.text: Temple. Even virgins (Haydock) out of the Church, cannot please the king.

44:17.text: This was the wish of those present. But it does not appear that the daughter of Pharao had any children, (Calmet) and thus it seems improbable, that she is here spoken of, as the psalmist foretells the establishment and glory (Berthier) of the Church, by means of the apostles, (St. Chrysostom) and their successors, who are made princes over all the world. Let those who are cut off acknowledge this, and come to the unity, that they may be introduced into the temple of the king.
Innumerable saints of all ranks, kings and emperors, acknowledge the Church for their mother, and submit to her.

44:18.text: They. Hebrew, Septuagint of Aldus and Complutensian, and the Greek Fathers, have "I will;" yet this is contrary to the Vatican and Alexandrian Septuagint, (Berthier) and seems less accurate.
The prophet was not to life for ever, so that the fame of the Church was to be spread by others.
Ever. There shall be pastors and faithful people to the end.

45:1.text: It may allude to the defeat of Sennacherib, (Houbigant) or might be sung by the Corites at the dedication of the second temple, when peace was restored to the world, after the death of Cambyses, Ezechiel xxxviii. The Fathers explain it of the Christian Church, delivered from persecution.

45:2.text: Troubles. Those of English Catholics have been very great; yet they increase.

45:4.text: Their. Hebrew, St. Ambrose, etc., read, "its."
Both sea and land may be in confusion; we shall fear nothing, having God for our protector.
Though many and noble personages have revolted from the faith in England, yet the Catholic Church will never fail, (Worthington) even if it should in these [British] islands.

45:5.text: Steam. Jerusalem was surrounded by placid streams, which are here opposed to the great waters, as in Isaias 8:6. The Church, after persecution, is restored to peace, and adorned with all graces.
Tabernacle. This is the source of our joy, Apocalypse xxi., (Menochius) and 22:1., and Ezechiel 47:1.
The advantages of the virtuous, both here and in heaven, are great.

45:7.text: Trembled. We have witnessed the commotions in the East, under Cyrus, and his son; the latter of whom seems to be styled Gog, (Ezechiel 38:19.) and perished in Judea, which he intended to plunder.

45:10.text: Shields. Hebrew, "the round things," which some explain, "chariots," without need.
Fire. The Fathers apply this to the peace which reigned at the birth of Christ, or to that which Constantine gave to the Church.

45:11.text: Still. We have only to admire the work of God.
He will bring all to a happy issue for his elect, though the wicked may rage.

46:1.text: This psalm appears to be a sequel to the former psalm, and is addressed to the Gentiles who were present at the dedication of the second temple, as Darius had ordered his governors to assist the Jews, 1 Esdras 6:15. See Esther 8:17.
Many explain it of the translation of the ark: but the Fathers behold the establishment of Christianity, and the ascension of Christ, ver. 6.

46:4.text: Feet. The Chanaanites were subdued by Josue, and others by David, etc. The army of Cambyses became a prey to the Jews, Ezechiel 39:10.
All who embrace the true faith, even kings, become subjects, and not heads of the Church.

46:5.text: Beauty. The temple, Ezechiel 24:21. Both Jews and Gentiles form the Church.

46:6.text: Trumpet. Christ ascended, accompanied by choirs of angels. His apostles proclaimed his truths.
They were not left desolate, but joyful; having the Paraclete sent to them.

46:7.text: King. Christ is God, by his divine nature, and our king, by his humanity.

46:8.text: Wisely. Hebrew mascil, which is so often rendered "understanding" in the titles. No one can do well, what he does not understand.
The union of faith and good works, is singing wisely.
Let each strive to know the mysteries of faith.

46:9.text: Throne. Christ reigns over the heart with all power, Matthew 28:18.

46:10.text: Gods. Judges appointed by the king of Persia over the Jews, etc., (Calmet) or rather the apostles, who were more than men, (St. Jerome) and exercised a greater power than any earthly monarch.

The richest princes have submitted to the God of Abraham, whose seed was to prove a blessing to all, Genesis 18:18. Hebrew as it is now pointed, "the princes of the people are gathered unto the people of the God of Abraham, for He is far elevated above the gods, the shields of the earth," as kings are often styled.
Protestants, "for the shields of the earth belong unto God: He is greatly exalted;" or (Septuagint) "the earthly potentates, who are of God, have been," etc.
We might explain the Vulgate in the same sense, if Dei were substituted for Dii, (Haydock) as it should be.
Dei sunt optimates terrae, et ipse summe elevatus est.

St. Jerome agrees with the Vulgate, (Haydock) which is the clearest, (Berthier) only he renders ham, "the people" of the God, as it may also signify, and retains the word shields, which we explain the strong gods, or the "strong ones of God."
