The blessed Trinity is not divided, but more distinctly professed in baptism than it was under the law.

47:1.text: On the, etc., is not in Hebrew nor Eusebius, etc. It means Sunday, (St. Ambrose; Worthington) or rather Monday, being sung on that day.
The subject of the former canticle is continued, in thanksgiving to God, for some signal victory, or for the peace which God afforded to his people, after the death of Cambyses.
The Fathers explain it of the propagation and peace of the Church.

47:2.text: City, or temple, which lay to the north of old Jerusalem, on Sion.
Yet Genebrard, etc., place this mountain south of Salem.
The concourse of priests and people gave it the appearance of a great city, Ezechiel 40:2., and Isaias 14:13.
The Church is built upon a rock. [Matthew 16:18.]
The Jews and Christians are under the greatest obligation of praising God.

47:3.text: With. Hebrew, "the beautiful situation, the joy of the whole earth, (Menochius) Sion, sides of," etc. Chaldean, "Sion, thou art beautiful as a bride." Christ's Church is thus described, Apocalypse 21:2.
Its figure, (Haydock) the temple, was the glory of the whole country, Psalm 25:8., and Lamentations 2:15.
But the Church alone extends to the sides of the north, or over the world.

47:4.text: Houses. Hebrew, "palaces;" Septuagint, "towers." Baresi, a word which has again been mistaken for gravibus or gradibus, as [in] ver. 14., and Psalm 44:9. God is the defence of his people, (Proverbs 18:10.) the Church, Matthew 16:18.
All particular houses, or churches, must come to the unity of faith, (Worthington) and to the seat of Peter.

47:5.text: Yet it is found in the Vatican Septuagint, etc.
The kings of the earth assembled against the Church, (Psalm 2:2.) as many came to oppose Jerusalem, under Cambyses, Ezechiel 38:2, 13.

47:6.text: Saw. They could not say, like Caesar, Veni, vidi, vici. For they no sooner came to invade the unsuspecting people, than they began to tremble, Ezechiel 38:11., etc., and Psalm 45:7.

47:7.text: There, denotes the promptitude of vengeance, as well as the following allusion, Isaias 13:8., and 1 Thessalonians 5:3.

47:8.text: Tharsis, such strong-built ships, as might go to Tarsus, in Cilicia, (Genesis 10:4.; Calmet) or to India.
The merchants of Tharsis, the naval officers, shall say, etc. There shall be a great commotion, etc., Ezechiel 38:13, 19.

The same storm proved fatal to the land and sea-forces of Cambyses. His navy is mentioned by Herodotus, (III. 11., and 44.) and was probably stationed over against Acco, or Ptolemais, as the king perished at the foot of Carmel.
Nothing maketh a deeper impression than the sentiments of religion. God's grace enableth the soul to sustain all conflicts, and to overcome.

47:9.text: Seen. All the promises have been fulfilled. How could any one have thought that we should have been permitted to dedicate this temple under the patronage of the king of Persia? (1 Esdras 6:8.) Who would not have feared, lest the Christian religion should perish, under such violent persecutions? Converts admire its beauty and strength.
The completion of the prophecies is a wonderful confirmation and comfort of Christians, (Worthington) whose faith is founded indeed for ever.
The gates of hell shall not prevail. [Matthew 16:18.]

47:10.text: Temple. Naou. Septuagint, St. Ambrose, etc., though the Vatican and Alexandrian copies have Laou, "people," with the Arabic, Ethiopic, St. Augustine, etc.
In the Church we receive many graces, (Calmet) even Christ himself, (St. Ambrose) to which those who refuse to be Catholics, can have no title.

47:11.text: Earth. All who hear of the wonders of God, must praise him; and who can be ignorant of what He has done, (Calmet) in the defeat of the enemies of his people, (Haydock) of Sennacherib, Cambyses, etc.? Yet all the earth will be instructed only by the propagation of the Gospel.
Justice, against thy enemies, (Haydock) though this word may here imply "mercy," in opposition to the judgments, ver. 12.
These perfections are never at variance.

47:12.text: Juda. Septuagint, Symmachus, etc., have "Judea," (Calmet) which would intimate, that the psalm was composed after the captivity. But the Hebrew reads Yehuda, "Juda," with St. Jerome.

47:13.text: Surround. Hebrew, "walk round, (Haydock) tell her towers."

47:14.text: Strength. Perhaps the outward wall, (Ezechiel 40:5.) not seen in Solomon's temple.
Houses. Hebrew, "palaces." Septuagint, "houses built like towers," Bareis. Jerusalem was not rebuilt or fortified, when the second temple was dedicated, 2 Esdras 1:3.

The fortresses of the Church are the holy Fathers and Doctors, who watch in her defence. Her pillars shall not fail. The particular Churches are all united in the same faith, and these reflections ought to prevail on all to embrace the same.

47:15.text: Our God. Christ incarnate works all this. He shall rule over the Church, not for three or four hundred years only, but as long as time shall last, and He shall have a Church triumphant in eternity.

Evermore. Hebrew hal-moth. The letters being differently arranged, are rendered, "in death." St. Jerome, "even unto death." Protestants, (Haydock) "in youth," (Chaldean) or "in the secret" of Providence. It may form a part of the following title, "over the young women," as Psalm ix., and xlv.

But then it would probably come after lamnatseach. The psalmist inculcates the perpetual duration of the Church under God's conduct, by three terms. In saeculum et ultra....usque ad mortem, "till death," (Pagnin) or "incessantly."
This psalm may also express the sentiments of a penitent, (Berthier) or of one who is put in possession of unchangeable felicity.

48:1.text: Psalm. St. Ambrose adds, "of David." It is written in an enigmatical style, like the book of Ecclesiastes, and is very obscure. But the drift is, to impress the captives with a contempt of worldly grandeur, which will end in death. The redemption of mankind and the resurrection of Christ are foretold, ver. 8, 16, etc.

48:3.text: Earth-born. Hebrew, "sons of Adam," a title belonging to the meanest. Progenies terrae. Perseus 6:56.
So Callimachus styles the giants, "mud-born." (Haydock)--- Yet Houbigant explains it of the rich, (Berthier) who have lands, and leave their names to them, ver. 12.
Ye just and (St. Augustine) and polite.

48:5.text: The ancients delighted in parables, which required attention to discern the meaning, and thus people had the pleasures of ingenuity. Music often accompanied their precepts.
The psalmist intimates, that he had attended the best masters, (Calmet) even the Holy Ghost.
He delivers the instructions which he had received from God, on the instrument of ten strings, to imply that we must keep the ten commandments.
He listens if the instrument be in tune.

48:6.text: The iniquity of my heel. That is, the iniquity of my steps, or ways: or the iniquity of my pride, with which, as with the heel, I have spurned and kicked at my neighbours: or the iniquity of my heel, that is, the iniquity in which I shall be found in death. The meaning of this verse is, why should I now indulge those passions and sinful affections, or commit now those sins, which will cause me so much fear and anguish in the evil day; when the sorrows of death shall compass me, and the perils of hell shall find me?
The old serpent is constantly laying snares for our heel, Genesis 3:15.

Original (St. Jerome) and actual sin, (Eusebius) particularly final impenitence, (Rabbins) and the punishment of our transgressions, (Abenezra) are much to be feared, (Haydock) as well as concupiscence.
All that will fill us with alarm in the day of vengeance, will be the having been supplanted, like wrestlers, by our iniquity, (Calmet) of which we have not repented.
Any such injustice must be dreaded, as it will bring on damnation.
Instead of heel, Symmachus has "steps," including all the unjust actions of life.

48:7.text: They that trust, etc. As much as to say, let them fear, that trust in their strength or riches; for they have great reason to fear: seeing no brother, or other man, how much a friend soever, can by any price or labour rescue them from death.
I address myself particularly to the rich, who are in the greatest danger.

48:8.text: No. Protestants, "none of them can by any means redeem his brother; nor, etc.
But the Septuagint translate as well, and the sense is the same, Matthew 16:26.
If Jesus Christ, thy brother, does not redeem thee, will any other do it? (St. Augustine) or though thy brother neglect, the man, Christ Jesus, will suffice.
But with respect to death, no redemption will be admitted. It is appointed for all once to die. [Hebrews 9:27.]

A man shall be more precious than gold, says Isaias, (xiii. 12.) of the Babylonians, whom the enemy will not spare, for any consideration. We must therefore make good use of our time, Ecclesiastes 9:10., and Proverbs 11:4.

48:9.text: And shall labour for ever, etc. This seems to be a continuation of the foregoing sentence; as much as to say, no man can by any price or ransom, prolong his life, that so he may still continue to labour here, and live to the end of the world. Others understand it of the eternal sorrows, and dying life of hell, which is the dreadful consequence of dying in sin.
The just, on the contrary, who have laboured for eternity, shall see the death of the wicked, or of the wise of this world.

It may also be a prediction of Christ's life of sufferings and future glory, (Bossuet) or express the sentiments of infidels, who deny a Providence; because both good and bad perish alike, Ecclesiastes 3:18.
Hebrew, "he rests for ever," (St. Jerome) or "shall he be undisturbed?"
Both those who disbelieve a future state, and those who live as if they did, shall suffer.

48:9.src: For the redemption....is precious, and it, etc.; Haydock
48:9.text: This verse is included within a parenthesis by Protestants,

48:11.text: He shall not see destruction, etc., or shall he not see destruction? As much as to say, however thoughtless may be of his death, he must not expect to escape: when even the wise and the good are not exempt from dying.
The endeavours of the wicked to establish their families, will be vain, while they themselves shall never more return from the graves hither.

48:12.text: Sepulchres. Hebrew Kobrom is better than the present Kirbam, their "interior," and is adopted by the Chaldean, Syriac, etc.
"Their inward thought is, that their houses shall continue for ever."

Called. That is, they have left their names on their graves, which alone remain of their lands, (Challoner) or, they have called cities and countries by their own names, as Alexander and Romulus did, Alexandria, (Haydock) and Rome.
They have spread their fame throughout the world.
Scarcely two translate the four last verses alike.

48:13.text: Compared. Hebrew, "he is like dumb, or perishable beasts."
So much is man degraded by his attachment to riches and pleasures.
Some explain this of Adam, (St. Chrysostom) reduced to the necessity of labouring, and dying, like brutes, ver. 21., and Ecclesiastes 3:18.
This is a very serious reflection, to think that man should so far neglect the gifts of reason, as to strive for temporal advantages only, like irrational creatures.

48:14.text: They shall delight in their mouth. Notwithstanding the wretched way in which they walk, they shall applaud themselves with their mouths, and glory in their doings, (Challoner) though it be to their shame, Philippians 3:10.

Hebrew, their posterity shall applaud their maxims, (Calmet) as many of their followers contributed to keep them in the delusion, (Haydock) which the damned will deplore, when it is too late, ver. 15.
A thirst after worldly advantages has proved their ruin; yet they obstinately persist in their evil ways.

48:15.text: In the morning. That is, in the resurrection to a new life; when the just shall judge and condemn the wicked.
From their glory. That is, when their short-lived glory in this world shall be past, and be no more.
Then the world shall be turned upside down.
The just shall have their day, (Menochius) when the beautiful palaces of the wicked shall be exchanged for darkness, and horrible torments.
"Their bodies shall grow old in hell, because they have stretched out their hand, and destroyed the habitation of the house of his majesty."
Their figure shall be destroyed in hell, after his dwelling.
They can rescue themselves no more than sheep. Those whom they oppressed shall be their judges. All friends will forsake them.
Crowds shall be confined to those mansions, where the fire is not extinguished.

48:16.text: Redeem. Chaldean and some Rabbins seem to understand this of purgatory. "He will draw me from hell, and give me a place in his habitation."
Others explain it of Christ's resurrection, or of the liberation of the patriarchs from limbo.
It seems a full solution of the enigma, ver. 6. I repent, and shall have nothing to fear.

48:18.text: Him. "The glory of a man increases with his prosperity, but it does not go down with him when he descends" (St. Ambrose) into the grave. He there finds the same reception as the most ignoble.

48:19.text: To him. The wicked are very selfish. They will seem grateful to those who are in power, and will cringe to get riches.

Yea, they will seem to thank God for their prosperity, (Worthington) or rather, they will assume these appearances with men. Hebrew, "he will bless his soul during life, (Berthier) with all pleasures," Luke 12:19., and Deuteronomy 29:19.
To him, is not in Hebrew, which insinuates, that people are flattered during their prosperity.
Yet Houbigant would restore this word, "and he will praise thee when thou shalt have done him a kindness;" c may have been placed for i, as Symmachus seems to have read auto.
The proper use of riches is to do good, Luke 16:9.

48:20.text: Fathers. Like them he shall die.
Hebrew reads in the second person, with Syriac, Aquila, etc.
Yet Protestants, Montanus, and others agree with us and the Septuagint.
And he. Hebrew, "they." The [] edition, however, has opsetai. Each individual, as well as the whole collection of the damned, shall be deprived of light and comfort.
Chaldean, "the just shall live like his fathers, a long and happy life; but the wicked shall enjoy no light in the life to come."

48:21.text: Understand. Hebrew yabin, though (ver. 13.) we find yalin, "shall remain all night."
But this is probably a mistake, as the prophet concludes with repeating this important instruction: (Berthier) Remember, O man, not to degrade thy rational soul.
Thou wast honoured by all, and made to the likeness of God.

49:1.text: For Asaph. The preposition L is placed before his name, as it is before David's.
Yet whether he was the author of the psalm, (Calmet) or only set it to music, (Worthington) is uncertain.
The 72nd, and ten following psalms, bear his name, and it is observed, that the style is not so flowing as those which are attributed to the royal prophet [David].
It is certain, that Asaph was a prophet, and chief musician in the days of David, 1 Paralipomenon 6:39., 25:2., and 2 Paralipomenon 29:30.

But the psalms that have this title relate to the captives, and may have been composed by some of his descendants. This and the following seem designed to shew, that something more than bloody victims is required by God; and thus the Israelites, who could not offer sacrifices at Babylon, were comforted; and the people taught by degrees, to look for something more excellent than the law of Moses.
The first and second coming of Christ are here described.
God's angels, just men, judges, (Calmet) idols, etc.
Hebrew El Elohim, Yehova, "the mighty God, the Lord."
From these three titles, some of the Fathers have proved the blessed Trinity.
But this argument is not conclusive.
They ought, however, to fill us with awe, when he shall come to judge the earth, his chosen people, (ver. 4.; Calmet) or all mankind.
Christ will come, surrounded by many legions of angels.

49:2.text: Beauty. This may refer to God, or to Sion, (Calmet) where the Church of Christ began.

49:3.text: Silence. Christ displayed the light of truth from Sion, at his first coming. But he would not judge any till the second, John 3:17., and 8:15.
Before him, at the last day, (Haydock) or in hell.
Our Saviour appeared formerly with great mildness: but he will come with majesty and terror, after fire shall have destroyed all transitory things.

49:4.text: Earth. As if they were animated, Deuteronomy 4:26., and 32:1., Isaias 1:2., and Jeremias 2:12.
Some understand the angels and apostles by heaven.
Judge. Literally, "to divide," discernere, (Haydock) the goats from the sheep, Matthew 25:32.
The whole earth, particularly the elect, will approve of God's decree, 1 Corinthians 6:2.

49:5.text: His saints. Hebrew, "my merciful ones," (Haydock) the chosen people, (Calmet) particularly priests, (Theodoret) who might have too high an opinion of the legal sacrifices, (St. Chrysostom) or all the elect are meant, Matthew 24:30.
The Hebrews were the only nation which then offered sacrifices to the true God, though some individuals might do it among the Gentiles.
Before, super, or, "who make a covenant with him respecting sacrifices."
Protestants, "those that have made a covenant with me by sacrifice," Malachias 1:12.
The Septuagint seem to have read v for i, more accurately, as the prophet speaks till ver. 7.
Judgment should begin at the house of God. And if first at us, what shall be the end of them that believe not the gospel of God?
Those who believe not, are already judged, John 3:--- Sacrifice generally precedes a covenant, Genesis 15:17.

49:6.text: God is judge. His sentence must therefore be just, (Menochius) and we ought to tremble, 1 Corinthians 4:4.

49:7.text: Testify. I will require thee to speak the truth, and attest the world, Psalm 80:9.

49:8.text: Sight. I complain of no neglect (Menochius) in these outward ceremonies.
God required no victims during the captivity; but he always demanded praise, (ver. 14.; Calmet) a contrite heart, Psalm 50:19., etc.

The prophets often admonished the people of this truth, (Isaias 1:2., and Jeremias 7:20.; Calmet) that they might not set too high a value on sacrifices, (Haydock) which, though pleasing to God, are of no service to him; as all the world is his property.

49:10.text: Oxen. St. Jerome and Protestants, "the cattle upon a thousand hills." But our version is very good, and adopted by the Syriac, Ferrand, etc.
Aleph means an ox as well as a thousand; and i may have been added to the preceding word, instead of u, at the beginning of this.
We find u here improperly in either, "beast."
No mention is made of fishes, because they were not used as victims.

49:11.text: I know your number, and have absolute dominion over all, Isaias 37:28.

49:11.src: St. Augustine; Lombard, 1 dist. 35.; F.; Amama
49:11.text: With God all things are present.

49:13.text: Goats? Can any of you be so stupid?
Some of the pagans believed, that their idols delighted in the smell of victims.

49:14.text: Vows. A faithless promise is very displeasing, Ecclesiastes 5:3. True religion must be interior, (Calmet) also 1 Corinthians 14:15.
We must discharge, not only our general, (Menochius) but also our particular vows, (Worthington) and obligations.

49:15.text: Call. Prayer is a perfect act of religion, and a confession of God's dominion. Qui fingit sacros auro vel marmore vultus, Non facit ille Deos: qui rogat, ille facit. (Martial 8:5:24.) To neglect prayer is, in some sense, to deny God.
He is pleased to exercise our confidence, (Haydock) and will have us to call upon him in distress.

49:16.text: Sinner. He is not blamed for praying: but his hypocrisy is condemned.
The world is full of such hypocrites, who have God in their mouths, but not in their hearts, and whose voice alone is the voice of Jacob, Genesis 27:22., Isaias 29:13., and Titus 1:16.
The wicked judges, who condemned Susanna, (Daniel xiii.) should have attended to these lessons.
Thou that teachest another, teachest not thyself, Romans 2:21.

It is surely to be expected, (Haydock) that those who undertake to teach others, should shew good example, and serve God with sincerity, (Worthington) and not content themselves with the glory of their vocation.

49:20.text: Lay. Hebrew, "slanderest." (Protestants) But dophi occurs on where else.
The sinner sits to detract, or with pleasure, (Menochius) habitually offends.

49:21.text: Silent, and deferred punishment, (St. Augustine) waiting for thy conversion, Romans 2:4.
Unjustly, is not expressed in Hebrew.
Face, judgment and hell, (Chaldean) or all these things, (St. Jerome) and thy manifold transgressions. The sight will be most intolerable.

49:22.text: Lest he. Hebrew, "I tear you in pieces."
It may be understood of death, (Theodoret) or of God.

49:23.text: Praise. This kind of improper sacrifice, and those of justice, and of a contrite heart, (Psalm iv., and l.) must accompany outward sacrifices, to make them acceptable. The latter has always been obligatory, (Worthington) as well as the former.

49:23.src: St. Augustine, con. advers. xx. orat. con Jud. vi. and ep. 120:18.; Worthington
49:23.text: This psalm proves, that the old victims should give place to one far more excellent, the body of Christ, the sacrifice of praise which the Church offers.
Eucharist means even "good grace," or thanksgiving, being intended to enable us to render that tribute of praise, which he requires.
There. Hebrew, " to him that ordereth his conversation aright, will I," etc.
The difference consists only in the points.
Syriac, "There I will shew him the way of his salvation," or, according to St. Chrysostom, "even my salvation."
By adoring God in spirit and truth, (Haydock) we may be saved.

50:2.text: Bethsabee. Septuagint, "Bersabee." Some copies add, "the wife of Urias."

The rest of the title is in Hebrew, etc., so that it is one of the most authentic. Nathan did not give the admonition till about a year had elapsed after the transgression, (2 Kings xii.) when David was made to enter into himself, by a prophet inferior to himself.
After his departure, he is supposed to have composed this psalm, to testify his repentance to all the world.
He had also in view the state of the captives.
The two last verses seem to have been added at Babylon, (Abenezra) as a similar addition has been made (Psalm 105:47., and 1 Paralipomenon 16:35.; Calmet) by some inspired author.

David knew that something more than confession was requisite, and that he must submit to temporal punishments, even though the prophet had assured him that his sin was remitted. He prays to be washed still more from evil habits, ver. 4.

50:3.text: Thy great mercy. Such is the purport of the Hebrew chasdec, though (Haydock) the Chaldean and Syriac omit great. My sin requires the deepest compunction. I must strive to repair the scandal I have given.
Mercies. I stand in need of many sorts, mitigation of punishment, true sorrow and perseverance, and that I may make some amends for my bad example, etc.

50:4.text: The true penitent never ceases to deplore his sins, like David, St. Peter, and St. Paul, Ecclesiasticus 5:5. The psalmist prays, that all the remains of sin may be obliterated, John 13:10.

50:5.text: Me. I do not forget it, but am covered with shame.
Sin is our greatest enemy, and continually cries for vengeance.
While David did not confess, his sin lay heavy upon him.

50:6.text: Only, or principally, who art the only God, (1 Timothy 1:17.; Worthington) the judge and witness of my crime.
David was a king, and acknowledged no judge among men.
Urias, whom he had injured, was no more.
The action had been done in secret: (2 Kings 12:12.) but many began to suspect, and to blaspheme.

Judged. St. Paul reads thus, (Romans 3:4.) though the Hebrew be, "when thou judgest." St. Jerome has also judicaberis, so that we might infer, that the Hebrew is now incorrect, or that beshophtec means in judicare te.
Houbigant changes the order of the verses, "cleanse me from my sins, that thou mayst be blameless when thou comest into judgment: For I know," etc.
Susanna was preserved from sinning by the thought of God's presence, Daniel 13:25.
If David fell, he confessed his fault.

God is faithful to his promises, and desires the conversion of sinners, though some would represent him as cruel, and unconcerned about his creatures. The psalmist prevents this unjust inference, (Haydock) and proves, that God is both just and merciful.
He acknowledges his ingratitude, as the captives confess, that their sins have brought on them this chastisement, though they had not injured the Babylonians.
God had often promised pardon to those who truly repent. An appeal is made to his truth and mercy.

50:7.text: Sins. Hebrew, "iniquity,....and in sin did my mother warm or conceive me." Original sin has a manifold deformity, and is the fatal root of other transgressions. See St. Augustine, Ench.; St. Thomas Aquinas, 1:2. q. 82. a. 2., est multiplex virtute.
The prophet speaks here undoubtedly of original sin.

No text could be more express, as the Fathers and the Jews agree. Yet Grotius, whose opinions are almost always singular, and dangerous, maintains, that the expression is hyperbolical, and only implies, that David had been long subject to sin, even from his infancy, as Job was naturally of a merciful disposition, Job 31:18.
Thus free-thinkers abuse the Scripture, and setting aside all authority, will only see what they think proper.
The weakness of man is a motive for pity: (Menochius) David pleads for it, yet allows, that the fault was entirely his own, and that he had grace sufficient to have avoided it.
The consideration of our sinful origin, ought to move us to beg, that we may be washed still more, and that we may not yield to our evil propensities.
We may resist them, and therefore David would not make vain excuses in sin, as God loves the truth, and a sincere confession.

50:8.text: Uncertain. Hebrew, "in the interior," I am full of sin, and thou requirest that I should constantly adhere to virtue. See Job 14:1.
To me. This increases my crime, (Calmet) as I cannot plead ignorance.
Those who have true faith, are more easily converted. But God gives to all some good, which he loves in them, and is ever ready to preserve his gifts, and to save his creatures.

50:9.text: Hyssop, which was used in sprinkling lepers, etc., (Leviticus 14:6.) not that the like ceremony, or even sacrifice, would suffice to heal the wound of the soul. Sincere contrition, (ver. 18, 19.) and the virtue of Christ's blood, are necessary.
The heat (Numbers xix.) and operation of this sovereign medicine was shewn to the prophet, John xix.
The sprinkling of the priest might be of service, if the heart was contrite, Hebrews 9:13.

50:10.text: Rejoice, when thou givest me an assurance of pardon. My bones, or virtue, shall then be restored. Hebrew, "the bones which thou hast broken may exult."
God gives contrition, after which the whole interior is filled with joy.
When the affections are purified, the soul takes delight in God's word, and revives.
The assurance of being pardoned, gives her fresh alacrity in his service.

50:11.text: Face. Anger. If the sinner consider his fault, God will forget it.
His justice requires that he should punish the impenitent.

50:12.text: Create. Hebrew bera, a term never used but for a new production. Whatever comes immediately from God must be pure, and as David had fallen into impurity, he earnestly implores this gift. He prays for the new heart of flesh, Ezechiel 36:26.
Thou hast said, Behold, I make all things new, Apocalypse xxi. Oh that I may be included, that I may sing a new canticle, having become a new man!
Right. Hebrew, "constant," (Haydock) the Holy Spirit, thy inspiration, or that uprightness, of which I have been deprived.
These sentiments ought to animate priests, when they hear confessions.

50:13.text: Spirit of prophecy, which is not withdrawn, except for some crime.
David had been without this privilege, till his repentance.
Yet St. Chrysostom and Theodoret maintain the contrary.
He prays for final perseverance, which is due to none, (Berthier) and that he may fall no more.

50:14.text: Salvation, or thy salutary joy, (Berthier) "the joy of thy Jesus," (St. Jerome) for whom he prays, (St. Augustine) knowing that He will save his people from their sins, and that there is salvation in no other name.
Perfect. Literally, "principal." Septuagint, "conducting;" such a spirit as may suit one who is to command.

This may denote sound reason, (4 Machabees; Philo Nobil) which keeps the passions under, (St. Chrysostom; Job 30:15.) or God himself, to whose Spirit all others should be subservient. Rance often inculcated to his Monks, the importance of having this principal spirit, which includes every virtue, particularly of liberality, as the Hebrew nediba, implies.
How earnestly should we endeavour to be disentangled from all the chains of our passions!
David might also fear, lest he had forfeited the throne, like Saul, whom the Spirit left, 1 Kings 10:9., and 16:14. Kings affected to be styled liberal, Luke 23:25.

He repeats his petition thrice, in allusion to the three persons in one God, (St. Augustine, etc.) and prays, that the Messias may still spring from him, notwithstanding his sins, and that he may have a constant and willing spirit to fall no more.
Principal, or liberal, may refer to the Holy Ghost, the fountain of all grace, or to the king, who ought to be generous.

50:15.text: Thee. The sinner cannot testify his gratitude better, than by promoting the conversion of others.
This is a sort of satisfaction.

While engaged in sin, David could not well exhort his subjects to repentance. His example was rather an inducement for them to transgress. (Berthier)--- But when they saw his grief, and knew that God had pardoned him, they were no longer tempted to despair. He also watched more carefully over their conduct.

50:16.text: Blood, from death, which I have deserved.
That of Urias, and his companions, (Worthington) cries to heaven for vengeance, 2 Kings 11:24. (Haydock) ---Hence the word sanguinibus, is used.
Justice, which has given place to mercy.
The latter word is here used by Symmachus, and justice may have this meaning.
Sixtus V reads exaltabit, instead of exultabit, which Septuagint agalliasetai, (Calmet) requires. Hebrew terannen, "shall sing aloud of." Protestants, "shall praise thy justice."
Extol agrees better with exaltabit, though both have nearly the same sense.
God's justice will pardon the penitent, as he has promised.

50:18.text: Sacrifice. If my crime were of such a nature as to be expiated by certain victims, I would surely have offered them: but my heart has offended, and must do penance.
The legal victims were not of themselves sufficient to remit sin.
Contrition was necessary, Isaias 66:2., and Ezechiel 6:9.
The Scripture often prefers internal, before outward sacrifices. This of the heart must precede those of justice, and of praise.
The heart must be broken, to make place for love. Compunction is thrice urged. The two first terms in Hebrew are the same, "contrite," (Haydock) broken, or disconcerted. Kateklasthe philon etor.
The captives might adopt this prayer, Daniel 3:39.
External sacrifices are commended in the next verses, as they are good, (Haydock) being instituted by God.

50:20.text: Deal. These two verses have no necessary connexion with the preceding: they may have been added by some prophet at Babylon, (Calmet) or David foresaw the destruction of the city by the Chaldeans.
He might fear that his sin would draw ruin on the capital, as a much less offence did, and as in all ages, the sins of the rulers have fallen on their subjects, 2 Kings xxiv.

Though the place was not destitute of fortifications, (Calmet) he might pray that they might be completed, (Berthier) as they were by Solomon, who built the temple and various walls, so that David might very will add this conclusion, (3 Kings 3:1., and 9:15.; Haydock) alluding to the sacrifices which should be offered in the future temple.
He insinuates, that his pardon may prove beneficial to his people, and sues for it to be granted for their sakes.

50:21.text: Justice; works of piety, (Psalm 4:6.) or victims vowed or prescribed by the law; the same which are afterwards styled holocausts (Calmet) by two different terms, hola and calil.
While we are in sin, our good works are less acceptable.

51:2.text: Achimelech. Sixtus V, Septuagint, etc., read Abimelech. But the former is the true name. See 1 Kings 22:9, 20.
The word understanding implies, that we ought to reflect on the misery of detraction, and bear our crosses with submission.
Doeg was but half a Jew, and persecuted the faithful.
Iniquity. Hebrew chesed, means also mercy, and some translate, "the mercy of God! or, the great mercy." Noble exploit!
But our version seems more natural. El may be a preposition, as Symmachus has Kath.

If Doeg, who was the most powerful of the shepherds of Saul, (1 Kings 21:7.) thought it his duty to give his master information of what had passed, he ought to have stated the matter fairly, instead of insinuating, that the high-priest was ill-affected.
Nothing could be more false, as he supposed he was acting agreeably to the interests of Saul, and of the state.

51:3.text: Achimelech. Sixtus V, Septuagint, etc., read Abimelech. But the former is the true name. See 1 Kings 22:9, 20.
The word understanding implies, that we ought to reflect on the misery of detraction, and bear our crosses with submission.
Doeg was but half a Jew, and persecuted the faithful.
Iniquity. Hebrew chesed, means also mercy, and some translate, "the mercy of God! or, the great mercy." Noble exploit!
But our version seems more natural. El may be a preposition, as Symmachus has Kath.

If Doeg, who was the most powerful of the shepherds of Saul, (1 Kings 21:7.) thought it his duty to give his master information of what had passed, he ought to have stated the matter fairly, instead of insinuating, that the high-priest was ill-affected.
Nothing could be more false, as he supposed he was acting agreeably to the interests of Saul, and of the state.

51:6.text: Ruin. Septuagint katapontismou, "drowning," or to make the innocent suffer "shipwreck."

51:7.text: Thy, is not found in Hebrew. Doeg would not dare to enter the tabernacle, after he had slain the priests.
Living. The Jews inform us, that Doeg slew himself with his master at Gelboe, and that David punished his offspring with death.

51:8.text: Laugh, at the last day, when they will have nothing to fear, nor the wicked to hope. In this life, the just are full of compassion; but they cannot but approve of God's judgments.

51:10.text: Fruitful. David foretells his own prosperity on the throne, (Worthington) when this wretch shall be no more.
He was at this time in great perplexity, (Calmet) in banishment from the house of God.

51:11.text: It punished the wicked, and asserted thy just providence.
Good. Thy saints find the greatest comfort in thee.

52:1.text: Maeleth, or Machalath. A musical instrument, or a chorus of musicians: for St. Jerome renders it, per chorum; (Challoner) and Aquila, "for dancing." Sixtus V, etc., read incorrectly, "for Amalec."
The psalm is nearly the same with the 13th, (Calmet) except ver. 6.
We know not the reason why David gave this second copy, omitting the name of Jehovah.
St. Augustine explains it of the dangers of the latter times.
It seems to speak of the return from captivity, ver. 7.

52:6.text: God hath scattered the bones, etc. That is, God hath brought to nothing the strength of all those that seek to please men, to the prejudice of their duty to their Maker.
That. Hebrew, "who besiege thee. Thou hast confounded them, because," etc. Septuagint seem to have read more correctly, as no one has been addressed before.

52:7.text: Glad. The Fathers explain this of Christ's redemption. The captives doubted not but that they should be speedily set at liberty, as the prophets had assured them.

53:2.text: Ziph lay to the south of Juda.
David was rescued from the most imminent danger, by an irruption of the Philistines. He then composed this canticle, expressing his sentiments in danger, and his gratitude to God.
It may also be used by any person in distress.
The Church orders it to be said by her ministers at Prime, that they may be protected from all their spiritual enemies.
The Fathers apply it to Jesus Christ, (St. Hilary) or to his persecuted members.

53:3.text: Name, which is a strong tower, (Proverbs 18:10.; Calmet) event thyself. Hence it is so criminal to take it in vain.
Judge. Ancient psalters have, "deliver." Saul and the Zipheans persecute me unjustly. I commit my cause to thee.
Defend me for the justice of my cause.

53:5.text: Strangers. Barbarous, (Worthington) enemies, though of the same tribe.
Hostis dicebatur quem nunc peregrinum dicimus.
The devil and our passions, as well as the world, are such to us.

53:6.text: Behold. He admires how God delivered him, though Saul seemed to have only one step more to take. Jesus Christ was secure amid the persecutions of the Jews, till he was pleased to deliver himself up, John 10:18.

53:8.text: Jesus Christ was offered, because he would, Isaias 53:7., and John 10:17.
Good, so to do, (Du Hamel) or sweet in itself, Psalm 51:11.

53:9.text: Enemies, from the hill which divided the armies. I saw them retire, (Haydock) and in security beheld their chastisement.

54:1.text: David. It alludes to some of his persecutions, particularly to that of Absalom, as well as to that of the Church, and of Jesus Christ. Bede explains it of Onias: who, being excluded from the high priesthood, retired into Egypt, and built the temple of Onion, 2 Machabees xiii.

54:3.text: Hear me. He repeats the same petition four times, (Haydock) to testify his fervour, and humility, Ecclesiasticus 35:21.
Exercise, among the wicked, (St. Augustine) or while I consider the sufferings of Christ.
David was perplexed what course to take, when he first heard of his son's revolt. Our Saviour was sorrowful unto death, Matthew 26:37.
Ldoleschia. Hebrew sichi, denotes serious (Haydock) meditation, Genesis 24:63.

54:4.text: Upon me. When a person has fallen into distress, the world is ever ready to attribute it to some fault. Absalom accused his father of neglecting to judge, etc., 2 Kings 15:2. The Jews calumniated and sought the death of Christ, whose agony in the garden is well described, (ver. 5.) as well as the consternation of David, at the sight of such a general revolt, (Calmet) which almost overwhelmed him.

54:5.text: Troubled, like a woman in labour, (Menochius) as yachil implies, (Calmet) in Hiphel.

54:7.text: Dove, which flies swiftly. He now adored the judgments of God, which chastised him, as he had threatened; (2 Kings 12:11.) though, while innocent, he had rejected a similar proposal, Psalm 10:1. The event shewed, that he acted wisely in retiring beyond the Jordan.
O that I could fly, and in the simplicity of the dove, be removed from these afflictions!
As I could not go to heaven, I retired into the wilderness.

54:9.text: Storm. The first fury of the rebels, which is most dangerous, is thus specified. David was convinced, that his son's party would dwindle away, when he was informed that he did not pursue him, following the advice of Chusai.
Hebrew, "I would hasten my escape from the impetuous wind and tempest." But the Septuagint may be equally correct.
God protected his weak servant in the greatest dangers.

54:10.text: Cast down. Hebrew, "swallow up," as the earth did Dathan.
Tongues, as at Babel, that they may not know how to proceed.
Hebrew, "swallow up....the torrent of their tongue."
Pallag, means also to "divide." Absalom was accordingly infatuated by David's friend, 2 Kings 15:31., and 17:7.
City of Hebron, or even of Jerusalem, which caused the king to leave no garrison in it. The city was still more abandoned in our Saviour's regard.
Contradiction. Their counsels agree not. They have their troubles, yet will not amend; but strive to oppress the poor.

54:13.text: From him. But how shall we guard against a traitor?
The injury received from a friend is most cutting.

54:14.text: Guide, the prime minister, (Berthier) and chief of the council.

Such was Achitophel, who had nevertheless been long (Calmet) secretly disaffected. See 2 Kings 15:12., and 16:23. He professed the same religion, and was trusted with the most important affairs, as Judas carried the purse.

54:15.text: Consent, or with expedition, as the Rabbins order people to go to the temple, though they must return slowly. All this designates Judas.

Dreadful lesson for all sacred ministers, who prove faithless! (Berthier) participating of the holy sacraments in the Catholic Church, (Worthington) and yet betraying themselves, and their master! Achitophel had probably to attend David in the temple, as Naaman did Benadad, 4 Kings 5:18.

54:16.text: Let death, etc. This, and such like imprecations, which occur in the psalms, are delivered prophetically; that is, by way of foretelling the punishments which shall fall upon the wicked from divine justice, and approving the righteous ways of God: but not by way of ill-will, or uncharitable curses, which the law of God disallows.

David shewed even too much tenderness towards the rebels, in the opinion of Joab, etc. He would not hurt Saul. His predictions were verified; as Achitophel became a suicide, Absalom perished miserably, suspended between heaven and earth, as an object of horror to both, while many of his accomplices were either slain, or fell into precipices, 2 Kings 17:23., and 18:8.
Hell, by a sudden death, like Antiochus, Core, etc., Numbers 16:30. The just are already dead to this world.
Those who sin on purpose, descend, as it were, alive into hell.

54:18.text: Evening. The Hebrew then began the day.
They had three times allotted for prayer, (Daniel 6:10.) as the Church had afterwards.
Evening song, matins, and the sacrifice of the mass, are the principal times for divine service.
David comprises all times, because his prayer was continual.

54:19.text: Among many, etc. That is, they that drew near to attack me, were many in company, all combining to fight against me.
Or, many also joined themselves to me with Joab, and the holy angels, (4 Kings 6:16.) though almost all Israel followed Absalom, 2 Kings 15:13.
In many things, schismatics agree with the Church; but their crime is the breaking of unity.

54:20.text: Change, or redemption for them, (Psalm 43:13.; Eusebius) or they will not amend, (Worthington) nor cease to blaspheme God, and to calumniate me.
Eternal. Literally, "He who is before ages."

54:21.text: Repay. Hebrew bishlomaiv, "in his retributions," or "against his peaceable ones." Houbigant too arbitrarily translates, "they have sent forth their hand against, " etc.

54:22.text: They are divided, etc. Dispersed, scattered, and brought to nothing, by the wrath of God, who looks with indignation on their wicked and deceitful ways.
They are separated from the good, (St. Jerome) slain by a look, 2 Thessalonians 2:8.
Some translate, "they have divided the butter, like words of his mouth." But this is less accurate, and the same idea is conveyed in the next words.
Protestants, "The words of his mouth were smoother than butter, but war was in his heart. His words were softer than oil, yet were they drawn swords."
My enemy has violated every law, under the appearance of friendship.
Darts. Absalom kissed the men of Israel to delude them, (2 Kings 15:2.) and the traitor gave this sign to those who came to seize our Saviour, Matthew 26:48.

The words of God are most excellent in themselves, but they seem hard to the incredulous. Thus the Capharnaites gave rise to the first heresy against the words of Christ, which St. Peter piously believed, though, as yet, he did not comprehend their meaning, John vi.
His heart and words may thus be understood of God: but they more probably relate to any one of David's numerous enemies, who is thus singled out.

54:23.text: Cast. The prophet had experienced the happy effect of this conduct.
In all troubles and doubts, we must have recourse to God.

54:24.text: Destruction; "into gehenna," (Chaldean) or "hell," (St. Jerome) to which the judge sentences the reprobate, without promoting their crimes.
Days. It is rare that murderers and notorious malefactors escape punishment in this world; but in the next, they will surely be requited. Absalom perished in the flower of his age.
Achitophel, and many others, have been suddenly cut off. God sometimes permits such to reign for a time, to exercise his servants, or that they may repent.
The Scripture often threatens sinners in this manner, Job 21:21., and Isaias 65:20.
Their days are indeed spent, when they die. But if they had altered their conduct, they might have prolonged their life, (Haydock) according to the usual course of nature.

55:1.text: Geth. Before (Berthier) or after his escape to the cave of Odollam, (Calmet) he composed this psalm, to comfort his followers with the consideration of God's protection.

See 1 Kings xxii., and Psalm xv. The title is variously rendered. St. Jerome, "to the victor for the dumb dove," etc. Protestants, "upon Jonath elem rechokim Michtam of David." This is to elude the difficulty, and we might as well adhere to the Septuagint, who seem to have only added, "from the sanctuary." The psalm may suit any one in distress, (Berthier) unable to attend the public service, (Worthington) or it may be understood of our Saviour's passion.

55:2.text: The sins of every man oppressed Jesus Christ.
All who live piously, must suffer many attacks.

55:4.text: The height of the day. That is, even at noon day, when the sun is the highest, I am still in danger.
Hebrew, "many fight against me from an elevation, or from day-break."
Fear. Many prefix a negation, which St. Jerome rejects, (ad Sun.) explaining this height of the divine majesty.
David felt the impressions of fear; but corrected them by his confidence in God.

55:5.text: My words. The words or promises God has made in my favour.
Praising God (Haydock) removed the dejection of David.
Detested. Protestants, "wrest." They put an evil construction upon what I say, (Haydock) and make me their laughing-stock, Psalm 37:13.

But I cease not to proclaim what God has declared in my favour, (Haydock) or what good I have been enabled to effect by his grace. My enemies may meet to devise my ruin, and to supplant me; yet all in vain.

55:8.text: For nothing shalt thou save them. That is, since they lie in wait to ruin my soul, thou shalt for no consideration favour or assist them, but execute thy justice upon them.

55:9.text: I have. Protestants, "thou tellest my wanderings: put thou my tears into thy bottle. Are they not in thy book?" St. Jerome, "thou hast numbered my most secret things: place my tears in thy sight," etc.
God has promised to relieve the distressed, who confided in him.

55:11.text: To me. This is almost a repetition of ver. 5.
Elohim and Jehova are mentioned (Haydock) as "the power and eternity" of God gave David the greatest confidence.

55:12.text: Houbigant chooses rather to follow the Syriac, "with thee, O God, are my vows:" which is clearer, though our version may be well explained, "I will perform my vows to thee," (Berthier) the sacrifice of praises, in this psalm.
I will endeavour to comply with my engagements and vows.

55:13.text: Living, in my own country, where I am no longer, as formerly during my banishment, in the region of the dead.
The Fathers explain this of Jesus Christ, or of eternal glory.
In the true faith and good works, I will strive to please God.

56:1.text: Destroy not. Suffer me not to be destroyed, (Challoner; Menochius) or I will not allow my men to destroy an implacable foe, (Haydock) as they entreat me to do. This conduct is worthy of eternal memory.
The words may also be an admonition to the reader, not to alter this piece, Apocalypse 22:18.

Al tashcheth, (or thosséth.; Haydock) is supposed to be an instrument, or a favourite song, such as that of Moses, (Deuteronomy 9:26.) which begins thus. But the point is uncertain. The same words occur, Psalm lviii., lix., (Berthier) and lxxiv. They are put in the mouth of Christ suffering, by St. Hilary, etc.
Cave of Odollam, or rather of Engaddi.

56:2.text: In thee. This obliges God to take pity, Psalm 46:11.
Wings, as a chicken retires to the hen, Ruth 2:12., and Matthew 23:37.

56:4.text: Sent his mercy, etc., (Calmet) above the power of man.

Reproach. Saul was forced to entreat David to preserve his family, as he had just spared his own life; which would, no doubt, mortify his pride, (1 Kings 24:18, 22.; Calmet) and seem a reproach to him.

56:5.text: Lions. Poetry gives life to all things. It represents mercy and truth as God's messengers; and Saul as a young lion. He might have entered the cave with his men, and destroyed David: but Providence caused him to enter alone, so that David had an opportunity to cut off the hem of his garment, and to shew his clemency.
Sword. Thus were the Jews armed, to demand Christ's death.
The persecutors use artificial weapons, and excite one another to fury, 1 Kings 22:16.

56:7.text: Down. Hebrew, "my soul was bowed down," (Berthier) or "to bow down my soul."
Saul strove many ways to destroy his rival, sending him to fight the Philistines, who, nevertheless, proved the ruin of Saul, 1 Kings 18:17., and 31:1.

56:8.text: My. This and the following verses, from the 107th psalm.

56:9.text: Glory. Soul, tongue, or rather instruments of music, (Calmet) and spirit of prophecy.

56:10.text: Nations. The psalms are now recited, (Worthington) in every language, and the wonders wrought by Christ are proclaimed.

56:11.text: Christ has mounted to the highest heavens, and his apostles have preached (Calmet) his saving truths, which, like clouds, render the earth fruitful in good works.

56:12.text: Earth. It is just thy mercy should be extolled.

57:1.text: Title. This psalm is a sequel to the former, (Worthington) and refers to the malevolent speeches of Saul's courtiers, (1 Kings 24:10., and 26:7.; Calmet) and to the proceedings of the Jews against Christ.
It is an invective against hypocrites, (Calmet) and detractors.

57:2.text: Men. If you be consulted by Saul, and act as judges, do what is right. Condemn not a man unheard.
Hebrew also, "O assembly," act not hypocritically.

57:3.text: Heart. Hence proceed evil thoughts, etc.
Quicquid vis et non potes, factum Deus computat.
Forge, as you endeavour to preserve the appearance of rectitude. Hebrew, "weigh" in scales.

57:4.text: They have followed the same course through life, being always bent on wickedness.

57:5.text: Deaf asp. This is the most dangerous species. The ancients attempted to charm serpents. But these courtiers were deaf to every proof of David's innocence, (Calmet, Diss.) and would receive no admonition, stopping their ears, like asps.
The prophet speaks conformably to the received opinion, (Berthier) without determining it to be true.

57:6.text: Wisely. "Cunningly." Many read, qui incantatur a sapiente.
He does not approve of the magical art.
Serpents may naturally be affected with music. The torpid snake by incantation bursts. (Virgil, Ec. viii.; Bochart 5:3. 385.) Parkhurst, chober.

57:9.text: Wax. Hebrew shabbelul, occurs no where else, and this signification is surely preferable to that of the Rabbins, "a snail."
Fire. Hebrew, "like the untimely birth of a woman, which has not seen the sun."

Septuagint may not have read th at the end of esh, "fire." But both version imply, that the wicked shall perish, without resource (Berthier) or struggle. This in enforced by a multiplicity of examples.

57:10.text: Before your thorns, etc. That is, before your thorns grow up, so as to become strong briers, they shall be overtaken and consumed by divine justice, swallowing them up, as it were, alive in his wrath.
You shall be cut off when you least think of it.

David probably alludes to the proposal mentioned, (Judges 9:14.) where the brier (rhammus) invites all the trees to come under its shade. Before you, my followers, shall fall under the oppression of our cruel persecutors, they shall be suddenly destroyed.
"Before your pots can feel the thorns, he shall take them away, as with a whirlwind, both living, and in his wrath."
This version of Pagnin is rejected by Montanus, who nearly follows the Vulgate. Sirothecem means, "your thorns, or pots," Ecclesiastes 7:7.

57:11.text: Shall wash his hands, etc. Shall applaud the justice of God, and take occasion, from the consideration of the punishment of the wicked, to wash and cleanse his hands from sin.

Hands. Hebrew, "feet," (though this is not certain. Psalm 73:4.; Berthier) and all his body; the carnage shall be so great. The just approve of God's judgments, (Calmet) in or "over," epi, to testify that he has no connexion with the wicked.
The just will purify himself still more at the sight of vengeance. This interpretation is good, but no so literal.

57:12.text: A God. Hebrew Elohim shophetim. Both are in the plural, which has induced many to suppose that angels, etc., are meant; yet the plural is sometimes used, when speaking of the true God, Genesis 20:13., and Josue 24:19.

Houbigant translates, "gods," which he explains of the pastors of the Church, or "judges." The reward of virtue, and punishment of vice, are very important truths. Let us wait till the time of harvest, and all will be in its proper place.

58:1.text: Watched. Hebrew, "they (the guards) watched." The psalm relates also to the resurrection of Christ, and vocation of the Gentiles, (Berthier) as well as to the reprobation, and future conversion of the Jews. It seems to be most applicable to the times of Esdras and Nehemias: (2 Esdras 4:1., and 6:1.) one of whom may have composed it.
But this is only a conjecture, (Berthier) and Saul's emissaries may be styled Gentiles, (Menochius) because they imitated their manners.
Saul sent repeatedly, and went himself to attack David. He gave him Michol with the same design.
But God turned her heart another way.

58:4.text: Caught. Hebrew, "laid snares for." Septuagint, "hunted." The enemy wished eagerly to take David, or Nehemias, (Haydock; Calmet) and they seemed to have so surrounded the former, as to be sure of him.

58:5.text: I ran. Hebrew, "they," etc. But the Septuagint would not have made such a mistake, (Berthier) and the Hebrew appears to be incorrect, though we may understand "without iniquity in me, they have run."
All this may be well explained of Jesus Christ, who alone could use these expressions with propriety, being without sin.

58:6.text: No mercy. Nehemias uses the like prophetic threats, 2 Esdras 4:5.
"Every sin must be punished, either by the penitent, or by an avenging God." The prophet supposes that his enemies died impenitent.
When the gospel was first preached, God visited the world with various afflictions, to make people enter into themselves.
The prophet prays that God would visit all nations with peace, and punish obstinate persecutors of the Catholic Church.

58:7.text: Evening, when they came to take David. But, out of regard for Michol, they providentially waited till he had escaped, 2 Kings xix.
Nehemias was obliged to watch continually, 2 Esdras 4:11, 23.

The Jews will embrace the faith at the end of the world, (St. Augustine) or they will be destroyed (St. Hilary) or banished by Titus and Adrian (A.D. 137); the latter of whom forbade them even to look at Jerusalem from an eminence. They could not enter it in the time of Eusebius, (Psalm xlviii.) and St. Jerome.
They have a hunger for God's word, of which they have lost the true sense.
Persecutors are never satiated, though they labour to destroy, all their lives.

58:8.text: Lips. They seek my ruin, 2 Esdras 4:2, etc.
Heard. Thus they deny Providence, Psalm (Hebrew) 10:11.
This thought and the occasion of sin have produced much wickedness. "Whithersoever thou goest, thou art seen by Jesus Christ, who made, redeemed, and died for thee."
A serious consideration of God's presence is the best preservative.
The wicked devise all sorts of cruelty, as if there were no God.

58:9.text: Laugh. Permitting them to become ridiculous.

58:10.text: My. Hebrew, "his," which seems incorrect. Chaldean (Calmet) and St. Jerome agree with the Vulgate. Houbigant would also substitute, "My strength, I will sing to thee," which affords a better sense, ver. 17.
Yet our version is very plain; I will make all my powers serve thee, and acknowledge that all comes from thee.
Such was the admirable humility of Nehemias, who never assumed any glory to himself.
David and all just men entertain the same sentiments. We are here assured (Haydock) that the Church and some virtuous souls will persevere, by God's grace.

58:11.text: His mercy. Protestants, "the God of my." Yet the text has "his;" i and v are easily confounded. The Keri here allows "my," which Pagnin translates. St. Jerome, "the mercy of my God;" (Haydock) or "my God, my mercy."
All comes to the same end. These words are most applicable to Jesus Christ.

58:12.text: Forget. Let them suffer a long time, (Menochius) that their punishment may be a greater warning. The ancients read, "thy law," instead of people, and apply this to the Jews, (Calmet) who still preserve the law, and bear witness throughout the world that the prophecies were not a fabrication of Christians.
Their exemplary chastisement and continuance, may serve to caution all not to follow their example. Judaei testes iniquitatis suae et veritatis nostrae.

"If all the Jews had been converted, we should have had only suspicious witnesses; and if all had been exterminated, we hould have had none." God permits our spiritual adversaries to remain for our trial, (Tertullian) that we may not forget ourselves in prosperity.
We may also translate Al, "O God, (as well as not) slay them, that they may attack my people no more;" (see 2 Esdras 4:4.) for what reason could Nehemias have to beg that they might be spared?

58:13.text: For, is not expressed. Literally, "bring down....the sign," etc. Let not their haughty speeches take effect, or escape punishment. The imprecations of the Jews against themselves, (Haydock) and against Christ, have brought on their destruction.

Of. Hebrew, "shall relate;" which has little sense. Laying aside the points, it may have the meaning of the Vulgate. This passage can hardly be applicable to David's persecutors, though it might predict the disasters of Saul. It alludes more to the enemies of Christ, (Berthier) who called down his blood upon themselves, (Matthew 27:25.) and most falsely accused Him.

Hence they are become the reproach of men, and are no longer a people. They behold the reign of Christ propagated throughout the world, (ver. 16.; Haydock) while they are wandering about and despised. They once would not serve; boasting that they were children of Abraham, John viii.

58:14.text: Consumed. At the destruction of Jerusalem, or for opposing Nehemias, 2 Esdras 6:16.
Earth. The Jews who were preordained to life, embraced the gospel.
How can those know, who are no more? Their condition will be worse than annihilation. They will exist in hell, though no more visible to us.
They will know the truth, when it is too late; and when they are on the point of plunging into the abyss. The measure of their crimes being full, they shall be accused and punished.

58:15.text: City. This is a sort of chorus, ver. 7. St. Jerome and Protestants, "Let them bark."
It insinuates, that the attacks of the enemies of Nehemias, (Calmet) David, and Christ, were unceasing.

58:16.text: Murmur. Hebrew also, "shall tarry all night." Protestants, "grudge."
Finding no oil for their lamps, they will repent like Judas, and blaspheme in hell.

58:17.text: Morning. With earnestness, (Haydock) I will fulfil this duty (Calmet) at the resurrection, (Worthington) in the morning of a glorious immortality.

58:18.text: Helper. St. Jerome, "my strength," virtutem meam tibi cantabo, ver. 10. I will sing, that all I have comes from thy pure mercy.

59:1.text: Changed. Psalm xliv. Title, Psalm xv., doctrine explaining what will be the progress of the Church.
It seems to have the same import as understanding, in other titles, but here is of little authority.

It has no connexion with the psalm, which seems to have been written before David had obtained the sovereignty over Israel, (Houbigant; ver. 8) or it expresses the sentiments of the captives, (Calmet) and of Jesus Christ, and his Church.

59:2.text: Set fire. Hebrew, "fought against Syria of Mesopotamia, and Syria of Soba."
Twelve. Abisai slew 18,000, perhaps on another occasion; (1 Paralipomenon 18:12.) so that this title does not contradict history.

59:3.text: Off. Chastising thy people frequently under Moses, etc.
On us. Redeeming mankind, which thou hadst condemned, for the fault of Adam, and giving us a more abundant grace, Romans 5:9.
Thou hast treated us like a good physician, (Deuteronomy 32:39.; Calmet) chastising us for our sins, that we might improve in virtue.

59:4.text: Moved. He personifies the earth, which had fallen into the hands of the Chaldeans, (Calmet) or had experienced various commotions under Saul, etc., (Haydock) which he denotes by the mention of an earthquake.

59:5.text: Sorrow. Hebrew, "muddy," such as is given to slaves or malefactors, (Matthew 27:34.) mixed with myrrh, or venom. Literally, "wine of trembling," (Calmet) or soporiferous.
All these expressions give the idea of something disagreeable.
The people became penitent, or were astonished.

59:6.text: Warning, to amend, (Worthington) the sign of the cross (Calmet) in baptism, (St. Jerome) or confirmation.
It was customary to erect a pole, on which some signal was placed, in case of invasion, Isaias 5:26., and 11:12., etc.
Bow. Hebrew kossoth, (Haydock) as St. Jerome, etc., have read, though the present Hebrew end with t, less correctly, and is explained, "because of the truth."

59:7.text: Save me. The king praying for all.
St. Augustine reads, me, (Calmet) though the Vulgate does not here express it.
Hebrew, "save thy right hand," the people, or man of thy, etc., Psalm 79:18.
Hear me. Hebrew, "him." But the margin has, me.

59:8.text: Holy one, Jesus Christ, (Eusebius) the prophet, (Menochius) the sanctuary, (Calmet) or oracle.
He had promised that the captives should return in seventy years time, Jeremias 25:11., and 29:10.
Sichem. (partibor Sichimam....metibor.) These two verbs are sometimes used for partiar and metiar, in ancient authors.
It may also signify the Arabs, who lived in tents.

David's dominion extended over these nations, (Haydock) and the captives at Babylon hoped to recover them, as the Machabees did. The kingdoms of Juda and Israel were no more divided, (Isaias 11:13., and Jeremias 31:8.) to shew the unity of the Church.

59:9.text: Head. This tribe was at the head of the kingdom of Israel, but submitted to David, (Haydock) and afforded excellent soldiers and captains.
His temporal kingdom was extended by God, who will crown his elect.
King. Hebrew, "law-giver," alluding to Genesis 49:10. Symmachus, "my general."
The word king implies all this.

After the captivity, Zorobabel was at the head of the people. Jesus Christ sprung from this tribe, and is the true king of the people, whom he has redeemed, and put in possession of the land of promise.

59:10.text: The pot of my hope; or my watering pot. That is, a vessel for meaner uses, by being reduced to serve me, even in the meanest employments.

Plautus (Mort. 2:scen. 1. 40) says, Ego vos pro matula habeo, etc. Symmachus adopts the sense of the Septuagint amerimnias, as réts, in Syriac means "to trust," (Daniel 3:28.) and "to wash" in Hebrew. It was customary to throw lots into a pot full of water, and that which came out last was most esteemed. To this custom the psalmist may allude, (Calmet) or he hoped that the fruitful region of Moab would supply him with food. It was subject to David, (2 Kings 8:2.; Haydock) and to the Machabees, 1 Machabees 5:6.

Shoe, to be untied, or carried, as by the meanest slaves, (Matthew 3:11.) or to take possession, Deuteronomy 11:24. Thus "Alexander threw a javelin, and danced on the shore of Asia, begging that those lands would not receive him unwillingly for king."
David conquered Idumea, (2 Kings 8:14.; Haydock) as Hyrcanus did afterwards.
So the Philistines are called, who had no kindred with the Israelites; whereas the Edomites, Moabites, etc., were originally of the same family.

Protestants, "Philistia, triumph thou, because of me." Marginal note insinuates this is spoken "by irony;" but (Haydock) Hebrew properly means, "make an alliance with me;" or, Syriac, "I will shout for joy over Palestine." This country was subdued by the Machabees, (1 Machabees 4:15.; Calmet) as it had been tributary to David, 2 Kings 8:2.
"I will make a league against the Philistines."

59:11.text: City. The capital of the aforesaid counties, or Jerusalem; (Calmet) but more particularly Petra, (Haydock) the strongest place in Idumea.

59:12.text: And wilt; or, "yet thou wilt not," etc.
Thou wilt not depend on our efforts for victory.

How can we expect to make such conquests, being in so forlorn a condition, when thou dost not lead forth our armies, as formerly? All that man can do is vain, but thou wilt look down upon us, and through God we shall do mightily, ver. 14.

60:1.text: Hymns. This denotes either the female musicians, or the instruments.
David, under persecution, (Ferrand) the captains, (Ven. Bede; Calmet) or any one in the Church of Christ, (St. Augustine) may adopt this psalm, (Berthier) to thank God.

60:3.text: Earth of Palestine, (Berthier) when David was at Mahanaim.
The countries beyond the Euphrates may be so styled, Psalm 64:6.
Rock, by inspiring Cyrus to grant us liberty, (Calmet) or by sending Jesus Christ.
The Church is firmly established; and, in all places, serves God.

60:5.text: Tabernacle. The words for ever seem to confine this to heaven, as the Fathers explain them, though they may also allude to a temporal protection (Berthier) in the temple.
Wings. Chaldean, "of thy deity." Chaldean, I will continue a member of thy Church here, and be happy for ever.

60:6.text: Inheritance. David had both a temporal, and an eternal one in view.
The captives express their gratitude for their deliverance.

60:7.text: King. They wish long life to the king, expecting that the predictions of the prophets insured to them temporal dominion. But when they saw Zorobabel possess no such splendour, they were naturally taught to extend their thoughts to the Messias, of whom the Chaldeans and ancient Jews, as well as the Fathers, explain this passage.

Chaldean, "the days of the future world thou wilt add to the days of the king, the Messias. His years shall be like the generations of this world, and the generations of the world to come." Eternity is clearly meant. If David speaks of himself, and of his successors on the throne for many ages, he must be considered as a figure of Christ, who is also designated.
The Church will continue to the end, and be triumphant throughout eternity.

60:8.text: Search? Who can comprehend what thou hast prepared for this king? He may allude to the promises made to David's family, (Psalm 88:30, 38.; Calmet) which should reign for ever, by means of Christ.
Who can explain the mercy of God in redeeming us; and his fidelity in granting his promised rewards?

60:9.text: Day. A Levite speaks, having dedicated himself by vow to serve God continually in his temple, after his return from Babylon, (Calmet) or David expresses his resolution to testify his gratitude, by sacrifice.
I will sing psalms, etc., in this life, and for ever.

61:1.text: He was one of the chief musicians under David, 1 Paralipomenon 25:3. The psalm may be explained of David, persecuted by Absalom, (Calmet) of the Machabees, (Theodoret) etc., or of Christ, and his Church.
St. Jerome considers it as a piece of excellent morality, (Calmet) and this may suffice, without referring it to any historical fact.

61:2.text: Subject. The just is resigned, though he feel like other men. David found a sort of resentment against the rebels, arising in his breast, which he presently repressed, by the thought of God's will, 2 Kings 12:11.
He fears nothing, because his soul is subject to God.

61:4.text: Fence. This may refer to the persecutors, who resembled a leaning wall.

Protestants, "ye shall be slain all of you, as a bowing wall shall ye be," etc. He threatens them with speedy destruction, (Haydock) or represents to them the baseness of attacking a man ready to fall.
He informs them, that their attempts will be in vain, though they be very numerous, and he himself apparently so weak.

61:5.text: Price. Septuagint, St. Hilary, etc., "my honour." They wish to dethrone me, and to represent me as unfit to govern.

I ran. Septuagint edramon, "they ran" likewise, as Hebrew implies, and as the Greek Fathers generally explain it. David thought proper to flee, that he might be at a distance from traitors, 2 Kings 15:14. His enemies sought his destruction.
They wished to deprive him of the reward of his labours; but he ran more earnestly.

61:8.text: God. The multiplicity of titles shews the prophet's love. See Apocalypse 5:12.

61:9.text: All. Hebrew, "always, O people." Septuagint seem to have read, adoth, for heth.
For ever. Here Selah is translated, (Berthier) though it be not in Septuagint, etc. David exhorts his followers to address themselves to God, with compunction and confidence.

61:10.text: Liars. They are so vain and light, that if they are put into the scales, they will be found to be of no weight; and to be mere lies, deceit, and vanity. Or, they are liars in their balances, by weighing thing by false weights, and preferring the temporal before the eternal.
They give false judgments; be not concerned; God is our protector.
God's servants strive to draw others to the practice of virtue.
All sinners (Haydock) will not outweigh vanity itself.

61:11.text: Them. Let the rich assist their needy brethren; and you, my followers, beware of enriching yourselves, by unjust rapine, during this civil war.
Raise your thoughts to something better.

61:12.text: Once, by the generation of his word, (St. Augustine) or when he promulgated the law, Exodus 20:6.
God's word is invariable, (Worthington; Job 33:14.; Menochius) and will be put in execution.
This he has often inculcated. (Vatable) (Job 39:35.) Amos 1:3. uses three and four, in the same sense.

61:13.text: Works. We must therefore refrain from every injustice.

God spoke once by Moses, and again by his own Son. Both the Testaments confirm the certainty of rewards and punishments, (Berthier) as God is able and willing (Worthington) to execute his decrees. Luther, followed by the Dutch, translates, "as he merits;" to which expression, concerning the just, Amama objects.

62:1.text: Several copies read Judaeae; which agrees with the Hebrew, etc. The Idumeans, after the captivity, occupied those deserts (Calmet) of Ziph, Haret, etc., (Worthington) where David had screened himself from the fury of Saul. This psalm may express his sentiments, (Theodoret; Muis) or those of the captives, returning home, (Calmet) or it may agree with all those, who thirst after their heavenly country.
St. Jerome explains it of the coming of Christ, and it was used in the morning service.
Catholics, who are imprisoned for their faith, and cannot approach the tabernacle, may here find comfort.

62:2.text: Day. The manna dissolved with the sun beams, and God will be served with diligence.
Flesh. The sensations of my soul affect all my body.
Thirst is more insupportable than hunger; and the psalmist could not express his ardour better.
He loves God with all his strength, etc.
"The soul desires, but the end can be attained only by good works." Carnis laboribus pervenitur.

O how! Protestants, "longeth for thee in a dry," etc. But come is no Hebrew verb, and occurring no where else, is little understood by the Jews. It seems that comu, "as," should be here, to correspond with the following so. "As my flesh thirsts in this, etc., so in the," etc. I shall find no less pleasure in attending thy worship, than a thirsty traveller does in finding water.
Theodotion agrees with the Septuagint, in considering this as a compound word posaplos, (Calmet) and we had best stick to the most ancient version.

62:3.text: Land. "O solitude," cries out St. Ephrem, "ladder of heaven, mother of compunction,....and piety, thou art the guardian of the eyes," etc.
Glory. The ark, (Calmet) or Jesus Christ, the desired of all nations.
I have attended thy worship in spirit, not being able to be present at Jerusalem.
Thus people, who cannot hear mass, ought to act.

62:4.text: Lives. For these must perish, while heaven remains for ever.
This consolation is preferable to any worldly prosperity.

62:5.text: Hands. Doing good works, as the Fathers explain it. This is the posture of people who pray, or take an oath.
I will, etc. Comfort is proportioned to suffering for Christ, 2 Corinthians 1:

62:6.text: Fatness. With the most exquisite delights of the temple, Jeremias 31:14.
Thy grace will enable me to give thee due praise (Worthington) with joy.

62:7.text: Bed. I will think on thee both when I retire to rest, and when I get up.
Thou art my first beginning, and last end.
At all times I will meditate on thy mercies (Calmet) in bed, and much more, when I arise, (Worthington) as I shall not then be so much oppressed with drowsiness.

62:10.text: Earth. To seek me out, (Houbigant) or they shall perish. The Babylonians were exposed to many miseries under Cyrus, as the prophets assert, (Isaias 13:1., and Jeremias 25:12.) though profane history be silent.
David foretells the destruction of his persecutors, either temporal or spiritual.
The devils may be styled foxes.
This may also be verified in the Jews, who persecuted Christ, and fell a prey to the Roman arms, etc.
Saul perished in battle, and his body was exposed on a gibbet to wild beasts, 1 Kings xxxi.

62:12.text: The king. David, (Berthier, etc.) or Zorobabel, for whom the Jews had the highest regard, though he was not an independent monarch, nor wore a crown, Aggeus 2:24., and Zacharias 3:8. It was customary to swear by the life of the king, 1 Kings 1:26., and 20:3. The Fathers understand Jesus Christ to be meant, and this is the true and primary sense; as Zorobabel was only a figure of Him, and never really possessed the throne, that the Jews might not think that the prophecies were fulfilled in his person.
By him. The king, (Du Pin; Calmet) or God.
Those who follow the true religion, (Deuteronomy 6:13., and Isaias 45:23.; Calmet) and act accordingly (Haydock) shall be kings for ever, Apocalypse.
As David came to the throne, on the death of his rival, so was Christ's name exalted, after the Romans had destroyed the Jews.

63:1.text: It has no relation to any historical fact. But it expresses the sentiments of any just man, surrounded with danger.
Yet many apply it to Daniel, in the lion's den, (Muis) to the captives, (Calmet) or to Jesus Christ and his Church.

63:3.text: Malignant. I am encouraged to hope by past experience.

63:4.text: Thing, or discourse. This describes the poisonous insinuations of heretics, (Eusebius) or the calumnies of the Jews against Christ.

63:6.text: Houbigant prefers "us," with reason, (Berthier) after the Syriac, Arabic, Cassiodorus, etc. Yet the Hebrew, etc., retain them, which is very easily explained.

The most wicked desire to preserve the reputation of honesty, (Calmet) and flatter themselves that no mortal is conscious of their deceit, and that even Providence does not regard things below, Psalm 10.

63:7.text: Search. Or, they have made the most diligent investigation, (Berthier) to no purpose.
Thus David and our Saviour were treated.
Heart. That is, crafty, subtle, deep projects and designs; which nevertheless shall not succeed; for God shall be exalted in bringing them to nought, by his wisdom and power.
Hebrew, "both the inward thought of every one of them, and the heart is deep."
But, without the points, the Septuagint is accurate.
The Jews, with all their deep machinations to prevent the belief of Christ's resurrection, bringing even sleeping witnesses, only made themselves ridiculous.
Achitophel was forced to give way, (2 Kings xvii.; Worthington) though he had been considered as an oracle.

63:8.text: The arrows of children are their wounds. That is, the wounds, stripes, or blows, they seek to inflict upon the just, are but like the weak efforts of children's arrows, which can do no execution; and their tongues, that is, their speeches against them, come to nothing.
Or, children themselves have wounded these crafty politicians, and exposed their folly.
Hebrew is "very perplexed in the last three verses. Let us adhere to the Vulgate and Septuagint, who generally read more correctly than the present Hebrew."
Yet St. Jerome gives a very good sense.
Wounds. God hath chosen the weak things of this world to confound the strong, (Worthington) and he hath taken the wise in their craftiness.

63:10.text: Afraid, at the sight of God's judgments on the Babylonians, etc.

63:11.text: Praised. All will begin to esteem the chosen race.
Hebrew, "shall glory." The Jews and Christians (Haydock) shall esteem themselves honoured by the title of God's people.
They shall be praised for rightly serving God, (Worthington) and shall be rewarded by Him (Haydock) with universal applause.

64:1.text: Of the captivity. That is, the people of the captivity of Babylon. This is not in the Hebrew, but is found in the ancient translation of the Septuagint.
It is of little authority. Jeremias and Ezechiel were never together.
Perhaps the former might have put this psalm of David into the hands of the people, when they were going to Babylon, and Ezechiel might have exhorted them to recite it at their return.

It seems to have been composed by David, in thanksgiving for rain; (Psalm xxviii.; Muis) or some of the Levites wrote it, after God had removed the scourge of drought, with which he had afflicted the people, in consequence of their neglecting to finish the temple, Aggeus 1:4., and Malachias 3:9.

David predicts the return from captivity, (Berthier) and the vocation of the Gentiles, (St. Hilary, etc.; Menochius) which the prophets Jeremias, etc., had insinuated, by the coming of the nations from Babylon, so as to forsake idolatry.

64:2.text: Hymn. Or Hebrew, "Praise is silent," (Haydock) "waiteth," (Protestants) or "silence is praise for thee, O God." (Pagnin) Favete linguis.
In Jerusalem, is not in Hebrew, etc., though Houbigant thinks it was originally.
"Only the vows of ecclesiastical religion are useful."
Praises of those who are out of the Church, are not acceptable to God.

64:3.text: O. Hebrew, "hearer of prayer," (Calmet) or "graciously hear my prayer, till all," etc.
To thee. At the last judgment, or (Calmet) at the vocation of the Gentiles.

64:4.text: Transgressions. These are the words of the Christian converts, (Eusebius) or of the Jews, who acknowledge that they have been justly punished with drought, for neglecting the temple and first fruits, Aggeus 1:4., and Malachias 3:9.
Hebrew, "thou wilt expiate our transgressions," which denotes Christ's satisfaction.
Though the wicked threaten, we fear nothing, as long as thou wilt pardon our offences.
These have been the occasion of our past sorrows.
