73:20.text: The obscure of the earth. Mean and ignoble wretches have been filled, that is, enriched, with houses of iniquity, that is, with our estates and possessions, which they have unjustly acquired.

Or the captives may thus complain, that they are forced to live among infidels, in constant danger of transgressing the law, (Calmet) while their children are brought up in sin, (Berthier) and ignorance.
Infidels are full of all sorts of iniquity, which they hide in their conscience.
Injustice is often the method of becoming rich.

73:21.text: Humble. Hebrew, "the contrite," whether of Israel, or of any other nation, Isaias 66:2.
The rich and presumptuous think not of thanking God.

73:23.text: Enemies. Septuagint and St. Augustine read, "servants," and the ancient psalters, "supplicants," (Calmet) which seems to be a mistake of transcribers, (Berthier) as it is contrary to the Hebrew, Chaldean, and Syriac.
The sense of both would be good. Erasmus reads iketon, quaerentium, in his edition of St. Jerome.
They blaspheme all holy things, and are hardened in wickedness.
Such are the times in which we live, 1 Timothy 6:20.

74:1.text: Corrupt not. 'Tis believed to have been the beginning of some ode or hymn, to the tune of which this psalm was to be sung. St. Augustine and other Fathers, take it to be an admonition of the Spirit of God, not to faint, or fail in our hope; but to persevere with constancy in good: because God will not fail in his due time, to render to every man according to his works.
Symmachus has, "concerning incorruption," (Haydock) whence some have explained the psalm of the general resurrection.
The Chaldeans refer it to David, praying that the angel would cease to destroy, (2 Kings xxiv.) while others suppose that he forbids Abisai to hurt Saul, 1 Kings 26:9.
This and similar difficult terms might resemble the anthems of Church music.

The psalm is a sequel to the former, (Calmet) or a moral instruction, given by the Son of God, (ver. 3.) after the author had admonished us to attend, and place ourselves in his presence. It is not necessary to suppose that it is written in the form of a dialogue.

74:2.text: Praise. The repetition shews the certainty of the event. Christ and his apostles, who sit as judges, praise the ways of Providence.

74:3.text: When I shall take a time. In proper times: particularly at the last day, when the earth shall melt away at the presence of the great judge: the same who originally laid the foundations of it, and, as it were, established its pillars.
This is God's answer to the longer prayer of Asaph, in the preceding psalm, which is here concluded.
When I shall have delivered my people.
Justices. With the utmost rigour I will punish Babylon.
No mere creature knows the time of the general judgment, as Christ, the sovereign judge, does.
Then the just themselves will tremble.

74:4.text: After the last fire the earth shall remain, though changed in quality.

74:5.text: Wickedly. This is an epitome of Christian doctrine.
God had severely punished Nabuchodonosor, Baltassar, and the priests of Bel. Yet the people would not attend to these salutary admonitions.
Horn. By pride, (Worthington) which is the origin of all evil, (Haydock) and an offence pardoned by God with the greatest difficulty.

74:6.text: God. Hebrew tsavvar means, "neck." But the Septuagint have not seen the a, and translate against God. Literally, "the rock," which is one of his titles; (Berthier) and this seems preferable to "speak not with a stiff neck;" (Calmet) or "with the old neck:" (St. Jerome) though this sense is not contemptible, as the sinner's wonted pride rises against God.

74:7.text: Hills. Hebrew harim, may also be considered as the nominative case; "not from the south are there heights" to which they may flee for succour.
Yet most of the ancients agree with us; though is there "refuge," must then be supplied.
None would be able to screen the Babylonians, Jeremias 25:15, 26.
Take the cup of the wine of his fury....The king of Sesac (Babylon) shall drink after them.
The cup is so great that all shall taste, and the last will have the most bitter portion.

74:9.text: Drink. The just themselves shall suffer something. But their part will be comparatively the clear wine, while sinners shall have the dregs. Many suppose that God holds in his had two cups, which he mixes according to each one's deserts. So the Septuagint, Syriac, St. Augustine, etc., seem to intimate. Jupiter is thus represented with two barrels of goods and evils near his throne.

But most interpreters suppose that only one chalice is here specified, filled with red wine, the sediment being reserved for sinners, though it was usually thrown away at feasts. Wine was mixed with water in those hot countries.
Yet here the mixture is of a different nature.

Fire, (Psalm 10:7.; Menochius) gall, brimestone, etc., compose the bitter chalice of the damned, who will never arrive at the term of their inexpressible misery. In this life, sinners are frequently punished: but their sufferings do not end here. They shall experience a variety of torments in heat and cold, Job xxiv.

74:10.text: Declare. Septuagint, "rejoice;" as St. Augustine, etc., read, contrary to the Hebrew.
Jacob. Christ did all for the glory of his Father.

74:11.text: Just. Zorobabel, (Theodoret) the figure of the Messias. The Jews were shortly after set at liberty by Cyrus, who was the scourge of their oppressors.
The virtuous, who use well their free-will, are thus rewarded.

75:1.text: Assyrians. Septuagint, "against the Assyrian," Sennacherib, 4 Kings 19:35.
David composed this after his victory over the Ammonites, and Ezechias used it when he was delivered from the Assyrians.
This part of the title is of no great authority, as it is not found in Hebrew, etc.
The psalm seems to speak of the victories of all the just; (Menochius) and instances one memorable example in the defeat of the Assyrians.
The Church triumphs over her persecutors.

75:2.text: This shews that the psalm was composed after the separation of the tribes, (Calmet) though not invincibly; as the names of Juda and Israel were used in David's time.
The divine worship was almost confined to the promised land till the birth of Christ; whose gospel has diffused light throughout the world.
A Christian is the true Juda, or "Confessor."
God was known to some philosophers, but not by such special benefits.

75:3.text: Abode. Hebrew, "tent or hut," an expression which shews, how much the finest structure of the East was beneath God's majesty.
He suffered the rest of the world to follow their own inventions, and false gods, reserving Israel for his Church.

75:4.text: There. In that favoured country. The army of Sennacherib perished on its road to Pelusium, 4 Kings xx.
All the opponents of the Church, or Sion, must perish.

75:5.text: Hills. Of Juda, which are styled eternal, on account of their stability, Deuteronomy 33:15. Hebrew seems to be incorrect.
"Thou art a light magnificently from (Haydock) or more than, (Berthier) the mountains of the captivity."
"Thou art more terrible....than the richest mountains."

Yet this comparison hardly suits in this place, (Calmet) and Houbigant prefers the Vulgate and Septuagint, who may have read terem, "before," or saraph, "of the seraph," (alluding to God's seat upon the ark) instead of tareph, "prey."
God grants victory to his people, and enlightens them with the true faith.

75:6.text: The haughty and blasphemous Sennacherib, Rabsaces, etc., were full of dismay, when the destroying angel slew 185,000 (Calmet) in the dead of the night. "What dire astonishment, ye men Of Media, sunk you to despair?"
Of riches, with which they are possessed, as with a fever, (Seneca, ep. cxix.) and of which they dread, Isaias 29:8.
Yet the most opulent must die, and are foolish in clinging to riches, since they can carry nothing away.
Hands. Hebrew, "the men of the army have not found their hands."
They could not use their arms against a spirit.
Christ has enlightened the mountains, his apostles; and fools despise their instructions, as the Athenians did St. Paul's, being too much attached to the world.
Though they may be troubled, they will not open their eyes to be convinced. Hence, they have no oil of good works, when they awake in eternity. [Matthew xxv.]

75:7.text: Rabsaces had boasted, that Ezechias could not find men to mount 2,000 horses, if he should give them to him, 4 Kings 18:23.
But God chastised his vain boasting.
While he defends his people, their enemies seem to slumber.

75:8.text: And. Hebrew, "thou, and who shall subsist before thee in the moment of thy wrath?" Houbigant rejects the second thou.
From. From the time that thy wrath shall break out.
Ex tunc, often relates to a distant period. We have long known the effects of thy indignation.
At the first notice of thy will the enemy is dejected, and fears thy potent anger.

75:9.text: Heard. Some editions of the Septuagint read, "thou hast darted judgment."
Still. All were filled with astonishment, and Sennacherib was glad to escape in the most private manner.
Persecutors will all be terrified when the signs of judgment begin to appear in heaven, which are here represented as past, on account of their certainty.
The divine power will be again displayed.

75:10.text: God. After the signs of dissolution, the Son of God shall come to judge.
The earth is now full of bustle: but then all shall be silent.
Meek. Ezechias had given large sums to preserve peace, 4 Kings 18:14.
Judgment will take place for the sake of the just.

75:11.text: To thee. The enemy shall repress his resentment, when he beholds the fall of Sennacherib.

The people who had been delivered, express their constant sentiments of gratitude. They revolve in mind the wonders of God, (Haydock) both in time and in eternity, and keep holidays in memory of such benefits.
Hebrew, "for the wrath of man shall confess to thee, thou shalt be girded with the remains of wrath."
The fury of the enemy shall only cause thy power to shine forth in his destruction.
Petau unites both these ideas, in his beautiful Greek verses, though it must be confessed, this passage is very obscure, both in the original and versions.
Men shall meditate on these benefits, and praise God with gladness, being moved to make vows, even of things left to their discretion, which they must perform.

75:12.text: God. Victims of thanksgiving, as was customary after a victory, Psalm xxi., xxvi., xlix., and xiv. He speaks to the people who had been spared, particularly to the priests, though it may be understood also of foreign nations, who complied with this invitation, 2 Paralipomenon 32:22.
Vows, and their completion, ought not to be separated, even though the thing vowed may have been before a matter of choice, as virginity, etc.

75:13.text: Away. Hebrew, "he will cut off," (Montanus) like grapes: which means rather to destroy, than to bereave of counsel, Isaias 19:13. This might be written after Sennacherib was slain, 4 Kings 19:37.
God is terrible, and will demand an account even of princes, respecting vows and other good works. Great discretion is therefore requisite.

76:1.text: Idithun. Hebrew, "upon Idithun," was not formerly in the text.

It may be the name of an instrument, (Bellarmine) or tune, (Muis) or this master of music and Asaph might sing alternately. There is nothing certain; (Menochius) though some would hence conclude, that Asaph was the author. The occasion of the psalm is also unknown, and may be applied to all the afflicted servants of God, (Berthier) or to the captives.

76:2.text: To God. These repetitions denote fervour, (Calmet) and that God alone must be the object of our desire.

76:3.text: Deceived, in my expectations, as I prayed with mind and body continually.
Good works are a strong recommendation. "They cry, though we be silent." Many have recourse to the great for assistance, and few to God. Yet in isto invenio omnia.
Hebrew is variously translated, and may have been altered. "My hand fell in the night, and ceased not." Symmachus and St. Jerome come near to the Vulgate.
They have, "and does not cease," which would be the case, if the person were deceived or rejected.
Joy can come from God alone.

76:4.text: Delighted. Hebrew, "cried out," which many explain through sorrow. But the Septuagint seem rather to take it in a different sense, as well as the swooning, which might proceed from ecstatic joy (Berthier) at the thought of God. The alternate sorrows and joys of the just are well described. They are seldom allowed to continue long in the same state. Protestants, "I complained, and my spirit was overwhelmed. Selah." St. Jerome, "I spoke within myself," exercising myself in meditation.
I was sometimes in such distress, that nothing seemed capable of giving me any comfort. But I relied on God, and was in an ecstacy.

76:5.text: My eyes. Vatican Septuagint, Arabic, and St. Augustine read, "my enemies," but our Vulgate follows the edition of Aldus and Complutensian (Berthier) very frequently, which here agree better with the Hebrew, "I hindered my eyes from looking up;" (St. Jerome; Symmachus) or, "thou hast kept the watches of my eyes," (Aquila) hindering me from sleeping; (Haydock) so that I did not watch three hours only, like the sentinels, but all night.
The sudden address to God seems incorrect.
I rose before the usual time, yet did not utter my sentiments, (Worthington) being quite oppressed both with grief and joy.
I durst not speak, as I was convinced that thy judgments were right.

76:6.text: Of old. And the favours which had been heaped on the nation.
Years. Both past and future times; (Haydock) yea, eternity itself, the great occupation of life.

76:7.text: Heart. Septuagint have read differently from the present [Hebrew].

Hebrew, "I recollected my canticle in the night, and communed with my own heart, and my spirit sought to the bottom;" or, "I swept, (or directed, scopebam) my spirit," (St. Jerome) from all things unbecoming. Septuagint Eskallon. "I dug and harrowed" it by earnest meditation, to extract the weeds, and make it fit to receive the divine seed, (St. Jerome, here and ep. ad Sun.) and to bring forth fruit; (Haydock) or I swept to discover the precious jewel (Berthier) of salvation.
Scopebam, is not deemed a good Latin word; but seems to be derived from skopeo, "I consider or direct my aim;" though some think it means rather," I swept," Isaias 14:23. Hugo reads scopabam.
I diligently examined my conscience, (Worthington) and left nothing unturned, like the woman in the gospel who sought the groat. [Luke 15:8.]
Hebrew yechapes, may also mean, "my spirit is set free," to say what might seem too bold, Will God, etc.

76:9.text: Ever. Hebrew adds, "is his word ineffectual?" which the Vatican Septuagint neglects, (Berthier) though gamar omer be thus rendered in other editions. "Has he completely fulfilled his word," which may be the true sense, consumabitur verbum.
"Will he execute this threat from generation?" etc.
God will never abandon his Church, (Worthington) though he may chastise his people.

76:10.text: Mercies? Turning the waters another way, (Muis; Calmet) or going against his natural inclination. Vincit illum misericordia sua.

76:11.text: Begun. By God's grace, I now perceive that my thoughts were wrong.
I see that we are chastised on account of our sins; (Theodoret) but now I hope for better things.
Hebrew may have this (Berthier) and many other meanings.
Protestants, "I said this is my infirmity. But I will remember the years of right," etc. De Dieu, "To pray, this is mine; to change the right hand, is of the most High."

All comfort and every good resolution must come from him. Challothi is derived from eel, by the Septuagint, and from chala, (Haydock) "he is sick," by others. Who will assert that the former are not the most ancient and learned interpreters? The sequel shews that the psalmist begins to entertain better hopes.
Now have I begun to follow wisdom, and to amend my life. St. Anthony advised all to make this resolution every morning.

76:12.text: Beginning. In favour of Israel, or rather of all the just from Abel.

76:13.text: Inventions. Protestants, "doings," (Haydock) or the secrets of Providence, (Calmet) and his "affections."
The just find an interest in all his works, (Berthier) as they work together for their salvation, Romans 8:28.

76:14.text: Thy ways are inscrutable, (Muis) but always holy.

76:16.text: Arm. Christ, (St. Jerome) or power, Deuteronomy 5:15.
Joseph, who was in Egypt, while the rest of the family dwelt in Chanaan.

76:17.text: Troubled. The dry land appearing, to let the Israelites pass.

St. Jerome and the Jews understand this of the storm of Sinai. But most people suppose that the catastrophe at the Red Sea is described, when Moses insinuates, that a dreadful tempest overwhelmed the Egyptians, as it is here specified. See Josephus, [Antiquities?] 2:7.

76:18.text: Waters. St. Jerome, "the clouds poured out waters," mayim, Septuagint may have read hamim, "sounds," and omit clouds, which come again in this verse.

76:19.text: Wheel. (Protestants) (Haydock) Hebrew, "a whirlwind," (Calmet) or "wheel," (Pagnin) in the air.
The noise of thunder is something similar to a wheel, rattling on the pavement.
Salmoneus foolishly tried to imitate it with his chariot.
Trembled. The preaching of the apostles was attended with success.
Earthquakes were felt, and men were under a general alarm.

76:20.text: Known. The waters resumed their usual course, Hebrews 3:15. The wheels of the enemy might be discerned long after.

76:21.text: Hand. By the ministry (Worthington) of those, who acted in God's place, in the desert.

77:1.text: Asaph. David composed this, to declare the rights of Juda to the throne, in preference to the tribe of Ephraim, (Lyranus) which had kept possession of the ark a long time; which was henceforth to be on Mount Sion.

It seems to relate to the times of Asa, who reunited several of the other tribes to his dominion, (2 Paralipomenon 15:8.; Calmet) and contains a moral instruction, delivered in the person of Christ, (ver. 2.; Eusebius; Berthier) and submitted to the attentive consideration of the faithful.
Law. Given to Moses, (Berthier) and sanctioned by the divine authority.

77:1.src: St. Gregory in Job ii.; Worthington
77:1.text: The law, and the people were not David's, but God's, in whose name he speaks.

77:2.text: Propositions. Deep and mysterious sayings. By this it appears, that the historical facts of ancient times, commemorated in this psalm, were deep and mysterious; as being figures of great truths appertaining to the time of the New Testament.
St. Matthew (xiii. 35.) has, things hidden from the foundation of the world. Hebrew minni kedem, "from of old." St. Jerome, "ancient riddles."
Mashal and chidoth, "parables and enigmas." frequently denote things very plain, but spoken in a sententious poetic style, Numbers 23:7.
The facts, etc., of the Old Testament, prefigured the mysteries of the New.

77:3.text: Fathers. Christ might thus speak as man, and he enforces tradition in the strongest terms.
The most ancient and universal mode of instruction, was by word of mouth.

77:5.text: Testimony. The tabernacle, (Eusebius) or the law which notifies his will.

He also thrice required the Israelites to perpetuate the memory of what he had done for them, by instructing their children, Deuteronomy 4:9., and 6:7., and 11:19. Both the written and the unwritten word must be carefully preserved, 2 Thessalonians 2:14. God had freely chosen Abraham, and given him the law of circumcision; as he directed his posterity by the mouth of Moses.

77:7.text: That, etc. This was the end of all the laws and monuments of religion, (Calmet) to increase our confidence, (Worthington) gratitude, and observance of our duty.

77:8.text: Fathers. Some were virtuous, like Moses, Josue, Samuel, etc., ver. 3., and 5.
To God. Or did not confide in him, or know that without God's grace, no good can be done.
Abraham instructed his house, (Genesis xviii.) and David his subjects, that they might avoid bad example. The same advice regards Christians, 1 Corinthians 10.

77:9.text: Battle. Many of this tribe were cut off by the men of Geth, (1 Paralipomenon 7:21.; Chaldean; Geier.) as they fought without God's command, Numbers xiv.

They did not defend the ark against the Philistines, though they seemed more bound to do so than the rest, since it was brought from their city, Silo, and they also set the others a pattern of infidelity; (1 Kings iv.; Abenezra) whence they are singled out likewise by Osee.
The famous victory of Abia against Jeroboam may be also designated, 2 Paralipomenon xiii.
This had not yet taken place, no more than (Haydock) their captivity, under Salmanazar, which is enigmatically foretold. After this reproach, the whole body of the Israelites is condemned, ver. 10.

77:12.text: Taneos. Hebrew Tsohan, (Haydock) which means, "spreading," either because the plagues spread from this capital, or because it was in a plain, (Berthier) or very extensive, (Haydock) on the eastern branch of the Nile, in the Delta.

77:13.text: Vessel. Literally, "bottle," like walls on either side.

77:14.text: But this is the real import of the Greek. The same cloud (Calmet) was both luminous and obscure. When the light side was turned towards Israel, the Egyptians were in darkness.
The eternal Son of God guided this pillar, and the Israelites tempted him, (ver. 18., and 1 Corinthians x., and Exodus 13:21.; Berthier) forgetting their baptism or initiation in the service of God.

77:15.text: Deep. Water was so abundant, and followed them in streams, 1 Corinthians 10:4.
The first miraculous grant of water was at Horeb, the second at Cades, ver. 20.

77:18.text: Desires. Literally, "souls," as if they were dying for hunger, though they had plenty of manna, Numbers 11:4.

77:19.text: Ill. Hebrew, "against," (Numbers xi.; Calmet) still distrusting in God's power.

77:20.text: Bread. Including all sorts of food.
Table. Hebrew, "flesh." It is true we have water and manna, but we want something more solid and agreeable.

77:21.text: Angry. This is the sense of the Hebrew. Distulit means, "he deferred" (Haydock) to put his threats, (Berthier) or promises, in execution.
The destroyer punished those who gave way to murmuring, 1 Corinthians x., and Numbers 11:1.
Their incredulity was punished (Worthington) for nearly forty years, and all the guilty who were twenty years old at the first numbering, were cut off in the desert.

77:23.text: And. Or "though he had." Berthier has mandavit. "He commanded," would be better rendered, this order being given before the complaints. God had supplied them abundantly with manna from the clouds, as from his granaries.
Therefore they ought to have trusted in his power and goodness.

77:25.text: Angels. Hebrew also, "of the strong ones."
Such is the blessed Eucharist, of which manna was only a figure, John vi. The angels prepared this food.

It was an effect of the divine bounty, not of the power of Moses, John 11:32. How it could be inferior (Berthier) to the bread which Christ would give, was a riddle to the Jews, as it must be still to all who do not admit the real presence. If both were figures, surely manna was better than common bread.

77:26.text: West wind. Literally, Africum, which blows "from Africa," in this direction, with respect to Jerusalem.
The same wind may be styled the south wind. Hebrew Kadim, "strong, eastern," etc.

These quails came from the banks of the southern ocean, or from the Red Sea, as the Israelites were still in Arabia, when they were furnished with them a second time, (Numbers 11:31.) for a whole month, (Calmet) though there were about three million people.
God changed the wind, so as to bring them into the camp, Exodus xvi.
It was before blowing from the south-east.

77:31.text: Israel. St. Jerome applies this to those who receive unworthily, particularly if they be priests, 1 Corinthians 11:29.
God selected the most guilty (Berthier) having allowed them to feast for a whole month.
Then he brought down by death, or "hindered," as it were, "by shackles," (Septuagint) the most valiant.

77:32.text: Still. Notwithstanding this instance of God's severity, they fell shortly after into greater sins, and would have stoned Moses, etc., despairing of ever taking possession of the promised land, which highly displeased God, so that he swore, that none of the rebels should enter it. Many were also slain in the sedition of Core, (Numbers 13:17.) and the rest did not live about thirty-eight years.
Thus about 600,000 perished, (Worthington) having done nothing worthy of praise.

77:34.text: Morning. Those who were spared pretended to repent.
Afflictions are the source of much good. But the Israelites are blamed for their inconstancy and deceit.
They came with apparent earnestness (Haydock) to offer the morning sacrifice (Worthington) under affliction.

77:38.text: Their and them, is supplied also by Protestants.
Hebrew, "he....will forgive sin," etc. This seems more beautiful.

77:39.text: Flesh. The inferior appetite wars against the spirit, Galatians 5:17., and Matthew 26:41.
Not, in the ordinary course. This does not contradict the faith of the resurrection, which is elsewhere clearly expressed.
Man may go astray, but cannot be converted by his own efforts, Proverbs 2:19.
God will never abandon the whole Church. The Jews here mark the middle of the psalter, and the 1263rd verse, (Worthington) or the division of the book.

77:40.text: How often. It would be difficult to specify. God mentions ten times, Numbers 14:22.

77:41.text: Grieved. Hebrew, "set bounds to," or "marked," holding up to scorn, Hebrews 6:6. Genebrard thinks we might translate, "crucified," hithvu, as this is the root of Thau, which formerly resembled a cross.
This would surely be one of the most striking enigmas. St. Jerome agrees with us.
"They pushed on," concitaverunt, as God's wrath must be greatly excited by setting limits to his power and goodness.

77:42.text: Not. How could they so soon forget these prodigies? We might ask, how came Adam to pay so little attention to God's command? How do many act contrary to their better knowledge? Upon occasion of this forgetfulness, the psalmist repeats many of the chief miracles recorded, Exodus vii., and xiii.

77:43.text: Signs. The turning the rod into a serpent. The rest of the signs were also plagues.

77:44.text: Many have asserted that it does not rain in Egypt: but pretty heavy showers fall, even above Cairo, (Vansleb.; Calmet) though seldom.

77:45.text: Flies. Caenomyiam. Many copies of the Septuagint have kynomiam, "the dog-fly," which St. Jerome, (Calmet) and St. Augustine properly correct.

77:46.text: Blast. Hebrew also "the bruchus," (Haydock) a sort of locust, which does great damage in the East.
Ovid speaks of the blast: Interea crescat scabrae rubiginis expers.

77:47.text: Vineyards. Herodotus (I. 77.) says, the Egyptians use "ale, because they have no vines." But he is contradicted by Athenaeus, etc.
Trees. Hebrew shikmoth, is supposed to mean sycamore trees.

77:48.text: Fire. St. Jerome, "who gave their pastures to the hail, and their cattle to the birds." Reshaphim is also rendered "coals," by Pagnin. It may denote the thunderbolts.

77:49.text: Angels. Hebrew, "messengers of evil," (Tirinus) as the Septuagint may also signify the good angels.
He ordered Moses and Aaron to denounce his judgments, which he executed either by the devils, (Origen; Worthington) or by the blessed spirits.
Hence from the effect, (Bellarmine) they may have the appellation of evil.
The other plagues are thus briefly mentioned.

77:51.text: Labour. "The first-born," (St. Jerome; Genesis 49:3., and Proverbs 5:9.) and their best effects.
This was the tenth plague. Cham was the father of Misraim, who peopled Egypt.

77:53.text: Not, after they saw the Egyptians destroyed, whom they had feared greatly before. In the desert they enjoyed rest, while their enemies were in the utmost confusion, (Berthier) having lost their king.

77:54.text: Mountain. Hebrew, "term." Oros, with the soft spirit, means a mountain, (Berthier) and the Vulgate has taken it in this sense in both places, as the Greek accents and spirits are not of greater antiquity or consequence than the Hebrew vowel points.
The land of Chanaan was very different from that of Egypt, being full of mountains, Deuteronomy 3:25., and Ezechiel 36:2. But Sion may be here meant.
Line. Thus were lands measured, Josue 13:8., and 17:5.
God had made a particular choice of this hilly country for his people, (Worthington) and for the chief seat of religion.

77:57.text: The covenant, is omitted in many Greek and Latin copies, as well as in Hebrew.
Yet it is found in the Vatican edition, (Calmet) as well as in the Alexandrian, esunthetesan.
Bow, which hits not the mark. It alludes to the faithless Israelites, (Calmet) particularly to Ephraim, ver. 9., Osee 7:6., and Jeremias 9:3. The bow of Jonathan was not such, 2 Kings 1:22.
A bad bow misses the aim, or breaking, wounds the person who uses it.
These people hurt themselves by their treachery.

77:58.text: Hills. The high places, in which they imitated the pagans, and which brought on their ruin, Leviticus 26:30. With so much difficulty are people taught to serve God in spirit and truth. They foolishly imagined that they would be nearer the gods.
Things. Protestants, "images." They have not forgotten to insert this word as usual, to make the ignorant believe that all images are forbidden!

77:59.text: Heard. So he did the crimes of Sodom, Genesis 18:20.
Reduced. Hebrew, "abhorred exceedingly several in Israel."
The people were not exterminated; but greatly reduced in the time of the judges.

77:60.text: Silo. Where it had remained about 350 years, (Berthier) before the ark was removed, never to be replaced there.
The tabernacle was afterwards at Nobe, and at Gabaon; whence it was probably removed to the treasury of the temple, (Calmet) and was hidden by Jeremias, 2 Machabees i., and Josue 18:1.
God gave his oracles more particularly where the ark, (Worthington) or the tabernacle, was found.

77:61.text: The ark was an earnest of God's protection, (Haydock) and the glory of Israel, 1 Kings 4:21.

77:62.text: Sword. So that they could not escape.
Despised. Hebrew hithhabar,is rendered distulit by St. Jerome, (ver. 21, 59.; Calmet) or non distulit, "he did not delay" to punish, as Erasmus reads.

77:63.text: Fire of God's indignation, (Menochius; Worthington) or of war.
Lamented. St. Jerome, "its virgins no one bewailed."

He seems to have read éullu, with the Septuagint. Others translate, (Berthier) "did not mourn," though they were now deprived of the hopes of marrying, (Calmet) or "the virgins were not praised" in the canticles used at the marriage-feast, (Chaldean, etc.) non epithalamio celebratae sunt, (Montanus) or "married."
There was no time to bewail the death of the young men, or the captivity of the women.
Each one was too solicitous for his own safety.

77:64.text: Priests. Ophni and Phinees, (1 Kings 4:11.; Calmet) the origin of this calamity, (Haydock) and the high priest himself broke his neck.
Mourn, plorabantur, intimates rather that the people did not mourn for him. But the Hebrew has this meaning also, fleverunt, (Montanus) sunt fletae.
The widow of Phinees died on hearing the sad news; and Eusebius seems to think that grief killed Ophni's widow likewise.

77:65.text: Surfeited. St. Jerome, Chaldean, etc., agree in this sense. But Hebrew may admit another, not quite so harsh, (Calmet) "like a hero who shouts for (Montanus) or sing after wine;" (Haydock) dialalon ex oinou, "rendered talkative by wine."
God allowed the Philistines to prosper for a time; but, at last, he covered them with ignominy.

We must reflect that the Oriental languages are bolder in their expressions than ours, and that this is simply a comparison, not more astonishing than that used by our Saviour, when he says that he will come like a thief in the night.
If the Spirit of God had not consecrated such comparisons, no one durst have used them.
God rose to punish the infidels, and to preserve his Church.
The psalmist speaks of him as of a man invigorated by wine, and filling all with confusion.

77:66.text: Parts. As they were fleeing, (Berthier) or with emerods.

77:67.text: Ephraim. Who had been preferred before his elder brother, and yet proved the most inclined to idolatry, ver. 9.
This tribe was deprived first of the ark, and then of the tabernacle, which were its greatest glory, and this strongly indicated the divine displeasure.

77:68.text: Sion. The ark was removed from Cariathiarim, in the tribe of Juda, to the house of Obededom, for three months, and afterwards to the palace or tabernacle on Sion, (Haydock) which God had probably chosen for its fixed abode, towards the beginning of David's reign.

77:69.text: As of unicorns. That is, firm and strong, like the horn of the unicorn. This is one of the chief of the propositions of this psalm, fore-shewing the firm establishment of the one, true, and everlasting sanctuary of God, in his Church.
It was preserved before Christ, and will remain till the end of time.

The temple was not built by David: but the spot was consecrated for it, (2 Kings xxiv.) and the ark was placed on Sion; which was the land which he had founded for ever for this purpose. Hebrew ramim, means "unicorns, (Chaldean; St. Jerome) heights, (Montanus) palaces," (Pagnin) etc. Santificium and sanctuarium, have the same import.

In the land. Hebrew, "as the land." Septuagint have read b for c, as well; (Berthier) and it is observable that Montanus translates the latter, though the former occur in the Hebrew text below, edition 1632: so easily may these letters be confounded!
The temple was to have the same stability as the earth, and was but one, like the horn of the unicorn, which is most solid and beautiful.
Yet this could not be understood of the material temple, which was the most magnificent structure in the world. It was fulfilled in the Church of Jesus Christ, who is also the true David, ver. 72.
The crowning of David, who was his figure, was a great blessing to Israel.

77:70.text: Young. Hebrew also, "giving milk." David was actually with his father's flocks, when he was sent for by Samuel. Saul was also engaged in the pursuits of a country life when he was chosen king: and it would indeed have been difficult to find people of another description among the Israelites, as all followed some business. Croesus observed that he first of his race (Gyges) obtained his liberty and the throne at the same time, as he had kept the flocks of the preceding king.

77:72.text: Skilfulness. Literally, "intelligences." Plural words are used to express the greatness of the thing. David was very upright and intelligent.
This enhances the ingratitude of Ephraim, etc., who divided the kingdom.
Yet David had fallen into some grievous mistakes, so that this can only belong strictly to Jesus Christ, who is the good shepherd, without sin.
He has here detailed what may serve to illustrate the law and the gospel, and may fill us either with confidence or with alarm.
We are astonished at the repeated infidelities of the Israelites. But if three millions of Christians were placed in the same circumstances, would they behave better?

78:1.text: Asaph, who might live during the captivity.
If the ancient (Haydock) Asaph, or David, composed this psalm, it must be considered as a prediction of the ruin caused by Nabuchodonosor, or by Epiphanes.
The author of 1 Machabees 7:17. accommodates it to the sufferings of those (Calmet) whom Alcimus destroyed; or rather the prophet had them also in view as well as Christian martyrs.
He cannot speak of the last ruin of Jerusalem, since it would have been improper to pray for its restoration.

Fruit. A mean village, (Menochius) as Isaias 1:8. had threatened. Hebrew, "a heap of stones," (St. Jerome) in the field, Micheas 1:6. Such was the condition of Jerusalem under Nabuchodonosor (Calmet) and Ephiphanes, 1 Machabees 1:
Catholics have been persecuted in every country, and forced to use mean houses for divine worship.

78:2.text: Saints. The Assideans, who were the most esteemed for piety, 1 Machabees 2:42. In the worst of times, there were always some pious Israelites, and the generality of them were less wicked than their enemies, who exercised a horrid barbarity in refusing them burial, after destroying vast numbers, 2 Paralipomenon 36:17.
This was done at least under Epiphanes, 1 Machabees 7:16.

Persecutors have hung the bodies of martyrs on poles to be the food of birds, (Worthington) as the missionary priests were treated in England not long ago. Hebrew is here rather inaccurate, (Haydock) "to the wild beast of the earth itself;" (Montanus) lechaitho arets, being put forth léith, earts, (Houbigant) as Protestants themselves translate.

78:4.text: Us. The Idumeans, etc., are hence blamed by the prophets, Ezechiel 25:12., and Abdias 10.
Christ and his disciples have been treated with scorn, (Berthier) being styled Galileans, Papists, etc.

78:5.text: Zeal, or jealousy, as God has the greatest affection for his people, and resents their infidelity as a kind of adultery.
Sin is the source of misery.

78:6.text: Name. Their ignorance was of course culpable.
This prayer is prophetical, (St. Augustine) or insinuates that those infidels were still more deserving of punishment.
By destroying Israel, the number of God's worshippers would be lessened.

Yet this consideration would not hinder God from chastising them; and it is not absolutely true that pagans are always more guilty. Those who know the will of their master, and do it not, shall suffer many stripes. [Luke 12:47.] Having the true faith, they may, however, (Haydock) be sooner converted.

78:7.text: They have devoured. So Pagnin ventured to translate the Hebrew. But Montanus substitutes the singular, though it be evidently incorrect, (Haydock) and contrary to all the ancient versions and the parallel passage, (Jeremias 10:25.) as well as to Manuscript 3, Lambeth 435, etc., (Kennicott) v being lost at the end of acol.
Place. Hebrew also, "beauty," the ark or temple.

78:8.text: Former iniquities, which we and our fathers have committed. The Hebrews generally pray for the remission of their parent's faults, Lamentations 5:7., Baruch 3:5., and Daniel 9:5.
But here the penitents' own transgressions may be meant.
God is ready to pardon such.

78:9.text: Help. The necessity of grace, and the co-operation of free-will, are here plainly asserted.

78:10.text: Their God. Let him rescue his people. Cicero (pro Flacco) speaking of the Jewish nation, says, "How dear it was to the immortal gods, appears from its being overcome, enslaved," etc.
Shed. He speaks not of revenge; (Calmet) but in order that chastisement may open the eyes of the infidels, that they may be converted.
Let none suspect that thou disregardest thy people. The event will evince the contrary.

78:11.text: Protect the successors of the martyrs.

78:12.text: Bosom. Punish them severely (Calmet) in this world.
Many of the surrounding nations were subdued by Nabuchodonosor, five years after he had conquered the Jews.

79:1.text: Testimony, or instruction, (Calmet) and proof of the psalmist's faith.

Psalm. Vatican Septuagint, St. Augustine, etc., add, "for (or against) the Assyrian:" whence some have inferred that it relates to the captivity of Israel. But as Benjamin is also mentioned, it seems rather to speak of the captives of Babylon, (Calmet) or of all Jews and Christians in distress.
The faithful pray for the coming of the Messias, ver. 2, 4, 16.

79:2.text: Joseph. He mentions these two as persons dear to God. All the tribes were equally led away captives, and the distinction of kingdoms was not regarded.
All Israel is denoted by Joseph, who composed two tribes, having the double portion; (Worthington; Menochius) and ruling in Egypt.

79:3.text: Manasses. These three tribes followed the ark in the desert, (Numbers 2:18.) and might better see the majesty shining over it.
Let all be united once more in the divine service.
Samaria, and Jerusalem in part, were in the tribes of Ephraim and Benjamin, (Menochius) and Manasses occupied both sides of the Jordan.

79:4.text: Saved. This chorus occurs three (Worthington) or four times.
With God's grace, we shall be able to act virtuously.

Thou canst easily rescue us from our misery. Be pleased to send us the Messias, thy substantial image, 2 Corinthians 4:4., and Colossians 1:15. If thou assist us, we may co-operate to obtain salvation.

79:6.text: The capacity of this measure is not determined. It might be the seah, or the third part of an epha, which would be very abundant, speaking of tears; though small to contain the earth, Isaias 40:12.
Yet their sorrow must be moderated by hope. (Eusebius) See Osee 9:4.
God punishes so as not to destroy us.

79:7.text: Such was the condition of Jeremias, 15:10.
Hebrew, "have scoffed among themselves." Chaldean and St. Jerome agree with us. Scorn is more difficult to bear than poverty. Hence to comfort himself, the psalmist repeats, O God, etc.

79:10.text: Sight. Hebrew, "Thou didst dig before it," (Montanus) making the ground ready.
The cloud went before the Israelites.

79:11.text: God. The highest cedars were surpassed by the branches, or even by the smallest shoots (arbusta) of this vine.
Most powerful nations were forced to submit to David.
The Israelites were exceedingly multiplied, (Worthington) and enjoyed the fruits of the country, Micheas 4:4.

79:12.text: River Euphrates, from the Mediterranean, Red, and Indian seas.

79:13.text: It? Thou hast withdrawn thy protection. The temple is destroyed, and all plunder with impunity, because thy vineyard has not rendered good fruit, Jeremias 2:21.

79:14.text: Singular. The wild boar, which does not go with other beasts. Nabuchodonosor is here designated, (Calmet) or Salmanasar, and all persecutors, (Berthier) particularly the devil, who goes about like a roaring lion, [1 Peter 5:8.] and stirs up his agents to disturb the world. Hence the enemy becomes more cruel than any wild beast.

79:16.text: And look down upon "the Messias," (Chaldean) the true vine, John 15:1., and Matthew 21:33.
Of man, is not in Hebrew, only, ver. 18.
Some perfection was wanting in the Church of the Old Testament.

79:17.text: Things set on fire, etc. So this vineyard of thine, almost consumed already, must perish if thou continue thy rebukes.
Things, would imply that incensa is in the neuter plural. But this is not the case in Septuagint or Hebrew.
St. Jerome has succensam, "Look down upon the vine or root, (17) burn up and without any branches. Let them perish at," etc., who have thus treated it.

79:18.text: The man of thy right hand. Christ, (Challoner) where he sits, (Calmet) being as man in the highest place of heaven, Matthew 26:64., and 12:32.
Zorobabel was only a figure of Him.
Christ's birth was miraculous, (Menochius) and he was appointed by the right hand of God to do great things; and, as man, is moved to take compassion on the distresses of his people.

79:19.text: From thee. This is our fixed determination for the future.
Will call. Hebrew, "Shall be called by thy name," (St. Jerome; Haydock) thy people.
But the Vulgate is equally correct, invocabimus.

80:1.text: For the wine-presses, etc., torcularibus. It either signifies a musical instrument, or that this psalm was to be sung at the feast of the tabernacles after the gathering in of the vintage, (Calmet) or on the feast of trumpets, on the 1st of Tisri, Leviticus 23:24. (Eusebius) (Pin.) (Calmet) See Psalm viii.
From the grape some good wine is extracted, and the rest is thrown away: so in this psalm we find the just rejoice, while the wicked complain, during the persecutions of the Church.
Some Latin copies add, "on the 5th day of the week;" and Genebrard supposes that these additions are owing to the Jewish traditions, (Calmet) as they might sing this psalm on Thursday.
We need not attempt to ascribe this piece to any particular time, though some have thought that it regards the captives, the translation of the ark, or the vocation of the Gentiles, etc.

80:4.text: Noted. Hebrew, "in the obscure, in the day of our solemnity."

Protestants, "in the time appointed, on our solemn feast-day." Cose may denote "obscure or appointed;" (St. Jerome) "in the middle of the month;" (Haydock) which alludes to the feast of tabernacles, when the Jews dwelt under the shade of tents, made of branches. This was esteemed "the most holy and greatest" of their festivals.

It may also be explained of the new moon of Tisri, (Menochius) when the people were admonished of the beginning of the civil year, or of the three great festivals to be then celebrated, perhaps (Haydock) in memory of the world's creation, at that season.
How carefully ought we not, therefore, to celebrate the Christian holidays, which are instituted to excite our gratitude for greater benefits!

The days of the new moons (Numbers 28:11.) were consecrated, to acknowledge God's constant providence; and that of Tisri in particular, (Numbers 29:1.) to thank him for the preservation of Isaac, Genesis 22:18.
But the Lord's day reminds us not only of the world's creation, but also of its redemption, etc., Romans 4:25.

80:5.text: Jacob. It is a duty which we owe to God, in obedience to his command. Hebrew may be, (Haydock) "a decree for the princes (gods) of Jacob."

80:6.text: Joseph. Who represents all the family of Israel, as he took care of it.

Not. The Israelites heard the voice of God at Sinai, (St. Jerome, etc.; Calmet) and for the last time, where forced to hear the insults of the Egyptians at their heels; (Haydock) whose language was barbarous to them, (Psalm 113:1.; Menochius) and not well understood by all, as they had very little society together. Joseph spoke to his brethren by an interpreter.
Some explain this of Joseph himself, when he first came into Egypt, (Chaldean. Bossuet) or of the Israelites, at their arrival there.

But this agrees not with the Vulgate or Hebrew, (Haydock) the latter of which is very confused and incorrect, though it be adopted (Calmet) by St. Jerome: "I heard a tongue which I knew not, I withdrew," etc., (Haydock) or, making a small alteration, "God hast established this festival in Joseph, when He appeared in the land of Egypt to rescue his people: then said the Lord, I made him hear a language which he knew not, that I was the protector of my people, I will remove," etc.

The authors of the Pin. disc. take this liberty, which would make the sense pretty clear. But the Hebrew means, "I heard," etc. Some not being able to understand this, have substituted, "He heard," with the German version.
The ancient Greek interpreters seem to have read the same, as no variation is noticed.

If, however, we must explain the Hebrew of St. Jerome, we must suppose that, "I knew not, means I condemned, as it often does; and God certainly reprobated the harsh language of the Egyptian task-masters, and came to deliver his people from oppression, Exodus 2:25., and 3:8., and 5:14.

80:7.text: "His hands shall pass from the pots."
The Hebrews were thus forced to carry mortar, (Menochius) straw, etc.

80:8.text: In the secret place of tempest. Hebrew, of thunder. When thou soughtest to hide thyself from the tempest: or, when I came down to Mount Sinai, hidden from the eyes in a storm of thunder; (Challoner; Exodus 19:16.) or when I afflicted Egypt, (Exodus 9:23.) and Pharao's army, Psalm 76:19.
Contradiction, at Mara, or rather at Cades, where Moses betrayed some distrust, Exodus 24:25., and Numbers 20:12.
After so many benefits, they still contradicted God, and would not serve him.

80:9.text: Testify. Instruct, or call heaven to witness our covenant.
Man has free will, and may choose whether he will obey or not.

80:10.text: New God. Who must of course be false.
An express law on this head was a great benefit, (Worthington) as most people had gone astray.

80:11.text: Fill it. I will grant all thy just requests, if thou be faithful.
God uses the title of Jehovah, "the Eternal self-existent Being."

80:13.text: Inventions. Ancient psalters read, "wills." This is the greatest (Calmet) of God's judgments, Romans 1:24.
He sometimes permits a person to go on, that he may be disgusted with sin. Ut saturati vitiis vel sic agant poenitentiam.
We ought all to dread this root of bitterness, (Hebrews 12:15.) which may cause us to resist God's grace, and to be abandoned by him, as the Israelites seemed to be, before their ruin came on.

80:15.text: Soon. Forsitan, "perhaps," (Haydock) does not here imply a doubt, (Menochius) but rather the ease and liberty (Worthington) with which God could have rescued his people (Hebrew) "in a moment."

80:16.text: Enemies. The faithless Israelites, (Calmet) or infidel nations.
Ever. Impenitent sinners shall suffer for ever.
The Jews will scarcely be converted at last.
For a long time God bore with their infidelities, and heaped favours upon them.

80:17.text: And. Or "though." This increases their ingratitude.
Filled. Hebrew, "I will fill thee;" which reading few admit. St. Jerome agrees with us.

Protestants, (16) "the haters of the Lord should have submitted (marginal note, lied) to him; but their time should have endured for ever. (17) He should have fed them, (v rather signifies "him;" Haydock) also with the finest of wheat, and with honey out of the rock, should I have satisfied thee." This sudden change seems rather abrupt, though God may have spoken either in the first or third person, from ver. 6.
Rock. He fed them in the desert with manna, and the water seemed most delicious, as they were thirsty; unless he speaks of real honey.
The promised land was very fruitful; but all this prefigured the favours which God bestows upon his servants, in the blessed Eucharist, 1 Corinthians 10:4.
How many, like Judas, partake of them, and prove faithless!

81:1.text: Asaph. Josaphat gave the like instructions to his judges, 2 Paralipomenon 19:6.
God rejected the Jews in the preceding psalm, and here the reason is assigned.

81:1.src: Catec. Trid. Symb.[The Catechism of Trent: The Creed: Article ix.?]
81:1.text: Congregation, or church of the Jews, who were gathered together like cattle, and sought after temporal advantages (St. Augustine, in Psalm 72:23.) alone.
This is not exactly true of all, (Hebrews 11:14.) though it might be of the people in general, who seldom raised their thoughts to spiritual delights.
Of gods. Hebrew, "of God," who presides in his own council, and gives authority to others.
Yet Syriac has, "the angels;" Chaldean, "the just;" Protestants, "the mighty;" and Aquila, "the powerful ones."

Gods here are put for judges, who act in God's name, Deuteronomy 1:17., and Exodus 22:28. To decide affairs of consequence, the priests and other judges met in the temple; and the Romans did the like, each senator offering incense and wine to the god who was honoured in that place.
All magistrates ought to be equally careful in what they determine, as if they heard God present giving them the following instructions.
Judgeth gods, or "God, our Saviour, is judged."
This bringeth on the reprobation of the Jews, ver. 5, etc.

81:2.text: Wicked. Contrary to the law, Deuteronomy 1:17., and Leviticus 19:15.

81:3.text: Do justice. This is the sense of justificate, "justify," as the poor must not be screened from justice, any more than the rich.
They are in greater danger of being neglected, Isaias 1:23., and Jeremias 5:28.

81:5.text: Moved. The ignorance, inapplication, and injustice of those in power overturn the state, which is supported by justice, Proverbs 14:34. They draw down God's curse upon the land, (St. Jerome; Calmet) and expose it to all the miseries of anarchy. This regards pastors also.
By gross ignorance judges walk in darkness, and throw all into confusion.

81:6.text: Gods. The people look upon you as such, and treat you with respect. But entertain not sentiments of pride on that account.

If judges, even those whom God condemns, may be styled gods without blasphemy, how much more might Jesus Christ, who was holy and did the works of God? He uses this argument to make the fury of the people abate: but then he continues to prove that he was God in a very different and proper sense, insomuch that the Jews, clearly perceiving his meaning, which Socinians would now mistake, took up stones to throw at him, John 10:34.

If he had not been God, He would surely have told them plainly, as St. Paul did when people would have offered sacrifice to him, and as St. John the Baptist confessed that he was not the Christ. The sons of Seth, priests, the just, and all the Israelites, were styled sons of God, as well as the angels and judges, Genesis 6:2., Psalm 28:1., Wisdom 5:5., and Job 1:6. But no individual is called the Son, except Jesus Christ the true God.

This passage may be understood of all the faithful, (St. Augustine) particularly of bishops: and Constantine the Great was hence deterred from judging them (St. Gregory 1:dec. Grat. p. 2. C. 11. q. 1.) or the clergy; (St. Melchiades C. 12. q. 1.) though this name seems inaccurate, as he died before (Glossa) the council of Nice. Amama restrains the text to men in power.
Their elevated station makes their ignorance and misconduct less excusable.

81:7.text: Men. Hebrew Adam, or "like a man" (Montanus; Haydock) of the meanest rank.

81:7.src: Eusebius; St. Justin Martyr, Dialogue with Trypho
81:7.text: Princes. Among men, (Calmet) or like Lucifer, the first of the rebel angels.
Most tyrants come to a miserable end.
At death, judges themselves are brought to the bar, and their case is then more terrible, as the mighty in guilt shall suffer more, Wisdom vi.

They are forced to taste of death; while Jesus Christ was master of it, John 10:17, 28. This comparison evinces Christ's divinity. But Socinians blind themselves by looking at the sun, and attempting to fathom all by the weak light of reason.

81:8.text: Nations. Those whom thou hast appointed judges, prevaricate, Genesis 18:25.
Come, therefore, thyself, Lord Jesus, (Apocalypse 22:20.) to whom all nations were promised for an inheritance.
His appearance at the last day is described in the 49th psalm; and most of the Fathers here discover a clear prediction of the conversion of the Gentiles.

82:1.text: Asaph. This psalm alludes to the wars of David, (2 Kings viii.; Berthier) against Ammon, (Bossuet) or of the Jews returned from captivity, (2 Esdras iv., and Ezechiel xxxviii.; Theodoret) or of the Machabees; (1 Machabees v., and 2 Machabees x.; Bellarmine) or rather of Josaphat, 2 Paralipomenon xx.
All in danger are taught to have recourse to God.

82:2.text: To thee? Hebrew, "be not silent to thyself."
But domi also implies "like;" (Bellarmine) and there would otherwise be a sort of tautology.
Christ on earth was like other men: but when he shall come to judgment, none will be comparable to Him.
Amama says this exposition is groundless: but others are of a contrary opinion.

The ancient Greek interpreters seem not to have varied from the Septuagint, though St. Jerome adopts the present Hebrew, "be not silent. Hold," etc., which may express the utmost fervour and want of protection. Thy own cause is now at stake: the enemy wishes to destroy religion.

82:3.text: Head. To revolt, 4 Kings 3:9., and 2 Paralipomenon xx.

82:4.text: Saints. Hebrew, "hidden ones;" (Protestants; Haydock) the people under God's protection, (Menochius; Berthier) or the treasures of the temple.

82:5.text: Nation. That there may be no more of this religion, or Catholics.
Israel delighted most in being styled the chosen nation of God.

82:7.text: Agarenes descended from Agar, though they took the name of Saracens, as if they had sprung from Sara, (Worthington) or they dwelt at Agra, otherwise called Petra, in Arabia; or on the east of Galaad, 1 Paralipomenon 5:10.

82:9.text: Assyrian, or "Assur," sprung from Dedan, (Genesis 25:3.) and dwelt near the Ammonites.
All infidels conspire against Catholics, (Worthington) and are willing to forget their private quarrels.

82:10.text: Madian: 120,000 of whom were defeated by Gedeon with 300 men, (Haydock; Judges vii.) as Sisara was routed by Debora, at Cisson, and slain by Jahel, Judges 4:

82:11.text: Endor. Near this place the Madianites fell upon one another.
All their princes of Madian, (Calmet) or treat all the princes of the people, who now attack us, like them.

82:15.text: Mountains. The prayer or prediction was not in vain. The enemies of Josaphat, (2 Paralipomenon 20:11, 24.; Calmet) and of David, etc., were destroyed.
The trees on the mountains, (Menochius) or the mountain itself, might burn like Etna and Vesuvius.
The destruction of the wicked is thus described.

82:16.text: Tempest. During the night, a tempest probably arose, which threw the enemy into confusion, who supposing that the Israelites had broken into the camp, fell upon each other in the dark.
St. Paul reminds us of God's indignation, which Christians as well as Jews ought to dread, Hebrews 12:25, 29., and Deuteronomy 4:24.

82:17.text: Name. This was the desire of the prophet; (St. Jerome; Calmet; Berthier) and for this end, God sends afflictions. The impenitent must perish for ever, ver. 18.

82:19.text: Lord. He who is, Exodus 3:14., and 6:3.
The name of the Lord is Himself.

83:1.text: Core. See Psalm viii., xli., and lxxx.

The Corites were musicians, as well as porters in the temple, 1 Paralipomenon xxvi. They here represent the faithful upon earth, (St. Augustine; Worthington) who sigh after the heavenly Sion. David was animated with these sentiments, more than with the desire of revisiting Jerusalem, during the revolt of his son.
This psalm might have been written by him under the persecution of Saul, (Grotius) or it may refer to the captives.
Yet, at those times, the tabernacle was not subsisting on Sion, as it seem to have been when this beautiful piece was composed.
The Jews are said to recite it every night, in hopes of seeing Jerusalem rebuilt, and it might very well be used by all Israelites, when they went to celebrate the three great festivals.

83:3.text: Fainteth. The eager desire of heaven sometimes deprives people of external satisfaction, and the body partakes of the inward joy.
Living. The idols of Babylon have no life.

83:4.text: Turtle. Moderns prefer to render "swallows," without reason.
Thy altars. They can rest in the ruins of the temple; (Kimchi; Muis) but in that supposition, the altars were destroyed.

It seems rather that this is an exclamation, (Berthier) which the enraptured psalmist is unable to conclude, giving us to understand that he desired his asylum and place of rest to be near God's altars, (Haydock) with the angels above, Isaias vi.

The faithful soul seeks to dwell in heaven, and in the mean time keeps in the Catholic Church, laying up store of good works. For, out of it, whatever good pagans and heretics may seem to do, by feeding the hungry, etc., as these things are not laid in the nest, they will be trodden under foot, conculcabuntur.

83:6.text: In his heart, he hath disposed to ascend by steps, etc., ascensiones in corde suo disposuit. As by steps men ascended towards the eternal temple by certain steps of virtue disposed or ordered within the heart. And this whilst he lives as yet in the body, in this vale of tears, the place which man hath set: that is, which he hath brought himself to: being cast out of paradise for his sin.
There is no standing still. "As the saint daily advances, so the sinner daily decreases."
Hebrew of these three following verses is variously rendered. The Septuagint are the most ancient, and very exact.
Heart. "The more you love, the higher will you ascend."

Hebrew, "the paths are in his heart. Passing in the vale of tears, they shall place (or deem) it a fountain. The teacher shall be clothed with benediction. They shall go from strength to strength: they shall appear before God in Sion."

Three words occasion the difference: ábri, "passing," means also, "disposing." Septuagint have only used it as a singular, to agree with man, Máin, "a fountain," may have been read máun, "for the place."

Amama says the points are different. But we have often shewn the futility of that objection; which might regulate the versions of the Masorets, but could have no influence on those who lived many ages before their invention. They are neglected here by the authors of Prin. disc., "the God almighty shall appear in Sion."

83:7.text: Tears. Protestants, "Baca." Marginal note, "of mulberry-trees," near Jerusalem, Judges 2:5., and 1 Kings 5:23.
It was perhaps used proverbially for any dry place. The Lord had promised to relieve the captives with water, Isaias 35:5., etc.
Place. The temple or tabernacle, (Haydock) which the Lord hath appointed.

83:8.text: Blessing. Abundance of water, and other necessaries, (2 Corinthians 9:6.) as well as (Haydock) spiritual graces, which help those who continue in the true Church to arrive at the vision of God.
Virtue, or "company," in which manner the Israelites went to the temple.
God. And not merely the temple, etc., as here on earth.

83:10.text: Christ. Chaldean, "the Messias," (Berthier) through whom we address all our petitions.
Protect thy people, (St. Jerome) and raise up the throne of David.

83:11.text: Thousands elsewhere, (Calmet) among sinners. He is so much affected, as to leave the sentence imperfect, ver. 4. But the meaning is clear. Temporal must yield to eternal happiness. Eternity is all as one point: it has no division of time, which has a thousand parts.
Heaven is represented as a palace, (Berthier) in which the blessed enjoy perpetual felicity.
With respect to future rewards, one day in the Church is better than thousands out of it. (Worthington), ver. 4.
Abject. Protestants, "door-keeper." Marginal note, "on the threshold."
This was the office of the Corites, (Calmet) and they prefer it before the finest occupations among sinners. Hebrew, "the tents of wickedness."
The poorest condition in the Catholic Church, is better than the highest dignities which the wicked can bestow.
Indeed poverty, and attention to God's service, is the most secure road to heaven, and gives even present content to those who are actuated by the divine spirit.

83:12.text: Truth. He is merciful, and always performs what he has promised: (Menochius) whereas sinners are noted for cruelty and deceit. Hebrew, "the Lord God is a sun and shield."
This sense is very good. But Theodotion agrees with the Septuagint, who have read differently, unless they have substituted the thing signified for the figure.
Glory, in the next world, (Worthington) or even in this. He will restore us to happiness, and cause even our persecutors to esteem us.

83:13.text: Innocence. After the remission of sin.

84:1.text: Psalm. It resembles the 66th [psalm], and seems to have been sung when the first-fruits were brought to the temple. Most people explain it of the captives delivered, (Theodoret; Du Pin) and of Christ's redemption.
David foresaw the afflictions and captivity of his people; and was aware of the miseries of mankind, to be removed by the Messias alone.

84:2.text: Blessed. Hebrew, "taken into favour," or "hast rendered fruitful."
God had bestowed many benefits upon his people, rescuing them from the Egyptian bondage, and not punishing them as much as they deserved.
Others explain it of the captivity at Babylon, or under the devil.
David speaks of the former event by the prophetic spirit, and the latter misfortune was always deplorable, and to be terminated only by the Messias.
The redemption of man was prefigured by the liberation of the Jews.

84:5.text: Convert. Bring back the remnant of thy people, dispersed through the world. Only a few returned under Cyrus; the rest came back by degrees principally during the reigns of Hystaspes and Alexander the Great.
While we continue unconverted, we are objects of God's wrath.
Our Saviour. Septuagint, "of our salvations." St. Jerome, "our Jesus."
Saviour of mankind, mitigate thy wrath against us.

84:6.text: Ever. The Pythagoreans settled their differences before sunset.
As long, O God, as we see not our brethren restored, we shall think that thou art not perfectly reconciled to us.

84:7.text: Turn, conversus. The ancient psalters read convertens. "Converting, O God, thou wilt bring us to life," free us from captivity, and redeem us from sin by Jesus Christ, the conqueror of death.
Before their conversion sinners lie dead in guilt.
O God, thou wilt again restore us to life.

84:8.text: Salvation. By Cyrus, or rather by the Messias, whose time drew near.

84:9.text: Hear. Hitherto the prophet had been distracted by the thought of his people's misery.
In me, is not expressed in Hebrew.
Heart. Some of the ancients add, "to him."

The Septuagint seem to have had a copy different from the present Hebrew, "But let them not turn again to folly;" (Protestants; Haydock) though the sense is much the same. They may have read lobom lie, "their heart to God," (Berthier) or lobsle, (Calmet) "the heart, Sela;" instead of lecisla, "to folly."

Those Israelites who had given way to idolatry, were little inclined to return to their own country, at the invitation of Cyrus. Though Christ came to save all, only men of good will obtained his peace, Luke 2:4., and John 1:5.
There is no peace for the wicked, Isaias 48:22., and Philippians 4:9.
The redemption of the world was here revealed.

84:10.text: Land. After the captivity, Judea flourished by degrees. But the glory of the second temple consisted in the presence of the Messias, Aggeus 2:8.
Those who were moved with godly fear, embraced the gospel, in order to be saved, while many rejected it through their own fault.

84:11.text: Kissed. Or, "embraced," like friends, as the ancient psalters read. The people practised these virtues after the captivity, and more particularly in the Church of Christ.
At the time appointed, He reconciled sinners to his Father, having satisfied his justice, (Berthier) and displayed his own mercy.
Thus justice is strictly observed, and peace made between God and man.
The justice of the Father and the mercy of the Son kiss each other.

84:12.text: Earth. Good men preserve a clear conscience.
Virtues of every description (Menochius) are become common among God's people, (Calmet) particularly Christians, though our Saviour may here be styled justice.
He was born of a pure virgin.
Jam redit et virgo; redeunt saturnia regna.

84:13.text: Fruit. By imitation, (Calmet) "we may give birth to Jesus Christ," says St. Jerome. God bestows grace, and so men yield fruit.

84:14.text: Him. The holy John the Baptist shall prepare the way of the Lord.
After the captivity religion shall reign. If we wish to enter heaven, we must follow virtue.

85:1.text: David. He might compose this psalm under any affliction, though prayer is always necessary (Berthier) for any person.
Others apply it to Ezechias, (Theodoret) the captives, or the Church.
Jesus Christ prays during his passion, and foretells his resurrection, and the vocation of the Gentiles.
Needy. Hebrew, "mild or afflicted." Humility is requisite to pray well, as those who think themselves rich, ask not with fervour.
God "inclines his ear if thou dost not lift up thy neck."

85:2.text: I am holy. I am by my office and profession dedicated to thy service, (Challoner) as a Levite, (Calmet) or a member of the true Church.
He may also speak in the person of Christ, who prays for us and in us.
Syriac and Arabic read, "thou art good."
Our necessities, firm purposes of leading a virtuous life, and confidence in God, move him to shew mercy.

85:4.text: Soul. I ardently desire to serve in thy temple, Jeremias 22:27. A holy importunity, (Luke 18:2.; Tertullian, Apol. 39.; Calmet) and fervour in prayer, (Haydock) with perseverance, attention, and a sense of God's infinite perfections, are requisite.

85:7.text: Heard me. This gives me confidence that thou wilt do it again.
Thou art not like senseless idols.

85:8.text: Gods, which have been set up by men; or among angels, etc., (Calmet) capable of working miracles by an independent power.

85:9.text: Name. This was partly verified after the captivity, and fully when the gospel was preached.
The Gentiles came by faith, and glorified God by good works, Matthew 5:17.

85:10.text: Alone. Vatican Septuagint, Arabic, etc., add, "the great." (Calmet) (Acts 17:24.

85:11.text: Truth. Let me see the justice of thy conduct in suffering the wicked to prosper, (Psalm 72:17.) or let me invariably observe thy holy law, which alone can give me true content.
Rejoice. Hebrew, as we read at present, "likewise fear," (Calmet) "unite," (Montanus) or "let my heart be one," solitary, disengaged from all, unicum, (St. Jerome) "to fear," etc.
This sense is very good. But yached, means also rejoice.
Our joy must be mixed with fear.

85:13.text: Hell of the damned, (Worthington) according to the Fathers: or out of captivity and dangers.
If it be understood of Christ, it must refer to limbo.
It seems equivalent to the lower pit, Psalm 87:7.

85:13.src: De Gen. contra Manch. 2:17. in Psalm vi.; Calmet
85:13.text: The Jews admit seven regions in hell, (Genebrard) and our theologians four: 1. Of the damned; 2. of unbaptized infants; 3. of purgatory; and 4. of the saints in Abraham's bosom. St. Augustine mentions the first and last here: but he speaks clearly of purgatory in other places.
David was rescued from the most imminent dangers, and Christ came out of limbo, (Psalm 15:10.; Du Hamel) by his own power.

85:14.text: Eyes. The Babylonians oppress us most unjustly.

85:15.text: True. This title is also applied to Christ, who, of course, is God:; since every man is liable to mistake, Romans 3:4., and Apocalypse 19:1. God authorized Moses and the prophets to address Him, as the psalmist does, Exodus 34:6., and Joel 2:13.

85:16.text: Command. Hebrew, "strength," (Haydock) to resist the enemy.
Order me as thou pleasest: I am entirely devoted to thee from my very birth, Psalm 115:16. Restore the throne of David.
The prophets frequently thus turn to the Church, the kingdom of the Messias, who was born of the handmaid of the Lord.

85:17.text: Good. Mark me for thy slave, (Calmet) which will be the highest honour.
Give me such a sign as the angel did when he was going to destroy the first-born of Egypt.
By some prodigy convince our oppressors that there is a God.

The resurrection, prefigured by Jonas, was the great proof of Christ and his Church; and this serves either for the conversion or for the condemnation of mankind. See St. Augustine. (Worthington) Quod precor eveniet, sunt certa oracula vatum, Nam Deus optanti prospera signa dedit.
Comforted. St. Cyprian was in sorrow while suffering, but was comforted when crowned. "All the martyrs, with Christ, pray for us."

86:1.text: Mountains. So far the Jews extend the title, supposing that the subject of this canticle was concerning Sion, Moria, etc. Others think that, thereof, refers to a part of the psalm which has been lost, (Calmet) or to the temple or city which occupied the prophet's thoughts; or it is sufficiently explained by the word Sion, which follows, as the relative sometimes comes first. (Du Hamel) See Proverbs 14:3.

This psalm might be composed (Berthier) by some of the Corites, during, or after the captivity, when Jerusalem received strangers within her walls, as a figure of the Christian Church; (Isaias 2:2., and Zacharias 8:20.; Calmet) or David (Berthier) might write it when he had removed the ark to Sion, which was to be the seat of the true religion.
Mountains. The apostles and prophets; (Ephesians 2:20.; Challoner) on which the Church is founded.
The city was styled holy, from the temple built on Moria. Several other mountains were included within its walls.

86:2.text: Jacob. Jerusalem belonged to some of his children. Hence the psalmist clearly speaks of something better, even of the Church of Christ, (Berthier) which God has chosen for his spouse, having abandoned the synagogue, (Apocalypse 21:9.; Calmet) which was only intended to be a pedagogue.

86:4.text: Rahab. Egypt, etc. To this Sion, which is the Church of God, many shall resort from all nations.
Christ gives his apostles command to preach to all, Matthew xxviii., and Luke 24:27.

Some have supposed that Rahab, who received the spies, is here put, to signify the Chanaanites. But her name is written Reb; and the word before us is Rahab, (Haydock) signifying "pride," (Menochius) or Egypt; particularly the Delta, which is still called Rib.
Me. I will receive into my Church not only the Jews, but also the most abandoned nations.
Were. Hebrew, "this man was born there."
In Christ there is no distinction of nations, Romans 10:12. The Jews will never shew us the completion of this prophecy any where but in the Church.
It is spoken of as past, to denote the certainty of the event.

86:5.text: Shall not Sion say, etc. The meaning is, that Sion, viz., the Church, shall not only be able to commemorate this or that particular person of renown born in her, but also to glory in great multitudes of people and princes, of her communion; who have been foretold in the writings of the prophets, and registered in the writings of the apostles.
We might also translate, "shall it not be said of Sion?"
Some may have read dicetur, instead of dicet. "Shall not a man say to Sion, yea, a man is born in her?"

Septuagint have, "mother," metre, which St. Jerome says should be meti, shall not. Other nations may have produced some great men. But Sion has given birth to the Man-God, who was brought into the world in its vicinity, and preached and rose again there.
She has had many heroes, and has been founded by the Highest.
Christ, both God and man, is the builder of his Church, and people, in admiration, recount how many great personages have embraced her communion.
John the Baptist, a man sent by God, announced the Messias; who, according to his humanity, was born of a virgin, among the people of Sion; while, by his divine nature, He was the most High.

Sion, or Jerusalem, shall be the mother of an infinite multitude, (Isaias 49:18.) the metropolis of Judea. Septuagint, Arabic, etc., read, "mother," in this sense. People deemed it an honour to be born there.
Christ "became man for our sakes, being God before us. How do we know this? The Lord has told us in the writings of peoples."

86:6.text: Writings. He alone can number the inhabitants: or He will enroll all nations as citizens of Sion.
The New Testament explains the vocation of the Gentiles, and the incarnation of Christ.

The Scriptures are the books of all mankind, as well as of princes. All are equally interested in their contents, and ought to become acquainted with them. Hebrew, "the Lord has numbered, writing down peoples: He was born in it for ever."

This refers to Jesus Christ, whose birth is also specified in the preceding verse, (Berthier) as ennobling Sion, far more than that of Homer, Alexander, or Caesar could do any of the pagan cities, Egypt, etc., ver. 4.

The mystery of the incarnation will shine forth at the last day, as well as the glory of the elect. But these things are already consigned in part to all nations, in the writings of the apostles, (Berthier) and in ecclesiastical history.
Princes, is not here in Hebrew.

86:7.text: The dwelling, etc. Hebrew, "the singers, like people dancing, (for joy) all my fountains are in thee." St. Jerome reads, "strong ones," and (Haydock) others, "my thoughts," instead of "fountains;" which shews that they have read differently. Vesharim, may signify and princes, taken form the line above, though of this we ought to have some proofs from manuscripts, etc.
The authority of the Septuagint may perhaps suffice, (Haydock) as they have read more correctly mauni, "habitation," rather than mahyanai, as we have at present.
If we adopt this sense, (Haydock) the fountains may denote the nations which shall spring from Sion, Psalm 67:27.
All the inhabitants shall be filled with joy in the heavenly Jerusalem, Apocalypse 17:15.
This short, but difficult psalm, allegorically describes the mystery of Christ, and of the Church.
Catholics may taste pure delights, having peace of conscience, etc. But the blessed enjoy the most perfect content in the Church triumphant.

87:1.text: Maheleth. A musical instrument, or chorus of musicians, to answer one another, (Challoner) in doleful music.
Hebrew may imply, "on infirmity, (Montanus) or sorrow," from ele.
The subject is very mournful, and relates to the captives, and to Christ's suffering.
Understanding. Or a psalm of instruction, composed by Eman, the Ezrahite, or by David, in his name.

We read of Eman, a descendant of Juda by Zara, (1 Paralipomenon 2:6.; Calmet) and if he composed this piece, as the Jews and Lightfoot improbably suppose, it must be the most ancient (Calmet) writing extant.

There was a son of Joel, and a seer of king David, of the same name, 1 Paralipomenon 6:33., and 25:1. But they are not styled Ezrahites. This person was probably the brother of Ethan, the Ezrahite, who might be young under David, and a man of consummate wisdom under his successor, 3 Kings 4:31. The psalm may express the sentiments of David, or of any other under tribulation, as well as those of Jesus Christ, (Berthier) who speaks herein, (Houbigant) and who expects that we should answer him by an imitation of his virtues.
Septuagint, etc., read incorrectly, "Israelite," (Calmet) and some copies have "Aitham," or Ethan, as in the following psalm, instead of Eman.

87:2.text: Thee. The psalm 21st is nearly similar to this. My prayer is continual.

87:3.text: Prayer. It is represented as a person prostrated before God. Homer (Iliad ix.) says, that "supplications are the daughters of Jupiter, lame....with the eyes downcast, and following after injuries," which admirably shews the conditions requisite for prayer.

87:4.text: Our Saviour said, My soul is sorrowful unto death. [Matthew 26:38.] David, Jeremias, or the captives, were not reduced to this extremity.

87:5.text: Pit. Like a slave confined every night in prison, Exodus 12:29.

87:6.text: Free. Hebrew also "separated" from society, 4 Kings 15:5., and 2 Paralipomenon 26:21.
Christ, after enduring the greatest miseries was still free. He could resign his life, and take it up again, John 10:18.
The Fathers adopt this explanation, which is very striking.
Hand. Thou actest as if thou hadst forgotten the corpse in the dust, till the time of the resurrection.

In the mean while, those who formerly made such a noise in the world, are effaced from the book of life, or from God's register. He is often represented as a great monarch, keeping an account of his troops.
He cannot forget any of his creatures, (Berthier) though he may not restore them to life as yet.
Christ possessed infinite power among the dead, (Worthington) who are free from the cares of this world.

87:7.text: Shadow. Hebrew, "in the depths," seem to have read a, v, and m, in those two places, which are now wanting.

The Chaldean has, the shadow of death, as well as the Vulgate. All this regards Jesus Christ, though it may be applied to any in distress. (Berthier) -- The wicked endeavour to kill the soul by sin, as well as the body.
Great difficulties entangle the psalmist: Christ descends into hell.

87:8.text: Waves. Of afflictions. Christ bore our iniquities.
