87:9.text: The disciples abandoned our Saviour; St. Peter, with a curse, denied that he ever knew him, (Mark 14:71.; Haydock) and Judas betrayed him. It is not so easy to explain this of the captives in general, though it might refer to the psalmist.
At Babylon, the Israelites were not imprisoned, but left to multiply, Jeremias 29:5.

87:10.text: To thee, for aid, (Psalm 27:2.) or to implore pardon for sinners. Thus Jesus prayed for us on the cross, (Calmet) with his hands stretched out ready to receive the penitent.
Septuagint have read rupaim ikimu for ropaim ikumu, "shall the dead arise?"

87:11.text: Physicians. St. Jerome, "will the giants rise again?"
These were heroes of great renown.
But they were consigned to hell, whence there was no redemption, Job 25:5., and Proverbs ix.
The author insinuates that if the true adorers be cut off, God's external glory will be diminished.

This argument is often pressed, Isaias xxxviii., and Psalm cxiii.--- Rephaim (Haydock) denotes physicians, as well as giants, Genesis 50:2., and 2 Paralipomenon 17:12. The Thalmud sentences "the best of them to hell."
Their power does not extend to the dead.
The psalmist prays to be preserved from death, not expecting to be raised again miraculously.
Yet Christ contemplates his future glorious resurrection.

87:13.text: Of, etc. Septuagint, "forgotten land." Securos latices et longa oblivia potant.
When dead, I shall not be able to sound forth thy praises before men: much less shall those do it, who are confined to the regions of darkness.

87:15.text: Prayer. Some copies of the Septuagint read "soul," with the Hebrew, etc.
Why dost thou neglect to grant my request, which I urge with all the earnestness of my soul?
This may relate to Jesus in the garden.
His prayer prevents, or is presented early, (Menochius) and with the truest fervour to the Lord, who moves us to pray.

87:16.text: Exalted. On the cross, or arrived at the years of manhood, (Berthier; Worthington) I have experienced the greatest contradictions. David was brought up in poverty, like our Saviour, and the exaltation of both was attended with great trials.
The life of Christ was a continual martyrdom.
He had all his sufferings in view from his first conception.

87:17.text: Troubled me. The enemy has laid waste the country. This agrees with Christ in his agony.
Unhappy the sinner, upon whom the wrath of God remains, (John 3:36.) and does not merely come; literally, pass, transierunt.

87:19.text: Misery. Hebrew, "darkness." Thou hast permitted my friends to abandon me, and hast exposed me to disgrace.
They were afraid lest they might be involved in my calamities, (Menochius) if they appeared in my defence, (Haydock) or seemed to know me.

88:1.text: Ezrahite. Septuagint, etc., "Israelite," as in the former psalm. The Jews think that Ethan or Eman lived during the Egyptian bondage. But this psalm was rather composed by one of the captives at Babylon who bewails the destruction of the kingdom of Juda, under Sedecias. After he had detailed the promises of God, (ver. 39.; Calmet) David might write it in the person (Haydock) of Ethan, or Idithun, 1 Paralipomenon 25., and 3 Kings. 4:31.
Most of the Fathers explain it of Christ's kingdom. See Psalm 136:11., and Jeremias 33:17.
The sceptre or administration of affairs was to continue in the tribe of Juda till his coming, as it really did, though kings were not always at the head of the people.

88:2.text: The. Septuagint and Houbigant, "Thy mercies, Lord."
Truth. Notwithstanding our distress, I know thou wilt perform thy promises.

88:3.text: For thou. Hebrew, "I." Yet St. Jerome agrees with the Septuagint, (Berthier) though he is quoted by Calmet as conformable with Aquila, etc., Dixi.
Heaven and earth shall pass away sooner than God's word.
If we do not see how his promises are accompanied we must confess our ignorance, or throw the blame on the sins of the nation: but never call in question the divine mercy.
Truth. I will perform what I have promised to thee.
The apostles, represented by the heavens, have, by their preaching, established by the Church for ever.
In them, is not in the Septuagint, St. Augustine, etc.
Houbigant would remove Dixisti, "for thou," etc., to ver. 4.

88:4.text: Elect. Abraham, and the whole body of the people to whom the Messias had been promised. David was assured that he should spring from his family, ver. 52.

88:5.text: Generation. David's posterity occupied the throne for a long time, (Haydock) and subsisted till the coming of Christ; so that if any conqueror of that family had then appeared, the Jews would not have hesitated to admit, that this prediction was fulfilled. It is there misfortune to understand the text in this sense, whereas God spoke of the spiritual kingdom of his Son, which is to be perpetual. They can never answer the argument which the Fathers urged in the 4th century, and which has attained fresh strength from the longer duration of misery under which the royal family of David has been depressed. It is plain, that it has enjoyed no power from many ages, and as God's word is invariable, He could not have promised an everlasting earthly dominion.

The temporal kingdom of David decayed at the captivity, and is now wholly destroyed. But Christ was of this family, and established the Church, his spiritual kingdom, which shall continue unto the end.
His ministers exercise a power, which is founded on truth and justice. See 2 Kings 7:9.

88:6.text: Saints. These alone, (Haydock) the heavens or angels, worthily proclaim thy praises.
Preachers announce the same in the Church, (St. Augustine) "the communion of saints," as none are found out of her society.

88:7.text: Sons. Angels (Calmet) to God the Son. None is like him.
Lucifer fell by aiming at it. I will be like to the Most High, Isaias 14:14.

88:8.text: About. God eclipses every created beauty.
The angels themselves tremble before him.

88:9.text: Truth. He often praises this attribute, as if to excuse himself for asking, why God had debased the throne of David?
God cannot be divested of this perfection.

88:10.text: Power. Hebrew, "pride." Thou canst raise a storm, or restore a calm.

88:11.text: Proud one. Hebrew Rahab, Egypt or Pharao, Psalm 86:4., and Isaias 51:9. (Calmet) He alludes to the plagues inflicted on the Egyptians, etc.

88:13.text: Sea. Hebrew, "the right," (Calmet) which here denotes the south, (Psalm 106:3.; Menochius) as Hermon may do the east, (Du Hamel) with reference to Thabor, which lies to the west, though this seems unusual.
The north, etc., more probably refers to the limits of the promised land, from Libanus to the Indian or Mediterranean sea; and from Hermon, on the north-eastern part, to Thabor, on the west.
These two mountains were particularly fertile, and seemed to rejoice.

88:14.text: Might. Others can make no resistance with all their armies.

88:15.text: He extols the mercy, and still more the fidelity of God.
Whether he punished, or reward, all tends to promote his glory, and is perfectly just.

88:16.text: Jubilation. Hebrew, "how to sound the trumpet," which was the office of priests. They marched near the ark, as it were under the eyes of God.
Those who consider, and adore the ways of Providence, are blessed, (Worthington) and secure.

88:18.text: He speaks like a virtuous Levite, who acknowledges that all good came from the Lord.
He cannot speak of temporal blessings alone.

88:19.text: Israel. The Lord our king, (1 Kings 8:7.) will protect us, (Haydock) or He will defend our King David, and his posterity, as he then promised to him, ver. 5, 20. These verses may be thus connected, as the psalmist had been led to praise the wonderful works of God, and now returns to his promises.

88:20.text: Then, may relate to a distant time, when God chose Israel.
Saints. Hebrew, "merciful ones." Samuel, or Nathan, Septuagint, Arabic, etc., have "sons." The rest read "saints."
People. As Moses had written, Deuteronomy 17:15.
This regards David, as a figure of the Messias, (Luke 1:32., and Ezechiel 34:23.; Berthier) in whom it was more fully verified, 2 Kings v., and Acts 13:22.

88:21.text: Oil. Vatican Septuagint has, "mercy," and is followed by St. Jerome in Ezechiel 55:3.
But is a mistake, eleei being put for elaio.

88:23.text: Him. The Jews contributed to the glory of Christ, and the redemption of mankind. The enemies of David fell before his feet.

88:26.text: Rivers. Of his kingdom there shall be no end, Luke 1:33., and Zacharias 9:10.
Every nation shall adore him. David extended his conquests over all Arabia, and from the Pelusium to the Euphrates.
In this sense, the text may be applied to him, though it belongs more to Christ.

88:27.text: Father. We never find that David used this title; (Du Hamel) but Christ did frequently, insomuch that the Jews were convinced, that he claimed the divine nature: though, as man, he called God his support.

88:28.src: Calmet; Colossians 1:15., Romans 8:29., and Apocalypse 1:5.; Berthier
88:28.text: First-born. Or favourite, Exodus 4:22., and Jeremias 31:9. What king could be preferred to David for piety, riches, etc.? Yet he was only a feeble type of our Saviour, who surpasses all kings, as much as the reality does a shadow.
High. Hebrew helyon, which is one of the titles of God, and belongs to Christ, (Haydock) who is King of kings, and heir of all, Hebrews 1:

88:30.text: Heaven. This can only be verified in Christ, who rules over all, and gives power to his Church unto the end. The family of David has been confounded with the rest of the nation for nearly 2,000 years.

The temporal dominion of those princes has ceased in Jechonias, (Du Hamel) like that of other monarchs; so that God had in view a different throne, (Berthier; ver. 5.) and the Messias, who would render the kingdom of David perpetual, (Menochius) in a spiritual sense.

88:31.text: And if. God foresaw the prevarications of the Israelites and Christians: But he speaks this to shew their free-will, and that he would treat them as children, (Berthier) and not with the utmost severity, (Calmet) unless they proved obstinate, 2 Kings 7:14.
Some shall always continue faithful, and shall be glorified, while the bad shall be cast away.
Christ will never lose his Church.

88:34.text: From him. David. Many ancient psalters read, "from them."
God, by degrees, punished the Jews, by depriving them of their kings; though the family of David was preserved, and some share of power remained till Christ's coming.

88:36.text: Holiness. Or by myself , having nothing greater, Hebrews 6:13.
I will not. Literally, "if I lie," which is a Hebrew idiom, (Berthier) implying as much.
I have sworn irrecoverably, once for all.

88:38.text: Witness. Which may refer to the throne, or to the sun, (Calmet) or to the rainbow.
As long as the stars subsist, so long shall his throne be established.
The Church shines like the sun, and is easily known.
God, (Eusebius) or Christ, attests the promises.
The rainbow was assigned as a memorial, that the world should no more be drowned, Genesis ix. The other covenants made by God have no less stability.
Christian souls may shine in virtue, like the sun, or full moon, (Worthington) particularly (Haydock) after the resurrection, when they will be perfect, and not liable to change.

88:39.text: Rejected. In all kingdoms, there are some interruptions, and God did not fail in his promises. He still maintained the sceptre in Juda, though not with the same splendour at all times.

The Israelites ardently wished for the coming of the Messias. The psalmist here contrasts the present forlorn condition of the people with the preceding promises; and bewails the fate of Sedecias, who was slain at a distance from home. Distulisti. See Psalm 77:21., and lxii.
Thou hast been angry with thy Christ, (king Sedecias) and even with our Redeemer, in some sense, (Calmet) as He was treated thus, in consequence of the sins of mankind.

The promises were not fulfilled in David, that we may look farther. Solomon seemed to answer all his expectations. But he fell, and God had not him but Christ in view. The kingdom and sacrifices of the Jews are no more. Christ was not taken from them, but deferred. Some Jews and many Gentiles believed in him, ver. 47., etc.

Anointed. Thou hast despised us, and delayed the coming of the Messias. We cannot accuse the psalmist of impatience, as a late commentator has done, his words being dictated by the Holy Ghost. He expostulates with love and confidence, (Berthier) and comforts himself with the thought, that the coming of the Messias is only delayed.

88:40.text: Overthrown the covenant, etc. All this seems to relate to the time of the captivity of Babylon, in which, for the sins of the people and their princes, God seemed to have set aside for a while the covenant he had made with David.
Yet he did not in effect, ver. 39.
Sanctuary. The temple, (Theodoret) or according to the Hebrew, "the diadem," by which the king was "set apart" from the common people, and rendered sacred, Psalm 131:18.
The psalmist speaks in the person of the weak, who complained, (Worthington) that the kingdom was a prey to invaders, and the Church oppressed by infidels.

88:41.text: Fear. All his forts can afford no refuge. The country is like an abandoned vineyard. The Assyrians and Chaldeans have ruined it, and the neighbouring nations of Samaria and Edom take possession of it.
Thou leadest on their armies, and renderest our efforts useless.
This conduct of God proceeded from mercy. This severe chastisement awakened his people, who after the captivity were more tractable.

88:45.text: Cease. The priests are absent; and he cannot be purified in the temple.
Purification may here denote the royal ornaments.

88:46.text: Joachim was only seventeen years old when he came to the throne, which he occupied three months.

The four last kings of Juda reigned but a short while, and most of them came to an untimely end. Instead of chronou, the Roman Septuagint, St. Augustine, etc., read thronou, "of his throne," or reign.

The Vulgate seems more natural, as the throne of David had subsisted above 400 years, so that it was not overturned in "its youth," or commencement, though the number of ages, promised to it, seemed now to be abridged.

88:47.text: How long. Here the third part, or the prayer of the psalmist, begins.
Away. Another interrogation might then commence, "shall it be unto the end?"
In this prayer he foretells that God will regard our weakness, and preserve his Church.

88:48.text: Remember what. Hebrew ani, "I." As this seems odd, Houbigant substitutes, adni, "Lord."
Substance is. That Christ will assume our nature, (St. Augustine, City of God 17:9, 11.) or "how long I shall live."
Even the world "passes" like a shadow, 1 Corinthians vii.
"Be mindful of me from the depth: else why hast thou in vain created the sons of men?"
In vain. To spend their days in misery? or rather, "are not all created subject to vanity?" If thou do not succour us, we shall presently perish, and who will glorify thy name on earth?
Will thy providence take no care of us?
If the Messias come not, we cannot be saved, and we shall appear to have been created in vain.

88:50.text: David. He was a man according to thy own heart, and thy promises to him were absolute. Ethan speaks not of the other kings, or of the people, who might have justly irritated the Lord. He excuses their failings, by the consideration or their mortal and frail nature, ver. 49.

88:51.text: Nations. Who continually insult us, and blaspheme thy name.
This fills me with the most poignant grief.

Which, etc. Aquila and St. Jerome, "For I have carried in my bosom all the iniquities of peoples." If we should read kul, "voice," for col, "all," which seems useless before rabim, "many," (Haydock) we might translate, "I bore in my bosom the discourse of many peoples."

88:52.text: Change. Hebrew also, "the supplanting or retardment." Why does not your Messias come? How are your kings fallen! though God had promised them an eternal kingdom! Boast no more of his power or veracity. This impious language disturbs me.
They deride the ignominious life of the Messias.
Sedecias had "exchanged" the promised crown for irons, which was a cutting reproach.
Christ appeared to have left his people.
Infidels objected, that David's piety was ill requited by God, and that the anointed had made a bad exchange; antallagma, Matthew 16:26.

88:53.text: So be it. Some suppose, that these words were added by the collector of the psalms into five books.
The psalmist loses not hope, under adversity.
He begins and finishes with God's praises.
We beg that all may praise thee, O Lord.
This is the only reply which he makes to the sarcasms of infidels, being convinced of God's providence.

89:1.text: God. This characterizes the Jewish legislator [Moses].

David composed it in his [Moses'] name, (Menochius) or it bears some analogy with his writings. St. Jerome maintains, that he [Moses] was the author of the nine following psalms, (Calmet) which have no title in Hebrew.
But St. Augustine thinks they would then have formed a part of the pentateuch.
The life of man was longer in the days of Moses than seventy or eighty years.
Moses cannot be the author of the 94th and 95th psalms.

In Psalm 98:6., Samuel is mentioned, and it is not necessary to have recourse to the prophetic spirit. One of the descendants of Moses, during the captivity, may have been the author, (Calmet) or David may have predicted that event.

89:2.text: Here Origen improperly concluded the sentence.
God, is not in Septuagint, Syriac, or ancient Latin psalters.
Al signifies both God, and not, and seems to be twice explained in the Vulgate, as Hebrew omits not, ver. 3.
The sentence would be very striking, if God were left out, as it is done by Houbigant. The eternity of matter is refuted by this text.
God's eternity is contrasted with man's short life.
New gods must of course be false.
Plato asserted, that "the creator of all must be unbegotten and eternal."

89:3.text: Turn not man away, etc. Suffer him not quite to perish from thee, since thou art pleased to call upon him to be converted to thee.
God wills not the death of the sinner.
Give him grace not to yield to his base passions.
Hebrew, "thou humblest man," (Haydock) that he may enter into himself.

89:4.text: For, et. This thought naturally tends to convert the sinner.
Watch. Consisting of three hours, (Berthier) which were of unequal length, according to the seasons.
A thousand years seem not so long to God.
What then must be the short life of man? See Job 7:8.
None ever lived one thousand years. Yet what would that be, compared with eternity?

89:5.text: Their years be. Hebrew, "thou strikest, (Calmet) or inundatest them: they are a dream." But our version is accurate.

89:6.text: Fall. Hebrew, "it is cut down and dries." The heat of the climate caused the flowers to decay very soon, Isaias 40:6. Man's youth touches on old age.
The present moment is all we can call our own.
"A young man may die soon: an old man cannot live long," says an English proverb.

89:8.text: Life. Literally, "age." Thou clearly discernest all our proceedings.
Man is condemned for Adam's sin. The Israelites who were fit for arms, and gave way to murmuring, were sentenced to die in the desert. He may allude to this event.
Hebrew may signify also, "our hidden things," or "youth," (Calmet) or "mistakes," (Houbigant) or "negligences."
From these we should always pray to be delivered, Psalm 24:7.
Sin occasions the shortness of life, (Worthington) as man was created to be immortal.
Before the deluge, men lived indeed longer.

89:9.text: As a spider. As frail and weak as a spider's web; and miserable withal, whilst, like a spider, we spend our bowels in weaving webs to catch flies.
Meditabuntur is here used in a passive sense.
Hebrew, "we have spent our years like one speaking a word."
When he has done, the sound is no more: so their memory has perished with a sound, Psalm 9:8.

Hebrew, Chaldean, etc., do not mention the spider: Yet it seems to have been originally in the text, as it is recognized by the Syriac, Septuagint, and the other Greek interpreters, and makes the sense more complete.
The transcribers might more easily omit than the Septuagint, etc., could insert this word.

89:10.text: In them. Years, (Calmet) "in the world." Chaldean, "altogether." Symmachus, years. This was the usual term of man's life in David's time, (Haydock) and about the captivity, when this was written. Many lived above one hundred years when Moses wrote.

Yet this proves nothing, as there are still instances of equal longevity, though it is true, that people in general seldom live above seventy, or eighty, or if they do, their days are a burden to them. The same might be the case under Moses. He probably here alludes to those warriors, who were cut off in the wilderness, few of whom would survive 80.
The author of Ecclesiasticus, (xviii. 8.) gives one hundred, for the utmost limits of life. The pagan sages speak in the same style as the psalmist.
Strong. Septuagint, "in dominion." But here it means in a vigorous constitution.

Princes lived no longer than others. Hebrew and Vulgate may be "the prime, or most of them," as even a great part of the time before seventy, as well as after, (Haydock) is usually spent in misery, Genesis 47:9.

Mildness. God's mildness corrects us: in as much as he deals kindly with us, in shortening the days of this miserable life; and so weaning our affections from all its transitory enjoyments, and teaching us true wisdom.
Hebrew, "we pass quickly and fly away, (St. Jerome) like birds of passage, (Calmet) or "it is cut down soon," (Montanus) "in silence," (Drusius) tacitisque senescimus annis.
St. Jerome wonders, that the Septuagint should have translated as they have done: But they are followed by Theodotion, and the Sext. edition, who may have had different copies, equally good.
But this is not the sense of the Septuagint.
It is a mercy of God to shorten men's lives, (Menochius) as many would sin more, if they had a probability of continuing upon earth.

89:11.text: Anger? God must punish actual sin severely, since he thus condemns mankind on account of original sin.

89:12.text: Thy right hand. Your Messias, to liberate and instruct us.
"Make us know how to number our days thus, and we shall come to thee with a wise heart."
Septuagint have read iminoc for yamenu, "our days."
Make us truly wise, (Sa) or acquainted with teachers of truth.

89:14.text: But the psalmist feels interiorly, that his petition has been granted.
The prospect of a glorious immortality fills us with hope in proportion to our sufferings for the truth.

89:17.text: Yea, the work. Each in particular. Roman Septuagint, etc., omit this sentence, which Houbigant improperly deems useless.

90:1.text: David. Septuagint add, "it has no title in Hebrew," and hence the Jews refer it to the preceding author, with St. Jerome, etc. But this rule is very uncertain.

Some suppose that Moses composed it when he led the Israelites out of Egypt, or in the wilderness; while others think that it is the work of David under some imminent danger. The Fathers apply it to Jesus Christ. Yet it may be considered simply as a moral instruction, (Calmet) superior in elegance to any Greek or Latin poem.
Aid. Hebrew, "secret place." Of heaven. Is not in Hebrew shaddai, which means, (Haydock) "the almighty self-sufficient, or destroying God."
We must keep close to God by mental prayer, if we would enjoy the divine protection.

90:3.text: Delivered me. Hebrew and Septuagint, "shall deliver thee." Yet the Alexandrian copy has me.
The psalmist addresses his own soul.
Word, verbo: we sometimes find "sword," printed by mistake. Hebrew dabar, signifies "word, thing, pestilence, etc."
The devil employs human respect to draw many into his nets.
Neither subtle craft, nor the cruelty of tyrants will disturb those who trust in Providence.

90:4.text: With. Septuagint, "upon." St. Augustine, "between," as the Lord carried Israel, Deuteronomy 32:11.
Hebrew, "he will cover thee with his feathers," (Haydock) like an eagle.

90:5.text: Shield. God's fidelity, or word, affords the best protection, Proverbs 30:5.

Having the spirit of faith, a man is secure. But he whose heart is hardened, (Berthier) is covered with the buckler of God's affliction, (Lamentations 3:64.; Haydock) abuses every thing, and seems bewitched with self-love, Galatians 1:
Night. Devils, spectres, etc., (Canticle of Canticles 3:7.; Calmet) and treacherous insinuations, that people are not bound to confess the truth, in time of danger.

90:6.text: Day. Neither open attacks, nor unforeseen accidents prevail.
The Hebrews suppose, that one angel presides over death in the daytime, and another during the night; or that various demons send maladies at these different times.
Invasion. Septuagint and old Italic, have, "ruin."

90:6.src: Theocrit. Idyl. i.; Lucan iii.; Calmet
90:6.text: St. Jerome, after Aquila, "from the bite of him who rageth, damonizontos, at noon. Keteb, (Haydock) according to an ancient tradition of the Jews, denotes one of the bolder devils, who attacks in open day, and seeks no aid from nocturnal craft. (Genebrard) The psalmist may allude to those popular notions, (Theodoret; St. Jerome) which were prevalent among the pagans.
Thou shalt fear no danger of the day or night, (Bellarmine) nor any which disturbs the life of man.
This author mistakes, when he supposes that Keteb is rendered devil.
He might also ask how the Chaldean, Aquila, and Symmachus came to discover, that the devil is here mentioned, as well as the Septuagint?
These seem to have read ussod, "and the devil," instead of issud, "from destruction which ravages," (Amama) vastabit.
But allowing that the Septuagint, etc., are accurate, what is meant by this devil? St. Peter seems to explain the idea, when he exhorts us to sobriety, 1 Peter 5:8.

Violent temptations of sloth, (St. Athanasius) or impurity, (Theodoret) or the persecutions against the faithful, may be meant. Four different sorts of attacks seem to be designated. 1. Such as assult the ignorant with the fears of the night, tempting them to secure their temporal estates, while they think not of eternal woe impending. 2. Others are attacked with the arrow in the day, and threatened with death, which they know they ought rather to endure, than abandon their faith. 3. The business, etc., imitates some grievous but latent temptation, as when the faithful are persuaded to take some unlawful oath. 4. But the greatest and most manifest attack, is styled, invasion, etc., when persecutors assail those who adhere to the true faith with a succession of torments, and subtle arguments, which have been the occasion of the fall of many, who had resisted the former attacks. Yet none of these yield, but by their own fault, trusting in themselves, and not in God.
Noon day. Grotius explains this of the heat of the sun, which is very dangerous to travellers in Palestine.

90:7.text: Fall. Or "attack,....but shall not come nigh to thee."
How great soever may be the number of thy adversaries, they shall not be able to do thee any harm. They shall fall at thy feet, and their dart shall not reach thee.
More forsake God in prosperity, than under adversity.

90:9.text: High. Hebrew helyon is a title of God, (Calmet) not the adjective to refuge, (Berthier) as Chaldean, Aquila, etc., have taken it. "Thou hast placed thy dwelling most high." So that there, etc., ver. 10. It is evident that the following promises relate not to the Lord, (Calmet) but to the just man. Protestants, "because thou hast made the Lord, which is my refuge, even the most high thy habitation." This transposition is not authorized by the text.

90:10.text: Scourge. Aquila has Aphe, "the leprosy," (Calmet) or any stroke of distress.
What the saints have suffered were not real evils, and they will be amply rewarded in heaven. They never complain, having God with them, (Calmet; ver. 15.; Haydock) and his holy angels.

90:11.src: Porphyrius, Ap. ii.; St. Clement of Alexandria, Strom. v.; Calmet
90:11.text: Angels. Many seem to be assigned to the just, to whom St. Hilary, etc., would restrain this privilege. But it is more generally believed, that each person has an angel guardian. This was the opinion even of the pagans.

To keep. Instead of this, the tempter substituted and, (Matthew 4:6.) finding it would not answer his purpose, (Haydock) and shewed that the question was about walking, and not about precipitating oneself.
To attempt such unusual courses, is the way of Lucifer, (Worthington) and tempting God, as our Saviour replied.
From the father of lies, heretics have learnt how to curtail and misapply the holy Scriptures.

God has highly favoured man, by intrusting him to the care of these sublime ministers of his court, (St. Bernard) and surely it is lawful for us to implore their assistance, as we may apply to our fellow-creatures for redress in our temporal necessities. To refuse to do so, on the plea that we expect all immediately from God, would be going contrary to his appointment. Else why has God given them for our guardians, since He could have done all without them? In vain is it objected, that this invocation is a religious worship. It may be so styled, because they are blessed, and help us to obtain salvation. But we only honour in them the gifts of God.
They protect us by his ordinance, (Worthington) and the very form of praying, shews in what light we regard them. Who durst say to God, pray for us?

90:12.text: All these expressions are metaphorical, to shew the assistance given by angels, to remove the obstacles which impede our progress towards heaven.

90:13.text: Asp. Which kills in eight hours time at farthest, making the blood congeal.--- Basilisk. "The little king" of serpents. What is related of it seems fabulous.
Yet there might be some species known by this name, possessing fascinating qualities like the rattle-snake.
The sight of it alone could not destroy a man; otherwise how could any account of it have been given? Hebrew ssel means a lion in Job, (Berthier) and phethen, "an asp," (Calmet) or basilisk.

The most noxious animals, both of sea and land, shall prove quite harmless to the true servants of God, when he intends to prove the truth of his religion, as he did in the cases of Daniel, and of the disciples of Christ, Mark xvi. If they be suffered to kill the saints here, it is in order that they may be glorified in heaven, ver. 15.
The devil is styled an asp, etc.

He sometimes attacks the Church, by craft, and at other times by open violence. But she [the Church] remains secure, (St. Augustine) and her children can only be preserved by continuing in her bosom. To know which is the true Church; "see, says St. Gregory, (Mor. 20:29.) which are the most recent sects."
Methodists may now wrest this honour from the rest of Protestants.

90:15.text: Tribulation. The just are not exempt from it.

90:16.text: Days. Eternity alone can satisfy the heart.
Salvation. Or Jesus, who promised to manifest himself, John 14:21, 25.
Abraham saw him afar off; Simeon at hand, John 8:56., and Luke 2:30. God insures the just a long life in this world, and an eternal one in the next.
That life is long enough which ends in happiness.

91:1.text: Day. The Jews say, that Adam sung this at his creation, (Chaldean) or that it refers to the reign of the Messias, which shall last one thousand years after this world is ended.
Others think it is a thanksgiving after the defeat of Sennacherib, (Ven. Bede) or Absalom.

It might be sung by the sons of Moses, who expected to be shortly delivered from Babylon, (Calmet) or by the people on the sabbath, (Berthier) though many of the Fathers think, that this word denotes "the repose of the life to come." The occasion or author of this psalm cannot be clearly ascertained.
When we enjoy rest from labour, we ought particularly to praise God's works.

91:2.text: Praise. Literally, "to confess," (Haydock) as we must be free from sin before we can worthily proclaim God's praises.
But here to confess means to praise, (Calmet) or give thanks.

91:3.text: Night. Of adversity, and at all times, (Berthier) as well as in prosperity.
Morning and evening prayer must not be neglected.
These times were particularly pointed out, Psalm 54:18.

91:4.text: Strings, upon. Hebrew, "on the hasor, and on the nabel, on the higaion with the cinnor." Yet the ten stringed instrument seems to have been the same with the psaltery, or nobol.
Bellarmine thinks and is redundant, and was not in the copies of the Septuagint, or it is only explanatory, as we know that the psaltery had ten strings, Psalm 32:2., and 144:9.
The matter is of small consequence.
Eusebius seems to insinuate, that instruments were not used in the Church of his time.
The observance of the commandments, and mortification, signified by the harp, are requisite.

91:5.text: Rejoice. Admiring thy providence, (Calmet) or the Messias. "What are all things compared with thee, O Lord!"

91:6.text: Deep. We cannot easily explain thy ways, (Menochius) in exalting some, and depressing thy people.

91:7.text: Things. Pretended sages hence take occasion to blaspheme whatsoever things they know not, Jude 10. The wise adore God in silence, (Haydock) and confess, that the misery of the just here proves a future life, while the wicked prosper, to be more tormented.
Carnal men, who think only of present things, see not this.

91:8.text: Appear. Hebrew, "flourish." Still they are but as grass, (Haydock) short-lived, and of small utility. The just resembles the palm-tree, ver. 13.

91:10.text: Enemies. The Babylonians, (Calmet) or all the wicked at the last day.
This shews God's power, and insures the exaltation of the just.

91:11.text: Mercy. Hebrew and some copies of the Septuagint have "oil," an emblem of mercy, Proverbs 21:20.
"I shall be anointed with fresh oil."
"My old age shall be like a verdant olive."
Those who have a good conscience, expect final happiness.

91:12.text: Me. I shall live to hear of the vengeance which God will take, Psalm 111:9.
The just pray for sinners here; but must approve of God's judgment.

91:13.text: Palm-tree. Septuagint phoinix, means also a "Phoenician, or the Phoenix" bird, of which the ancients have said so much, Job 29:18., (Calmet) and of which Tertullian, (de Res. xiii.) and St. Ambrose, (de fid. Res.) seem to understand this passage.

But it must be explained in the sense of the Vulgate, as the Hebrew Tamar evinceth. This tree, and the cedar, were the most famous in those countries; the former for its fruit, and the latter for buildings and duration. The palm-tree will shoot forth again, after it has been cut down or burnt, (Pliny, [Natural History?] 13:14.) so the just will rise up from oppression.

91:14.text: Courts. In the Church triumphant, as well as in the militant.
The piety of the faithful induces strangers to embrace the truth.

91:15.text: Bene patientes, eupathountes, "flourishing," (Grotius) tranquil, (St. Augustine) or in a prosperous condition.
Erasmus, to shew the utility of consulting the originals, informs us, what a multiplicity of authors he consulted in vain, to know the import of this word.
"They shall be fat and covered with leaves," (St. Jerome) alluding to the aforesaid comparison.

91:16.text: In him. The general judgment will set this in the clearest light. At present, the ways of Providence may be mysterious, ver. 6.

92:1.text: Founded. Septuagint, "inhabited." This title occurs not in Hebrew (Theodoret) nor are the copies of the Septuagint uniform, which shews that it is a later insertion, intimating perhaps, that it was sung on Friday, when the works of creation were completed, (Berthier) and the world redeemed.

It seems to refer to the translation of the ark, (Muis; 1 Paralipomenon 16:30.) and the establishment of the Church, the house of God, though the universe may be so styled, (Berthier) or it speaks of the return from captivity, (Ven. Bede) as well as the preceding and following psalms.
Himself. As if to perform some great work, the liberation of his people from the captivity of Babylon, and of the devil, (Calmet) or to create the world.
Moved, or disturbed in the order established by him.
This does not prove that the earth moves not on its own axis daily, and round the sun every year.
The Church shall not fail, (Menochius) which Christ has redeemed by his own blood, and with great power. In it He reigns, and will draw all to himself, John 12:31.

92:2.text: Of old. Literally, "that time," ex tunc, (Worthington) alluding to some distant period, Isaias 16:13.
God's throne is established over creatures, as soon as they exist.
Yet he is eternal, (Menochius) as Christ is likewise. At his ascension, He placed his sacred humanity on the throne.
His kingdom, the Church, continues for ever.

92:3.text: Floods. The apostles, (Eusebius; St. Augustine) or persecutors.
Waves. This sentence is not in the Roman Septuagint, etc.
But it is in the Alexandrian and Aldine editions. Storms and tides fill all with awe and astonishment.
The motion of waters, when they were first confined to their channels, and the persecutions of the Church, and rebellions against God, are described.

92:4.text: On high. In heaven, more to be admired than all the phenomena of nature, and more powerful than all the vast armies of the Babylonians, Isaias 40:15.
The conversion of many nations, (Apocalypse 17:5.) is the work and glory of God, (Haydock) and his preserving the Church in the midst of the most violent attacks, shews his power.

92:5.text: A person must shut his eyes not to see the prophecies fulfilled concerning Christ, and his Church.

The majesty of God should strike us with a holy fear, and teach us to observe his commandments, and to behave with the utmost respect in his house, (Calmet) which is the whole world, or the society of the faithful.
Articles of faith are perfectly credible to those whose hearts are moved by grace, (Worthington) though they cannot be understood.
All the members of the Church should carefully approve themselves to God, who is her spouse, (Worthington) and protector, (Menochius) to the end of the world.
Days. Since thy power and fidelity are so great, suffer not thy temple to be any longer in ruins, or profaned.

93:1.text: Himself. This intimates, that he was inspired to write.
Week. Wednesday, on which day Judas sold our Saviour, and his punishment is here foretold.
"The title is not in Hebrew" and has been added since the times of the Septuagint.
It refers to the persecutions of David, (Jansenius) or to the captives, (Calmet) or it contains an important instruction on Providence, and on the judgment which Christ will pronounce.
Freely. Hebrew, "Lord God of vengeance, God of vengeance, shine forth."
This agrees better with the sequel. To appear or act freely have the same meaning.
God executes judgment publicly, and without restraint.
To Him revenge belongs, Deuteronomy 32:35., Romans 12:20., and Nahum 2:
It is time to punish the haughty Babylon.
God more usually take the title of merciful. But he is equally just, and will respect no dignity or power, but the merits of each one.

93:6.text: Fatherless. Septuagint places this after widow, and have here the stranger, or "proselyte," (Haydock) including those who were circumcised, or had only renounced idolatry.
Jeremias and Ezechiel describe the cruelty of the Chaldeans.
Similar acts of impious rage are but too visible in all ages.

93:7.text: Of Jacob. A wretched people in captivity. This is spoken insultingly, he knows not, or cannot hinder, their distress, Psalm 13:1., and 72:6.
The insolence and cruelty of infidels are reprobated.

93:8.text: Fools. Who talk in this manner, (Haydock) whether you really believe, that God is thus ignorant and inactive; or you only act as if you did.
There are but few of the former description. But very many, even among Christians, act as if they admitted no Providence.

93:9.text: Consider? He does not say, "hath he not eyes?" lest any might attribute members to God. (St. Jerome) "In evil works, mankind thou mayst deceive, Thy hidden thoughts the gods above perceive." (Theognis.) Ou leseis de Theous oude logizomenos.
Thales being asked, if the actions of men were unknown to the gods, replied, "not even their thoughts."
It seems those whom the psalmist attacks, denied the interference of God in human affairs, though they allowed that he created all.
Hence he justly stigmatizes them as fools, and inconsistent.
It is impossible that God should be ignorant of our actions, since he knows our most secret thoughts.

93:13.text: Rest from the evil days. That thou mayst mitigate the sorrows to which he is exposed, during the short and evil days of his mortality; (Challoner) or mayst protect him, while the wicked are overwhelmed.
The pious bear afflictions with greater resignation, (Menochius) meditating on the law and the holy Scriptures, where the ways of Providence are justified.

93:15.text: Until justice be turned into judgment, etc. By being put in execution: which will be agreeable to all the upright in heart.
They will be round the tribunal, and sit as judges with Christ. Hebrew ci had, "For to," may have been originally ad ci, "Until."

This does not imply that the just will not be protected afterwards. Cyrus was a figure of the Messias, and all his counsellors, or the Jews, attached themselves to him, when he punished the Babylonians, Isaias 41:2., and 53:11.
The Church will never be rejected by a just God, whose judgments must take place, and be pleasing to the righteous.

93:17.text: Almost. Or shortly, as the Hebrew means, (Berthier) "within very little."
Hell. Hebrew, "silence," which is often put for the grave, 1 Kings 2:9.

93:18.text: Me. I was no sooner in danger, than I was relieved.
Feeding and complaining of my misery, I obtained thy aid.

93:19.text: Soul. Our future joy will bear proportion with the sufferings which we endure for the sake of justice, 1 Corinthians 11:13., and 2 Corinthians 1:3.

93:20.text: Doth the seat of iniquity stick to thee? etc. That is, wilt thou, O God, who art always just, admit of the seat of iniquity; that is, of injustice, or unjust judges, to have any partnership with thee? Thou, who framest, or makest labour in commandment; that is, thou who obligest us to labour with all diligence to keep thy commandments; (Challoner) as faith alone is not sufficient, (Worthington) and who art pleased that we should find therein some difficulty or repugnance of nature, that we may be the more entitled to consolation, ver. 19.
Commandment, or "instruction." St. Augustine observes, that the sufferings of this life are for our instruction, to admonish us of future punishments and rewards. This idea is very luminous.
Christ was first to suffer, and so to enter into his glory, Luke 24:26.
Yet the yoke of Christ is really sweet. The devil tempts us to look upon it as insupportable.

Septuagint, St. Augustine, etc., Fingit, which may refer to the seat, or to unjust judges, who tyrannize over others, as the Babylonians did, (Calmet) and as the Jews conspired against the soul of the just. "Should the tribunal of perversity have any commerce with thee, which, under the name of law, forms impious machinations?"

93:22.text: Sufferings force us to have recourse to God, whom few remember in the days of prosperity.

93:23.text: Will destroy them. Hebrew yatsmithem, occurs twice, to denote the certainty of the event, though the Septuagint, etc., omit the repetition.
Cyrus overturned the cruel empire of Babylon.

The wicked at the last day shall seek to hide themselves, and will for ever be removed from the sight of the just, aphaniei autous. We may say of each of them, it had been better if he had never been born, Matthew 26:24.
The retribution of the good and bad is eternal.

94:1.text: Himself, as David wrote it by inspiration.

Complutensian Septuagint, "It is without a title in Hebrew." St. Paul (Hebrews 4:7.) quotes it as the work of David. But this is only done incidentally, and it may have been written by the descendants of Moses, (Psalm lxxxix.; Calmet) as the apostle only says, in David, (Haydock) referring to the psalter, which the common opinion attributed to him.

This opinion, it must be owned, acquires hereby great authority, (Haydock) as an inspired writer could not mistake; and Calmet himself, on the epistle to the Hebrews, doubts not but as the drift of the apostle requires, he attributed this psalm to David.

It might be used in the removal of the ark (Muis) and contains an exhortation to the Jews to return to the service of God, under king Josias, (Theodoret) or after the captivity, (Calmet) or at the preaching of the gospel.

The Church adopts the version of the Roman psalter in her office books, as they were corrected by St. Pius V, and this psalm was considered as a hymn at the beginning of matins, though the Vulgate is retained in other parts.
But this shews that the Church does not condemn every deviation from the Vulgate.
Saviour. St. Jerome, "to the rock, our Jesus."
He who created us, has also been our Saviour.

94:2.text: Come. Earlier than usual, (Menochius) before the day be far spent; praeoccupemus, to shew our diligence in prayer, (Haydock) and to obtain God's favour, as Aman was first at the palace of Assuerus, Esther 6:4.
This sentence has probably determined the Church to place it at the beginning of matins.
Let not others get before us in performing this duty.
We cannot prevent God's grace by an good works, since without it we can do nothing (Worthington) as we ought.
The same word, exomologesei, is used for sacramental confession; (Berthier) and this, or at least contrition, (Haydock) ought to go before our expressions of praise, Ecclesiasticus 15:9.
The prophet exhorts us both to lament and to praise.

94:3.text: Gods. Complutensian Septuagint, "the earth." But the best editions agree with us, and God must be acknowledged superior to all angels, etc. Some copies of the Septuagint, St. Augustine, etc., add, "for the Lord will not cast off his people," (Calmet) which seems to be taken from Psalm 93:14.

94:4.text: Are his. This is grandeur, that "he beholds," as in the Roman psalter.
Virgil (Geor. 4.) says: Deum, namque ire per omnes Terrasque et tractusque maris, coelumque profundum.

94:5.text: Formed. Like a potter, plasmaverunt, (St. Jerome; Calmet) or "have laid the foundations of the dry land."

94:6.text: And weep. Hebrew also, "bend the knee;" though this sense would seem less proper, after he had mentioned prostration. Tears of contrition and tenderness may accompany our canticles.
Kneeling in prayer is a posture pleasing to God, Philippians 2:

94:7.text: The Lord is not in the Hebrew or Septuagint.
The people. Roman Psalter and Syriac, "his people, and the sheep of his pasture, taken from Psalm 99:3.
God is the only shepherd, who creates his sheep.
He feedeth us, and it is most just that we should adore him.

94:8.text: To-day. St. Paul beautifully illustrates this passage, Hebrews 4:
He follows not the present Hebrew punctuation, which would join half this verse with the preceding.
His. God speaks of the Messias according to the apostle, who intimates that to-day comprises all the life of man, Hebrew 37:13.
Harden not. Man is the author of his own obduracy, (Theodoret) which God only permits.
We have free will, and may resist God's grace, as we may also consent to it, and thus co-operate to our first justification.
the captives (Calmet) and first Christians were exhorted not to imitate the depravity of the ancient Jews.
Though a man may have frequently resisted the Holy Ghost, he may still repent.

94:9.text: Provocation, (irritatione.) Roman Breviary exacerbatione. Hebrew meriba, "contradiction," (St. Jerome; Haydock) at Raphidim; (Exodus 17:7.) unless this be styled temptation, (Massa) and the former provocation was that at Cades, Numbers 20:13.

The Israelites murmured frequently. But that rebellion which took place at the return of the spies, and which causes God to swear that the guilty should never enter the land of promise, seems to be chiefly meant, Numbers xiv.

They murmured on account of the desire of water and flesh-meat, though they were abundantly supplied with manna, which answered every purpose. Thus some require to communicate under both kinds, as if one did not contain as much as both.
Proved me; to know by experience if I were so powerful as to work miracles; and I condescended to gratify them, (Calmet) or I have done it already.

94:10.text: Offended. Hebrew and Septuagint, "disgusted." Roman Psalter, St. Augustine, etc., "I was very near to;" (Calmet) ready to punish, and eye-witness of their infidelity. St. Paul reads prosochthisa, infensus fui, "I was against, or disgusted with," and seems to refer the forty years to the Jews, who saw God's works.

But there is a variation in the Greek copies, as some omit, For which cause; and Hebrews 3:10., and 5:17, intimates, that the indignation of God was roused for forty years, at intervals, as often as the people rebelled.

The apostle also plainly shews, that this psalm was written long after that period, and consequently not by Moses, as the Jews would now assert. He limiteth a certain day, saying in David: To-day, after so long a time, etc., Hebrews 4:7.
Always. Hebrew, "a people of those who err in the heart are they."

94:11.text: So. Roman Psalter and Milan, "to whom." Both occur in St. Paul, and answer the Hebrew asher, (Berthier) quibus.

Not. Literally, "if they shall." The Israelites were excluded from a settled abode in Chanaan, on account of their repeated transgressions, particularly at Cades; (Numbers xiv.) and Christians, who do not continue faithful to the law of Jesus Christ, can never expect to enter heaven, Hebrews iii., and 4:
David made the like observation to his subjects; and clearly speaks of the heavenly repose to which the virtuous alone are entitled.
St. Paul takes great pains to inculcate this truth, and shews that the return from captivity could not answer the import of the promises made by David in God's name.

95:1.text: When the house was built, etc. Alluding to that time, and then ordered to be sung; but principally relating to the building of the Church of Christ, after our redemption from the captivity of satan.

Captivity. The greater canticle of David, (1 Paralipomenon xvi.) was probably divided, on that occasion, (Calmet) into three. This forms the second part, from ver. 23; as the psalm civ., to ver. 16., does the first. The three last verses of David's canticle, (ver. 34.) occur [in] Psalm 105:1, 47. But in reality, there are so many variations, that it seems most probable, (Haydock) that he revised that work, and left us the three psalms in their present form. This perhaps relates to the establishment of the Church, though it might be also sung at the return from Babylon.
Modern Jews understand it of their future re-union under the Messias.
The rebuilding of the temple is foretold, as a figure of man's redemption.
New canticle. As the blessed do, (Apocalypse 5:9., and 15:4.) and those who receive the Messias, Isaias 42:10.
Earth. And not Judea alone, ver. 7.

95:2.text: Lord....his name. As ver. 7., and 8. The plural and singular denote the Trinity.
"Tell the glad tidings" of salvation incessantly. This preaching shall continue for ever.

95:5.text: Devils. Hebrew elilim, "diminutive gods, (Haydock) nothings, (Calmet) vain things."

We have idols, as Protestants read here. These were in fact, either devils, or vain imaginations of men. St. Paul says, we know that an idol is nothing in the world, 1 Corinthians 8:4. They cannot claim self-existence, and if the true God were not to support those creatures, the sun, etc., which have been the objects of adoration, they would presently cease to be.
This most plausible species of idolatry is therefore refuted, since the Lord made the heavens.
The Creator alone can be considered as God; the devils prompt the people to adore other things.

It would appear but a small praise for the Lord to be feared above all gods, (Haydock) if they were "nothing." Hence the Septuagint have used the word devils, to signify, that these potent, but maleficent beings, which the pagans adored, were infinitely beneath God, and worse than nothing.

95:6.text: Before him. At his disposal; whereas the idols can bestow nothing.
Sanctuary, or "sanctification." (Worthington) 1 Paralipomenon, in his place.

95:7.text: Kindreds; patriae, or families, as it is expressed, 1 Paralipomenon xvi.

95:8.text: Sacrifices. Hebrew Mincha, "the oblation" of flour, etc., (Haydock) which denotes the blessed Eucharist, and the spiritual sacrifices of prayer, etc.
Victims shall cease, but the pure oblation shall continue among the Gentiles, Malachias 1:11.
Courts. This shews that their conversion is predicted, since they could not otherwise come thither.

95:9.text: Moved. Hebrew, "in labour," (Isaias 26:17.; Theodoret; Calmet) or "fear ye before him all the earth."
In Paralipomenon, the sentences are in a different order.

95:10.text: Reigned. St. Bernard says, "the kingdom of Jesus is in the wood."

St. Justin Martyr (Dialogue with Trypho) accuses the Jews of retrenching apo tou xulou, "from the wood," which all the Latin Fathers, except St. Jerome, acknowledge in their copies. That ancient author, being born among the Samaritans, could hardly be so ignorant of the Hebrew text, and his antagonist does not attempt to refute the charge; so that it seems probable, that they were in the original, (Berthier) and since erased by the Jews, from the Septuagint, who added them, (Worthington) by the spirit of prophecy.
But how came Christians to permit this to be done in their Hebrew, Greek, and Latin copies? The words in question may have been, therefore, a marginal gloss, which had crept into the text.
They do not occur in the parallel passage, (1 Paralipomenon) nor in the Vulgate, though they be retained in the Roman breviary.

Lindan objects this perfidy of the Jews to the Reformers, not reflecting, that he thus condemns the Vulgate. Genebrard is of opinion, that "the Septuagint were inspired to add these words, which some half-learned critics have thought proper to expunge with an impiety which is now but too common." The Popes have not, however, thought that the cross stood in need of this support.

The Chaldean and Syriac, as well as all the copies of the Septuagint extant, and the Arabic and Ethiopic versions taken from it, and all the Greek interpreters and Fathers, (except St. Justin) with St. Jerome, both in his versions from the Hebrew and Septuagint, omit these words, which are found in the Roman, Gothic, and other psalters. Origen's Hexapla seem to have most enabled the Greeks to discern the interpolation, which the Latins retained longer, not having such easy access to that work. Whatever may be the decision on this important matter, it is certain that the reign of Christ was propagated from the wood, in a wonderful manner, as he there began to draw all to himself, and the prophet seems evidently to allude to the times when Christ proclaimed, the kingdom of God is at hand, and when the conversion of the Gentiles, and the institution of the blessed Eucharist (ver. 8.) would fill all the world with rapture.

The positive testimony of St. Justin, and the Italic version used by the Latin Fathers, (Berthier) Tertullian, St. Augustine, etc., (Worthington) seems of more weight to prove the authenticity of the words, than the simple omission in the copies of Origen, and St. Jerome, etc., to evince the contrary.
Corrected. Evil morals and idolatry, (Menochius) rather than the physical order of the globe, Psalm 92:1.
The Christian faith shall not be abolished, (Menochius) or corrected.
Justice. Ancient psalters add, "and the Gentiles in his wrath," ver. 13., and Psalm 98:8.

95:11.text: Fulness. Its raging billows, (Calmet) fishes, (Menochius) those who live upon the water.
Let al testify their joy. Every thing is animated by the psalmist.

95:13.text: Judge. Or "rule," as he invites all to rejoice.
But this will be done by all nature, when God shall punish the wicked.
He now judges by his ministers, and will pass sentence at the last day.
This verse is added, instead of the last three in 1 Paralipomenon xvi., which occur in Psalm cv.

96:1.text: Same. Huic. The title is the same as usual in the Septuagint.

It occurs not in Hebrew. The psalm may refer to David's establishment on the throne, after the death of Saul, or Absalom, or to the return from captivity, and to the first and second coming of Christ.
This last seems to be the most literal sense.
To him. Christ's body on the third day, and many souls were restored to life. [Matthew 27:52.]
Islands. We have great reason to rejoice in being educated in the true faith, and we may hope that the Catholic religion will once more flourish in these isles.
The Son of man shall have dominion over all, Daniel 7:14. His Church is persecuted, as the waves beat against an island.

96:2.text: Clouds. We could not bear the blaze of the divine majesty, Psalm 17:12. Christ veiled himself in our human nature, (Theodoret) in the womb of the blessed Virgin Mary.
God gave the law with terror; and so he will come to judge with integrity.

96:3.text: A fire. Preceding the last judgment, 2 Peter 3:12., Daniel 7:10., and Wisdom 5:22.

96:5.text: All. Which is conformable to the Hebrew, etc., though the Vulgate terra, means, "all the earth" melted.

96:6.text: Heavens. Apostles, Psalm xviii. The judge appears publicly.

96:7.text: Angels. Hebrew Elohim, means also, all who have power. Chaldean, "idols." St. Paul, (Hebrews 1:6.) quotes this in the third person; Let all the angels, (Calmet) or rather he alludes to the Septuagint, (Deuteronomy 32:43.) which clearly speaks of the Messias, and may therefore have been altered in the Hebrew text, which St. Jerome translated. The psalmist expresses the same idea as Moses had done.
Both those who adore idols of their own imagination, and their graven things, are condemned.

96:9.text: Gods. Princes or angels, elohim, ver. 7.

96:11.text: Light. Prosperity. The Babylonians are punished.

96:12.text: Holiness. Or sanctuary. Praise for ever his adorable name, (Calmet) since he is holiness itself, and sanctifies others.

97:1.text: David. His name occurs not in Hebrew, though the psalm is worthy of him.
It may refer to the return from captivity, as a figure of the world's redemption.
Things. In rescuing his people from slavery, and in the incarnation.
Christ raised himself by his own power.
He redeemed mankind for his own glory, sibi.

97:2.text: Salvation. Cyrus, or the Messias, whose gospel is preached every where, (Calmet) and who has saved the world.

97:3.text: Israel. The prophets foretold the liberation of the Jew, and of mankind. The blessed Virgin Mary seems to allude to this passage, Luke 1:55.
Some Jews were converted, Romans xi.

97:6.text: Cornet. This was a crooked horn; the trumpets were of metal, Numbers 10:2.

97:8.text: Hands. These strong oriental expressions hardly suit our language.

They contain a metaphor, and denote the inhabitants of the world, (Haydock) or those rivers, which spring from Jesus Christ, and mountains, which are raised to heaven by his grace, to praise the Redeemer.
Then the just are exhorted to lift up their heads.

98:1.text: Himself. There is no title in Hebrew. Hence many have attributed it to Moses, with the preceding psalms. But the name of Samuel destroys this pretension, and most people suppose that David wrote it at the removal of the ark.
Others refer it to the return from captivity, (Theodoret) or to the dedication of the second temple, (Calmet) and the reign of the Messias.
Angry. Though many enemies rage, and the whole earth be stirred up to oppose the reign of Christ, he shall still prevail.
His will shall be done, (Worthington) for He sitteth on the Cherubim.

Ferrand infers from this expression, that the psalm cannot regard the times after the captivity, as the ark existed no more. But this is not clear; and, at any rate, this idea might still be retained, as God is represented on a chariot drawn by Cherubim, Ezechiel 10:1., and Daniel 3:55.

98:4.text: Hebrew, "the king's strength loveth judgment." He does nothing unjustly, as the enemy is forced to confess.
This is the highest glory of a king.
God requires that we should honour him, by correcting our faults.
Directions. Most right and just laws to direct men.
Jacob. There God principally instructed mankind, and punished the perverse. Other nations he seemed to have left to themselves.

98:5.text: Adore his foot-stool. The ark of the covenant was called, in the Old Testament, God's foot-stool: over which he was understood to sit, on his propitiatory, or mercy-seat, as on a throne, between the wings of the Cherubim, in the sanctuary: to which the children of Israel paid a great veneration. But as this psalm evidently relates to Christ, and the New Testament, where the ark has no place, the holy Fathers understand this text of the worship paid by the Church to the body and blood of Christ in the sacred mysteries: in as much as the humanity of Christ is, as it were, the foot-stool of the divinity. So St. Ambrose, 50:3. de Spiritu Sancto, C. 12., and St. Augustine upon this psalm.
The last mentioned holy Doctor inculcates the obligation of adoring Jesus Christ in the blessed Eucharist, and refutes the Capharnaites, etc., John vi.
The Jews adored God, shewing a relative honour, by prostrating themselves before the ark, in the same manner as Catholics do before holy images.
It is. Septuagint and some psalters, "he is holy."
"I discover how I may adore the foot-stool,...without impiety. Christ took flesh of Mary,...and give it us to eat for our salvation. But none eats that flesh, till he have first adored it."

98:6.text: Moses and Aaron among his priests. By this it is evident, that Moses also was a priest, and indeed the chief priest, in as much as he consecrated Aaron, and offered sacrifice for him, Leviticus viii. So that his pre-eminence over Aaron makes nothing for lay church headship.
Samuel is only mentioned as a Levite. See 1 Kings 25:1.

98:7.text: Cloud. As he did to Samuel in the night.
Them. By their example, the psalmist insinuates, that priests in the new law shall pray for the people.

98:8.text: All their inventions. That is, all the enterprizes of their enemies against them, as in the case of Core, Dathan, and Abiron. (Challoner) (Numbers xvi.) (Worthington) Syriac, "injuries." The failings of these great men were punished in mercy.
Thou hast made them harmless in all their works.

98:9.text: Mountain. On Sion, or in the Catholic Church.
"Those who do not adore on this mountain, are not heard unto eternal life."

99:1.text: Praise. Theodoret reads, "A psalm of David for confession, without a title in Hebrew." But now that text and the Septuagint agree with us. The psalm refers to the same events as the preceding.

99:2.text: Earth. Both Jews and Gentiles, (Worthington) converted to the faith of Christ.
Gladness. God loves a cheerful giver. [2 Corinthians 9:7.]
He hears our prayers in all places. Yet appoints the temple more peculiarly for that duty.

99:3.text: Not. St. Jerome seems to have read lu for lo, and translates, "and we are his," which Houbigant follows. But the Vulgate gives a very necessary instruction, as some might perhaps forget that God preserves their life every instant, (Berthier) and some have foolishly imagined, that the collection of things might give existence, or be eternal.

99:5.text: Truth. This is commonly joined with mercy.
God faithfully executes what he has promised (Worthington) for the liberation of his people, and the conversion of the Gentiles.

100:1.text: Himself. He describes the conduct which he was resolved to follow in ruling, (Calmet) or alluding to the pious king Josias. (Theodoret) (4 Kings xxii.) The sentiments are not confined to rulers alone, (Berthier) though this psalm might be styled, "the mirror of princes."
Mercy and judgment. The chief qualifications of a king.
Whether I am treated with clemency or with rigour, I will praise thee.
No one should presume, since there is judgment, nor despair, since mercy goes before.
I will sing, and I will understand. Piety is one of the best means to acquire the intelligence of these heavenly hymns, and of the whole scripture.
We might translate the Hebrew (Calmet) and Vulgate, Mercy and judgment I will sing, to thee, Lord, will I sing psalms.

100:2.text: I will understand, etc. That is, I will apply my mind, I will do my endeavour, to know and to follow the perfect way of thy commandments: not trusting in my own strength, but relying on thy coming to me by thy grace.
I will watch over my conduct.
When thou. So St. Jerome and Houbigant read, though Septuagint have "when wilt thou come to me?"
He is impatient of having the ark: but is well apprized that God will not dwell in a polluted soul, or in a wicked city: therefore he promises to remove such obstacles.
I walked. Or "will walk." All the other verbs should be in the future; (Calmet) though that is of no consequence.
David had studied to regulate his interior and his family before, as much as he did afterwards.
His fall was most likely (Berthier) subsequent to this event, 1 Kings 6:11.
He acknowledges that he had done what he here mentions, by God's assistance.

100:3.text: Thing. Or Hebrew, "word of Belial." I will neither suffer an evil word or action to pass without reproof.

100:4.text: Know. I looked upon them with such contempt, (Eusebius) or I highly disapproved of their conduct, (St. Augustine) and would have no society with them.
David was, in a manner, forced to tolerate Joab; but he shewed sufficiently his displeasure, and ordered his successor to punish him, as soon as it could be done with safety to the state.

100:5.text: Persecute. Or abhor (Worthington) with a perfect hatred. We must shew our displeasure if we hear detraction, (Haydock) that we may not partake in the crime.
The court is most likely to be infected with this vice, as people are continually endeavouring to supplant their rivals.
Secret thoughts of resentment must be stifled in their birth.
Heart. Hebrew, "wide heart," which is sometimes taken in a good sense, 3 Kings 4:29. But here it denotes the ambitious and misers, Proverbs 28:25.

100:6.text: Faithful. A prince cannot do every thing himself. But he ought to make choice of the most virtuous and skilful ministers.

100:7.text: Prosper. Hebrew, "shall not be pleasing," (St. Jerome; Haydock) or "maintain himself."

100:8.text: Morning. Without delay, (Worthington; Jeremias 21:12.; Haydock) and with a mind composed.
The Jews explain this of the judgment of zeal, by which a person might kill a notorious criminal, without any trail, (Calmet) in imitation of Phinees and Mathathias, 1 Machabees 2:24.
God alone will punish all malefactors at the last day.
The Fathers admonish us to resist temptations at the beginning. (Calmet) Principiis obsta; sero medicina paratur Cum mala per longas convaluere moras.

101:1.text: Poor. Hebrew, "afflicted." This may refer to some of the captives, who were returning, (ver. 14.; Calmet) or to Jesus Christ, (St. Augustine) to whom St. Paul applies ver. 26, 28., and whose redemption was prefigured by the former event.
David might have both in view.
Yet most believe that the psalm was written by Daniel, or Jeremias, etc. (Calmet) -- It may have two literal senses, like many others.

101:2.text: Cry. Fervent petition, though only in the heart, Exodus 14:15.

101:3.text: Turn not. We first abandon God, but must humbly beg for grace.

101:4.text: Fire. Cremium denotes any combustible matter.
While in mortal sin, our best actions, alms, etc., avail nothing, 1 Corinthians xiii.

101:5.text: Bread. Through excessive sorrow, (Haydock) I am fainting in captivity.
The human race is relieved by the bread of life, the holy Eucharist.
Sinners have no relish for spiritual food.

101:7.text: A pelican, etc. I am become, through grief, like birds that affect solitude and darkness.

Kaath comes from a root that signifies to vomit (Haydock) as this bird lives chiefly on shell-fish, which it swallows, and when the heat of its stomach has caused the shells to open, it throws them up again, and eats the fish.

It seems to be the onocratalus, which resembles the heron. What many of the ancients have related concerning its giving life again to its young, by its blood, etc., must be accounted fabulous. The Fathers have not expressed these facts as certain, (Calmet) though they have beautifully accommodated them to the tenderness and grace, which Christ has shewn to lost man. See Eusebius and St. Augustine.
Raven. Owl, or rather another species of pelican, so called from having a bag under its chap, "to inclose" fish, etc.
With its prey it retires to solitary places. So the distressed love solitude and silence.

101:8.text: Sparrow. Hebrew Tsippor means any "quick moving" bird, etc., (Idem.[Parkhurst in cose.?]) and as the sparrow is not a solitary bird, it may here signify the owl.
But these etymological reasons are not very certain, nor important.
We may adhere to the Septuagint, as many interpreters do.
The sparrow having lost its mate, mourns in or near its nest.

101:9.text: Against me. To kill me, (Acts 23:12.) or to prove my guilt.
They are my sworn enemies, (Worthington) who formerly seemed my friends.

101:10.text: Weeping. These figurative expressions denote excessive grief, Psalm 79:6.
Meat and drink gave me no more satisfaction than ashes.
Rolling on the ground, through grief, my food was spoiled.

101:13.text: Memorial. Thou wilt be remembered by us with gratitude for all eternity, (Haydock) or wilt thou punish for ever such short-lived creatures?
St. Paul understands this of Jesus Christ, (Hebrews 1:11.; Berthier) or he rather refers to ver. 26.
The hope of the Messias gives me comfort.

101:14.text: Come. Pointed out; (Jeremias 29:10.; Calmet) or David wishes to repair the ravages caused by Absalom, or foretells the return from captivity, (Haydock) and the grace granted to the Church, and to every faithful soul.

101:15.text: Thereof. They had a great regard for the very soil, 4 Kings 5:17.
Hebrew, "dust," as it was then uncultivated.
Esdras, etc., repaired the ruins of Sion, as Christ and his apostles established the Church.

101:16.text: Glory. The conversion of nations is often predicted as about to take place after the captivity; yet not so fully, till the time of Christ.
His glory is so manifest, that all kings know it, although they be not converted.

101:17.text: Jerusalem had attained its ancient splendour before the coming of the Messias.

101:18.text: Humble. Patriarchs, priests, and all true penitents.

101:19.text: Generation. Literally, "in another," by the subsequent writers of the Old and New Testaments.
Let all posterity become acquainted with this psalm, and know under what obligations we have been to the Lord.
Created. The Jews after the captivity, and, in a higher sense, (Calmet) Christians, the new creature, 2 Corinthians 5:17.
This interpretation seems much the better, as kings and nations were converted only by the Messias, and his apostles.

101:22.text: That. The faithful in the Church endeavour to serve Christ.

101:23.text: Kings. This did not take place till the gospel was preached, (Calmet) though some kings offered sacrifice before, yet without being converted. See Zacharias 2:10.

101:24.text: He answered him in the way of his strength. That is, the people mentioned in the foregoing verse, or the penitent, in whose person this psalm is delivered, answered the Lord in the way of his strength: that is, according to the best of his power and strength; inquiring after the fewness of his days: to know if he should live long enough to see the happy restoration of Sion, etc.

The sense of the Vulgate is as good as that of the present Hebrew, "he hath weakened (afflicted; Montanus) my strength, in the way; He hath shortened my days. I said, my God, make me not depart in the midst," etc.
The captives, or those who were set free, address God, though the psalmist himself may express what prayers he had poured forth in his bonds.
The variation of the Septuagint and Hebrew originates in the points; and the text has, his force, very properly, though the Jews would substitute "my."
The stability of the Church is hence proved by St. Augustine.

101:25.text: Days. Allow me time to grow in virtue. (Worthington) If I be cut off in my youth, I may not be prepared.

101:27.text: Perish. Or be changed in their qualities, (Worthington) as well as all other things, like a garment. This does not convey the idea of annihilation; and the Fathers are divided on this subject, 2 Peter 3:10., and Apocalypse 21:23.

101:28.text: Fail. This regards Christ, (Hebrews i.) who has established his Church, (Calmet) to be perpetual (Worthington) in this world, (Haydock) and triumphant in eternity, ver. 29.

The psalm must therefore be understood literally of Him; unless it may have two senses, which those to whom St. Paul wrote must have known, otherwise it would not prove Christ's divinity. The Socinians are greatly puzzled how to evade this argument.

102:1.text: Himself. All agree that David wrote this psalm as a model of resignation. The occasion is not known.
It may express the sentiments of the captives, (Calmet) or of converts to Christianity, (Eusebius) and is written with inimitable sweetness.

102:3.text: Diseases. He had described captivity as an illness, Psalm ci.
God graciously forgives sin, and removes bad habits. He preserves us from falling, and grants us the victory, with all our reasonable requests.

102:5.text: Eagle's. Which get fresh feathers every year, like other birds, Isaias 40:31.
The eagle retains its vigour for a long time, (Haydock) though many fabulous accounts have been given of its renovation.

The new birth in baptism, (Theodoret) or by faith, (Eusebius) or the resurrection of Christ are thus insinuated, (St. Leo, ser. i.) as well as (Haydock) our resurrection and state of grace. The one is necessarily connected with the other, and both senses are good.

102:6.text: Mercies. Hebrew, "Justice" in protecting the innocent.

102:9.text: Ever. He executes his threats, but soon pardons us.
Hebrew, "he will not plead always, nor watch to surprise us for ever;" (Calmet) or "retain" his anger.
He is inclined to pity us, and only inflicts a temporal punishment on the penitent, as Christ has paid their ransom.

102:11.text: Earth. There is no proportion between God's mercy and our crimes.
Sins are perfectly washed away, (Worthington) and not barely covered, as the east cannot be the west.

102:14.text: He remembereth. Roman Septuagint and psalter, etc., "Remember." Other copies agree with us.

God compassionates the frailty of those who fear him. Origen falsely inferred from ver. 9., that the devils and the damned would one day be saved. But this is contrary to Scripture, ver. 17., Matthew xxv., and Apocalypse xx., etc.

102:16.text: In him. Or "over it;" the flower. Or the spirit of God's indignation will overwhelm him. The soul of man departs, and cannot naturally be reunited with the body, though it greatly desire that union.
The longest life is but like the duration of a flower, (Isaias 40:6.; Haydock) and the splendour of the noblest families is no better in the sight of God.

102:17.text: Justice. In protecting the oppressed, (Calmet) and rendering to every one according to his deserts abundantly.
God never punishes more than a person's crimes have merited, Exodus 20:5., and 34:6.

102:19.text: All. When Christ shall sit in judgment, (Berthier) and the wicked be suffered no longer to disturb the order of things, and the joy of the elect.

102:20.text: His angels. You who have executed the orders of God for our delivery, help us to return him thanks.
Hearkening. Literally, "to hear," (Haydock) or that all may learn to obey God's mandates when they perceive how carefully the angels put them in execution.

102:21.text: Hosts. Sun, etc., which never deviate from their regular course.

102:22.text: Soul. In vain should we behold all nature praising God, if we neglected that duty.
All the works of the Most High praise him, being under his dominion.

103:1.text: Himself. Complutensian Septuagint adds, "a psalm on the creation," (Haydock) as this is the subject; but there is no title in the original. (Berthier) The psalm contains "a divine and natural philosophy," (Eusebius) respecting the creation and providence. Plato has written something similar in his Timaeus, (Calmet) though this comparison is indecent.
The imitation is no disparagement, however, to this divine word, and we may surely notice the concord between the inspired and profane writers.
This psalm seems to be a continuation of the preceding one.

Great. Literally, "magnified," in the same sense as we say, hallowed be thy name, [Matthew 6:9.] praying that God may be honoured by all his creatures; (Haydock) though He cannot increase in holiness, etc.
We become acquainted with his greatness, by considering his works.
Thou art entitled to all praise.

103:2.text: Light. In this manner he always appeared, 1 Timothy 6:16., and Exodus 3:2.
Christ only once assumed such a glorious form at this transfiguration, because he came to instruct our mind and heart.

Stretchest. Hebrew and Septuagint have the verbs in the third person, till ver. 6., as the Vulgate has here extendens. But St. Jerome and others agree with us, though St. Paul quotes according to the Hebrew, ver. 4.
Pavilion. The idea of the heavens resting like a tent upon the earth was very prevalent, Job 9:8., and Isaias 40:22.

103:3.text: Water. The Fathers are not agreed about the nature or situation of these waters. Some take them to be angels.
Others suppose that the waters in the clouds, (Calmet) or the crystalline substance in the region of the stars are designated.
These waters are represented as the roof of God's palace.
Winds. With surprising velocity, his providence being every where.
The psalmist accommodates himself to our capacity, to shew that God does all with the utmost ease.

103:4.text: Fire. The elements execute his will, (Calmet) or rather, as the apostle, and Chaldean, etc., intimate, the angels do this (Berthier) with zeal and activity, (Hebrews 1:7.; Haydock) and ease.
Some would attribute to the angels some sort of light bodies, and Grotius attempted to revive this opinion, which never could prevail over the contrary one, which is universally received.

103:5.text: Ever. The established order shall subsist, though the earth may move, Psalm 101:27.
It is fixed by its own gravity in the centre.

103:6.text: The deep. Hebrew, "thou hast covered it with the abyss." This proves that there was no pre-existent matter, otherwise God would not have begun with plunging his work in the abyss, and in confusion. But he chose to bring this beautiful world out of a state of disorder in the space of six days, as Moses and the psalmist relate.
At first, all was created, covered with the waters, Genesis i.

103:7.text: Fear. Hebrew, "retire precipitately" to their beds, at thy command, (Haydock) on the third day.
Some think that a storm or the deluge are here described, (St. Chrysostom) which is less probable.
The waters would naturally cover the earth; but are confined to their proper channels by God.

103:8.text: The....descend. This should be within a parenthesis, (Berthier) as the sequel speaks of the waters.
