103:8.src: St. Ambrose; Hex. 3:22.; Job 7:12., and Amos 5:8.; Calmet
103:8.text: Earth. This seemed miraculous to those who supposed that the earth was flat.
Providence points out the place for every thing.
God derogated from this law, which he had appointed for the waters, when he brought them again to overwhelm the guilty earth.

103:9.text: The....descend. This should be within a parenthesis, (Berthier) as the sequel speaks of the waters.

103:9.src: St. Ambrose; Hex. 3:22.; Job 7:12., and Amos 5:8.; Calmet
103:9.text: Earth. This seemed miraculous to those who supposed that the earth was flat.
Providence points out the place for every thing.
God derogated from this law, which he had appointed for the waters, when he brought them again to overwhelm the guilty earth.

103:10.text: Pass, to supply the wants of all creatures. Springs and rain afford the necessary moisture.

103:11.text: Except, waiting for one another, as all cannot drink at the fountains at the same time. The Chaldean has the same idea, though the Hebrew is explained, "shall break" (Houbigant, "shall satisfy") their thirst. Berthier)

103:14.text: Service. Cattle, or beasts of burden, are thus fed for man's service, though he may also eat legumes, etc.
Bring. God gives the increase, 1 Corinthians 3:7.

103:15.text: Oil. This was an article of food, (Berthier) and deeded almost as requisite for anointing the body, as bread and wine to support nature. (Pliny, [Natural History?] 14:22.) Hence it was prohibited in days of fasting.
These three things are put for all sorts of food.

103:16.text: Field. Hebrew, "of Jehova." Houbigant would substitute ssodi, "field," (Haydock) as this name of God is never used to denote "high" trees, etc.
God provides for the wants of all the creation, even of those things which seem less necessary to us.

103:17.text: Highest; or, literally, "the tribe of the heron is their leader," (Haydock) first making their nests. Septuagint seem to have read better than the present Hebrew, "the stork, the fir-trees are its house."

103:18.text: Irchins. St. Augustine reads also, "hares." These desert places are not useless.

103:19.text: Seasons and festivals, which it points out: mohadim.
During the night wild beasts seek their prey, as men and cattle may labour in the day-time. The sun, etc., were made for man's use, (Calmet) and not to be adored.
These bodies move with the same regularity as if they had intelligence.

103:21.text: God, like all other creatures, Psalm 144:15., and 146:9.

103:24.text: Hebrew, "The fulness of the earth is thy possession." Septuagint and ancient psalters, "creature;" ktiseos. The other interpreters read kteseos, (Calmet) which the Vulgate adopts.
The world derided Jesus Christ, the wisdom of God, and Creator of all.

103:25.text: Arms. Man acts thus to denote something very extensive.
The sea is frequently put for the same purpose, Job 11:9., and Lamentations 2:13. Arms is omitted in the Septuagint and the ancient psalters.
Creeping. Fishes, (Genesis 1:20.; Calmet) and all animals without feet (Worthington) in the waters.
Nothing multiplies so fast as fishes.

103:26.text: Go. The sea, which seems to be placed as a barrier, tends, by means of navigation, to the general convenience of nations.
Dragon. Leviathan, a huge fish, (Job 40:20.; Worthington) which Bochart takes to be the crocodile; though that monster is found rather in rivers.
It designates here all whales, and other great fishes which play in the sea without experiencing any fear like mortals. Some translate "to play with it," (Calmet) as auto cannot agree with Thalassa.
The prodigious size of these fishes costs God nothing.
The whole creation is but a plaything for him, ludens in orbe terrarum, Proverbs 8:31.

Some Jews impiously pretend that God plays three hours a day with the leviathan, and that he only created two; one of which he killed to make a feast for the elect, and the other causes the tides, by turning itself, etc. See 4 Esdras, written at least in the second century.
Though this monster be too strong for man, it cannot defend itself out of water; (Worthington) and even in its own element man gains the victory by his skill.

103:28.text: All, is not in Hebrew [nor] Septuagint. (Calmet) But the sense is the same.

103:30.text: Thy. Chaldean adds, "holy;" and the Fathers explain it of a spiritual renovation, or of the resurrection of the body.
Animals are still preserved by Providence.

103:31.text: Works. Seeing that they are good, Genesis 1:31. May all continue to answer the end for which they were created.

103:32.text: Smoke. Exodus 19:18., and 20:18. God is terrible as well as clement.

103:35.text: No more. So St. Paul pronounced sentence on those who did not love the Lord Jesus, (1 Corinthians 16:22.) yet without any animosity.
The saints thus pray for the conversion of all, (St. Athanasius) or express their approbation of God's sentence against the damned.

104:1.src: St. Augustine, ep. ad Casulan.; Calmet
104:1.text: Alleluia. This word occurs at the end of the preceding psalm in Hebrew, and means, "Praise ye the Lord," though it is also used as an exclamation of joy; for which reason it is left untranslated. See Apocalypse xx.
It implies that we must praise God with all our power; and Catholic writers retain this (Worthington) and similar words in the original.
The first 15 verses of this psalm nearly agree with that which was composed by David, when the ark was to be removed from the house of Obededon, 1 Paralipomenon 16:8, 22.
But the last part seems to have been added by him, or by another afterwards, with some small alterations.
It was perhaps adapted to the dedication of the second temple, with the two following psalms.
Gentiles. Their conversion is thus insinuated.
How much more ought we not to celebrate the mysteries of Christ?

104:3.text: Literally, "be praised," (Haydock) knowing to what an honor you have been raised, (Deuteronomy 30:7., and Jeremias 9:23.) and live accordingly.

104:4.text: Evermore. Be assiduous to obtain his favour, and present yourselves in his holy temple as often as you are able.

104:6.text: His servant. Or "servants." (Septuagint) Vulgate is ambiguous. Hebrew declared for servant here; but, his chosen, is certainly plural, and refers to all the people, and [in] 1 Paralipomenon 16:13., we read, seed of Israel, his servants.

104:8.text: Generations. Or from the call of Abraham. But this was to be understood if the Israelites continued faithful, (Deuteronomy xxviii.) or the promise regards the spiritual children of Abraham, who are blessed for ever, (Berthier) and continue to the end of the world.

104:11.text: Lot. Literally, "line," with which land was measured. (Calmet) -- The descendants of the patriarchs would never have lost this inheritance if they had observed the law. The promise was therefore conditional; though it was absolute, in as much as all were to be blessed in their seed: as they had begun to be, when Jerusalem was destroyed by Titus.

104:12.text: Very few. Hebrew, "as it were reduced to nothing," to avoid a tautology.
But this version is not necessary.

Jacob at this time was not married, (Berthier) and his parents had no part of the land. This manifests the power of God, and the faith of the patriarchs, (Calmet) who doubted not but that he would realize what he had promises.

104:13.text: People. This seems to be a fresh obstacle to their possession of Chanaan.

104:15.text: Prophets. The word anointed is thus explained, as the patriarchs were not kings over any but their own families, though they were equal in riches to many kings. They foresaw future events, and offered sacrifice to God, as priests, in which sense also they may be styled anointed. No visible unction, but the divine appointment, might be requisite. God protected them in a wonderful manner, and selected them for his peculiar people.

104:16.text: And. The psalmist continues to mention the favours of God, till the Israelites entered the promised land, which the author of 1 Paralipomenon xvi., passes over.
Bread. In time of famine, people are not strengthened with their food.
God permitted, (Worthington) or caused the famine to rage.

104:17.text: He sent. Drawing good from the malice of Joseph's brethren, (Haydock) in which he had no hand.
Who could have thought that this event would have tended to Joseph's exaltation, and to the safety of his father's house?

104:18.text: Fetters. Hebrew cebel, (Haydock) "a chain, or the stocks." The meaning is not ascertained.
Soul. Luke 2:35. He was in great distress, (Haydock) and in danger of perishing.

104:19.text: His word. By which he foretold his own glory, or what should befall the king's two officers, which brought him into notice, Genesis 37:9., and 40:22. His word, may also refer to God's decree.
Inflamed him. He received the gift of prophecy, (Haydock) and being inflamed with the love of God, resisted the solicitations of Putiphar's wife.

104:22.text: Instruct. Hebrew, "bind" by precepts or chains, (Berthier) as he had all power.
Wisdom. Joseph was considered as the oracle of Egypt, and the prime minister.
We cannot doubt but he would strive to undeceive the people with regard to many superstitions.

104:23.text: Cham. Who resided there, and was worshipped under the name of Jupiter Ammon.
Misraim was his son, and peopled the land, Genesis 10:13.

104:24.text: Exceedingly. In 215 years, 600,000 warriors sprung from seventy people, (Calmet) or from seventy-five, as Septuagint and St. Stephen read, comprising eight wives, Jacob, and sixty-six of his descendants. Seventy might naturally produce two million and a half in such a space of time. But the Hebrews had been cruelly oppressed. God's blessing enabled them to increase as they did.

104:25.text: He turned their heart, etc. Not that God (who is never the author of sin) moved the Egyptians to hate and persecute his people; but that the Egyptians took occasion of hating and envying them, from the sight of the benefits which God bestowed upon them.

He permitted the malice of the Egyptians, (Theodoret) and employed them to chastise his people, who had adored idols. (Eusebius) (Acts 7:43.) Felix, Protestants, etc., translate the Hebrew, "their heart was turned."
The perversity of the Egyptians came from themselves, though it was turned to a good account.

104:27.text: He gave. Hebrew, "they placed in them the words of their signs."
But the Chaldean and St. Jerome are conformable to the Septuagint.

104:28.text: Grieved not his words. That is, he was not wanting to fulfil his words: or he did not grieve Moses and Aaron, the carriers of his words: or he did not grieve his words, that is, his sons, the children of Israel, who enjoyed light whilst the Egyptians were oppressed with darkness.
He performed what he had threatened without reluctance.

We may also translate, "he added no threat (Haydock) before this ninth plague was inflicted." Hebrew, "and they did not irritate (or transgress) his word." The envoys of God were obedient to him. The copies of the Septuagint are not uniform; (Calmet) some omit the negation, which would give a very good sense; though it must be allowed to be inaccurate, unless the Hebrew be read with an interrogation, "did they not resist his word?" alluding to the Egyptians.
Almost all the ancients have the plural exacerbaverunt, except St. Jerome, in his commentary.

104:29.text: Blood. In reality, so that the fishes were killed.

104:30.text: Land. Including the rivers, Exodus 7:1., and 8:3.

104:31.text: Flies. This was revealed to David, or known by tradition, as it is not recorded by Moses.
Some copies of the Septuagint have, "the dog-fly," cinifes (Exodus 8:16.) gnats, (Haydock) which are very common and tormenting in Egypt. The Jews understand lice.

104:32.text: Rain. Which fall in Egypt, though less frequently.

104:34.text: Bruchus. An insect of the locust kind, (Challoner) or a worm which spoils corn, etc.

104:36.text: Labour. This explains the first part of the verse, Genesis 49:3., and Psalm 77:51.
The fifth and sixth plagues are not specified.

104:37.text: Gold. Which the Egyptians gave to get quit of them, (Exodus 11:1.) and that with joy, (ver. 38.) though they might at first only intend to lend.

Feeble. This refutes the notion which the enemies of the Jews had propagated, saying that they were banished on account of the leprosy. They felt none of the inconveniences which fell on the Egyptians, and God removed every obstacle which might retard their flight.
According to the course of nature, out of two million and a half, 150 people die daily.

104:40.text: Asked. Chaldean and ancient psalters add, "for meat." Hebrew, "he (Moses or all the people) asked, and the quail came," at Sin, Exodus 16:13.

104:41.text: Rivers. The waters from the rock were so abundant.

104:44.text: People. Whose crimes have deserved death, Numbers 33:51., and Deuteronomy 7:5.

104:45.text: His justifications. That is, his commandments: which here, and in many other places of the Scripture, are called justifications, because the keeping of them make men just. The Protestants render it by the word statutes, in favour of their doctrine, which does not allow good works to justify.
The design of God in granting Chanaan to the Israelites, was to encourage them to preserve the true religion.

105:1.text: Alleluia. The word is repeated in some copies.
But Hebrew has only one at the end of the preceding psalm: of which this appears to be a sequel. As in the former, God's favours were specified, so here the ingratitude of his people is acknowledged.
It may refer to the Jews during, (Eusebius; Vatable) or after the captivity, (Calmet) and to all in the state of sin.
The 47th and 48th verses agree with the three last, which were sung at the translation of the ark, 1 Paralipomenon 16:34.
Hence that canticle now makes a part of our Psalm civ., xcv., and cv., with various alterations made by the prophet.
Give glory. Literally, "confess" (Haydock) your sins, to obtain mercy, (St. Chrysostom) or rather praise God, (Theodoret) who is the source of all good.
This was the usual prayer of the Israelites, in ceremonies of thanksgiving, 2 Paralipomenon 5:13.

105:2.text: Powers. See Ecclesiasticus 43:29. No one can sufficiently praise God's power or mercy.

105:3.text: Times. He only who perseveres to the end shall be crowned, Matthew 24:13., and Ezechiel xxxiii.
Such as these can alone worthily sound forth God's praises, (Berthier) being so happy as to preserve their innocence, or at least to repent.

105:4.text: Us. Hebrew, "me." But Aquila, Syriac, etc., agree with the Septuagint. Some of the Jews were already returned, and pray for the liberation of their brethren.
The Fathers explain it of Christian converts.
People. According to thy gracious promises, that we may enjoy the blessings of thy elect.

105:6.text: Sinned. Here commences the confession of sins. The just man first accuses himself, Proverbs 18:17.
The same prayer is repeated, Judith 7:19., and Daniel 9:5., as it might be applicable to the captives.
We have injured ourselves, our neighbours, and our God.

105:7.text: Going up. Or proceeding. The banks of the sea were more elevated than the country from which the Israelites came, (Calmet) and the land of Chanaan was still higher. See Psalm 106:23., and Genesis 12:9.
Sea even. Is not in the Septuagint.

105:8.text: Known. Isaias 63:12. He might justly (Worthington) have abandoned them to the Egyptians, Exodus 14:11.

105:10.src: St. Augustine, Ser. xlii. de Temp.; Worthington
105:10.text: Redeemed. What price was given? This is a figure of baptism, in which the fruits of Christ's blood are applied to our souls. All sins are there destroyed, as the Egyptians were in the Sea.

105:11.text: Left. Manetho and Lorin assert, that Pharao reigned afterwards. But this is contrary to the Scripture. All who came to attack Israel perished.
They supposed that the waters were enchanted.

105:12.text: Words. And Moses his servant, Exodus 14:31., and 15:1.

105:13.text: Counsel. Three days after the passage of the sea, God laid injunctions upon them at Mara, which they would not observe, Exodus 15:22.

105:14.text: Desire. Or they greedily sought for flesh meat, (Haydock) at Sin, or Pharan, Exodus 16:13., and Numbers 11:31. Chronological order is not observed.

105:15.text: Fulness. Or disgust: plesmone. Septuagint probably read zore, (as Numbers 11:20.) instead of razon. Hebrew, "leanness," (Calmet) which is a natural consequence of immoderate repletion.

105:16.text: Holy one. By his function (Worthington) of priest. Core wished to take his place, as the rest did to supplant Moses in the civil government, Numbers xvi.

105:17.text: Abiron. With Core, though his children were preserved.
God presently resented the injury done to his ministers.

105:18.text: Wicked. Two hundred and fifty accomplices, (Numbers 15:35.; Calmet) and fourteen thousand seven hundred murmurers.

105:19.text: Graven. Hebrew, "molten." The image must first be graven.

105:20.text: Grass. This enhances their folly. God is often styled the glory of his people.
Osee 10:5. contemptuously calls the golden calf, the glory of Samaria.
This idolatry was truly blameable, (Romans 1:23.) and not the veneration paid by Catholics to the images of Christ, etc.
Those who pretend that the Hebrews meant to adore the true God, and not the idol Apis, are here clearly refuted; they forgot God, etc., Acts 7:40.

105:23.text: Breach. Like a valiant captain, (Calmet) or pastor.

God complains of those who neglected this duty, Ezechiel 13:5., and 22:30. The psalmist alluded to the conduct of Moses, after the people had adored the calf, (Exodus 32:10.) or when they had joined with Core, Numbers 16:44.

105:23.src: St. Jerome ad Vig. ii.; 2 Machabees 15:14.; Berthier
105:23.text: The intercession of the saints is useful, (St. Augustine) still more after their victories.
God spared the people for the sake of Moses.

105:24.text: Word. Which Moses, Josue, etc., announced, Numbers xiii., and 12:29.

105:26.text: Over them. Swearing (Calmet) to destroy them, (Haydock; Ezechiel 20:23.) and scatter them, as they now experienced, Leviticus 26:31., and Deuteronomy 28:36, 64.

105:28.text: Dead idols, without life, (Challoner) particularly Adonis (Calmet) whose death is bewailed by the poets, etc.

105:29.text: Multiplied. All who had murmured perished, or this may relate to the 24,000, Numbers 25:9.

Ezechiel 20:23. St. Paul (1 Corinthians 10:8.) speaks of those 23,000 slain by the Levites. The Hebrew which has only 3,000 must be inaccurate. Temporal chastisements were intended to prefigure those which are eternal, as the Jews knew that such rewards and punishments were to be considered (Matthew 19:16.; Berthier) as the spiritual sense of the law.

105:31.text: Evermore. Phinees thus by his pious zeal, merited (Worthington) God's favour, and the high priesthood (Numbers 25:13.; Calmet) for his posterity, for 900 years, though the latter promise seems to have been conditional.

105:33.text: He distinguished with his lips. Moses, by occasion of the people's rebellion and incredulity, was guilty of distinguishing with his lips; when, instead of speaking to the rock, as God had commanded, he said to the people, with a certain hesitation in his faith, Hear ye rebellious and incredulous: Can we from this rock bring out water for you? Numbers 20:10.
For thus doubting of God's will, he was temporarily punished, Deuteronomy 1:37.

The death of his sister, and the ingratitude and rebellion of the people disturbed him. (Theodoret) See Isaias 63:10. Houbigant follows the Arabic, "he delayed or hesitated." Yebatte means, "he spoke rashly." Some refer this, (Berthier) to God. "He reproached....that they," etc., (Menochius) which is not necessary, (Berthier) or he sentenced Moses to die.

105:34.src: Josephus, Jewish Wars 2:8.; Exodus 23:32., and Josue 13:13.
105:34.text: Them. Commanding no idolatrous government to be left in the land, nor any Chanaanite to be permitted to live, who should make opposition. The law of extermination must probably be thus restricted. The Jews neglected these ordinances, and hence became so often infected with idolatry, (Berthier) even to the end of the republic, when many still adored the sun.

105:38.text: Chanaan. These unnatural sacrifices were perhaps practised under the judges, or at least under the kings.
Some pretend that the children were only made to pass over or between fires. But this is refuted by facts, Leviticus 18:21., and 4 Kings 16:3., and Jeremias 7:30

Polluted. Infecta: ephonoktanethe is used by Septuagint, in this sense, though it signifies also, "was slain;" interfecta, as most Latin Bibles and Fathers read, before the correction of Clement VIII. The former expression is more natural and conformable to the Hebrew.

105:39.text: And was. Hebrew, "and they were defiled."
Inventions. Idols of their imaginations, (Haydock) and lusts.

105:45.text: Repented. Ceased to punish, (Eusebius) when the people repented.
God then regarded them in mercy, having given them grace to repent.

105:46.text: Mercies. He inspired their enemies with pity for them. Cyrus, Darius, and Artaxerxes shewed them favour, Psalm 83:12.

105:47.text: Save. These verses, which may be well recited in times of schism, (Worthington) occur, 1 Paralipomenon xvi., being probably inserted by Esdras, or some other prophet. But here they are in their proper place, as many of the Israelites are still in captivity.

Yet as this is only a system, we may rather suppose, that David wrote this conclusion. He had to encounter various nations, who might have made captives; and he may beg that all may be united in the same faith, and avoid the society of the wicked.

105:48.text: So be it. The second Amen occurs not in Hebrew. But Alleluia is placed instead, which the Vulgate makes the title of the next psalm.
This conclusion of the fourth book seems to be added by the collector, Psalm xl.

106:1.text: Alleluia. St. Augustine repeats this word. But it occurs in the Hebrew, etc., at the end of the preceding psalm, of which this is a continuation, shewing how God pardoned his people, and delivered them from captivity, (Calmet) and mankind from sin.
Glory. Literally, "confess" your sins, thay you may praise God, (Psalm cv.; Haydock) and adore his mercy and providence.

106:2.text: Countries. The Jews from Babylon, and all who were redeemed by Christ.
He was promised immediately after the fall. [Genesis 3:15.]

106:3.text: Sea. Hebrew miyam, for which we should read imim "the right," denoting the south.
But the sea, or ocean, is properly used in the same sense.
The Ammonites, Philistines, Syrians, and Idumeans, from these four quarters, often reduced the Israelites to servitude, under the judges.
From all parts, the Jews of the ten tribes returned in the reigns of Darius and Alexander.
But the texts which are applied to this event speak rather of the conversion of the Gentiles, which the prophet has here in view.
None are excluded from the benefits of this redemption, but by their own fault.

106:4.text: Habitation. So were the Jews distressed at Babylon, (Calmet) as many are forced to wander in the world, and all are involved in sin.

106:5.text: Hungry. As both Jews and Gentiles were for the word of God, (Amos 8:11.) when Christ appeared.
Their wants cried aloud, (St. Augustine) though they sought him not, Isaias 65:1.
Those who call upon God are relieved by him, in the manner which is most for their welfare.

106:8.text: Mercies. We are unworthy to open our mouths. This chorus is repeated, (ver. 15, 21, 31.) by the people, after the Levites had sung the intermediate sentences.
The ver. 6, 13, 19., are of the same nature, and refer to different sorts of calamities.
All the benefits of God, proceeding from his mercy, and not from man's deserts, praise him.

106:10.text: Darkness. This second allegory represents the condition of the captives, and of mankind, before Christ's coming.
The former enjoyed some liberty, Jeremias 29:5.
But the latter were most miserable, (Matthew 9:15., and Luke 4:18.) and of these the words are most naturally explained.

106:11.text: Exasperated. Hebrew, "changed, or frustrated." This is the source of all misery, (Calmet) as calamities are commonly inflicted on account of sin.

106:12.text: Labours. There were slaves of various descriptions: some were confined to hard labour, and chained down at night.

106:14.text: Bonds. Of their passions. See St. Augustine, Conf. 8:11.

106:17.text: Iniquity. Making them alter their conduct. Hebrew seems less correct: "they are foolish on account of their way, and they," etc. Illness is sent to punish sin, Job 33:19., Matthew 9:2., and John 5:14.

106:20.text: Word. Jesus Christ, according to the prophetical sense, adopted by the Fathers, (St. Athanasius, or. 3., etc.) though it literally implies, that at God's command the sick were healed, Matthew 8:7.
This allegory again represents the state of captivity.

106:22.text: Praise. Instead of victims, Psalm xxvi., etc.

106:23.text: Down to the sea, etc. Captivity is here compared to a tempest.
The apostles carry the tidings of salvation to all places. Persecutions are raised; but God grants peace to his Church under Constantine.

106:24.text: Deep. The fury of tempests. Various nations, and the treasures of the deep.

106:26.text: Heavens. So Virgil says: Tollimur in caelum curvato gurgite et iidem Subducta ad Manes imos descendimus unda.

106:27.text: Up. They knew not how to proceed. Ambiguis ars stupet ipsa malis.

106:31.text: Men. We must thank God for having enabled us to repent, ver. 8.

106:33.text: Wilderness. God caused his people to pass through the Red Sea, and the Jordan, to possess the fruitful country of Chanaan, part of which had been cursed for the crimes of the Sodomites.
He has punished Babylon, (Jeremias 25:12.) made a straight road for his people, (Isaias 35:8.) and enabled them to cultivate their country again, ver. 41.
The synagogue has been abandoned, and the Church chosen.
The power of God is displayed, who caused the land of promise to be more fertile for his people, which is now very barren.

106:34.text: He alludes to the environs of Sodom, Genesis 13:10., and 19:24.

106:37.text: Of birth. A plentiful crop, facient fruges germina.
Abundance of fruit shall grow in some places, while others are punished on account of sin.

106:40.text: Princes. Jechonias was humbled, (Haydock) and exalted, with Daniel, etc., 4 Kings 25:27. Others explain it of Nabuchodonosor and Baltasar, who were reduced to the state of ignominy and death.
The Jews experienced the greatest miseries at the last siege of Jerusalem.

106:41.text: Sheep. The Gentiles took the place of the obstinate Jews.

106:42.text: Mouth. The psalmist saw Babylon before its ruin, (Calmet) at least in spirit. God's judgments strengthen virtue, and repress the wicked. (Haydock) The propagation of the gospel put to silence the oracles of the pagans.

106:43.text: Lord. This is the part of wisdom, and deserves our serious consideration, (Haydock) as Osee (xiv. 10.) and our Saviour admonish, Matthew 11:15., and 13:9., and Mark 4:9., etc.

107:1.text: Himself. By repeating here what occurs in Psalm lvi., and lix. David teaches us to excite our fervour by such sentiments as have formerly made an impression upon us.
He spoke before of his own victories: now he considers those of Christ, and of his Church, (Genebrard) as well as the state of the captives.

107:2.text: My heart, etc. Is not repeated in Hebrew. But it is, [in] Psalm 56:8.

107:3.text: Arise, my glory. Is found also in this psalm, though not here in Hebrew, etc. St. Jerome (ad Sun.) thinks some copyist has inserted it.
But it seems to be here in its proper place.

107:4.text: Nations. David made various conquests, which prefigured those of Christ, to whom the rest of the psalm belongs.

107:8.text: Holiness. Or holy one, sancto: (Haydock) meaning Jeremias 25:11., who foretold the end of the captivity, (Calmet) or David himself, and the Messias, his son, in whom he will rejoice.

108:1.text: David. St. Peter attributes it to him, and gives us the key to this psalm, by applying (ver. 8.) to the traitor Judas, Acts 1:16, 20.

Our Saviour seems to allude to it, when he styles Judas, the son of perdition, John 17:8. It may also have a reference to Doeg, or Achitophel, who were his forerunners. Ven. Bede thinks it was composed by the Machabees, against apostates. The Church used it in the deposition of bishops, and against the usurpers of ecclesiastical goods: (Grotius) and, in times of ignorance, some thought hereby to discover thieves. The style is very vehement, (Calmet) containing the sentence pronounced by the sovereign judge against the reprobate.

108:2.text: whom I always adore, under every dispensation of Providence.
Yet the original may agree with the Vulgate, and Christ desires to be glorified, (John 12:23., and 17:5.; Berthier) and styles his passion, his praise.
Man. The Pharisees, etc., endeavoured to entangle Christ, Matthew xxii.
Saul's courtiers strove, by calumnies, to excite his rage against David.

108:3.text: So Catiline gratuito potius malus quam crudelis erat.
The sanctity of Jesus Christ could not be overwhelmed, or hidden, (Haydock) even with the torrent of abuse thrown upon him.

108:4.text: Detracted. Hebrew and Septuagint, "calumniated," as [in] ver. 20., and 29.

Prayer. Jesus Christ prayed for his enemies, (Luke 23:34.; Flaminius) and did many good works for the benefit of all, John 10:32., and Acts 10:38. David had also signalized himself in the defence of his country, and yet was banished. He shews that he was animated with the perfect spirit of the gospel, though the law permitted retaliation, Exodus 21:24.

108:6.text: Set thou the sinner over him, etc. Give to the devil, that arch-sinner, power over him: let him enter into him, and possess him. The imprecations, contained in the thirty verses of this psalm, are opposed to the thirty pieces of silver for which Judas betrayed our Lord: and are to be taken as prophetic denunciations of the evils that should befall the traitor, and his accomplices, the Jews; and not properly as curses.
The devil entered into Judas, (John 13:2.; Calmet) who hung himself in despair.

The Jews were abandoned over to cruel masters, as Doeg and Achitophel presently perished, Psalm 51:5. It was customary at trials, for a satan, or "adversary," to be stationed at the right hand of the accused, Zacharias 3:1.

108:7.text: Prayer. Or may his supplication to judges for pardon irritate them, (Calmet) and let it not move God to mercy any more than a sin.
God rejects such prayers as are destitute of the proper conditions, as he did those of Antiochus, (St. Augustine; Calmet) and they are a fresh sin.
Yet the prayer of a sinner is not so, when he acts sincerely.

108:8.text: Bishopric. Episkopen, "inspection or pre-eminence" of any kind. Judas lost his spiritual office, and the Jews all dominion in a very short time. St. Peter quotes this passage, [Acts 1:20.] and only the Socinians will assert that he does not adhere to the literal sense.
The apostleship (Worthington) was given to St. Matthias.
The priesthood of Aaron was forced to give place to that of Melchisedech.

108:9.text: Wisdom. Judas was married, (St. Augustine) and stole for his family; (Lyranus) though as the Scripture is silent, St. Chrysostom believes the contrary.
The synagogue being rejected, the Jews are in a manner orphans.
The posterity of persecutors prosper not long.
All sorts of imprecations are used that some may fall upon the guilty.

108:10.text: Out. Hebrew, "seek." St. Jerome, "be sought after," which implies that the are rejected.
The being reduced to beg, is terrible to one who has been brought up in a better manner.
Dwellings. Septuagint (Menochius) and St. Jerome, "ruins." The Jews were forbidden to weep over the ruins of Jerusalem, and are become vagabonds.

108:11.text: Usurer. The Romans forced the miserable Jews to pay taxes, and Domitian banished them from Rome, to live in a wood, where their furniture was only a basket and some hay, and their wives came to beg. Cophino faenoque relicto Arcanam Judaea tremens mendicat in aurem.
At the last siege, some of their bodies were ripped open, to discover the gold which they might have swallowed. (Josephus, Jewish Wars 6:15.) See Deuteronomy 24:10.

108:13.text: In one. Hebrew, "another." Let none remember them. The ruin of Jerusalem took place within forty years time.
Arch-heretics are not long followed; their disciples make fresh discoveries.

108:14.text: Out. As children derive great advantages from the piety of their parents, so they also feel the punishment of their crimes, when they imitate them, Deuteronomy 24:16., and Ezechiel 18:20. If they share in the afflictions of their family, and are free from sin, this must be considered as a trial, and they will be amply rewarded in a future world. God is the arbiter of the life and death of all. External miseries may afflict both the just and sinners; but the motive for their being sent is very different. The Jews prayed, Let his blood be upon us, and upon our children; (Matthew 27:25.) and they became chargeable with all the crimes of their ancestors, Matthew 23:35.

108:15.text: They. Iniquities, (Haydock) or may the sinners be "against the Lord," and He against them, Leviticus 26:21.

108:16.text: Mercy. Therefore he, the chief, and each of his adherents, can expect to find no mercy, James 2:13.
By the most crying injustice, the Jews put to death, the man of sorrows, [Isaias 53:3.] (Calmet) who had been pleased to assume our nature, and had not where to lay his head, Luke 9:58.

108:18.text: Bones. This strikingly describes the malediction of a person who has swallowed iniquity like water, Job 15:16., and Proverbs 4:17. The Jews had spoken ill of Christ, and of his disciples, Matthew 27:25., and John 9:17.
Let all who delight in cursing, attend to this sentence.
They like those things, which bring both temporal and eternal misery.

108:20.text: Work. They effect their own ruin.
The Jews pretended to act for the glory of God, being influenced with a zeal without knowledge. Some translate, "let such be the recompense;" which is not amiss.

108:21.text: With me. Many ancient psalters supply, "thy mercy," with the Arabic.
Grace must always accompany our endeavours, 1 Corinthians 15:10.

108:22.text: Within me. In the garden. Christ did penance for sin all his life. He wept over Jerusalem, Luke 19:41.

108:23.text: Locusts. David was forced to wander about. Christ continually sought after the lost sheep, (Calmet) and was hurried to different tribunals.
St. Jerome applies this to the Jews, and St. Augustine to Christians under persecution.

108:24.text: For oil, propter oleum. The meaning is, my flesh is changed, being perfectly emaciated, and dried up, as having lost all its oil, or fatness.
Or, "on account of the privation of perfumes," (Berthier) which the Jews looked upon as necessary. David and Jesus Christ were exposed to many inconveniences.

108:25.text: Heads, and saying, Vah, etc. (Matthew 27:40.) as Semei insulted David, (2 Kings 16:5.; Calmet) though he is not said to have shaken his head, for which reason the prophecy may rather relate solely to Christ.
"To pay our ransom, he was crucified; to be crucified, he was despised; and to be despised, he appeared in a state of abjection."
These three steps were requisite, as the Jews would never have dared to lay hands on him, if he had appeared in glory.

108:27.text: Jesus Christ prays for a glorious resurrection, and that his enemies may be covered with all possible confusion.

108:29.text: Cloak. Diplois, means the outward robe. Hebrew mehil.

108:29.src: St. Leo, ser. xviii. de pas.
108:29.text: "The cross of Jesus Christ shall be the glory of believers, and the confusion of infidels."
A salutary and inward shame may be of great advantage.
Christ prays that his enemies may feel such a sorrow, and be converted.
"He speaks not against, but in favour of, the Jews."

108:30.text: Many. In the Catholic Church, (Haydock) till the end of the world.

108:31.text: My. Hebrew, "his soul from the judges," Pilate and the Jews, (Calmet) particularly the Sanhedrim. How many still continue to persecute Christ, and do not tremble!

109:1.text: David. It is of faith that he wrote this psalm on the Messias. The Jews, in our Saviour's time, were convinced of it, (Matthew 22:42.) so that their posterity (Berthier) in vain attempts to explain it of Abraham, David, Solomon, Ezechias, or Zorobabel.
Even some of them candidly own that it can relate to no other, (Thalmud) and Christians are universally of this belief.
The Lord. Hebrew Jehova, (Haydock) the Father.
To my Lord. Hebrew Ladoni, (Haydock) the Son incarnate, (Menochius) Lord of all, though the son of David.
Who else could be David's superior? as Christ argues.
The title Adonoi is given to God, (ver. 5., etc.; Calmet) as my is never united with the ineffable name.
Hand. In equal power (Berthier) as God, and in the highest dignity as man, after the ascension.

This thought should encourage us to suffer patiently, (Colossians 3:1.) as Christ was to suffer, and thus to enter into his glory. The saints did not strive to divide him. But we would suffer nothing, and yet be glorified at the hour of our death!
Until. This word does not always mark the term of a thing. When all shall be subdued, then Christ will continue to sit with greater majesty, (1 Corinthians 15:25.; Calmet) for ever.
Footstool. As was customary with conquerors, Josue 10:24.

109:2.text: Sion. Whence the empire of Christ extended over all the earth, Isaias 2:3., and Luke 24:47.
In spite of opposition, he reigns in the Church, and will one day make all submit.
On Whitsunday the new law was promulgated, to continue unto the end of time.

109:3.text: Principality. Christ says, All power is given to me, Matthew xxviii., and this he will display (Haydock) in the day of judgment.
Arche is used in this sense by Xenophon, etc., (Calmet) as principium is by Suetonius, (in Aug.) yet it may also signify, This is the "origin," or source of thy authority, from the womb," etc.
The consubstantiality of the Son is hence manifest, and this ensures every perfection.
The Father and the Son are both principals.

Christ was in the beginning, (John i.) and the very beginning. His eternal birth is here mentioned, though some have explained it of his temporal nativity, which took place before the rising of the day-star.
This, however, would seem a trivial circumstance, (Berthier) whereas the birth of Christ before the whole creation is of great consequence.

Saints. Or "holy places," sanctorum. Hebrew, "In the beauties (behadre.; Haydock) St. Jerome has read berri, in the mountains, (Calmet) of holiness, (Montanus) or of the sanctuary." Christ will come to judge surrounded by his angels, (Calmet) and saints.

I begot thee. This expresses the sense more clearly (Haydock) than the Hebrew tibi ros emissio (Hebrew tal.) nativitatis tuae. St. Jerome's version must be deemed inaccurate, and the Hebrew points, (Berthier) which render the modern versions so very different from ours, may be safely rejected. (Haydock) See Muis.
Robertson mentions fourteen different translations of this text, and many more might be given.
But ours is clear, and beautiful.
Protestants, "Thy people shall be willing in the day of thy power, in the beauties of holiness, from (Marginal note, more than) the womb of the morning: thou hast the dew of thy youth."
Thy offspring shall be very numerous, (Isaias 48:1., and 45:8.) and people shall willingly join thy banners, or rather come to offer victims in the sanctuary.
The eternal birth of Christ, (Micheas 5:2.) from his father's substance, establishes his principality, so that he rises triumphant, etc.
The present Hebrew text seems to be purposely rendered obscure, or unintelligible by the Jews, both in this verse, and in the following.

109:4.text: Repent. Not that He can ever do so, or give way to error: but the sacred writer expresses himself thus, to give us the greatest security.

The order. Hebrew dibrathi, "my order," Melchisedech. The i has been perhaps designedly inserted, to render the argumentation in the epistle to the Hebrews of no weight, "as the force of the text sinks into just nothing."

Protestants and Pagnin here abandon the Hebrew. But Montanus corrects the latter, and substitutes "upon my word," which is more honest, as he deemed the Hebrew text unerring, though here it be not so unquestionably, as the Almighty would thus address Melchisedech, unless that title be here given to Christ. St. Jerome takes no notice of my, no more than the apostle, etc.
This instance "may perhaps put all serious Christians upon deliberating---whether they should any longer maintain the absolute integrity of the present Hebrew text." See Psalm 15:10.

Melchisedech. Christ is declared king and priest for ever, (Worthington) like Melchisedech, who united in his person both dignities, and presided not over a particular people, nor stood in need of any stated place. His succession is not recorded, and his sacrifice consisted of bread and wine; in all which respects he differed from the Levitical priests, and prefigured Christ, who is immolated under the same species throughout the world, Malachias 1:11.

We read in Scripture of three orders of priesthood: 1st, of kings, 2d, of the first-born, and 3d, of Aaron. Melchisedech, in quality of king, exercised the priestly office, as both functions were formerly united: and hence the word Cohen signifies both a temporal and spiritual prince. This light of nations ensured to his order a perpetual duration, while that of Aaron was to have an end. Thus Christ offered to his Father from all eternity the sacrifice of his obedience, and future sufferings; and in time, he presented that of his own life, which he continues to offer in the Catholic Church, (Calmet) by priests who are only his ministers, 1 Corinthians 4:

The apostle does not specify the oblation of bread and wine, as it was unnecessary, the sacrifice of Christ on the cross having put an end to the sacrifices of the old law, which could only be offered by the children of Aaron, from whom he did not spring. This was enough for his purpose. But as Melchisedech offered bread and wine, Christ must also have done the same, to be of his order. St. Cyprian, and the other Fathers, with great unanimity observe, that the sacrifice of Melchisedech was a figure of that of Jesus Christ, in bread and wine; and of course (Berthier) our sacred mysteries must contain the substance.
By their application, Christ still pacifies his Father in behalf of sinners: so that the effects of his priesthood do not cease, as those of all the priests in the Old Testament did by their death.

109:5.text: The Lord. He speaks to God the Father concerning the Messias, (Menochius) or God assisted the sacred humanity.
In the Godhead the persons are equal. The Father is at the right of the Son, as He is at his Father's, ver. 1:
Yet it seems more probable, that the discourse is addressed to the first person.

109:6.text: Ruins. Hebrew and Septuagint, "with dead bodies," (Calmet) or he will fill up the places of the fallen angels.
Christ was placed for the fall and for the resurrection of many.
Of many. Hebrew, "the head in an extensive territory." We might render the Vulgate, "he shall crush the heads of many in the land."
Rebellious kings, with their populous kingdoms shall be destroyed.
The power of the devil, and of all his agents, shall be crushed, though they may fill the greater part of the world, (Haydock) in terra quam multi occupant.

109:7.text: Way. By the torrent Cedron, the passion of Christ is insinuated.
During life, he and his faithful servants shall be exposed to many sufferings, for which they will be amply rewarded.

A torrent often denotes affliction, Psalm 17:5., and Isaias 30:28. Yet here it may signify, that Christ will supply every thing requisite to establish his Church. To find water for an army was of the greatest consequence, Jeremias 31:9., Psalm 77:20., and 4 Kings 3:9.
Like a valiant conqueror, Christ seeks for no delicacies.
Those who come nearest to this divine pattern, will obtain the highest place in heaven.

110:1.text: Alleluia. This psalm consists of praise, and is alphabetical, (Berthier) the sixteen first letters being found at the head of each hemistic, to ver. 8; and in the 9th and 10th, we find the six last Hebrew letters, which is also the case in the following psalm.
In some Latin copies, we also find the same title in both, as they may relate to the captives.
The Church thanks God for the institution of the Eucharist, etc.
Congregation. Where there are few or many together, (Calmet) in private for my own sake, and in public for edification.

110:2.text: Sought out. Exquisite, or designed for our benefit. He saw that all was good, (Genesis 1:31.) though He could have made them better.

110:3.text: Is praise. Or worthy of praise, and magnificent.

110:4.text: Remembrance. He has delivered us from captivity, as he did our ancestors from the Egyptians bondage, Isaias 63:11.

110:5.text: Food. Manna in the desert, and what is necessary for us who were in distress in Babylon, Psalm 106:5. In the spiritual sense, (Calmet) He has given us Jesus Christ, (St. Augustine) or the holy Eucharist.
St. Chrysostom understands the word of God, (Calmet) which profits those only who fear the Lord, (Berthier) and serve him with love.
Covenant. God has not acted contrary to the covenant with Abraham, in proposing the gospel, which was all along foretold, as St. Paul shews.
He will for ever protect his Church, and shew forth the powerful operation of his death, and of all his mysteries.

110:7.text: Gentiles. Under Josue, or rather in the days of the gospel.
Judgment. He will perform his promises, and chastise the guilty.

110:8.text: Equity. Here we have the conditions of a just law.
Those who keep God's commandments are justified.

110:9.text: Redemption. Under Moses, Cyrus, and Jesus Christ, of whose redemption the former were figures.
Christ redeemed man, in order that he might be able to observe the law.
Terrible. Holy to the just, and terrible to sinners.
Most are lost by too much security.
The Hebrews treat the name of God with a respect, which might appear superstitious, (Calmet) as they will not blot it out, or lean upon the book where it is written, etc.
The Turks have nearly the same regard for the Koran, which should make Christians ashamed of their irreverence and want of faith.

110:10.text: Fear. Hebrew, "is the chief part of wisdom."
This fear intimates the virtue of religion, to which the Israelites gave the preference over prudence, etc.
This distinguished them from other nations, Deuteronomy 4:8.
Fear is the beginning, charity the end of wisdom.
Beginning with fear, we ascend to true wisdom, which are the first and last of the gifts of the Holy Ghost.
Do it. Act conformably to this fear.
Speculative wisdom is good, but not sufficient, Romans 11:13.
Many read ea, "those things," with the Hebrew, while the Septuagint have eam, this wisdom. But Houbigant adheres to the Vulgate.
His. God's, (St. Chrysostom) though some would refer ejus to intellectus.
He shall be praised for ever who has been so well informed as to adopt the fear or wisdom of the Lord for his guide.

111:1.text: Of the returning, etc. This is in the Greek and Latin, but not in the Hebrew. It signifies, that this psalm was proper to be sung at the time of the return of the people from their captivity: to inculcate to them, how happy they might be, if they would be constant in the service of God.
Yet all Greek copies have not this title, (Haydock) but only Alleluia, with the Hebrew, Syriac, etc.

It might be composed by Aggaeus, etc., as it relates to the captivity, (ver. 4.) and to the overthrow of Babylon, (ver. 10.; Calmet) or David might thus describe the happiness of the virtuous, (Berthier) and give the captives to understand, that sin was the source of all temporal as well as spiritual miseries.
Delight. We must love God for his own sake.
Those who sincerely fear God, will take great delight in keeping his commandments.

111:2.text: Earth. Temporal rewards were proposed to the carnal Jews; but the more enlightened knew what was to be most desired. They sought after the riches, mentioned by the apostle, 1 Corinthians 1:5., and 1 Timothy 6:18.
The just and their seed shall prosper, (Worthington) at least in the next world.

111:3.text: Justice. Or mercy shall be for ever remembered by men, and rewarded by God.

111:4.text: Darkness. Christ appeared when the world was most corrupt.
God rescued his people from captivity.
He is. St. Augustine and St. Chrysostom add, "The Lord God is," etc., in which sense this is commonly explained, (Calmet) though it may also refer to the just man.

111:5.text: Give, and it shall be given to you. [Luke 6:38.]
Judgment. And by liberal alms, prepared for the great accounting day.
He will say nothing indiscreetly, nor throw pearls before swine; (Matthew 7:6.; St. Jerome) neither will he condemn others rashly, (Calmet) but give prudent advice to the afflicted.

111:7.text: Hearing. Though detraction may assail him, he shall not fear, (Calmet) since God is the judge.
He shall have no cause to apprehend being condemned, (St. Jerome) nor be disturbed about "news," because his goods are in a place of safety, (St. Chrysostom, etc.) where thieves cannot steal.

111:8.text: Until. Not that he will be disturbed afterwards, (Psalm 109:1.; Calmet) when his enemies shall be punished.
The captives saw the fall of Babylon.

111:9.text: Poor. We must know whom we ought to relieve. Though we may be allowed to retain what is necessary, (2 Corinthians 8:13., and 9:11.) yet the saints have often very laudably stripped themselves, to clothe others, abandoning perishable goods, that they might obtain heaven.
Justice. Works of mercy are so called, because they concur to man's justification.
Horn. Power, etc. Cyrus, and the best of his successors, honoured the Jews.
The liberality of the just towards the indigent, is far more glorious than that which prompts the vain to give shews, etc.
The praise of the latter is presently at an end.

111:10.text: The wicked. The devil, enraged to see the converts to Christianity, (St. Athanasius) or the Jews, (St. Chrysostom) instigated by him.
All the damned shall repine at the happiness of the elect, (St. Augustine) as the Babylonians did, when they beheld the prosperity of those who had been captives.

112:1.text: Alleluia. The Jews style this, and the five following psalms, "the great Alleluia," which they sing after eating the paschal lamb. Eusebius adds, "a psalm of Aggaeus and Zacharias," as it seems to speak of the captives, who return thanks for their deliverance; (Isaias 45:6., etc.; Calmet) or David has left this model of thanksgiving for all.
The Greek and Latin may signify both.

He addresses the Levites, who were addicted to God's service, or the new converts, who were become docile like little children, (1 Peter 2:2., and Psalm 8:3.; Calmet) and in a word, (Haydock) all God's servants.

112:3.text: Same. All the day long, (Calmet) or rather in all places.

112:6.text: Earth. Providence watches over all, Psalm 137:6.
With respect to God, even those who are in heaven are low.

112:7.text: Poor. Jesus Christ and his servants, as well as Joseph, Mardochai, etc., 1 Kings 2:8., and Luke 1:51.

112:9.text: Children. Anna and Elizabeth, who were naturally barren, were blessed with what they so eagerly desired, by the divine power.

It may be understood of the Gentile world, after it had embraced the faith, (St. Jerome; Galatians 4:17.; St. Chrysostom) or of the captives, (Isaias 56:1., and 49:21., and Psalm 67:7.; Calmet) though Isaias may also refer to the Church.
Joseph was wonderfully advanced in glory, and Sara, Rebecca, etc., were made fruitful.

113:1.text: Alleluia. This word is placed at the end of the preceding psalm in Hebrew, though it seems to have been there originally, (Haydock) as [in] ver. 2., we find his, with reference to "the Lord," who has not been otherwise mentioned before.

The psalm may be joined with the former to ver. 9, when the Hebrew begins a fresh one, relating to the captives, with the two which follow. (Calmet) the division is of no great importance, (Berthier) and we cannot easily decide whether it be here necessary.
The Hebrew copies have not been always uniform, no more than the Greek in this place.
Barbarous. Cruel, (Berthier) or which spoke a language unknown to them.
Barbarus his ego sum, quia non intelligor ulli.
The Greeks styled all others barbarians, (Fest.) as the Egyptians did.

Joseph at first did not understand the language of the latter, (Psalm 80:6.) and spoke to his brethren by an interpreter, Genesis 42:22. Lohez, denotes one who speaks an unknown tongue, which Chaldean expresses by the word borbra, "a stranger, or desert."
Infidels, and those who persecute the true religion, are styled barbarous, though otherwise the Egyptians were very polite and learned.
Not. Some Jews here commence the 115th psalm.

But St. Augustine shews, that this part is well connected with the preceding, the true God being known by his works, while idols are senseless, and therefore can have no pretensions to divine worship.

It seems that the psalmist would not break off so abruptly, without praising God for his wondrous works, and the Fathers are silent about the present division of the Hebrew, (Berthier) though Eusebius and St. Athanasius had occasion to examine the text, as some Greek copies end here, and others at ver. 4(12)., the idols, etc.
Glory. We claim no share in these miracles; or we confess our unworthiness, but do thou deliver us.
Thou hast done these wonders to fulfil thy gracious promises, and to prevent blasphemy.

113:2.text: Judea. Hebrew, "Juda," though the sense of the Vulgate is very good, (Berthier) as that country which had been so abandoned, became holy, when God's people dwelt there.
After the departure from Egypt, the Israelites were more known as God's inheritance, over whom he reigned.

Hence He complains, when they asked for a king, (1 Kings 8:7.) though the throne is still called the Lord's, 1 Paralipomenon 29:23. The distinction of Juda and Israel insinuates that the kingdom had been divided.
But this had taken place for a time, after the death of Saul.

113:3.text: Saw. He speaks in a poetical manner. All creatures obey God's will.
Heaven. Septuagint add, "and on earth," which St. Augustine joins with the following words, he, etc. We cannot indeed point God out, as we might do idols. But then what sort of gods are they?

113:4.text: Skipped. Through joy, exultaverunt, (Haydock) or rather through fear, ver. 7.

There was an earthquake, not specified by Moses; or the psalmist speaks of what took place at Ar, (Numbers 21:15.; Worthington) unless he alludes to the waters of the Jordan, rising up like mountains.

Men. All Catholics agree, that idolatry is the "giving of divine honour to any creature." St. Justin Martyr, (contra Gent.) St. Augustine in the ten first books of the City of God, and other Fathers, refute all the species of idolatry. The Platonists adored the angels, or devils, intelligentias separatas. Others worshipped dead or living men renowned for their achievements, like Jupiter and Hercules; while some paid the same sovereign respect to animals, or even to inanimate things, both in themselves and in their images. The psalmist here derides the most gross species of idols, which are made by men, and are incapable of any vital action, being thus beneath the very beasts. Yet some were so absurd as to confide in them, (ver. 16.[8(16)?]; Worthington; or ver. 8.[8(16)?]; Haydock) and thereby neglected the light of reason, becoming slaves of the devils, who were either the objects of adoration, as in the compacts made by sorcerers, or at least seduced mankind to pay such worship to creatures. Hence all the gods of the Gentiles are styled devils, Psalm 95:5.

How unjustly do heretics apply these words to the holy images used in the Church! though they must know (Haydock) that Catholics do not consider them as gods, no more than the saints and angels, whom they reverence only as the friends of God: treating their pictures with a relative honour, and endeavouring thus to excite themselves to the pursuit of virtue, by the memory of what they have done.

113:7.text: Throat. Roman and Milan Psalters add, neither is there any breath in their mouths, which occurs, (Psalm 134:17.) instead of this sentence.
Juvenal (Sat. 13.) laughs at the silence of Jupiter's statue.

113:8.text: Waters. They are mentioned twice, as referring to different miracles, Exodus 17:6., and Numbers 20:8. Inanimate things are introduced, giving this reply; or the psalmist gives it himself.
He uses the figure prosopopeia, as if senseless things could understand.

Let. Zeal prompts him to make this imprecation, (Calmet) or prophecy. Hebrew, they "are or shall be." The pagans (Haydock) could not well find fault with this wish, (Menochius) as it would be a great honour to resemble real gods. Yet none of their statuaries would be willing to become such statues, or be charged with the wicked conduct of Jupiter, etc.
The psalmist justly conforms his will to God's decree; and still would rejoice if he should give the idolaters grace to repent.

113:9.text: The house, is not now in Hebrew. But it occurs in the parallel passage, (Psalm 134.) where the imperative is used, as the Hebrew is here pointed.
"Israel trusts....house of Aaron, trust ye in the Lord," (Montanus) which is much in favour of this text, though St. Jerome, etc., agree with the Septuagint.
Houbigant rejects the Hebrew reading, and the house of Israel occurs, ver. 12(20).

113:9.src: St. Chrysostom; Acts 2:5., and 10:2., and 13:16.; Calmet
113:9.text: All the people, the priests, and converts from paganism, are invited to praise the Lord.
The Church always comprised two distinct orders, the clergy and the laity.

113:12.text: Hath. Hebrew, "will be," which seems better. Let him bless us.

113:16.text: Of heaven. Or the highest heaven, in which God displays his glory, though he fill every place.

His benefits to man claim a return of gratitude, and we are not dispensed from shewing our adoration, as deists would hence unreasonably infer. (Berthier) -- Worldly men say this in their hearts, abandoning their pretensions to heaven.

113:17.text: The dead. People who are thus affected, give no praise to God, when they die, but descend into hell.
Criminals are therefore said to be dead, while the saints only sleep.
Hell. Hebrew, "silence," or the tomb, (Berthier) where none can sound forth God's praises, (Haydock) though the soul in a state of separation may adore him. (Berthier) See Psalm 6:6., and 29:10.

113:18.text: Live. In the state of justice, and aspiring to God's kingdom. While we use this world only as the means to ascend thither, we shall praise him for evermore.

114:1.text: Alleluia occurs in Hebrew at the end of the foregoing psalm, (Haydock) with which this and the following have an intimate connexion, alluding to the liberation of the captives. Hammond discovers some Chaldeisms, which confirms the supposition that it was composed about that time.

Yet this does not deter Muis, etc., from ascribing the psalm to David, reigning in peace, (Haydock) though others think he alludes to some of his persecutions, and it certainly appears to be of the same nature with the 55th [psalm], which was written after his escape from Achis.
The sentiments of our Saviour, or of his Church under persecution, (St. Augustine) or those of a saint entering into glory, are here expressed.
The faithful sigh after their heavenly country.
Christ speaks here as in the 21st psalm.
Prayer. He always hears me, which prompts me to love.
Because. Hebrew ci maybe rendered "therefore," as in the next psalm, ver. 1., and Luke 7:47.

114:2.text: Faith, hope, and charity, (St. Augustine) as well as gratitude, are here commended.
Love and confidence are necessary conditions of prayer; and increase the more it is employed.

114:3.text: Perils. Hebrew, "the straitness of the grave, (Calmet) the fortifications of hell."

I am like one buried after the manner of the Egyptians, who bound up the dead, and laid them in small holes cut out of a rock. David uses similar expressions to denote the dangers to which he had been exposed, 1 Kings 21:12., and Psalm 17:6.
They may be applicable to all the just.
When I offended, death and hell, which are due to sin, threatened me unawares; affliction opened my eyes, and I thereupon prayed to thee.

114:6.text: Little ones. In their mother's womb, and infancy.
He delights to protect those who have no dependence on any other.

114:7.text: Rest. The peace of the soul, which must precede eternal happiness.

Bountiful to. Hebrew, "rewarded." The Chaldean termination i, occurs twice in this verse, whence some would prove that it was written after the captivity. This argument is weak, as such things have been observed in the books which were certainly written before. It would only follow, that Esdras might make such alterations, (Berthier) or they may be attributed to some negligent transcriber.

114:8.text: He. Hebrew, "thou hast." Yet St. Jerome and the Chaldean read like the Vulgate.

114:9.text: Living. In Judea, which is opposed to Babylon, the region of tears and of death, ver. 8., and Psalm 55:13. In a spiritual sense, the Fathers understand heaven, where the blessed cannot forfeit their felicity.
I will strive to please God among the faithful, who live in grace and glory.
Christ here promises the propagation of the gospel.

115:1.text: Alleluia is not in Hebrew. There seems no necessity to join this psalm with the preceding, as the subject is not so much alike as that of many others, which are distinct pieces. St. Paul quotes two passages in their literal sense, (Berthier) as applicable to all who thirst after a future life. It may relate also (Haydock) to the captives returning, (St. Chrysostom; Calmet) to Ezechias, (Ven. Bede) or to the Machabees.
Exceedingly. We must not be deterred from professing our faith by any danger.
I never ceased to publish that I trusted entirely in thy promises, that we should be delivered (Calmet) in due time.
I believed that God woud help me, and, as I ought to do, make profession of my faith, under the greatest tribulations.
With the mouth confession is made unto salvation.
I confess that there is a future world, (ver. 9.[Psalm 114:9.?]) though I do not see it, but remain in distress.

115:2.text: Excess. Septuagint, "ecstacy." Enlightened from above. If he had spoken thus by his own spirit, any one might have replied, that no dependance could be placed in what he said. We are all liable to mistake.
Many explain it of David's "flight" before Absalom. Hebrew, "in my precipitation," (Calmet) or "astonishment."
Liar. Weak and inconstant of his own corrupt nature, (Haydock) though not always guilty of lying.
St. Paul contrasts this natural weakness with the veracity of God, (Berthier) and the preaching of his apostles, Romans 3:4., and 2 Corinthians 1:17.
In the midst of my afflictions, I professed that all man's help is vain, and I had recourse to God.

115:3.text: To me. I have deserved chastisement; how, therefore, shall I express my gratitude for God's innumerable benefits?

115:4.text: Chalice. I will submit to any afflictions, (St. Augustine) seeing they procure such an ample reward. I will unite my sufferings with those of Jesus Christ, (Haydock) and accept the great benefit of his blood, shed for mankind.

Chaldean seems to have had this in view, calicem redemptionis levabo mundo venturo, and Houbigant explains it of Christ, who prayed that the chalice might be taken from him, (Berthier) but presently resigned himself to God's will. It may also (Haydock) imply, the cup of thanksgiving, which was used in pacific sacrifices, Psalm 21:27. Our Saviour followed this custom, when he instituted the blessed Eucharist, as (Calmet) the Jews do on solemn occasions, (Leo. 3:7., and 9:2.) The pagans had something very similar. Homer speaks of the "free bowl, or cup of liberty," (Iliad 2.) and Athenaeus (I. 23., and 2:2., and 15:5.) of "the cup of Jupiter, the Saviour," in which, after tasting a little wine, water was mingled, with invocations of Jupiter, and all drank what they pleased.
The custom of drinking healths might originate in the same jovial humour.

115:5.text: Vows. Voluntarily, (Worthington) which I could not do at Babylon, Psalm 65:13. St. Augustine and the ancient psalters omit this, perhaps supposing it to be taken from ver. 18.

115:6.text: Precious. I am ready even to make a sacrifice of my life, if God's glory should require it; for he esteems the death of the saints.

He will not easily abandon them to destruction in this world, as the Hebrew implies, (Tirinus; Calmet) though this meaning is not certain, nor verified by facts; the just being often slain by the wicked.
God rewards the sufferings of his servants; (Calmet) the psalmist will not therefore be deterred from paying his vows by the fear of death.

115:7.text: Handmaid. To be blessed, we must be in a state of grace, and children of the Church.
Out of it, death itself endured for Christ would profit nothing, (St. Augustine) but only be "a punishment of perfidy."
No worship can please God, unless we be joined in communion with her [the Church], as the Fathers observe.
The psalmist glories in being a servant of God for ever.

115:8.text: Praise. Or thanksgiving, Psalm 26:6., and Leviticus 7:12.
Lord. There is but one, as there is but one faith. Without the latter, it is in vain to call upon God, Ephesians 4:4.
"Whosoever eats the lamb out of this house, (the Church) is profane."

116:1.text: Alleluia. This word is found at the end of the last psalm, in Hebrew.

The captives invite all to thank God for their delivery, which was a most striking figure of the world's redemption, in which sense the apostle (Romans 15:11.) quotes this psalm, with the holy Fathers, though Ven. Bede puts it in the mouth of Ezechias, after the retrogradation of the sun. Kimchi allows that it regards the times of the Messias.
Praise him. St. Paul reads, magnify him, as the Hebrew means, "extol" by your praises.
All are invited to praise, as the redemption is sufficient, (Worthington) and designed for all the posterity of Adam.

116:2.text: Remaineth is not in Hebrew. Mercy and truth confirm us.
The psalmist acknowledges that he also stands in need of them, and St. John assures us, that grace and truth came by Jesus Christ. [John 1:17.]
No promise had been made to the Gentiles (Worthington) by the law; though they were all included in the original promise, Genesis 3:15.
God hath withdrawn them from idolatry, to impart to them his mercies.

117:1.text: Alleluia is borrowed from the end of the former psalm in Hebrew. Our Saviour and the apostles determine us to explain this solely of the Messias, though some would also see another literal sense, applicable to the victories of David, or of the captives, at their return, over God or Cambyses.

This supposition seems very probable, 2 Esdras 6:16. Yet the Jews saw that some passages belonged to the Messias, and were accustomed to use ver. 26., in praying for his manifestation. This psalm is very pompous, and in the dramatic style, (Calmet) though this is not certain.
Praise. Or "confess," and praise God for his great mercies.

117:2.text: That he is good, is not here in Hebrew. The Septuagint insert the words in the two next verses. The people, priests, and all who fear God among the Gentiles must praise him (Psalm 113:11.; Calmet) particularly now under the new law, since they have received greater benefits.
Our Saviour seems to allude to this passage, observing that God alone is good, (Luke 18:28.) to intimate that the man who gave him that title, must also acknowledge his divinity.

117:5.text: Enlarge me, or set me at liberty in a spacious place.

117:7.text: My helper. Hebrew, "to me among the helpers," (Montanus) or most powerful, (Houbigant) and the mover of all, who lend assistance. Jesus Christ prayed with tears, and was heard for his reverence, Hebrews 5:7. We must expect afflictions, (Job xxxiii.) and must have recourse to God.

117:9.text: Princes. For vain is the salvation of man, Psalm 59:31., and Jeremias 17:5.
So neither can man hurt those whom God protects, Romans 8:31.

117:10.text: Revenged. Hebrew, "I have broken them," (Calmet) or "will render to them."
This may relate to David, Nehemias, Christ, and his martyrs.
The Church, or any just man in the midst of enemies, confidently hopes for victory.
None shall prevail against Christ and his Church.

117:12.text: Bees. Septuagint add, "do a honeycomb." But this is not in the original.

Burned. Hebrew, "were extinguished." The Septuagint and Chaldean seem to have read better, as it would then be useless to add, as is the former verse, "but....I will destroy them;" and therefore Protestants and Duport here put, "for," etc., (Berthier) though the ci be still used.
The rage of the enemy is well described by the similitudes of bees and fire.
Christ was attacked with mortal hatred by the Jews.
The fury of the multitude was fierce, but short-lived, as in God I have overcome them all.

117:13.text: Fall. I was very near falling. Hebrew, "pushing thou hast pushed me that," etc.

Those who stick to the Hebrew suppose, that the psalmist addressed the chief of his enemies. But a letter or two may have been added, as St. Jerome, Felix, etc., admit not the second person. Houbigant prefers, "they have pushed me." (Berthier) ---By God's grace I was prevented from yielding to the force of temptation.

117:15.text: Just. Such were the Jews by their vocation, though many answered very ill the designs of God.
Christians thank God for their redemption, and confessors are full of joy in their prisons and torments, Acts 16:25.

117:16.text: Me is not in Hebrew. All salvation is from God.
Right hand, repeated thrice, insinuates the blessed Trinity, as the word Lord is applied to Christ, who effected man's redemption with singular efficacy.

117:17.text: Live. The captives were dying daily, so that this rather belongs to the Church of Christ.

117:18.text: Septuagint may also signify, "has instructed me," (Calmet) by means of tribulation.
God chastiseth as a parent, that his children may not perish eternally.

117:19.text: Justice. Of the temple, where the undefiled Israelites alone can enter, (Psalm 14:1.) or the land of Judea, Isaias xxvi. The Fathers explain it of the Church, and of heaven, to which none can be admitted, who have not departed in the communion of saints, (St. Chrysostom; St. Augustine) having walked in the narrow path.
Christ styles himself the way, (Haydock) and the gate.
Formerly penitents were not allowed to be present during all the Mass; and heretics, etc., are cut off from the bosom of the Church.
The just here beg for instruction, which they promise to follow.

117:22.text: Corner. This was a sort of proverb, and is applied to David, Zorobabel, or the Jewish nation; but they can only be considered as figures of Christ, in whom this prediction was fulfilled, when he established his Church, and made one people of those who were before divided, Isaias 28:16., Matthew 21:42., and Acts 4:11.
He was rejected by most of the Jews; yet he chose some of them, who, with the Gentiles, formed his Church.

The Pharisees pretended to build for the glory of God, when they opposed the designs of Christ, which, nevertheless, succeeded. They could not object to his application of this text, as they would have done, if it had been already verified in David, etc.
He has laid the foundation. We must be living stones, if we would co-operate in this heavenly building, 1 Peter 2:5.

117:24.text: The Church often repeats this during the paschal time, though God is equally the author of all days. (Haydock) See Ephesians 4:30., and John 8:56.

117:25.text: Save me. The person is not expressed in Septuagint. Or, (Calmet) Hebrew hoshiha na, "salvifica nunc."

Quaeso, Domine, salvum fac, obsecro: Quaeso, Domine, fac prospere agere, obsecro. This formed the acclamations of the Jewish children, Hosanna, Matthew 21:9. The branches which were carried at the feast of tabernacles, were also styled Hosannas.
When Christ entered Jerusalem, the children sung his praises, holding palms.

117:26.text: We. The Levites, (Calmet) or Christ and his ministers pronounce this blessing, (Worthington) or the psalmist gives it, after having expressed his thanks for the graces brought by the Messias.

117:27.text: Us. Christ, who comes in the name of the Lord, "is himself God," our instructor.
Day. The feast of tabernacles, for which this psalm was probably composed. The Jews dwelt under tents.

Altar. Hebrew, "Bind a festival with cords unto the horns," etc. To make sense, the Chaldean inserts, bind the lamb for the festival. But this Houbigant ridicules, and he believes that the solemn entrance of Jesus Christ into Jerusalem is here foretold. Scarcely any prediction in the Old Testament is more clearly verified in the new, Matthew 21:8. Hebrew Bahabothim certainly means, "in ramis opacis," and St. Jerome translates, "frequent the solemnity in shady boughs."
The victims were never tied to the altar, but slain in the porch of the northern gate, Ezechiel 40:39.

117:28.text: I will, etc. This might be in the copies of the Septuagint. It occurs [in] ver. 26., (Berthier) though it was here retrenched in the Hexapla.

117:29.text: For ever. Praise is our first and last duty, ver. 1.

118:1.text: Alleluia. There is no title in Hebrew. But (Haydock) this psalm contains the praises of the Lord, and of his holy law, under fourteen different names, (Worthington) of way, testimony, etc., repeated in every verse, except the 122d, (Muis) with surprising variety, so as to avoid tautology, and to give a most perfect system of moral doctrine. It is written according to the order of the Hebrew alphabet, (Haydock) that we may learn it from our infancy.

Eight verses begin with each of the twenty-two letters. St. Jerome (ad Paulam Urb. et Proem. in Lam.) moralizes on the signification of these letters, which he renders, 1. Aleph, etc., "the doctrine---2. of the house; 3. the plenitude, 4. of the tables (or holy scriptures)---5. This 6. and 7. this 8. of life---9. a good 10. beginning---11. the hand 12. of discipline (or the heart)---13. From them 14. everlasting 15. help---16. the fountain (or eye) 17. of the mouth 18. of justice---19. the calling 20. of the head 21. of teeth 22. the signs." By thus connecting the letters, he forms sentences to shew that the holy Scriptures bring us to the knowledge of the Church, and of Christ, etc. See Worthington. Thus every thing serves to excite the devotion of the saints, though the proud may deride their ingenuity. The sacred writers have certainly found some pleasure and utility in writing so frequently in the alphabetical order, though we may not perceive the advantages of it.
David is supposed to have written this psalm for the instruction of Solomon in his youth, (Berthier) though others believe that he composed it while he himself was young, and persecuted by Saul.
It seems very probable, that Daniel wrote it for the consolation of the captives.

Origen and Ven. Bede refer it to those times; though it seems in reality to appertain to all who desire to live piously, (Haydock) and it is only a conjecture that any other but David was the author, to whom it is generally attributed. Its excellency cannot be denied, and the Church has adopted it for her daily office, dividing it into eleven psalms.

St. Augustine has written thirty-two, and St. Ambrose twenty-two sermons on the contents; and St. Basil observes, that David has here comprised in one psalm the sum of all that he has written in the rest. Among other points of morality and doctrine, we may remark, that the psalmist insists on the necessity of God's grace, and the co-operation of free will, (Worthington) and overturns the Protestant system of justification.
The Israelites might recite this psalm on their journey, three times a-year, to the temple, as the fifteen gradual canticles which follow, were to be sung on the steps leading to the house of God.
Lord. Such only are happy here, (Worthington) or hereafter.
All aim at happiness, but only the virtuous take the proper means to attain it.
The way may here designate this life, (Worthington) or the law, (St. Augustine) or Jesus Christ, John 14:6. (St. Ambrose) The psalmist evidently presupposes, that some can and do keep the law.

118:2.text: His testimonies. The commandments of God are called his testimonies, because they testify his holy will unto us. Note here, that in almost every verse of this psalm, (which in number are 176) the word and law of God, and the love and observance of it, are perpetually inculcated, under a variety of denominations, all signifying the same thing.
We must search the law, not out of curiosity, but to practise it; (Haydock) otherwise we shall become more guilty.

118:3.text: Ways. They may, however, repent. The just are subject to fall, 1 John 1:8. But venial faults are not incompatible with justice.
Hebrew, "They also do no iniquity: they walk in his ways."

118:4.text: Diligently. Nimis. Literally, "too much." But this is a Hebrew idiom, to imply the greatest diligence.
Some would refer it to "God's strong injunction;" which is not necessary. The psalmist henceforward speaks to God.

118:5.text: O! that. Conscious of his own insufficiently, he prays for grace to be justified.
Moses acknowledged, that man could not observe the law, without Christ, Deuteronomy xxx 11., and Romans 10:6.

118:6.text: All. At the day of judgment, it will not suffice to have observed only some of the commandments. See St. Jerome, St. Ambrose, etc., who all seem to follow Origen.
The breach of any law brings confusion.

118:7.text: Justice. That all thy ordinances are most equitable, (Worthington) and when I shall have faithfully put them in practice, Ecclesiasticus 15:9.

118:8.text: It may be advantageous to us to be left awhile, that we may know our own weakness.
He does not beg never to be tempted, or in tribulation; (Haydock) but only that he may not yield to sin.
He may always at least have recourse to prayer, 1 Thessalonians 5:17.
The neglect of this duty occasions so many falls.

118:9.text: The observance of the law is the only method to preserve innocence, or to regain it.
The Holy Ghost gives this direction to youth, and to all who are exposed to the dangers of pleasure, (Worthington) as David might do to his son, 2 Kings 2:3.
In the same sense as we pray, Lead us not into temptation. [Matthew 6:13.]

118:10.text: Let. Literally, "do not cast me off."
God rejects none but the negligent.
The just, or the Church in general, here confess (Worthington) that perseverance is a gift of God.
Deprived of grace, we should fall, no less than if God "made us err," as the Hebrew strictly implies.

118:11.text: Heart. To guard against the temptations of vanity.
Christians formerly concealed the mysteries of religion with the utmost care.
Moses had given the letter of the law only, insinuating, that it must be kept with all the heart, as David here more fully explains.

118:12.text: Justifications. He considers himself as placed at the feet of his divine Master.
Though just, he wished to increase in virtue, Apocalypse xxii.
