118:13.text: Mouth. I have concealed them in my heart. Now I am not ashamed to publish them.

118:14.text: Riches. I give thy law the preference, Psalm 18:9.

118:17.text: Give. Hebrew, "avenge," Psalm 12:6., and 137:9. Draw me from this state of oppression, (Calmet) or rather, give me abundant grace, (Berthier) and eternal life.
I cannot fulfil the law, without thy grace.
Enliven me. So the Septuagint of Aldus reads, though the Roman and Hebrew have, "I shall live," Deuteronomy 30:19.

118:18.text: Law. In rewarding, punishing, etc. This thou wilt enable me to perceive, (Worthington) as thy law is too much above my comprehension.

118:19.text: Earth. At Babylon, or in the world, which is a pilgrimage, (Calmet) and I am unacquainted with the roads.
The latter sense is much better, 2 Corinthians 5:6., and Hebrews 11:10.

118:20.text: Coveted. Hebrew, "burns, (Aquila; Houbigant) or is bruised, (Berthier) and faints through the desire of thy laws," (Calmet) or "judgments."
If I have but a short time to live, I ardently seek for instruction, (Worthington) and wish to advance daily in virtue.
His humility makes him fear, lest his desire should not be sincere.

118:21.text: Cursed. Becoming victims of hell, Matthew xxiv.

118:23.text: All who would live godly in Christ Jesus, shall suffer persecution, 2 Timothy 3:12.
The servant of God will adhere to his duty, though his adversaries may be very powerful.

118:24.text: Counsel. Hebrew, "the princes of my counsel." (Houbigant) in opposition to those who endeavoured to make him fall, ver. 23.
The laws of God afford the best advice. An ancient king observed, that the dead were the best counsellors, as they will not flatter, etc.
In every trial, we must reflect on the rewards and punishments held out.

118:25.text: Pavement. Hebrew, "dust," (Berthier) weighed down by concupiscence, (St. Augustine) and infected by the union with the body.
The just, in great distress, beg to be delivered, conformably to God's promise.

118:26.text: Ways. Or sins, (Theodoret) or miserable condition, ver. 25.

118:28.text: Slumbered. Enustaxen, for which Origen, thinking it a mistake of copyists, substituted estaxen, "has melted," (Calmet) or "distilled," (Aquila, etc., Heracleot.) as more conformable to the original, though the sense is much the same. Loss of blood often causes people to slumber.
St. Hilary would not abandon the Septuagint.
Heaviness, being in such anxiety, as to be almost distracted.
Chaldean, "has been in an agony." Sleep is often put for death.
Hence the three apostles slept, Luke xxii.
Akedia, or torpor of mind, hinders the persecution of any business.

118:29.text: Iniquity. Hebrew, "lying." Let me not imitate the wicked.
Remit the punishment of my sins,
also original sin, and its effects.

118:32.text: Heart. Man runs, but God must impart grace.
An enlarged heart sometimes denotes the capacity of understanding, 3 Kings 4:29.
But it is a singular mercy of God to make us love his commands.

118:33.text: Always. Hebrew hekeb, means also, "for the reward," (Pagnin; Haydock; ver. 112.) or step by step.

118:34.text: Heart. Enable me to keep and to understand thy law, ver. 1, 2.
We must observe what we know, that we may receive greater lights.

118:36.text: Covetousness. Either of money, or any unlawful object, 1 Timothy 6:10. (Haydock) The word is very comprehensive.
Batsah is rendered mammon by the Chaldean.

118:37.text: Vanity. Idols, worldly prosperity, etc., (Psalm 36:1, 7.; (Calmet) shews, (St. Ambrose) and all dangerous objects.
We must pray that God will take away the occasions of sin, and help us to advance in virtue.

118:39.text: Reproach. Hidden sins of thought, (St. Ambrose) or the sarcasms of the Babylonians.
Sin is odious, because it is contrary to God's law, which is most delightful.
The saints have been often ridiculed, Jeremias 20:7., and Acts 26:24.

118:40.text: Quicken. The just prays for greater perfection, and for perseverance.

118:41.text: Salvation. The Messias, foretold by the prophets, (Genesis xlix., Romans 3:23., and Titus 2:13.; Berthier) or grace, freely promised to all who ask for it.

118:42.text: In any thing. Literally, "the word" that I, etc. Thus he stops their mouths, by professing his confidence in God.
The Babylonians insultingly ask, Where is their God? If thou free me from captivity, I may reply that my hopes were not vain.
This answer I will give boldly, if I obtain thy grace, which will strengthen my weakness.

118:43.text: Mouth. Let those who preach the truth, live up to it.
Though the psalmist may be under some fear, he prays that he may not omit to make open profession of his faith.

God never hinders his servants from doing this, though he sometimes suffers them to fall. But the Church will never cease to proclaim the truth. She will not give up one article of faith, though all heretics would thus embrace every other part of her doctrine. She will never open her bosom to those who would alter one point, nor say that such can enter heaven.

118:44.text: Ever. Hebrew, "and after." The sanction of the law will remain after this world is at an end. These expressions relate to the Church.

118:45.text: Large. The Jews could not practise the law out of their country, (Calmet) as to the ceremonial part.
He hopes to be soon set at liberty. The verb should be explained in the future, as St. Jerome has them, (Calmet) though this is immaterial.
David had already observed this line of conduct, which he resolved always to pursue, (Haydock) or he speaks in the person of all confessors, as he had not to be tried by any kings.
Those who suffer for the faith, receive great joy.

118:46.text: Of thy, (de, etc.) but the true reading is, in testimoniis, "conformably to," etc., as in the Hebrew, Septuagint, and Vulgate.

Ashamed. Thus many even of the female sex have professed their faith boldly, with St. Agnes, St. Catharine, St. Winefrid, etc., answering the reproachful objections that were put to them, as if it were dishonourable to be a Christian, Catholic or Papist. No. These are most glorious titles, importing the true service of Christ, in unity with the Catholic Church, and under the Vicar of Jesus Christ, the Pope.

But as Protestants mean an undue attachment to the Pope by the last title, we shew, that in that sense it is unjustly and illiberally applied to us, (Haydock) and no gentlemen or scholar would now make use of it.
See N. G.'s letters to J. Slack, Whitby, 1813.
Daniel spoke with great freedom to Nabuchodonosor, etc., Daniel 2:27., and 5:17., and 6:22.

118:48.text: Hands. To pray, labour, or rather to swear an eternal fidelity.
Confessors rejoice in meditating on God's words, which they shew forth in all their actions.

118:49.text: Mindful. He does not intimate that God can forget, but shews his fervour, (St. Augustine) and begs that he may be worthy to receive the effects of God's promises.
Though his decrees be most certain, means must be employed, which the just pray may not be wanting.

118:50.text: This hope, haec, though some would suppose it is put for hoc.
"This is my consolation in my distress."
Word. The expectation of thy promises has given me courage.

118:51.text: But I continued to observe thy law (Calmet) with patience, notwithstanding their provocations.

118:52.text: Of old. Upon the rebel angels, and sinful men, whom thou wilt punish for ever. This encourages me to adhere to the cause of virtue.
In many dangers our ancestors have been protected by God. Is his arm shortened, or are we no longer his people?

118:53.text: Fainting. So much was the psalmist grieved at the sight of sinners!
He would have died through zeal, if he had not seen God's justice.
Apostates particularly filled him with horror.

118:54.text: Song. Here on earth I am comforted with singing the praises of thy law, which makes the observers just.
The captives would not give holy things to dogs: but, among themselves, they sung canticles, Psalm cxxxvi.
These pious exercises were opposed to the scandalous discourses of sinners.

118:56.text: This. Haec facta est mihi. The feminine is put for the neuter, (Calmet) which the Hebrew have not.
We may also understand, This night of calamity, ver. 55.

118:57.text: Portion. With the just tending to perfection, I desire no other inheritance.
"He who possesses God, has all things."

118:59.text: But this is the true sense of the Hebrew, etc. The consciousness of having adhered to thy commands, makes me hope that thou wilt not abandon me.
I have bewailed my past transgressions, and am resolved henceforward to live piously.
I have reflected on my former actions, and taken up this firm determination, trusting in thy mercy, ver. 58.

118:60.text: And am. Hebrew, "and delay not." For cursed is he who does the work of God negligently, (Septuagint) or with deceit, Jeremias 48:10.

118:61.text: Cords. Temptations of the devil, (St. Augustine) and of the wicked, who strive by force, or by caresses, to ensnare the virtuous, ver. 110.

118:62.text: Midnight. Under tribulation, (St. Augustine) or to avoid the malice of those who pried into my conduct. I was so delighted with thy law, that I meditated on it both in the day-time, and at night.
This pious custom was observed by St. Paul, (Acts xvi.) as it is still by many religious orders, ver. 164.

118:63.text: Partaker. Hebrew, "a friend." I do not fear to approve of their conduct, (Calmet) and condole with them.
The true living members of Christ enjoy the great benefit of partaking in the prayers and good works of the whole Church militant and triumphant, in the communion of saints.

118:66.text: Hebrew, "wisdom," or discernment how to act, and knowledge of what regards thy law and revealed truths.

118:67.text: Humbled. St. Jerome, "before I heard, I was ignorant." He speaks of the gift of prophecy: or "before I was afflicted, I went astray."

118:67.src: Berthier; ver. 71., and Jeremias 10:24., and 31, 19.; Calmet

118:67.text: Before, I often fell; but sorrow has made me understand.

118:68.text: Goodness, or sweetness, denoting the unction of grace.

118:69.text: Iniquity. Hebrew, "the proud have forged lies against me," (Haydock) which was verified in Daniel, (Calmet) David, etc.

118:70.text: Curdled. Grown hard, like milk that is curdled and turned to cheese.
Hebrew, "like fat." They have no compassion or wisdom, Isaias 6:10.
Without the points, cheleb means also milk.
The wicked combine together for my ruin: but I will still be faithful.

118:71.text: Humbled me. "It is good for the proud to fall into some open sin, (Calmet) whence they may be displeased at themselves."
A patient thanks his physician for having used a necessary severity.

118:73.text: We may confidently pray to our Creator for light, (Worthington) and that he may perfect his work.

118:74.text: The angels rejoice at the conversion of a sinner, (Luke 15:10.) as the Church does at her children's progress.
All the just will be glad to see me delivered.

118:75.text: Truth. Though we may not discern the particular causes of our suffering, we must be convinced that they are right.
Sinners are afflicted that they may amend, and the just for their advancement.

118:76.text: Mercy. Jesus Christ, according to St. Jerome.

118:78.text: Ashamed. Literally, "confounded," not accomplishing their wicked designs.
Thus may they be induced to entertain that shame for sin which bringeth life, (Haydock) and cease to offend.
Let them be converted: though it be also (Haydock) lawful to desire that obstinate sinners may undergo just punishment.
The saints pray thus out of zeal for their good. It may also be a prediction, as the Hebrew word (Berthier) yebshu is in the future.
Done. Hebrew, "falsely perverted," (Montanus) in desire; (Calmet) or "having wished to pervert me without cause."
But to pervert, means also to treat ill; and hivvethuni has that sense here, (Haydock) according to the best authors.

118:79.text: Turn to me. Let the wicked (Worthington) among those who adore the true God, enter into themselves, that we may form a holy league to support each other.
May we all obtain our liberty, (Calmet) or may they imitate my constancy.

118:81.text: Salvation. All the saints sighed after our Saviour's coming, (Matthew 13:17.) as they still do, 2 Timothy 4:8.
The deliverance from Babylon was a figure of redemption. The next verse is of the same import.

118:83.text: Like a bottle in the frost. In the Hebrew, like a bottle in the smoke. That is, I am become, through my sufferings in this mortal pilgrimage, as a leathern bottle, shrunk up because of being exposed to the frost or smoke.
So the bodies of the just are exposed to mortification, that they may become like new bottles, capable of containing the new wine of the gospel doctrine, Matthew 9:17.
Goats' skins, with the hair inward, are still used in some places to contain liquors.
They were hung over the smoke, to make the wine old, or fit for drinking sooner. (Colum. 1:6.) Amphorae fumum bibere institutae, Consule Tullo.

118:84.text: Servant. The just may lawfully desire the term of their sufferings, with submission to God's will, (Worthington) and to be freed from the power of persecutors, (Hebrew, "the proud;" Berthier) and from exile, after their enemies are punished, Psalm 38:5.

118:85.text: Fables. Idle tales, not agreeable to God's law.
Such were the theology and histories of the pagans.
Such are still the false maxims of the world, and many books designed to corrupt the morals of the age.
The Jews confined themselves to their own divine books. Hebrew, Chaldean, etc., "The proud have dug pits for me, which is not conformable to thy law," (Calmet) but forbidden expressly.
The Septuagint seem to have followed a better reading.
Houbigant rejects the word pits, and in effect, we may understand the Hebrew in the sense of the Vulgate, "They have prepared (or told me) vain discourses."

118:86.text: Help me. He does not pray to be exempted from trials, but that he may have grace to gain the victory.

118:87.text: Earth. Babylon; (Calmet) though this is never styled simply the earth. It refers to all the enemies of salvation, particularly to our own passions.

118:89.text: In heaven. In the faithful angels, (St. Augustine) or Jesus Christ.
The promises seem not to have their effect here; but they will in heaven.
The word of God is as unchangeable as heaven.
The psalmist praises God's works, which observe the order established by him.
How then can we doubt but his law and promises shall be accomplished?

118:90.src: St. Hilary, St. Ambrose, etc.; Calmet
118:90.text: All. Literally, "unto generation and generation," which the fathers explain of the true faith, which has subsisted in the synagogue and in the Church of Christ alone.

Those who did not belong to the former, in the first ages of the world, might still form a part of the latter, like Job, etc. Without faith it is, and always was, impossible to please God, Hebrews 11:6.

118:91.text: Serve thee. Except man, (Worthington) and angels who rebel; and yet they also are forced to serve as victims of justice, if they have refused to accept of mercy.

118:92.text: Abjection. Man cannot rise from sin without grace, and attention to the law.
This supports him under the punishment of sin.
Meditation alleviates his grief, which might otherwise have proved mortal.

118:95.text: Understood. Or "shall understand." I am already able to encounter my enemies, Philippians 2:15.

118:96.text: All have their limits. But God's law bindeth for ever, as the rewards and punishments are eternal.
Broad. Charity, which extends both to God and our neighbour.

The law is above my comprehension, though I am able to discern its superiority over all created things. Some understand Jesus Christ, the end of the law; or martyrdom, the height of charity; or extreme distress, by the word perfection.
I have been exposed to the greatest misery: but thy law has set me at large, Sophonias 1:18.

118:97.text: Loved. Constraint takes away all merit.
Son, give me thy heart, Proverbs 23:26.
The just man wonders that he should feel such love, as he knows it is not of his own growth, but a gift of God.

118:98.text: Enemies of salvation, (Berthier) or the Babylonians, whom Daniel far surpassed; though we would not absolutely assert that it refers to him, Daniel 1:19., and 13:1., and Ezechiel 28:3.
David was no less enlightened (Berthier) by the prophetic spirit above his earthly instructor, how aged soever.

118:99.text: Teachers. Who do not follow the rules of virtue. The ancients, (ver. 100.) of the same description, must yield the palm to those who are less advanced in years, but more observant of God's law.

118:101.text: Every. We cannot follow both the broad and the narrow path.

118:103.text: Honey. St. Ambrose, etc., add, "and the honeycomb," Psalm 18:11. Homer (Iliad A.) compares the speech of Nestor with honey.

118:104.text: Hated. We must come to the practice of the law, (Calmet) and hate sin.
Iniquity. Hebrew, "lying." But every sin is contrary to truth.

118:105.text: Thy word. Jesus Christ, (St. Hilary) who enlightens every man, John 1.
Lamp, (2 Peter 1:19., and Proverbs 6:23.) while sin is the light of the wicked, Proverbs 21:4., and 24:20.
The law of God, proposed by his prophets and pastors, in the ordinary method of instruction.

118:106.text: Sworn. By the ceremony of circumcision, etc., as we now engage by vows in baptism, to observe all the commandments.
Those who receive not the sacrament, are still under a strict obligation to obey the truth; as all were created only for this purpose.
The psalmist means thus to express his firm determination to do all that God should require of him, (St. Augustine) and renews the solemn covenant entered into with the Deity by his ancestors.

118:107.text: Quicken. This petition, with the praise of the law, is the subject of this psalm.
All the godly must suffer, 2 Timothy 3:12.

118:108.text: Free offerings of praise and thanks, (St. Augustine) or the works of supererogation, (St. Hilary, Theodoret, etc.) which are not commanded, though acceptable to God, (Worthington) and undertaken for the sake of greater perfection.

The evangelical counsels of voluntary poverty, etc., are of this nature. Still we acknowledge that we are useless servants, (Luke 17:10.) with regard to God. We can give him nothing, which he has not first given us. Our piety will redound to our own advantage.
The psalmist may allude to his solemn engagement, (ver. 106.; Calmet) which he made with perfect freedom and willingness, though it was not a matter of choice.

118:109.text: My hands. In danger of being thrown by, or of falling, (Worthington) ready to appear before God's tribunal. I watch over myself, and strive to keep my soul tranquil. The prophet might have all this in view.

Most of the fathers read, "in thy hands," under God's protection. This is denied by St. Jerome (ad Sun.) though the commentary which goes under his name, and was written in the same age, has thy. The other reading is, however, more correct. The Greeks made use of a similar expression, to shew the danger of losing a treasure.
Such is our soul, which we must fear to lose.

118:110.text: Snare, ver. 67. In such dangers, I still remain faithful.

118:111.text: Inheritance. I will strive to imitate the faith of Abraham, etc.
I will stick to the law as to my portion, through pure love.

118:112.text: Inclined. He had said, (ver. 36.) incline, as the work proceeds from grace and free-will.

Reward. St. Jerome, "for the eternal reward." All agree, that the Hebrew may have this sense. He is influenced by hope, though the motive of charity is placed first. Whether both motives can produce the same act, is an useless inquiry. The Scriptures frequently propose reward, the second, though less excellent motive, Matthew 5:12., Genesis 15:1., and Romans 8:18.
This text evidently shews, that the keeping of the commandments merits a reward, for which we may labour.
Protestants evade this, by reading, "always, even unto the end;" because hekeb is ambiguous, and means also, the end.
"As if the Septuagint were not sufficient to determine the same....But....they are resolved to take their liberty, though contrary to St. Jerome, and the ancient Fathers."
God authorizes us to aim at the reward, though he would not have this to be the only motive, ver. 33.

118:113.text: Unjust. Inasmuch as they oppose thy law.
So Christ orders us to hate our parents, when they are an obstacle to our salvation. We must love their persons and welfare, (St. Augustine) but hate their iniquity.
Hebrew, "the turbulent." St. Jerome, "vain thoughts," and inconstant men. The meaning of sehaphim is not well ascertained.
But the psalmist might have all these senses (Haydock) in view, as they are all good; and hence we may admire the copiousness of the Hebrew language.

118:114.text: Helper. Hebrew, "asylum and buckler." We must keep in his presence, (Haydock) and avoid the society of the wicked, if we would search the law, ver. 115.

118:116.text: Live. St. Augustine (tr. 124. in Joan.) beautifully describes the life of the just here and in heaven.

We may thus pray for grace, and spiritual life with confidence, if we be resolved to keep the commandments. Yet we must not fail to entreat God not to suffer us to be confounded, which will not be the case, if we entertain hope, and charity be poured on our hearts, Romans 5:5.

118:119.text: Prevaricators. For though they be not acquainted with revelation, (Haydock) they have the natural law written in their hearts by God, Romans 2:12.
There is no such thing as the philosophic sin, (Berthier) which abstracts from every offence of the Deity, though it be allowed to be contrary to reason.

Septuagint seem to have read differently essbothi, "I have," etc., instead of hishbatta, "thou hast destroyed." Sigim, scorias, may have been sugim in their copies, though both may be derived from sug, "he departed."
"Thou hast accounted as dross, or froth," etc.
Calmet says, that St. Jerome and Hebrew read in the first person, which is inaccurate.
The just entertain the same sentiments of the wicked as God does.

118:120.text: Afraid. Servile fear is therefore profitable, though perfect charity expel it, and move us to do well for the love of God, 1 John 4:
Yet fear sometimes returns, that the just may not give way to presumption.

118:121.text: Justice. This he declares out of zeal, praying to be freed from calumniators, (Worthington) particularly the devil, Apocalypse 12:9. The Babylonians probably laid falsehoods to the charge of the Jews, in order to oppress them, as they frequently accused Daniel.

118:122.text: Uphold. Hebrew, "answer for," (St. Jerome) as a bondsman.
Unto good. Thus the law is insinuated, though it is not here expressed.
In attacking the devil, who is so experienced, we must take God with us, or we shall surely be overcome.

118:123.text: Salvation. The Messias, (St. Hilary; ver. 41.) or liberty, ver. 82. I have been fatigued with looking up to heaven for aid, like a woman who looks for the return of her husband to port.

118:124.text: Mercy. When we have been most diligent, there still remains much to be done, and in many things we offend.

118:126.text: To do. Our best, since so many prove rebellious, (Hebrews 4:11., and Ecclesiasticus 5:8.; Berthier) or we stand in the utmost need of the Messias, since even the Jews give erroneous explanations of the law.
Hebrew, etc., "it is time for the Lord to act," by punishing the guilty; or, "to act for the Lord," faciendi Domino, by striving to repair the injuries done to his name and worship.

118:127.text: Topaz. Hebrew paz, which denotes "the purest gold" of Phison, Genesis 2:11.
The topaz was discovered only in the reign of Ptolemy, father of Philadelphus.
Yet St. Jerome and others have the topaz, (Berthier) which St. Ambrose represents as the finest of precious stones.
The more the law is despised by the wicked, the more are the just in love with it.

118:128.text: Pagnin, "I judged all, yea all thy precepts to be right."
The hatred of the wicked made the love of the beauty of the law increase in my breast.

118:129.text: Wonderful. Under the letter, I discovered many mysteries, which makes me study them diligently.
God's works and laws deserve our most serious attention.

118:130.text: Declaration. By God's ministers renders them intelligible, though so wonderful in themselves. Hebrew styles this "the door of opening."

The Scriptures are full of difficulties, and Daniel 9:2. made them his study. Since the coming of Christ, the mysteries and prophecies have been more developed. But those who refuse to acknowledge him, grope at mid-day, Matthew 11:25., and 18:5., and 1 Corinthians 14:20.
The first entrance into the knowledge of holy Scripture illuminates the understanding of the humble, whence they proceed to know more.

118:131.text: Panted. Like one afflicted with the asthma, (Berthier) or most eager to obtain something.
He asked and obtained the holy Spirit, (St. Augustine) enabling him to understand the law, (Haydock) and to comply with it. See Ezechiel 3:2., and Psalm lxxx 11.

118:132.text: Judgment. Or custom, (Calmet) as thou art wont to treat such.
Let us not be looked upon as criminals.
The just are persuaded, that God will not reject the humble, (Berthier) according to the opinion (sententiam) of, etc.

118:134.text: Calumnies. That they may not cause me to abandon virtue.
A person must be well grounded not to yield on such occasions, when he is exposed to ridicule, etc., ver. 122.
Even the psalmist dreaded this situation.

118:135.text: Shine. (ver. 33.) Let me never go astray.

118:136.text: Law. This shews that David composed this psalm, as he fell by seeing Bethsabee.
They. Men. (Duport.) Septuagint, "I." But this is unnecessary. The eyes and all our senses lead to our ruin, Romans 7:18, 24.
True repentance requires lamentation, as well as a firm purpose of amendment, (Worthington) commissa diluere et abluta non iterare.

118:137.text: Right. He therefore makes people just indeed, and does not barely impute justice to them.

118:138.text: Truth. Thy laws are just and true in all respects, though we may not be able to discern it always, Romans 9:20., and 11:33.
God punishes the wicked in his justice, and rewards the just in his truth, or according to his promises.

118:139.text: My. Septuagint, "thy zeal." The just are animated with the zeal of God, like St. Paul. The enemies here mentioned were the Israelites, who attacked David, and not the Babylonians, who never knew God's law, or apostate captives, since we do not find that they attempted to injure Daniel, etc.
Many, however, both at Ninive and Babylon prevaricated, Tobias 1:12.

118:140.text: Pure as any thing which has passed through the fire.
"It consumes sin, and enlightens the penitent."

118:141.text: Young. The Fathers explain this of David, who was preferred before his brothers; and of the Gentiles, who were chosen by Jesus Christ.
It may refer to Daniel, who was enabled to prophesy very soon, or to the disciples, for whom this psalm was composed, (Calmet) whether Solomon, or any of the faithful.
The just are often judged to be ignorant by the worldly wise; but they follow the law, the true wisdom.

118:143.text: Trouble. Such is the portion of the just, Romans 5:3.
The sense is the same. Septuagint generally give the former meaning, as they render by seeking what moderns would restrain to signify observing, ver. 145, etc.

118:144.text: Live. In justice, as thy law enjoins.

118:145.text: Cried. With requisite fervour, (Worthington) and attention.

118:147.text: The. Literally, "in maturity." Some think that we should read immaturitate, aoria, (St. Augustine) "in the dead of the night." The psalmist not only rose at midnight, but before sun-rise to meditate.
Kimchi says, he only indulged sleep the first three hours. But others believe, that he arose in the third and last watch, ver. 148.
He got up before the ordinary time, and prayed with earnestness.

118:147.src: St. Chrysostom, ser. 42. ad pop.; Berthier
118:147.text: St. Ambrose encourages people to come early to the church, to offer the first-fruits of their hearts, and voice to God; (Calmet) and St. Augustine informs us, that such was the practice of St. Monica. (Confessions 9:7.) You are not in a higher station than the holy king who said, I rose, etc., ver. 22.

118:148.text: Morning. Both night and morning, I prevented the usual hours of prayer.
To thee, is not in Hebrew, "my eyes prevented the watches."

118:150.text: Law. There is no medium between faith and infidelity. If we do not observe the law, we sin. Not to advance is to go back.
Shall we hesitate whether to follow the narrow or the broad road?

118:151.text: Near. To reward or punish. We wander from thee, yet there is no place between.
God is ever ready to hear our just requests.
His law may be easily known, Deuteronomy 30:11.

118:152.text: Ever. God's law is always the same in substance.
That of Moses receives its perfection in Christianity.
Though after this life we can no longer observe them, the reward of our past virtue will remain for ever.

118:153.text: Humiliation. Or "humility," as St. Augustine understands it of that virtue.

118:155.text: Sinners. Such cannot expect to be liberated.

Yet, as the captivity was fixed for seventy years, and many who were almost ignorant of the law, and had married strange wives, returned, this verse overturns that system, and shews that eternal salvation is meant.

118:157.text: Many. The Babylonians on one hand, and false brethren on the other, attack me: but I am grieved most to see God offended.
All the earth is stained with the blood of martyrs, whom the Church honours with festivals, and whose intercession heals many sick.

118:158.text: Transgressors. Septuagint, "fools," asunetountas. If o were placed before e, the exact meaning of the Hebrew would be preserved, though the wicked are often styled fools.
The prevaricating Jews are here designated, ver. 136.

118:159.text: Behold. A person may profess his innocence without arrogance.

118:160.text: Truth. Hence all such commandments are immutable.
The threats of God have been put in execution, and we cannot doubt but the promised liberation will soon take place.

118:161.text: Princes. Of darkness, or the chiefs of the Philistines, etc.
Daniel was much exposed to the fury of the Babylonian princes, but he was more afraid of the terrors of the law, (Leviticus xxvi.) than of all that they could do against him.
Thus the martyrs despised the threats of tyrants.
Cause. The powerful men of this world have no just reason to persecute the just, nor can they make them abandon virtue.

St. Basil answered Valens with great intrepidity, shewing how little he apprehended what the emperor could take from him, as we read in St. Gregory of Nazianzus (or. 20.) who says, (or. 6.) let us fear only one thing, which is, to fear any person more than God.

118:162.text: Spoil. Having just mentioned fear; lest any should think that he entertained any secret dislike for the law; he adds, that it gives him more content than the greatest treasures or conquests can the miser or the hero, ver. 14, 72, and 127.
He rejoiceth thus in keeping the commandments, how difficult soever they may be.

118:163.text: Iniquity. Hebrew, "lying." The Babylonians have attempted to draw me over to their false religion; but I perceive its vanity, (Calmet) and stick closer to the truth, (Haydock; ver. 85.) and to thy holy law.

118:164.text: Seven. Often, (Worthington) as the word signifies, Proverbs 24:16., et.

Yet here it may determine the precise number, as the Church seems to have taken it, by instituting the seven canonical hours of the day, and matins and lauds for the night, in imitation of the psalmist.

R. Solomon understands it literally, prescribing prayer twice before the reading of the decalogue, and once after in the morning; and in the afternoon, twice before and after the same lecture. The Church has enjoined matins to be said at night, lauds in the morning, prime, tierce, sext, none, vespers and complin, in the course of the day.
This ecclesiastical office consists of hymns, psalms, etc.

Against it some have risen up, particularly against that part which was said in the night, pretending that God had made the night for rest; and hence they were called nuctazontes, or "drowsy" heretics.

118:164.src: St. Basil, reg. fus. 37.; St. Gregory, dial. 3:14.; Ven. Bede, Hist. 4:7., etc.
118:164.text: St. Jerome styles Vigilantius Dormitantius, for the same reason; as if it were better to sleep than to watch. Wycliff (Wald. iii. Tit. 3:21.) and Luther have oppugned the same holy practice, though it be so conformable to Scripture and to the fathers.

St. Clement, as many suppose, (Worthington) or at least some author before the fourth century, (Haydock) explains the reason why we should pray at these set times; but cautions us not to join with heretics, neither in the Church nor at home.

For what society is there between light and darkness? (2 Corinthians vi.) St. Cyprian, (or. Dom.) St. Jerome, (ep. ad Eust.) and St. Augustine (ser. 55. de Temp.) mention several of these hours, and exhort the faithful to be diligent in attending these public prayers.

118:165.text: Peace, in their own conscience, (Worthington) and prosperity, without fear of danger. Not even death can disturb those who belong to God, Romans 8:38.
But all things work together for their good, Romans 8:28.
The perfect are not moved to imitate bad example, and the weak are not excused for so doing, as they ought to be constant.
The virtuous man is neither scandalized at the law, though he may not understand it perfectly, nor at the fall of those who had been most eminent for sanctity.

118:166.text: He is actuated by real charity.

118:168.text: Sight, whom I would not offend.
The presence of God ought to deter us most effectually from sin.

118:169.text: Supplication. Hebrew, "cry." Eusebius reads axioma, "dignity," which St. Ambrose thinks has been substituted for axiosis, "prayer;" though all our copies of the Septuagint have deesis, (Calmet) a word of the same import.

118:171.text: Like a spring sending forth a copious stream.

118:173.text: Save me. Though a person be endued with grace, he requireth more grace to resist temptations.

118:175.text: Live in grace and glory. To serve God, it was not requisite that one should be at Jerusalem. Daniel, Tobias, etc., were saints elsewhere.
Yet it was more easy to worship God in his temple, where every thing moved to piety; (Haydock) and this the psalmist desires, deeming it a new life.

118:176.text: All men have been involved in sin, and Christ came to save them; but only such as keep the law will obtain salvation.

The conclusion contains a confession of misery in the name of all, and an allusion to Christ, the good shepherd. The prophet admits that notwithstanding his love of the law, he may have still many ways transgressed; and that without faith in the Messias, he could not belong to his fold, or escape the many dangers of the world, 1 Corinthians 4:4., and 9:27.
Seek. St. Hilary and some Greek copies have read incorrectly "quicken."

119:1.text: A gradual canticle. The following psalms, in number fifteen, are called gradual psalms or canticles, from the word gradus, signifying steps, ascensions, or degrees; either because they were appointed to be sung on the fifteen steps, by which the people ascended to the temple; or that in the singing of them the voice was to be raised by certain steps or ascensions: or that they were to be sung by the people returning from their captivity, and ascending to Jerusalem, which was seated amongst mountains. The holy Fathers, in a mystical sense, understand these steps, or ascensions, of the degrees by which Christians spiritually ascend to virtue and perfection; and to the true temple of God in the heavenly Jerusalem.
Both these last interpretations seem more plausible and literal, as given by St. Chrysostom, etc.
The allusion to the steps of the temple (Ezechiel xl.) is very uncertain, as well as the raising of the voice in higher notes during each psalm.
They might be sung on a pulpit, 2 Esdras 9:4., and 2 Paralipomenon 20:19.
The authors seem to have lived at the close of the captivity, (Calmet) though David might well compose these canticles during some of his trials, or foreseeing this event.
They contain a consoling assurance of mankind's redemption, prefigured by the liberation of the Jews, and also that the power and fury of persecutors shall cease.
Shir, hamahaloth, may denote a very excellent canticle.
Trouble. No time is more proper for prayer.
Heard. I am encouraged by past experience to hope for redress.

119:2.text: Tongue. From the Babylonians, who seek to delude me, (Calmet) and from detraction, which is most dangerous.

119:3.text: Added. This is an usual form of denouncing vengeance, Ruth 1:17. The Babylonians are threatened with God's judgments, ver. 4. Some place these words in the mouth of God, answering the captives. How shall you be screened from the shafts of detraction? Fear not. The sharp, etc.
What punishment is great enough for this sin?

119:4.text: Waste. Hebrew, "of juniper" or thorn trees, Job 30:4. The former is said to retain its heat a long time, and the latter is easily inflamed, Psalm 117:12. Such fiery weapons have been often used, Psalm 7:14., and 75:3. Spiculaque et multa crinitum missile flamma.
How will God punish detraction? He will hurl his darts against the guilty, Habacuc 3:11.
This is their reward, (Berthier) and what they deserve.
Charity and good example will best counteract their baneful influence.

119:5.text: But Houbigant rejects this as a place unknown; and the word may have the former signification, given by the Septuagint and St. Jerome.

Moses speaks of Meshec, (Genesis 10:2.) or of the mountains separating Cholcis from Armenia, where the Jews might be dispersed, (4 Kings 17:23., and 1 Esdras 2:59., and 8:15.) as well as in Cedar, or Arabia Petrea, (Isaias 42:11.) where the Saracens afterwards inhabited, according to St. Jerome.
Inhabitants. Hebrew, "tents," in which the people chiefly dwelt.

From Cedar, the son of Ismael, sprung Mahomet, whose tyranny has been long felt. Cedar denotes the "darkness" of sin and error. The Jews bewailed their absence from the temple, and Christians their being unable to meet for the divine worship, and their banishment (Worthington) from heaven.

119:7.text: Peaceable. Hebrew, "I spoke peaceable, and they warlike things.
Literally, "I was peace, and when I spoke, they flew to war."
Cause. This is easily understood from the context, (Berthier) though not expressed in the original.

120:1.text: Canticle. David wrote this during his flight from Absalom; (Grotius) and de Muis judges from the martial air, that it was composed in the midst of danger. It relates to the captives, (Origen; Calmet) and to all in the pilgrimage of this world.
Mountains. Jerusalem, and heaven, whence all our help must come. God most readily hears the prayers which are poured forth in places appointed by him.
Jerusalem was situated among mountains, and the Jews turned towards it in prayer, Daniel 6:10. They did not depend on human aid, Jeremias 3:22.

120:3.text: May. Hebrew, "he will not." Many have read in the second person, both in the Hebrew and Septuagint, "Suffer not thy," etc.

120:4.text: These figurative expressions shew that God will never cease to protect his people.

120:6.text: Night. Neither prosperity nor adversity shall hurt thee, (St. Jerome) or the Church.

Cold is said to burn or parch up, Genesis 31:40. Justin (2) writes of the Scythians, Quanquam continuis frigoribus urantur, as the effects of extreme heat and cold are similar. The Jews were protected from both at their return; (Isaias 4:6., and 49:10.; Calmet) though we may doubt of this explanation, as the text is applied to those in heaven, Apocalypse 7:16.

120:7.text: Keepeth. Hebrew also, "shall or may." The words of a prophet are always true, and the tenses are varied at pleasure by St. Jerome, etc.

120:8.text: Coming in. Hebrew has, "going out," first.
This expression denotes all the occurrences of life, Deuteronomy 28:6.
We may discover a beautiful progression in this psalm; God protects us from each and from every danger.
He is not like earthly friends, who have not always the will or the power to do it.

121:1.text: Canticle. Hebrew, Chaldean, and Syriac add, "of David," (Calmet) who saw in spirit the glory of the temple under Solomon, or the return of the captives, and the felicity of souls in heaven.
What is said of the earthly Jerusalem, is beautifully applied to heaven by St. Augustine, etc. The captive Levites might write this psalm.
Lord. Many prophets assured the Jews of their speedy deliverance, as preachers still set before the people the joys of heaven; all which filled the psalmist with rapture.
The motive for this joy is disinterested and edifying. The captives had begged for redress in the former psalms.
Before they had been chastised, they profane the temple.

121:2.text: Were. Hebrew also, "shall," etc. Yet many of the Levites had officiated in the temple, 1 Esdras 3:12.
Courts, or gates, where justice was administered.
We may better (Haydock) rejoice in the Church, (Worthington) and in the prospect of heaven.
"At the news, it seemed as if our feet had been," etc.

121:3.text: Together. Well built and inhabited. The Jews throughout the world considered it as their most dear country.
The participation of spiritual graces (Psalm 118:63., etc.) is a great comfort to Catholics, (Worthington) who look upon the chair of St. Peter at Rome as the centre of unity.
David established order in Jerusalem, when he had made the conquest, and various families contributed to the rebuilding of it.
All the tribes were united under the dominion of David.

121:4.text: The. This was the testimony, (Haydock) or ordinance of the Lord, (Calmet) which Israel had solemnly engaged to perform.
All were obliged to repair thither thrice in the year; (Exodus 23:17.; Menochius) and this contributed most to the splendour of the city.
Houbigant would translate "the congregations of Israel;" or juxta may be understood, "according to the testimony."
The tribes shall be all united, (Calmet) and come to Jerusalem, as all mankind should embrace the true faith in the Catholic Church.

121:5.text: The Jews made their sanhedrim superior to the kings, as the Chaldean here insinuates. But the text rather means that both the ecclesiastical and civil courts shall be re-established, and bring an immense concourse of people to the city.
The Church is empowered to pass sentence, (Worthington) and the apostles shall judge both men and angels.

121:6.text: Pray. Septuagint, "ask Jerusalem for," etc., (Haydock) as if she were to give it.
And may there be abundance. Hebrew, "those who love thee shall be at rest."
Christ encourages us to ask; as Catholics exhort each other to pray for the Church.

121:7.src: St. Chrysostom, de Laud. S. Paul. iii.
121:7.text: He insists so much on the blessing of peace, because he foresaw that Jerusalem would one day neglect it, Luke 19:42. Charity dwells in the towers or saints, (Berthier) and makes us resemble God.

121:8.text: 1:Hebrew adds, "I will now;" (Montanus) or rather, "I have now spoken."

I was forbidden to beg for the peace of Chanaan, Deuteronomy 7:3. But Jeremias (xxix. 7.) exhorts the captives to desire this blessing for the city, to which they were going, as it would redound to their own advantage.
Christ leaves his peace to his apostles, and to their successors; and in heaven, all good thing are prepared for the elect.

122:1.text: Syriac also attributes it to him, though Zorobabel, etc., might recite it at their return. It may also refer to the captives groaning under oppression, (Calmet) and to every Christian, who must live in expectation of a better country.

122:2.text: Masters. Expecting liberty, or rather food; though it may also imply that they are ready to run at the first sign, which they observe with attention. Thus Menelaus had his eyes on Agamemnon.
As servants, and particularly handmaids, are very attentive, and hope to receive sustenance, so we ought to pray with all earnestness to God for what is necessary.
Until. Or "waiting for his having mercy on us." We shall not cease to look up to Him afterwards.
"Take care not to turn thine eyes away from mine."

122:4.text: A reproach. Hebrew, "with the reproach of those at ease," etc., (Haydock) or "let reproach fall upon," etc. We are treated with too much scorn, Lamentations 3:30.
Yet God will not permit his friends to be overcome, but encourages them to hope for speedy redress, when their sufferings are great.

123:1.text: Canticle. Hebrew, etc., with some Latin copies add, "of David," (Calmet) who might write it after being delivered from some danger. It may also be applicable to the martyrs, and to all who have been freed from temptation.
The captives might compose it in thanksgiving for the leave to return, (Calmet) or when they had been delivered from the assaults of the neighbouring nations.

123:3.text: Perhaps. This word is here affirmative. Hebrew, "Then." Septuagint, "Surely."

He modestly leaves it to others to judge what would have been the event if God had not sent help. The weak would have been destroyed, as soon as if they had become a prey to wild beasts, as Jonas was swallowed up.

123:5.text: Insupportable. Without bottom, or beyond our strength, anupostaton.
Hebrew, "Then the swelling waters it had passed over our soul."
St. Jerome has, "perhaps they," etc., which is more correct. See Psalm 68:3.
A torrent implies sudden great troubles.

123:7.text: Sparrow. Hebrew, "bird." This comparison shews at once the dangers to which the Jews had been exposed, and their miraculous deliverance.
Men may deceive others: but they cannot impose on God.
Grace preserves the soul from the most imminent dangers of temptation, sin, etc.
We must therefore fly; but who will give us wings except God?

124:1.text: Canticle of thanksgiving, relating to the times of the Messias, (Abenezra) or to the Jews, who overcame the attempts of the nations at their return, 2 Esdras iv., and vi.
It exhorts all to confide in God.
Trust. The Jews complied not with this condition, and are become vagabonds; but the faithful inherit this promise, (Hebrews 10:19.; Berthier) which is verified in the Catholic Church.
To be secure, like Sion, which is defended by other mountains, we must belong to her society.

The situation of Jerusalem was very advantageous. It was difficult to bring any cavalry against it, except by the way of Idumea. A handful of men might defend the passes leading to the north, as we see in the history of the Machabees.

124:2.text: About it. Coming from Joppe, travellers cannot see the city till they are very near it, though with respect to Judea, it is very elevated. Hence Josephus styles it "the navel of the land."
The construction of the Vulgate is very natural.
For the promise regards the inhabitants, rather than the place, as Hebrew would insinuate.
Lord. Zacharias 2:5. Heresiarchs have in vain risen up against the Church, though they were able men, like mountains.

124:3.text: Rod. Sceptre, or violent dominion, Isaias 10:5.
That, Or "therefore." (Interp. in St. Chrysostom) God always concludes his threats with promises of pardon to the penitent, 1 Corinthians 10:13. (Calmet
He tries his servants for their good, and will not abandon them.

The captivity lasted only 70 years: the ten persecutions of the Christian Church were terminated in 300 years; and though the wicked should even put the just to death, then their rod remains no longer upon them. If the virtuous were always suffering, the temptation might be too strong. But those who are sincere, at last emerge from the cloud, and force their enemies to applaud their conduct.

124:4.text: Heart. This God will certainly perform.
He asks not for himself alone, and leaves all to the divine disposal.

124:5.text: Bonds, obligationes. "Knots," as straggalias, also means, (Berthier) rather than duties.
Some suspect that obliquationes was put originally, as Hebrew means "crooked ways," (Calmet) "embarrassments."

He may allude to the dark machinations of false brethren, who endeavoured to thwart the pious designs of Nehemias, 6:14. Apostles shall be treated like infidels, (Calmet) or rather worse, as we shall be if we act not up to the lights, (Haydock) and graces which we have received.
Those who enter into any covenant, etc., to uphold a false religion, though they may despise it in their hearts, must expect to be punished, while the Church shall have peace.
Israel. St. Paul adds, of God, to shew who may be entitled to this blessing.
Heretics can neither give nor receive this peace.

125:1.text: Sion. It cannot be doubted but this regards the captives of Babylon: but still David might compose it, as he was a prophet; and herein the redemption of mankind may also be described.
The captives pray for the return of the rest of their brethren.
They could hardly believe their own eyes, like St. Peter, Acts 12:9. This extraordinary joy is felt by devout souls, when freed from sin.
The Greek cities which the Romans declared free, could scarcely believe that they had understood the herald. Majus gaudium fuit, etc.
Chaldean, "we were like convalescents," which comes nearer to the sense of the Septuagint.

125:2.text: Shall. Or "did;" (Calmet) though the future is here well employed.
The prophet uses both tenses, shewing the certainty of the event.
It would require some time before the Gentiles would become sufficiently acquainted with the concerns of the Jews.
As soon as they did, they expressed their admiration, while the former were careful not to imitate the conduct of those who murmured at leaving Egypt.

125:4.text: South. As the Egyptians hope for the overflowing of the Nile; (Hammond) or as the south wind melts the snow, so as to make the Jordan overflow its banks.
The return of our brethren will be as agreeable to us as water to a thirsty soil.
Make them come quickly, and in great numbers, Isaias 60:3., and 66:12.
Esdras brought back some, and Nehemias others, from Babylon.
The ten tribes returned from Assyria later, and by degrees, (Calmet, Diss.) if at all. Those who arrived first at Jerusalem pray for the rest.
The prophet foreseeing this event, desireth its perfect and speedy accomplishment, (Worthington) though it were scarcely to be expected, no more than (Haydock) a copious torrent in the south.

125:5.text: Joy. This was the case of the martyrs, etc., (Luke 6:21., and John 16:20.) as well as of the captives, Jeremias 31:9., Isaias 66:10., and Baruch 5:6.
Tribulation commonly attends the virtuous in this life. Their reward is reserved for the next.
Sowing, we know not whether we shall ever reap.
This is a sort of proverb, which is applied to the captives.

125:6.text: They. Hebrew, "he," which must be taken distributively for all.

126:1.text: Of. Or "for" Solomon. This word is not in the Septuagint.
Some suppose that David put the psalm into his hands, to teach him that all depends on God.
He was to undertake various important works during his reign, (Haydock) particularly the temple, at the dedication of which this might be sung.
The chiefs of the captives might also appropriate it to their use, (Berthier) when they were rebuilding the temple, 2 Esdras iv., and vi.
It seems to refer to the times of the Messias.
House, or temple, and grant children, Exodus 1:21., and Genesis 30:2. Without God's assistance, all your endeavours to rebuild the temple and city will prove fruitless.
It. Nehemias had ordered the citizens to watch the attempts of Sanaballat.
But still depended more on Providence than on his own industry.
David, Solomon, Esdras, etc., may have held this language.
Yet inactivity is not encouraged. We must labour, and still expect success from God alone.
God must be the principal agent, (Worthington) and all the glory must be given to Him.

126:2.text: Light. That is, your early rising, your labour and worldly solicitude, will be vain, that is, will avail you nothing, without the light, grace, and blessing of God.
Nehemias divided the people into companies, to prevent their being too much fatigued.
Without light it is impossible for man to work, John 9:4.
The labours of those who live by the robbery of the distressed, are vain.
Rise ye, is not in Hebrew.

Sitten. Allow yourselves proper time for rest, after your labours and sorrows: for his beloved, whom he favours with his grace, shall sleep and rest under his wing, and yet abound with offspring, and all blessings.
Sorrow. St. Jerome, "of idols." This worship of God is odious.

Yet some explain the Hebrew in the plural, as it is applicable to all the people. The Jews were under great alarms: but were encouraged to hope that God would protect them, and give them a numerous progeny; though, as the country was probably never so well peopled as under Solomon, this may rather refer to the elect, who after the sleep of death (Berthier) shall behold those whom Christ shall acknowledge for his children, (Haydock) and obtain an eternal reward.

Whatever people may think they have done well, without God's grace, is all useless, and they must begin again; whereas those who are guided by it, perform all with as much ease as they would sleep, and yet merit a reward, which is promised in heaven to the true children who are born to God in the Catholic Church.

126:4.text: As arrows, etc. The offspring which God shall give his servants, that have been shaken and tossed about, (as the children of Israel were in their captivity) shall be like arrows in the hand of the mighty, which shall prosper and do great execution.
The patient sufferer will obtain a glorious recompense.
Such may be able to assist their aged parents, whereas those who are born in their old age must frequently be left orphans, (Haydock) and distressed.

Yet the Hebrew may well admit the sense of the Vulgate, as children will be more animated to revenge the wrongs of those, from whom they have received their life. The Jews are here represented in the state of persecution.

126:5.text: Desire. Who has as many children as he could wish. Hebrew, "his quiver." They are like arrows for his defence.
The Septuagint may have put the thing signified instead of the figure; or ashpatho may not be confined to the formal sense.
He shall. Hebrew, "they," father and son: yet the Chaldean, etc., retain he.

The children of Christ, and of his martyrs, who have been persecuted, are declared to be very powerful protectors; (Berthier) while such as imitate them, (Haydock) shall easily answer the objections which shall be put to them at the day of judgment, and be happy.
Gate. The enemies' envoys were not admitted into the city, 4 Kings 18:17.
In law-suits, children will prove excellent assistants.

127:1.text: Canticle. Being a sequel to the former [psalm], (Calmet) and similar to the 111th, as both promise felicity to the captives at their return, if they prove faithful, 2 Esdras 9:38.
It is a sort of epithalamium.
Both temporal and spiritual blessings are set before us.
Ways. Many saints have not received temporal rewards; and this confirms our faith that there is a world to come.
In effect, no temporal advantages are here specified, as they are below.

127:2.text: Hands. The idle are not entitled to blessings, which God will bestow on the industrious who fear him, preserving them from evils, Leviticus 26:16., and Deuteronomy 28:30.
Some have explained karpon, "fruits:" though it here means hands.

127:3.text: Sides. Against which vines were planted.

The married people who fear God, shall commonly have a numerous posterity; or their souls shall produce many good works in the Church, which springs from our Saviour's side. Children denote such good works.

127:5.text: Sion. Where he displayed his bounty.
Mayst thou live happily in the holy city, after thy long captivity.

127:6.text: Israel. This is best secured by a well-regulated and numerous people.
The good works, performed on earth, will be rewarded in heaven.
We shall rejoice in the spiritual children, whom we shall have begotten.

128:1.text: Canticle. In which David, (Haydock) or the Jews, at their return, after they had got the better of their enemies, render thanks to God.
It may suit the Church, Jesus Christ, and every person: as none can escape trouble.
Expugnaverunt might seem to imply that they had obtained the victory. But this was not the case, at least eventually, though the people of God might sometimes be oppressed, and yield to sin.
Youth. Since the Israelites left Egypt, Osee 2:15., and Jeremias 2:2.
From the beginning, the just Abel, Seth, Abraham, etc., have been persecuted.

128:2.text: But. Or "for," etenim. On this account they repeated their attacks.
But the psalmist testifies that they will not succeed.

128:3.text: Back. Hebrew, "labourers have laboured on my neck," (St. Jerome) or "back." They have made me bear the yoke, or have ploughed up my back. This proverbial expression shews the cruelty of the Babylonians, (Calmet) and of the enemies of Christ, (Isaias 50:6.) and the martyrs.
Hebrew charash, means also to work like a blacksmith, Genesis 4:22.
The Church bears patiently all crosses. Sinners build on her, or even on God's back, when they go on in their wicked ways, presuming that they will be saved at last by the sacraments, etc.

128:4.text: Necks. Hebrew, "collars," (Theodotion) "snares," (Symmachus) or "bands," with which they have oppressed us. Cyrus abandoned the Babylonians to be slaves of those Persians who had taken them, and made them till the land, etc.

128:5.text: God will cover the presumptuous with eternal confusion, so that none shall bless them, ver. 8.

128:6.text: Houses. Which were flat, so that grass might grow, but the heat of the climate would not suffer it to come to perfection.

Chaldean, "flourish," as some copies of the Septuagint read. Yet Hammond, etc., declare for our version. The precise import of the Hebrew is not known. The same comparison occurs, (4 Kings 19:26.; Calmet) and Plautus says, Qualis solstitialis herba paulisper fui, etc.

128:8.text: Upon you. Chaldean adds, "and they did not reply."
The custom of blessing reapers continued in the days of St. Augustine, Ruth 2:4.
The wicked render themselves incapable of deriving benefit from the good wishes of others.

129:1.text: Canticle. David might compose it after his sin, though it might suit the captives, and all sinners, as well as the souls in purgatory.
It has long been recited in their behalf.
Depths of the prison of expiation, or from this vale of misery, (Berthier) captivity, (Calmet) and from the bottom of my heart.

129:3.text: It. Hebrew, "who shall stand upright, (Calmet) or make opposition."
We all stand in need of mercy, as none can stand before the rigours of divine justice. (Worthington) Si quoties homines peccant, sua fulmina mittat Jupiter, exiguo tempore inermis erit.

129:4.text: Law. That promises of pardon contained therein.
Hebrew is now different from what the ancient interpreters read.
Symmachus and Theodotion agree with us.

129:5.text: Word. And promises that the captivity should end, (Calmet) and sin be remitted.

129:6.text: From. Or Hebrew, "more than the morning watch; yea, more than the morning watch." I expect my deliverance with greater eagerness than sentinels do the return of morning. All the day and night long I am filled with these sentiments.
The hope of penitents resembles the watches of the day, which are more comfortable than those of the night.

129:7.text: Redemption. Our Saviour affords the greatest consolation.
He will save the people, Matthew 1:21., and 1 John 2:2.

130:1.text: Of David, is not in Septuagint. But he probably composed this psalm to exculpate himself from the accusation of pride.
It may agree with Esther, Nehemias, etc., 2 Esdras 5:15.
David proposes his own humility to the imitation of others, without any evil intention.
Sometimes the saints may speak their own praises, as St. Paul, did, particularly when they are inspired.
Above me. This deportment is admirable in the great.
We must neither undertake nor pry into things above our ability.

130:2.text: So reward, etc. The meaning is, that according to his disposition with regard to humility, so he expected a reward in his soul to return to him, and stick as close to him as the child just weaned, which would willingly never be separated from the mother.

If I was not humble, may my soul be treated like an infant, etc. Hebrew, "If I did not render (or humble and silence) my soul, like an infant weaned from his mother, let my soul be to me as a weaned child." I willingly submit to all thy rigours, if I did not adore thy ways in silence and in humiliation.
The text may be differently rendered.
As children come willingly to their mother, so the child of God approaches to Him, though he may not always find the same sweetness; and he expects that he will be rewarded accordingly.
Gemul means, "a weaned infant, or a reward."

130:3.text: Israel. This occurs in the preceding psalm.
David encourages all to hope by his own experience.

131:1.text: David. Jesus Christ, (St. Hilary, etc.) or the pious king David, when he intended to build the temple. Solomon adopts some of the verses at his dedication, (ver. 8., and 2 Paralipomenon 6:41.; Haydock) and some have attributed the psalm to him, to the captives, or to some prophet in the time of the Machabees, though the prophets then, in fact, appeared no longer. (Berthier) See Psalm xliii., lxxiii., lxxvi., lxxxviii., and xcviii.
The Jews allow, that some verses regard the Messias, of whom the whole may be explained in a spiritual, (Calmet) or even in the second literal sense.
It is usual for David, Moses, etc., to speak of themselves in the third person.

Meekness. Hebrew also, "humility," (Calmet) or "affliction," (St. Jerome) as the prophet might have all this in view. David gave abundant proofs of his humility (2 Kings 7:2, 13.; Berthier) and moderation, and was greatly afflicted all his life.
Blessed are the meek, Matthew 5. Christ has set before us his own example.

131:2.text: Jacob. Probably after he had removed the ark to Sion.
What is omitted in one place, is thus explained elsewhere.
David had a most earnest desire to build the temple; and though the honour was reserved for his son, he prepared the materials.
He acknowledges that without God, he cannot perform his vow.
We have engaged to be the temples of God.

131:3.text: If. He expresses his vow in the form of an imprecation, without expressing the penalty, as he submits to the rigour of God's justice, if he proves faithless.
He vows to take no rest, till he might, if it so pleased God, find a place to build a temple.
This he discovered; yet was not permitted to execute his pious designs. How does his fervour confound our neglect of salvation!
Self-love shuts the door of our heart against Christ.

131:5.text: Temples. This seems to have been in the copies of Septuagint and Theodotion. It is not correct to say that the former added it by inspiration, as they were only interpreters.
It was marked as an addition in the Hexapla.

131:6.text: Heard of it in Ephrata. When I was young, and lived in Bethlehem, otherwise called Ephrata, I heard of God's tabernacle and ark, and had a devout desire of seeking it; and accordingly I found it, at Cariathiarim, the city of the woods: where it was, till it was removed to Jerusalem. See 1 Paralipomenon xiii.

Or it was revealed to David, that the temple should be built in that part of Jerusalem, which looked towards Bethlehem, and is surrounded with woods. All the plan was laid before him, 1 Paralipomenon xxviii.

But it is not probable that Jerusalem should be thus described, and there is no proof that the threshing-floor of Ornan was woody. It seems rather, that the psalmist alludes to the ark, first at Silo, secondly in the country of Ephraim, or the Ephratheans, (Psalm 77:60, 67., and Judges 12:5.) for 328 years, and afterwards at Cariathiarim, for other 70. The captives may also recount its different stations, and pray that it may be restored; though it seems never to have been placed in the second temple.
It, (eam) the tabernacle, which in Hebrew is feminine.

Yet as the text has tabernacles, or "dwellings," mishcanoth, (ver. 5.) and as the Mosaical tabernacle was kept at Silo or Gabaon, and was not with the ark at Cariathiarim, we may perhaps suppose, that the psalmist alludes to the ark, (Haydock) or to the thing indefinitely, (Berthier) where the glory of the Lord was displayed. St. Jerome and Houbigant have "him," the God of Jacob.

The Fathers explain it of Jesus Christ, (Theodoret) who was born at Bethlehem, (Worthington) and was prefigured by the temple, (Calmet) styled "the fields of wood." Hebrew sede yahar, to intimate the great extend and quantity of wood used in it; though (Haydock) Cariathiarim, "the city of the woods," may be meant.

131:7.text: Stood. If David did this out of devotion, why may not Christians visit the places sanctified by the presence of our Saviour?
God had not chosen to have a temple before the time of Solomon, 2 Kings 7:6.

131:8.text: Sanctified. Hebrew, "ark of thy strength," which title proceeds from the sanctity of God, who resides there, 1 Kings 6:20. Our heart ought to be his resting place, Isaias 66:2.
Leaving Silo, etc., come into thy temple, with the ark where thou sanctifiest thy people. He contemplates a higher mystery, the coming of the Messias, and his glorious resurrection.
The same words were repeated, when the ark was removed in the desert, and by Solomon, as they might be also by the captives.

131:9.text: Justice. And all virtues, (Berthier) of which their robes were emblematical.
Let both priests and Levites perform well their sacred functions, (Worthington) and may all the faithful act up to their vocation.
Rejoice. Hebrew, "sing," which was the office of the Levites.
Yet the original term is more comprehensive, and denotes all the emotions of joy.

131:10.text: Sake. What confidence are we not taught to place in the merits of the saints! The captives remind God of the virtues of David and Solomon, and use their expressions to move him to shew mercy, and to restore the temple to its ancient splendour; (Calmet) or rather, (Haydock) the consideration of David's being a man according to God's own heart, to whom various promises had been made for the establishment of his seed, is adduced to move the Lord to send the Messias speedily.
The future obstinacy of many Jews in rejecting him, was foreseen and deprecated.
Anointed. Do not cover me with confusion, (Calmet) or delay the promised Redeemer.

131:11.text: Make. Literally, "deceive him." Hebrew, "will not turn from it," the truth.
Thy. St. Cyprian and St. Hilary read, "my throne," which belonged to the Lord, 1 Paralipomenon 29:23.
But it might also be styled David's, as the promises were made to him.
Christ was born of the virgin's womb, without having any man for his father.
This promise actually was fulfilled in Solomon, and in Christ, who would infallibly possess the throne, 2 Kings vii., and Acts 2:But Christ reigns over all.
St. Peter hence proves our Saviour's resurrection, (Worthington) and power in the Church.
After the captivity, the royal power was not enjoyed by the family of David: which ought to have persuaded the Jews to acknowledge Christ and his eternal spiritual kingdom.

131:12.text: If. The promises made to the carnal posterity of David were conditional, and seem to imply, that they would forfeit them.
But those regarding the Messias are absolute.
Yet heaven is only promised to his children in the Church, if they observe God's commandments, Romans 8:17.

The kings of Juda seem not to have believed these threats; and many Christians live as if heaven required no conditions on their part. God knew what would be the event; but he speaks thus to testify their free-will, and his desire to preserve the temporal throne of David, at least till the coming of the Messias, if the Jews had proven faithful.

131:14.text: This. God is introduced speaking to the end.
Chosen. Hebrew, "desired." This relates to his spouse, the Church.
He dwelleth in this Sion for ever.
It was the figure of the Christian Church, as this is of heaven, Hebrews 12:22.

131:15.text: Widow. Whose name is often joined with orphans, and the poor, as the Hebrew Tsedah may imply one "desolate," 1 Timothy 5:5.
The Greek copies vary: some have widow, and others, "prey," which is most commonly given as the sense of the Hebrew.
The poor priests and Levites, who had no land, shall be abundantly supplied.
The Church, though deprived of Christ's visible presence, is replenished with many blessings, and her humble children are relieved with the holy Sacraments.

131:16.text: The Church hath always had some virtuous priests and laics.

131:17.text: There. Literally, "thither," (Haydock) Illuc, as Christ came from heaven.
Horn. Power and glory. This regards Solomon, and the Messias. Hebrew, "I will make to bud," etc., alluding to Christ's miraculous birth, Isaias 4:2.
Lamp. St. John the Baptist, (John 5:35.; St. Athanasius) or Christ himself, (Luke 2:32., and Apocalypse 21:23.) though it may also be understood of David's son and successor.
Yet this lamp was too dreadfully extinguished.
Christ protects his Church, which is placed for the instruction of all.

131:18.text: My. Hebrew, "his diadem," (S. Ser.[St. Jerome?]) or "consecration." The glory of David was great, as a figure of Christ, to whom this more properly belongs. He is the king of ages, without sin, etc.

132:1.text: "Of David," as we read in Hebrew and the Alexandrian Septuagint, being composed by him, when all Israel acknowledged his dominion, (Berthier) after his third anointing.
Others explain it of the captives returned, and particularly of the sacred ministers resolving to perform their duties with unanimity.
Esdras might propose the example "of David," for the imitation of others.

132:2.text: Precious. Is expressed in Hebrew. The name of Aaron would sufficiently denote that it was of this nature. (Berthier) See Exodus 30:23.

Moses poured it on his head, with such profusion that it ran upon all his beard, and even be the skirt, or neck, as the Septuagint insinuate, though the Hebrew means "the mouth," or orifice, through which the head entered, (Calmet) as the robes resembled our sacred vestments.
The union of priests is no less sweet.

Of this we need no other proof than to behold the advantages of concord in every community, and especially in the Church of God. The unction of Aaron denoted grace and unity descending from the head of the Church, to the other priests, and to the meanest of the faithful; (Worthington) or that which Hebrew, "of Hermon descending on the mountains of Sion," Tsiyon.
There is a hill written Ssiyon at the foot of Hermon, Exodus 4:48., and Ecclesiasticus 24:17. It is not probable that dew should flow 50 leagues across the Jordan.
Unity is equally desirable, as moisture to a thirsty soil.
Sion may here denote any "dry" mountain.

There was also a Hermon in the tribe of Issachar, and the dews in that country resembled showers of rain. Such copiousness would be very refreshing, like fraternal concord. The enemies of Christianity observed how the disciples loved each other, being of one heart and soul, (Berthier) as their divine master had taught them to be thus distinguished.
As the snow or dew which descends from Hermon, causes fruitfulness, so does concord promote piety.
There, in concord, or in the temple.
The holy Spirit came upon the disciples when they were together, Acts ii., and 4:32.
Life everlasting. Jonathan was not preserved from dying young, though he was so faithful to his friend, and the possession (Berthier) of temporal prosperity cannot satisfy the heart.

132:3.text: Precious. Is expressed in Hebrew. The name of Aaron would sufficiently denote that it was of this nature. (Berthier) See Exodus 30:23.

Moses poured it on his head, with such profusion that it ran upon all his beard, and even be the skirt, or neck, as the Septuagint insinuate, though the Hebrew means "the mouth," or orifice, through which the head entered, (Calmet) as the robes resembled our sacred vestments.
The union of priests is no less sweet.

Of this we need no other proof than to behold the advantages of concord in every community, and especially in the Church of God. The unction of Aaron denoted grace and unity descending from the head of the Church, to the other priests, and to the meanest of the faithful; (Worthington) or that which Hebrew, "of Hermon descending on the mountains of Sion," Tsiyon.
There is a hill written Ssiyon at the foot of Hermon, Exodus 4:48., and Ecclesiasticus 24:17. It is not probable that dew should flow 50 leagues across the Jordan.
Unity is equally desirable, as moisture to a thirsty soil.
Sion may here denote any "dry" mountain.

There was also a Hermon in the tribe of Issachar, and the dews in that country resembled showers of rain. Such copiousness would be very refreshing, like fraternal concord. The enemies of Christianity observed how the disciples loved each other, being of one heart and soul, (Berthier) as their divine master had taught them to be thus distinguished.
As the snow or dew which descends from Hermon, causes fruitfulness, so does concord promote piety.
There, in concord, or in the temple.
The holy Spirit came upon the disciples when they were together, Acts ii., and 4:32.
Life everlasting. Jonathan was not preserved from dying young, though he was so faithful to his friend, and the possession (Berthier) of temporal prosperity cannot satisfy the heart.

133:1.text: Canticle. David composed it for the Levites going on duty, (Haydock) or Solomon for the dedication of the temple. The people might use it on the last day of the three great festivals; or the Levites are exhorted to watch carefully. They may excite each other, (ver. 2.; Calmet) as all Christians should do, 1 Peter 2:9.
In the courts, etc., seems to be taken from Psalm 134:2., (Calmet) as it is not in Hebrew.
The court of the people was divided for the men and women, and perhaps there was a separate place for the priests, and for the Levites, in the court of allotted to them, where the latter kept watch.

133:2.text: Lift up. This posture was very natural, and expressive, 1 Timothy 2:8.
Agatharcides testifies, that the Jews did no servile work on the sabbath, but "stretched forth their hands to pray in the temple, till the evening."

133:3.text: Thee. "A Levite on guard answers the cantor," (Houbigant) or the priests say this to the people, when they were departing home, Numbers 6:23.
Earth. All things were made to praise God.

134:1.text: Alleluia. The psalm turns on God's praises, and might be composed by David, after he had settled the order of the Levites, though it may suit all people.

The latter part agrees with Psalm cxiii., and Jeremias x., which might lead us to conclude that it was composed after the captivity, perhaps for the dedication of the second temple. The next psalm is a repetition of this, with the chorus interspersed.

134:2.text: Courts. Both clergy and laity praise God for his favours and power.

134:3.text: Sweet. Occupation, and very useful, Psalm 146:1.

134:4.text: Possession. Hebrew, "jewel," "property." Chaldean, "well-beloved." See Deuteronomy 52:8., and Ecclesiasticus 17:14.
The first patriarchs corresponded with God's goodness: but their descendants were for the most part ungrateful: so that Christians have now entered into their place, and must praise God.

134:5.text: Gods. Whatever is so esteemed by men.
He then proceeds to shew the vanity of such false gods.

134:7.text: Earth. The Mediterranean sea is so called, Luke 12:54.
The most incompatible things are united.
The sinner melts into tears at the sight of God's judgments.
Stores, which are his will and decrees.

134:20.text: Fear. It was important to distinguish the priests from the people.

135:1.text: Alleluia, in Hebrew, is at the end of the last psalm.
It is omitted entirely in St. Augustine, etc. Origen thinks that the captives sung this psalm at their return, as it greatly resembles the 106th, 134th, etc.

We read, (2 Paralipomenon 7:6.) the priests stood in their offices, and the Levites, with the instruments of music of the Lord, which king David made to praise the Lord, "because his mercy endureth for ever," etc. This chorus is found only in this psalm, which seems therefore to have been sung at the dedication of the temple.

The first part of the verse was sung by the priests, or cantors, (Haydock) and the second was repeated perhaps by the people, (Calmet) or by other musicians, as we now answer at the litanies, and often repeat the Rosary, Glory, etc., (Worthington) which surely may be done without any superstition, though Leigh, in his Battologeo, and Casaubon (Exer. 14.) be pleased to ridicule the Jesus psalter, on this account.
Praise. Literally, "confess" (Haydock) your sins, and praise God.
For ever. Or is unbounded. His heart overflows at this thought.

135:3.text: Lord. By these three repetitions the blessed Trinity is insinuated, (Worthington) as some of the Fathers have remarked, though the argument is not convincing.

135:4.text: Wonders. If saints or others work miracles, it is only by God's permission.
None can do any thing against his will.
Only He can perform true miracles, above the course of nature.

135:5.text: Understanding. Or Son, who is the uncreated Wisdom, (St. Augustine) or to lead us to the knowledge of him. All has been made by the divine power and wisdom. Cajetan, etc., would assert the heavens to be intelligent, or governed by angels, Genesis 1:14.
But this opinion is generally rejected.

135:6.text: Waters. On which it was supposed to rest like a vessel.

135:7.text: Lights. The stars, (St. Jerome) or the sun and moon, ver. 8., and Genesis 1:16.
All are great lights, though the moon be comparatively small.

135:10.text: First-born. Justice was exercised on them, and mercy on Israel.

135:13.text: Parts. Two, not twelve, as the Rabbins pretend, (Berthier) and as Origen, etc., seem to allow, Exodus 14:22.

135:16.text: Desert. This was very astonishing, as they were not incommoded by the heat, or destitute of food and clothes for forty years, (Calmet) except when God tried them for their improvement, or for their sins.

135:23.text: Affliction. Literally, "humility," (Haydock) when we entered into ourselves under the judges, or during the captivity.

135:25.text: Food. Hebrew, "bread," which denotes all sorts of eatables.
The particular and general favours of God proceed from his mercy.

135:27.text: Give, etc. This verse is marked as superfluous in the most correct psalters, being a repetition of ver. 3., and unknown to the Hebrew, Septuagint, etc., though it is found in the Arabic.

136:1.text: For Jeremias. For the time of Jeremias, and the captivity of Babylon.
Or "of" (Jeremiae.; Haydock) Jeremias; on which subject he composed his Lamentations, as the Septuagint thus insinuate.

The title may be a later insertion, and is not the same in all the Greek or Latin copies. It is wholly omitted in Hebrew, etc. Theodoret blames those who have written the name of Jeremias, as he was never at Babylon.
He might send the psalm to the captives, (Grotius) though it were written by David, (Gerer.) who was a prophet. See Psalm lxxviii.
