The captives express their sentiments at Babylon, (Bossuet) or at their return; (Calmet) and thus, under the figure of the earthly Jerusalem, (Berthier) aspire to heaven.
Rivers. Euphrates, etc. The Jews retired to such places to pray.
Sion, and all the ceremonies of religion.

136:2.text: Willow. With which the Euphrates was lined, Isaias 15:7. It passed through the city, which was adored with trees, and contained extensive tracts of land for cultivation.
Babylon may also include all the territory.
Instruments. Hebrew Kinnor means properly the ancient lyre, but here it is put for all instruments of music.

In grief, music was laid aside, Isaias 24:8., Ezechiel 26:13., and Apocalypse 18:21. Yet Christians may unite spiritual canticles with holy compunction, Colossians 3:16. We must not expose the word of truth to the ridicule of infidels.

136:3.text: Sion. The Levites were trained to singing from their infancy, at the expense of the nation. Under David, there were 288 masters, and 8,000 Levites who played on music, 1 Paralipomenon xv. The Babylonians wished to hear them, (Calmet) or (Worthington) spoke insultingly, (Theodoret) as the pagans asked what good Christ had done?

136:4.text: Land. They were oppressed with grief, (Ecclesiasticus 22:6.) and unwilling to expose sacred things to profanation, though there was no prohibition for them to sing out of Judea, for their mutual comfort.
They excuse themselves on both accounts.

136:5.text: Forgotten. May all that is most dear to us perish, if we do not serve God, (Worthington) and seek to procure the welfare of Jerusalem.
Yet they knew that the joy of their earthly abode in that city could not satisfy their desires. They allude, therefore, ultimately to heaven.

136:7.text: Day. When Jerusalem was taken, or when it shall be re-established.
The Idumeans incited the Babylonians to destroy it entirely, and even cut in pieces such as had escaped, Abdias 5:11., and Jeremias 12:6.
But Nabuchodonosor punished them five years afterwards, and Hircanus forced them to receive circumcision.
Isaias (xxi. 11.) denounced their ruin, as the psalmist does here.

St. Chrysostom thinks that the latter expresses the vindictive sentiments of the Jews: but he rather desires that God's cause should be maintained in the manner in which He should judge best. The illusions of our own imagination and vanity are most dangerous and domestic enemies, as the Edomites sprung from the same stock as Israel.

136:8.text: Miserable "plunderer," (Symmachus) or "ruined," (Aquila) or "which shalt be given up to plunder."
Cyrus reduced the city to a state of abjection, and it has since experienced other miseries, (Calmet) so that its situation is now unknown.

136:9.src: Challoner; St. Augustine; St. Gregory; Psalm l.; Worthington
136:9.text: Dash thy little ones, etc. In the spiritual sense, we dash the little ones of Babylon against the rock, when we mortify our passions, and stifle the first motions of them, by a speedy recourse to the rock, which is Christ.
We do not read that Cyrus treated Babylon with this rigour; but such practices were then customary, (Osee 14:1.; Homer, Iliad xxii.) and Darius cruelly punished the revolted city.
God will reward those who execute his decrees (Haydock) against Babylon.
The psalmist contrasts the felicity of the conqueror, with the misery of the citizens, without approving of his conduct.

137:1.text: David. It contains a form of thanksgiving for him, or for any other.

Some Greek copies add, "of Aggeus and Zacharias." But this is of small authority, though they might sing this psalm (Calmet) when the Jews had been delivered from all their enemies, (Origen) under Darius, who married Esther. It might also be used by Ezechias, after his recovery, (Calmet) or by David, when he found himself at rest, (Muis) and had the Messias in view.
Praise. Literally, "confess," (Haydock) by repentance, (St. Jerome) and praise.
For, etc. This sentence is placed after angels, in Septuagint and is not at all in Hebrew, (Berthier) Chaldean, Syriac, etc.
It seems probable that the Septuagint found this reason for praise in their copies.

137:1.src: Berthier; Apocalypse v., and viii.; Apostolic Constitutions 8:4.
137:1.text: Angels. Some would translate "judges, gods," etc. But the psalmist would rather pray before the angels, who would attend in the temple, and present his petitions.
Their presence ought to full us with awe, (Luke 12:8., and 1 Timothy 5:21.) and with confidence, if our prayer comes from the heart.
Such God will hear, and the angels will present, Tobias xii., Acts x., and 1 Corinthians 11:10., and Psalm 24:10.

137:2.text: Towards. Thus the Jews acted, when they were at a distance from the temple, and in captivity.
Truth. Which thou hast displayed in my regard.
Holy. Septuagint may have perhaps written Logion, "Word," agreeably to the Hebrew, though our version seems more easy.
St. Jerome (ad Sun.) explains it of Jesus Christ, the Word, whose name is so exalted, (Philippians 2:9.; Calmet) as well as the divine majesty.
The holy doctor translates, "thou hast magnified thy word above every name." But thy is joined with name in Hebrew. Others would supply, "thy name and thy word."
If my prayer be granted, as formerly, I shall advance in virtue.

137:4.text: Mouth. All kingdoms shall, sooner or later, become acquainted with the gospel.
The predictions of Jeremias, etc., had been communicated to the neighbouring princes, (Calmet) and all might know that God had executed his threats and promises.
Yet none of them embraced the true religion, soon after the captivity, so that this relates to Christ.

137:5.text: Ways. Of Providence, (Calmet) and mercy, etc., Psalm 24:10.
Some have read, "canticles," wdais, being substituted for odois.

137:6.text: The high, etc. The proud and haughty, who exalt themselves , God knoweth afar off; that is, he despiseth them, and setteth them at a distance from him; whilst he stoopeth down to favour and embrace the low and humble.
He treats the proud as strangers to him.
If kings would sing in the ways of the Lord, they also must love, and be humble.
God knows all things before they happen, (St. Chrysostom) and both high and low are equally open to his eye, Psalm 112:5., and 138:2., and 8.

137:7.text: Wilt quicken me. And not otherwise.
There is no going to heaven without crosses.
Wrath. Most furious and cruel enemies.

137:8.text: Repay. Taking me under his protection, (St. Chrysostom) he will punish my oppressors; or he has done it already.
Hands. Rescue me from the oppression of my temporal and spiritual enemies.

138:1.text: David. Some Greek copies add, "a psalm or alleluia of Zacharias in the dispersion," when the Israelites were at Babylon. But Theodoret greatly disapproves of those additions, and explains this psalm of king Josias, while others refer it to David, though it may have no reference to any historical fact, being designed to praise the knowledge of God.
It is the most beautiful and sublime piece in all the psalter.
Proved me. God makes trial of his servants, to let them know themselves, as all thing are open to him.
Me. Houbigant supplies the omission in Hebrew, "hast known."
Up. Or all the occurrences of my life, Lamentations 3:63.
The expression is similar to that of coming in and going out, Acts 1:21. This may be explained of Christ, whose sufferings and resurrection were foreseen.
God sounds the very heart, Jeremias 17:9., and Romans 8:27.

138:3.text: Line. Or the measure of my life and actions.
Hebrew, "my lying down," alluding to hares, etc., which are followed to their resting places.
Some read, "my direction," (St. Hilary, etc.) or "limit."
The utmost extent of my intention, and the words yet unuttered, are not unknown to thee.

138:4.text: No speech, etc., viz., unknown to thee: or when there is no speech in my tongue, yet my whole interior and my most secret thoughts are known to thee.
Vatican Septuagint, "no evil speech." Aldus, "no deceit."

138:5.text: Last. Hebrew, "what are behind and before:" or, joining it with the sequel, "thou hast formed behind," etc., which seems not so noble.

Chaldean, "thou hast afflicted me before and behind, and hast stricken me with thy hand." Hence the Rabbins have ridiculously inferred that God reduced the extraordinary size of Adam after his sin to 100 cubits! The sense of the Chaldean may be adopted, though the hand upon me, in Hebrew, may also signify the divine protection, Psalm 79:18.

138:6.text: To me. Literally, "from me," ex me. Hebrew, "above me," (Haydock) or "more than I," (Houbigant) which seems little. "The knowledge which thou hast of me is admirable."
To consider attentively the workmanship of man, we shall be filled with astonishment, (Menochius) as the Jews explain this passage, (Calmet) and as the Vulgate seems to intimate.
The formation of the body, and its union with the soul, are truly astonishing. This gives the highest idea of the divine wisdom.
By experience we are convinced that his knowledge surpasses our comprehension.

138:7.src: St. Augustine; Chaldean; Calmet; Isaias 30:28.; Menochius

138:7.text: The power of God extends every where, Wisdom 1:7.
The third divine person is truly God, and immense.
As God's knowledge comprehends every thing, so his presence reaches to all.

138:8.text: Descend. Hebrew, "make my bed the grave or hell," Job 17:13. The living and the dead are equally in God's power.

138:9.text: In the morning. Shachar is so rendered by St. Jerome.
Some would understand "the black eagle," (Lyranus) or the east, (Origen) or aurora. The poets assign wings to the sun, etc., Malachias 4:2.

138:11.text: Cover. Literally, tread me under foot," conculcabunt.
Hebrew, "shall cover or be my aurora."
Pleasures of the most secret kind. All is naked before God.
The prophet speaks in the name of all who are curious, that darkness itself cannot hide any thing from him.
He seems also to represent one who seeks to avoid being seen in his pleasures.
Hebrew, "around me." But it also has the former meaning, and shews that if we seek to indulge ourselves in darkness, it will be all in vain.

138:12.text: Thereof. Alluding to day and night.

138:13.text: Nothing seems more hidden than a man's entrails, or a child in his mother's womb, who is formed by God, ver. 16., and 2 Machabees 7:22.
Protected. Hebrew, "covered," which may also mean formed.

138:14.text: Magnified. Chaldean and St. Jerome, "hast magnified me." Hebrew seems less correct, "I have been terribly magnified," though the sense is much the same.
I have been stricken with awe and astonishment at thy works.

138:15.text: Septuagint have read rather differently, and give a very good sense; "and is my substance in?" etc. Most explain this also of the embryo. But it seems rather to refer to the corpse in the grave, Psalm 62:10.

138:16.text: My imperfect being, etc., viz., When I was as yet but an embryo, in my mother's womb; and even then, in the book of thy knowledge, all the parts and members of my body were exactly set down; which by succession of days were formed, and brought to perfection; and no one of them but what was fashioned by thee.
Or none shall return from the grave, nemo in eis.
No man can form the body, Job 10:8.
None of these days, (Haydock) or men, shall escape thee, (Calmet) or "live for ever."
The embryo is formed successively, but nothing yet appears of what it shall be.

Protestants, "in thy book all my members (marginal note: all of them) were written, which in continuance were fashioned, (marginal note: or what days they should be fashioned) when as yet there was none of them."
Book here alludes to the list of the living, kept by God, (Exodus 32:32.) or to the decree of predestination, Philippians 4:3.
Our first and last state are both horrible, (Haydock) so that hardly any vestiges of man can be discovered.
We shall find ourselves entire only in eternity, if we have spent our days well.

138:17.text: Friends. Many pervert this passage, translating "thoughts," because the word may have that sense in Chaldean, (Worthington) though the paraphrase agree with us; (Calmet) and all confess that our version is accurate. The other may also be admitted. While the dead seem to have no longer any existence, the saints are still with God, and the psalmist hopes to rise with them.

Principality. St. Jerome and Theodoret, "poor," who are generally employed by God in his greatest works, that no flesh may glory in his sight. Though the elect be comparatively few, yet they cannot be numbered (Haydock; Apocalypse 7:9.) by man.

138:18.text: I rose up, etc. I have taken a resolution to rise up from sloth and sleep; and to be still with thee, whose friends are so honourable and so happy.
I hope to persevere in thy service.
With thee. At my rising, I am still employed in the same manner, and cannot find out the number of thy servants or thoughts.

138:19.text: From me. How long shall I be forced to live among the enemies of the Lord?
Since he will reward the good, and punish the wicked eternally, I renounce the society of the latter.
He specifies murderers, as he had described the wonderful formation of man.

138:20.text: Because you say in thought, etc. Depart from me, ye wicked, who plot against the servants of God, and think to cast them out of the cities of their habitation; as if they had received them in vain, and to no purpose.
Thy cities. Sixtus V, etc., read "their."
Schismatics, and all innovators, endeavour to withdraw all their dependencies from the truth; (St. Augustine) and infidels wish to prevent the propagation of the gospel.
The enemies of Nehemias, etc., may be here meant, 2 Esdras 4:2.
Depart from me, all you who would represent the felicity of heaven to be a dream.

138:22.text: Hatred. Christ commands, "Love your enemies;" not those who hate God.
We must love in them what God loves, and detest what he condemns.
The Jews abused this passage, thinking they might hate those who were not of their nation.
The example of the prophet might have rectified them.
Fervent zeal against God's enemies is commendable.

138:23.text: Paths. No one knowing, without a special revelation, whether he be worthy of love or hatred, (Ecclesiastes ix.) the just submit to God's examination.

138:24.text: Way. If I be in sin, pardon me, and prepare me for heaven.
Or I refuse not to be punished, if I imitate sinners.

139:1.text: David. He, (Calmet) Ezechias, (Ven. Bede) the captives, (Bossuet) or Jesus Christ and his servants under persecution, speak in this psalm. (Holy Fathers) See Psalm lv.

139:2.text: Man. Saul, (Calmet) Judas, (St. Hilary) or self-love, the old man, who is our most dangerous enemy.

139:3.text: Battles. And formed plots to destroy the just.

139:4.text: Serpent. So Plautus says, "A double-tongued and wicked man is like a creeping beast," in Persa.

139:6.text: Side. The enemies of David calumniated him, and strove to make him follow idols, 1 Kings 26:19.

139:8.text: Battle. Against Goliath, or against the attempts of Saul, etc.
Prayer and divine grace are necessary, to guard us from sin.

139:9.text: Desire. If I yield to my passions, (St. Augustine) or after I have expressed my request, contrary to my desire, etc. Hebrew, "Grant not the desire of the sinner, complete not his thought. They will be elated." Some supply, "lest they be." The ancients seem not to have read in the same manner.
Abandon not my soul, which is all my care, (Psalm 21:21.) to the sinner.

139:10.text: Head. Hebrew also, "poison," (Berthier) or "sum" of their mischievous devices.
Their subtle persuasions shall turn to their ruin.

139:11.text: Fire of hell, (Worthington; Chaldean) as well as temporal afflictions, from lightning, etc., Psalm 17:9.
Some have ridiculed the belief of hell. But the universality of this opinion is a strong proof of it, as it is also confirmed by revelation.

139:12.text: Tongue. This member is very dangerous.
The just man is not styled "a man of tongue," but "of heart;" for which reason Christ exhorted his disciples not to make long speeches in prayer, Matthew 6:7.
Into. Literally, "in;" though (Haydock) it should be into, conformably to the Septuagint. (Berthier) Raro antecedentem scelestum Deseruit pede poena claudo.

139:13.text: I know. Hebrew, "thou knowest." But some copies read more accurately.
Poor. Thus, in the end, was Lazarus treated. [Luke 16:22.]

139:14.text: Countenance in glory, when thou shalt appear, 1 John 3:2.
They shall dwell under thy special protection in the tabernacle.

140:1.text: It is very difficult, and was used in the evening service in the oriental churches, (St. Chrysostom) as it is on Friday in the Roman Breviary. The psalm may be applicable to all in distress, (Berthier) who are members of Christ.
Hearken. Hebrew, "hasten to me, hear my voice," etc.

140:2.text: As. May be understood. "My prayer is an evening sacrifice," or equally acceptable.
Sacrifice. Hebrew, "offering," which may be the same with the incense, or the evening holocaust.

The Church prays that her petitions may ascend to the throne of God, and be accepted. The morning sacrifice was rather more solemn. But the evening one is here specified, in allusion to Christ's, at the last supper, and on the cross, (Worthington) which was consummated in the evening.
It may denote a perfect worship.

140:3.text: And. Hebrew, "keep guard at the door of my lips," (Montanus) or "on the poverty of," etc., (St. Jerome) that I may not speak too much, or imprudently; as I shall do, if left to myself.
Houbigant rejects this version, which is very expressive.
Hebrew dal is rendered "elevation," (Chaldean) and may here stand for "a door." Nature has included the tongue within a double restraint of the teeth and lips, to counteract its too great volubility.
David prays that he may not be unguarded in his speech. He always spoke to Saul with the greatest respect, 1 Kings 24:18.
The lips must be like a door, neither always open, when we ought to be silent, nor always shut, when we should confess our sins.

140:4.text: Sins. Such excuses are always vain.
God does not incline any to sin, as the Manichee elect pretended.
The pride of the human heart would throw the blame of sin on any but itself.
Sometimes, fate, predestination, etc., are charged with it. Our first parents excused themselves; but David candidly confessed his sins, and was presently pardoned.
Choicest. Hebrew also, "I will not eat of their most delicious meats." I will have no society with libertines, (Berthier) nor with those who teach false doctrine, 2 John 10., and Titus 3:10.

140:5.text: Let not the oil of the sinner, etc. That is, the flattery, or deceitful praise.
This is pernicious; while the reprehension of the just may do us good.
Sinner. Hebrew, "of poison, or of the head, (vass) break, or fatten, (St. Jerome) or be broken upon my head." The vessel was usually broken, Mark 14:3.
Protestants, "Let the righteous smite me, it shall be a kindness, and let him reprove me, it shall be an excellent oil, which shall not break," etc.
Or "he shall not," etc., as his only aim tends to my advantage. Many other senses may be given.
For my prayer, etc. So far from coveting their praises, who are never well pleased but with things that are evil; I shall continually pray to be preserved from such things as they are delighted with.
Against. Literally, "in or concerning their desirable things."
I pray to God that I may be preserved from their malice, in which they take delight, (Menochius) and that they may cease to run on to their ruin.
The Church still prays for sinners, though as yet they delight in their criminal pursuits, (Worthington) in order that a wholesome bitterness may wean them from such things.
My prayer shall subsist amid their pleasures.

140:6.text: Their judges, etc. Their rulers, or chiefs, quickly vanish and perish, like ships dashed against the rocks, and swallowed up by the waves. Let them then hear my words, for they are powerful and will prevail; or, as it is in the Hebrew, for they are sweet.
The Israelites were long governed by judges, and the supreme magistrates at Carthage had the same title.
Septuagint, "their strong ones" have or shall become victims of the divine justice.
They shall know that the prayers of the Church are effectual, and obtain the grace of constancy for her children, whom she instructs in the doctrines of peace, which cannot injure the commonwealth.
Prevailed. Or, "been able" to hear. Israel submitted to David, after Saul's death.

140:7.text: As when the thickness, etc. If this is to be connected with what goes before, it declares the efficacy of those powerful words, capable to break up the thick and hard earth, of the hearts of man. But if it is to be connected with the following words, our bones are scattered, etc., it means, that the malice of our enemies has, as it were, scattered our bones near the confines of death and hell; as the husbandman, plowing up or digging the earth, scatters and breaks the clods.
These are made small, that they may bear fruit, which is the effect of persecution upon the faithful.
Hebrew, "like the labourer breaking up the earth, so our," etc.

Houbigant would read, "their," (Berthier) with the Arabic, and many Greek copies. David alludes to his distress, (Calmet) while his people were still divided into parties, (Haydock) whose union he foretells.
Dry bones point out the state of the captives, Ezechiel 37:11.
Some persecutors rage against the bones and relics of the saints, which they would willingly hurl into hell. But the Church places her confidence in God.

140:10.text: His. "Own," (Protestants) the devil's, (St. Hilary) or God's net.
Saul twice fell into David's power.
Alone. Singularly protected by the Almighty, until I pass all their nets and snares.
Though alone, I fear no danger.
I will have no connection with the wicked in this life, (St. Chrysostom) which is beset with snares.
Hebrew, "I will be a witness till I pass."
Sinners shall at last be entrapped; but the Church shall be for ever protected.

141:1.text: Cave. Of Engaddi, (Bossuet; Calmet) or Odollam.
Here David was a figure of Christ praying in the garden, etc.
The psalm may relate to the captives, (St. Chrysostom) or to martyrs, (St. Augustine) and to all under trial. David might recite it in the cave.
Voice. He did not speak aloud, for fear of being detected.
But the fervent prayer of the just, "is a cry to God."

141:3.text: Pour out. Explaining all the sentiments of my heart, Psalm 61:9.
This is never perfectly done with earthly friends, 1 Peter 5:7.

141:4.text: Knewest. With approbation, (Psalm 1:6.; Haydock) my deportment towards Saul, and also the dangers to which I was exposed, and the means of escaping.
They. Ancient psalters add, "the proud," from Psalm 136:6.
The devil and his agents attack the just at all times.
When I was reduced to such distress that I could not help myself, thou didst approve of my conduct, and deliver me.

141:5.text: Hand. For succour, Psalm 15:8. So was David situated at Engaddi, as our Saviour was, when he was abandoned by his disciples.
None would appear to be acquainted with those in distress.

141:6.text: Living. On the earth, (Berthier) or land of Judea, (St. Chrysostom) in this life, (Calmet) or in the next. In all, God is our only protector, and reward, Psalm 15:5.

141:8.text: As David was never in prison, this is more applicable to Christ, seized by his enemies, and consigned to the tomb.

Name. God expects it from us, Psalm 49:15. The motive of David in praying to be delivered, was most disinterested, as he had in view God's glory, and the gratification of his just servants, who would join with him in returning thanks, and promoting the divine worship.

But Aquila adopts the Chaldean and Syriac sense of the word, with the Septuagint, (Calmet) and this may very well be the meaning, as David and his friends did not pretend to reign till the death of Saul.

He wished to be at liberty in the country where God's worship was observed, (Worthington) and determined to promote religion with all his power; as, under Saul, the good were under great restraints, and the priests were even slain.

All this happened to David, as the figure of Christ. He had then a clear understanding of what the latter would have to endure from the Jews, and how he would pray to his Father. In the first part, he complains of Judas, and in the second, he desires to be set free from hell, because the faith of all the saints depended on his resurrection.

142:1.text: Absalom is not specified in Roman Septuagint, etc., (Calmet) though it be in other copies.
The latter part of the title has been added by the Greeks.
If David composed the psalm, on occasion of his son's revolt, he considered it as a punishment of his former transgression. The Fathers explain it of Jesus Christ, who suffered for our sins.
It might also relate to the captives, (Ferrand) or to the same event as the preceding psalm, (Bossuet) though there is no reason for abandoning the title.
Absalom had no just cause of complaint, (Haydock) and God had promised to protect David on the throne.

142:2.text: Justified. Compared with thee, (Calmet) and without mercy. God is bound by his promise to hear the penitent.
David and St. Paul had been assured of the remission of their sins, yet never ceased to beg for pardon.

142:3.text: Of old. Literally, "of an age," (Haydock) who are quite forgotten, Lamentations 3:6.
The devil had violently tempted me, by means of temporal and spiritual calamities.
The greatest darkness is that of the heart, Ephesians 4:17.

142:4.text: Troubled. David knew not what course to take, 1 Kings 15:25, 31. Our Saviour was in the greatest agony.

142:5.text: Of old. What God had done formerly for me and others.
The sacred records of 3,000 years tended to raise the hopes of David.

142:6.text: Water. We can add nothing to this idea.
Man can do no good without God's grace, which David implores with his hands stretched out, both to mortify himself, and to denote fervour.

142:7.text: If man be left to himself, he will presently yield to sin, from which he will not be delivered without God's grace.

142:8.text: Morning. At the first assault of temptation, (Worthington) or speedily. David might address this prayer to God during the night, after he left Jerusalem. He was only relieved the next day, when his troops had crossed the Jordan, 2 Kings 17:12.
To thee. With the most ardent desire, Jeremias 22:27. This might suit the captives, ver. 5, 11.

142:9.text: Fled. Hebrew, "To thee I have hidden myself," (Montanus) or "am protected."
But the Septuagint seem to have read better, esithi. "I have hoped."

142:10.text: Spirit. I look for a favourable wind, like one at sea, in danger of suffering shipwreck, and I apply to God, to the Holy Ghost the Comforter.
Right. Even (Calmet) and not like this country, full of precipices. In Judea the right worship was observed.

The penitent may rest assured, that God will free him from all perils, (Worthington) and bring him (Hebrew) "into the land of rectitude," which is heaven. The Holy Ghost is here represented as a distinct person.

142:12.text: Mercy. Towards me. Justice required that the rebels should be punished, as they were, even contrary to David's intention, 1 Kings 18:5.

143:1.text: St. Hilary thinks that the Septuagint added the rest by inspiration, (Calmet) because this was David's first exploit in war.

But others pay no deference to this part of the title. The Chaldean, however, seems to allude to the same victory, (ver. 10.) and the Syriac to that which David obtained over Asaph, brother of Goliath, 1 Paralipomenon 20:5.
David prays that he may overcome the Philistines, and gives thanks in Psalm xvii.

These two psalms are very much alike, and this seems to have been written after the rebels were discomfited, while the 17th expresses the sentiments of the prophet, towards the end of his life, for all his victories.
Zorobabel after the defeat of God, (Ezechiel xxxviii.; Origen) or the Machabees; (Greek paraphrast.) or Jesus Christ might adopt these sentiments.
Kimchi and Goan refer the psalm to the Messias.
Fingers. Which chose the five pebbles. He was to exert himself, and yet to acknowledge that all success came from God.
He had not been trained to war, when he overcame Goliath.

143:2.text: Mercy. "All the titles of God remind us of his benefits."
My people. After the defeat of the rebels, (Calmet) and the death of Isboseth.
Hebrew may also be "peoples," as Psalm xvii, (Syriac, etc.) including them, and the various nations that were subdued by David.
Conquerors are generally in confusion, while those who keep their passions under are free, 2 Corinthians 3:17.

143:3.text: To him. Hebrew, "thou dost acknowledge him."
In the transport of gratitude, he reflects on his own weakness, Job 7:17., and Romans 8:31.
Before Christ, all mankind were undeserving of revelation.

143:4.text: Vanity. Hebrew hebel, "nothing, a vapour," etc., Ecclesiastes 1:1., and James 4:15.
Our lives resemble a shadow, which is the less distinct, the more it increases.
As it cannot subsist of itself, so neither can man without God.

143:5.text: Come to my assistance, as thou didst appear on Sinai, Exodus 19:16., and Psalm 17:8.
This is a poetical description of God's aid.

143:7.text: Children. My rebellious subjects, (Haydock) who lead bad lives in the true Church.
Foreign nations continued faithful, while Israel rose up against their sovereign.

143:8.text: Iniquity. Hebrew, "lying." They have sworn fidelity, and have prevaricated.
They adhere not to their engagements of keeping God's law.

143:9.text: Psaltery. Hebrew, "on the Nebel of ten strings," (Haydock) the chief instrument, fit for a new canticle of thanksgiving.

143:10.text: Kings. Their power cannot protect them.
Malicious. Hebrew, "his servant from the evil sword" (Montanus) of Goliath, (Chaldean) or of Saul, (Berthier) and all his other enemies.
He represents himself in the midst of danger, from the rebels.

143:11.text: Children. Both Jews and Christians who live ill, are like strangers, who frame to themselves a temporal felicity, making riches and pleasures their god.

143:12.text: Whose. Hebrew, "our." This makes quite a different sense from the ancient versions, which refer what follows to the rebels, who had no cause to complain of David's government, ver. 14.
St. Jerome, however, agrees with the Hebrew, "that our sons may be," etc. Protestants asher means "whose (ver. 11.) and that."
If we supply, they said, the text and versions will give the same sense, (Genebrard; Berthier) as it is inserted [in] ver. 15.
Decked. Hebrew, "our daughters, like corner-stones cut like a temple," (Montanus) or "palace."

143:13.text: That. The partitions are too small; or fresh fruit come before the old ones are consumed, Leviticus 26:10.
Fruitful. Hebrew, "our sheep (or small cattle, pecudes) producing a thousand, bringing forth ten thousand, in our streets."

143:14.text: Fat. Hebrew, "our bulls (oxen or cows) are burdened." etc.
Of wall. Symmachus, "nor burying nor mourning in their places." The other interpreters cited by Theodoret, have also "their."

143:15.text: They. Hebrew, "happy the people, to which such things belong; happy," etc.
This text speaks all along of the temporal blessings attending the virtuous.

But the Septuagint, being convinced that these were rather the sentiments of David's enemies, give it this turn, (Berthier) and shew, that real happiness consists rather in the possession of God, as the psalmist intimates, by the concluding sentence.
Worldlings are satisfied with temporal advantages, Psalm 72:4.
But the saints take God for their reward.
The devil promises riches, that he may kill, and Christ promises poverty, to save us.
True happiness consists in preferring God before all.

144:1.text: Praise. The remaining seven psalms relate to the praises of God, to intimate that this occupation ought to be our glory, both in time and in eternity, as all were created for that purpose.

This is the seventh of the alphabetical psalms, the four last of which are only recognized by St. Jerome as perfect. See Psalm xxiv., xxxiii., xxxvi., cx., cxi., and cxviii. Yet here the ver 14., which should commence with N, is wanting in Hebrew, though it was probably there at first, as it is in the Greek and Latin, (Worthington) as well as in the Syriac and Arabic.
Hence it appears, that our versions ought not always to be corrected by the Hebrew, which might be rendered more perfect by a collation with them.

The Jews assert, that whoever reads this psalm thrice-a-day, may be sure of obtaining heaven, provided, says Kimchi, that his heart accompany his words. The new baptized used to recite it in thanksgiving, for having received the body and blood of Christ.
Ferrand supposes that this psalm was composed after the captivity. But there seems to be no ground for this supposition, and the author had probably no particular event in view.
My king. On whom I entirely depend.

And ever. St. Jerome, "and after," (Haydock) both in time and in eternity. Christ is styled king, to whom the nations were promised; (Psalm ii.) and David gives the highest honour to the blessed Trinity.
David still praises God by the mouths of the faithful, as also in heaven.

144:3.text: End. Hebrew, "finding out," because he is infinite.

144:4.text: And. Hebrew, "to generation." The vocation of the Gentiles is insinuated.

144:5.text: And shall. Hebrew, "and I shall relate the words of thy wonders," (St. Jerome) or "shall meditate on," etc.
Yet our version is more followed.

144:6.text: Acts. Miracles which strike people with awe, (Worthington) such as those which overwhelmed the Egyptians, etc.
And shall. Hebrew, "and shalt," etc. But Chaldean (St. Jerome) read more naturally with the Septuagint.

144:7.text: They shall approve of thy judgments.

144:8.text: Patient. Hebrew, "slow to anger," which is more expressive.

144:9.text: Works. The people of Israel (ver. 10., and Psalm 89:18.; Ferrand) and all mankind, who are all invited to embrace the true faith, and the mercy of God.
The effects of mercy shine forth above all his other works, in the redemption, and in the recalling of sinners, when they have gone astray.
This sense is good, but not literal. His mercy extends to all.
Yet he punishes the reprobate for ever, chastising their works.

144:10.text: Works. They shew his power, and excite us to praise him.

144:12.text: Thy. Hebrew, "his." But the Septuagint read more correctly, with the Chaldean, etc.
Men. The Gentiles, to whom the saints, (Berthier) or converted Jews preached.

144:13.text: Ages. The kingdom of God in his Church is very magnificent, but not so much as in heaven.
The. Hebrew, Chaldean, Aquila, St. Jerome, etc., omit this verse, which is necessary to complete the alphabet. It probably commenced with Namon, "Faithful."
The Septuagint could not insert it by inspiration, as they were only interpreters.
It was consequently in their Hebrew copies.

144:14.text: Lifteth. Hebrew, "upholdeth all who are falling."
No one can stand or rise without God.
He is ready to lift up every one.

144:15.text: Hope. For sustenance, Psalm 122:2., and Matthew 6:26.

144:16.text: Blessing. Abundantly (Calmet) "satisfieth the desire" (Protestants; Haydock) even of brute beasts, giving to all what is requisite.

144:17.text: Just. Before, his fidelity was notices, ver. 13.

144:19.text: Will. He will obey their voice; (Josue 10:14.) or rather he will grant their requests (Calmet) of eternal happiness.

144:21.text: Flesh. Every human being, though even the least favoured, must praise God, as all have received much from him.

145:1.text: Of, etc. This addition of the Septuagint intimates that these prophets would thus exhort the people to trust in Providence, and to prefer his service before worldly cares. See Psalm cxxxvi.

They might compose this psalm after Cyrus had revoked the permission to build the temple, (ver. 2., and 1 Esdras 1:3., and 4:4.) as the following psalms seem all to have been sung at the dedication of the walls.
This might be the case, but the titles afford but a slender proof, and David might write this to excite himself and people to confide in God.
In my. Hebrew begins here the second verse, with the answer of the soul to the prophet's invitation. It is immortal, and promises always to praise the Lord.

145:3.text: Children. Hebrew, "sons of Adam." The greatest prince is of the same frail condition as other men. He is not always willing, nor able to save. He must die, and all his projects cease.

If we could have depended on any, Cyrus seemed to be the person. Yet he has been deceived, and now forbids the building of a temple. We must, however, be grateful for the liberty which we enjoy by the goodness of God.
In one Son of man (Christ) we may trust; not because he is the Son of man, but because he is the Son of God.

145:4.text: Forth. From the body, which shall be consigned to the earth from which it was taken, Ecclesiastes 12:7.
And he. Man, (Calmet) or each of the princes, (Haydock) with respect to the body.
It does not refer to the spirit, which in Hebrew is feminine.
It is the want of faith, which causes people to confide in great ones, rather than in Providence.

145:7.text: The disposition of Cyrus towards the Jews had changed, in consequence of some false insinuations of their enemies.
Wrong. The Babylonians have been, and the Samaritans will be, punished.
Fettered. We may hope to be freed from the dominion of the Persians.

145:8.text: Enlighteneth. Hebrew, "openeth the eyes." Septuagint, "gives wisdom to the blind." Many of these favours seem to be understood in a spiritual sense, and allude to the times of Christ, when these miracles were performed. (Berthier) (Isaias 35:5., and Matthew 11:5.

145:9.text: Strangers. He charges his people to be compassionate towards such, Exodus 22:21., and James 1:27.
Sinners, who have calumniated us, ver. 7.

145:10.text: Sion. Figure of the true Church. God is now more attached to Sion than to any other place.
He lives for ever, and therefore alone deserves our confidence.
Generation. Hebrew adds, "Alleluia," which we have in the next title, as the psalm also begins with the same word.

146:1.text: Alleluia. In some editions of the Septuagint (Haydock) and in Syriac, Arabic, etc., the same inscription occurs, as in the former psalms. Many ascribe this to the same authors, and to the same occasion. Hebrew and Chaldean have no title. Yet the psalm seems to be a thanksgiving (Calmet) for the permission to build the temple and walls of Jerusalem, (Origen) which had been neglected, till God visited the people with a famine, ver. 8., 2 Esdras 5:1., and Aggeus 1:6.
Zorobabel, etc., urge the people to build.
Still David might compose this psalm, as he was a prophet, (Berthier) and he may allude to the beginning of his reign, when the people were all united.
Good. Agreeable and advantageous for us.
Praise. This consists in purity of life, rather than in the sweetest accents.

146:2.text: Jerusalem. After the captivity, (Worthington) or at the beginning of David's reign, when he had taken Sion, and Israel acknowledged his dominion, 2 Kings 5. It may also allude to the Church, (John 11:51.) and to heaven, Hebrews 12:22., and Apocalypse xxi.

146:3.text: Bruises. God delivered the captives, after chastising them, Deuteronomy 32:39.
He gives life to the penitent, as Christ healed the sick, etc., Isaias 61:1.

146:4.text: Stars. Which to man are innumerable. Though some have counted 1022 with Ptolemy, yet the discovery of telescopes has shewn that many more are discernible, (Calmet) and none would dare at present to fix their number.
Cicero (Of. i.) treats this as a thing impossible. See Genesis 15:5.
Ptolemy could only ascertain the number of the more notorious.

Kimchi admits 1098 created to shine, besides innumerable others, which have influence over plants, etc. God has the most perfect knowledge of all. They are like his soldiers, whom he knows by name, (Isaias 40:25.) as the good shepherd does his sheep, John 10:3.
We read that Cyrus knew the name of all his officers, (Cyrop. v.) and that Adrian, and Scipio, the Asiatic, could even name all the soldiers in their armies.

146:5.text: Power. God the Son. Earthly monarchs are forced to depend on others for the execution of their orders. But God is infinite.
Number. He knows innumerable things: (Worthington) or rather, (Haydock) the divine wisdom hath no parts, Jeremias 10:6.

146:6.text: Ground. As he has done to the Egyptians, etc.

146:7.text: Praise. Literally, "confession," (Haydock) including both compunction and praise.

146:8.text: Clouds. This is represented as something wonderful, (Job 5:9., and 37:6.) though conformable to the laws of nature. The preservation of things is like a new creation.

And the herb, etc. Herbam, (Psalm 103:14.; Haydock) is now wanting in Hebrew, as it was in the days of St. Jerome and the Chaldean, though the Septuagint, Aquila, etc., read it, and it is not probable that they would borrow it from another psalm.
Their copies must therefore have varied.
The herb, may denote corn, and all vegetables for food. These productions evince the goodness and wisdom of God, (Berthier) as well as his power.

146:9.text: Young. Literally, "the sons of ravens," which may denote those birds in general, as well as their young. God provides for all. Many fables have been recounted concerning ravens, as if they neglected or forgot their young ones; and the Hebrews seem to have entertained some of these opinions, to which the sacred writers conform themselves, Job 38:41.
St. Luke 12:24. specifies ravens, though St. Matthew 6:26. has the birds, when relating the same speech.
Upon him, must be understood in Hebrew. See Psalm 103:21., (Berthier) and Joel 1:20.
If God take such care of the neglected ravens, how much more will he provide for his servants?

147:1.text: Alleluia. This word is not in Hebrew.
Many with the Septuagint add, "of Aggaeus and Zacharias."
The psalm has the same object in view as the preceding.
The Fathers explain it of the Church, and of heaven.

Sion. This place was highly favoured before the captivity, and rebuilt afterwards so as to enjoy many blessings. But all this was only a figure of the privileges belonging to the Church, and to the heavenly Sion, the true vision of peace.
The earthly Jerusalem was too often faithless; (Galatians 4:26., and Hebrews 12:22.) so that the psalmist cannot have it alone in view.

147:2.text: Within thee. Jerusalem was better fortified than (Haydock) other cities. But the Church is built upon a rock, and enriched with many graces, while heaven is free from all danger, and its inhabitants secure.
Nehemias repeopled the city, which he had surrounded with walls, (Calmet) despising the sarcasms of his enemies, 2 Esdras 7:4.

147:3.text: Peace. To obtain this, the peace of Jesus Christ must triumph in our hearts, Colossians 3:15.
Borders. People perceiving that Assuerus favoured the Jews, durst no longer attack them, 2 Esdras 6:16.

They enjoyed peace and plenty. In the Church we have remission of sin in baptism and penance, and the spiritual food of Christ's body and blood in the blessed Eucharist, with the graces of the other sacraments. In heaven peace and joy subsist for ever.
Fat. The best (Haydock) of all sorts of fruit.
The Fathers understand the blessed Eucharist, or the holy Scriptures.

147:4.text: Speech. Rain, (Kimchi) Jesus Christ, (St. Augustine) or the gospel, (St. Hilary; St. Jerome) which was presently propagated over all the earth, Romans 10:18.
No sooner did he speak to Saul, but he was converted, Acts 9:4., and Hebrews 4:12.

147:5.text: Like wool. Because it is white, and light, and covereth the earth as with a fleece, which keeps it warm, and makes it fruitful. On which account also, in the following words, mists (or as it is in the Hebrew, hoar frosts) are compared to ashes, which give a fruitfulness to the ground.
Trees and fruits are preserved by the snow resting upon them. Vota arborum frugumque....nives....insidere.
Mists are succeeded by clear weather. By penance and austerity sins are remitted, and devils expelled, Isaias 1:18.

147:6.text: His chrystal. Some understand it of hail, which is as it were ice, divided into bits, or morsels.
In summer and winter God sends proper moisture for the earth.
The most hardened are sometimes converted: but this must be the effect of grace.
Cold. If it were to continue, all would perish. He therefore sends the warm (Calmet) "south wind."

147:7.text: Wind. "Spirit." The blessed Trinity (St. Jerome) grants compunction.
By preaching and grace conversions are wrought.

147:8.text: Jacob. These spiritual blessings pertain only to the true Church.

147:9.text: Them. He hath not announced his decrees to them. This glory was reserved for Israel, Deuteronomy 4:7.
The written law was granted to the latter. Yet God had sufficiently manifested his will to all mankind, so that they must also appear before his tribunal.

Alleluia. Considering that all were in the mass of sin, and that God justly let many perish; those whom he justifies are more bound to praise him; and therefore the prophet concludes this and the following psalms with Alleluia.
Christians, and particularly Catholics, are most concerned, since many have rebelled against the light of faith, (Berthier) and abuse the gift of the holy Scriptures.

148:1.text: Alleluia. Septuagint, Syriac, etc., add, "of Aggaeus and Zacharias," as it seems to have been composed after the captivity, ver. 13.
But why might not David thus invite all to praise God? He descends from the highest creatures to those on earth.
Heavens. All ye blessed spirits, preach God for the excellence of your nature, and for your numbers.

148:2.text: The three children made the same invitation to them, rejoicing in this holy communion. [Daniel iii.]

148:3.text: And light. Hebrew, "of light," meaning the planets.
The harmony of these things invites us to praise the Lord. Origen and St. Hilary seem to represent them as intelligent. But (Calmet) when God is praised on their account....they all praise him.
Irrational things shew forth the excellence of their Creator.

148:4.text: Of heavens. The highest and most excellent, (Calmet) though not the abode of the angels, which had been specified before.
Heavens, in the clouds. It is not necessary to allow these waters to be above the stars.

148:5.text: He spoke, and they were made, is not in Hebrew or Chaldean, being taken from Psalm 32:9., (Calmet) or lost in the original.

148:6.text: Away. The heavenly bodies observe the most constant order.
They are not liable to change, like sublunary things, (Calmet) though God may suspend the laws which he has established.

148:7.text: He then addresses whales, and all in the seas.

148:8.text: Word. For the punishment of the wicked, (Calmet) and to strike the saints with awe.

148:9.text: Mountains. Thales, Origen, etc., have attributed souls to waters, etc. But this cannot be proved from this poetical personification.

148:11.text: Kings, etc. God is to be praised for the diversity of states, whereby the whole community is preserved.

148:13.text: Alone. God, and not any idol, has disposed all things.

148:14.text: Earth. All contribute towards the divine praise. Yet they cannot afford as much as God deserves.
Horn. Glory, (Calmet) or the Messias; though this cannot easily be proved to be the literal sense.
Saints. Israelites, (Berthier) particularly the sacred ministers, Deuteronomy 4:7.
To him. By free-will, assisted by God's grace.

149:1.text: Alleluia. Theodoret repeats this word here and in the following psalm. The author of this psalm is unknown. It was sung after the captivity, to thank God for the favours and peace granted to his people, and to foretell the conquests gained under the Machabees, and more fully under the Messias.

The Jews still looked for them in vain. Why do they not open their eyes, to see all accomplished by the propagation of the gospel? Muis refers the psalm to the beginning of David's reign over Israel, when the people entertained the strongest expectations of victory; and this opinion is very plausible, though we prefer that of the Fathers.
The prophet undoubtedly invites the faithful to praise God.
New, and excellent; (Calmet) nova carmina, (Virgil, Ec. 3.) is explained magna miranda, by Servius. This psalm deserves the title, as it speaks of the new covenant.
Saints of Israel, dedicated to God's service, Exodus 22:31.
Though all creatures are bound to praise the Lord, he accepts only praises of those who live in the Church.

149:2.text: In him. Hebrew, "them." The plural is used out of respect, (Rabbins; Calmet) or rather to insinuate the blessed Trinity, as in Genesis i., Job 35:10., and Isaias 54:5.
King David, or the Lord, who was the true king of Israel, 1 Kings 8:7.

149:3.text: Choir. A musical instrument, though it imply also a dance, etc.

149:4.text: Unto. Hebrew, "in Jesus," (St. Jerome) or "he will adorn the meek with salvation," as with a precious robe, Psalm 131:9, 16.
The captives shall be restored to glory.

149:5.text: The prosperity of the Israelites but feebly represents the happiness of the elect.

149:6.text: Hands. He seems to allude to the regulation of Nehemias, (2 Esdras 4:17.; Haydock) or to the Machabees, who were priests and soldiers. They shall proclaim God's praises, and defend the nation. In the spiritual sense, Catholics employ the two-edged sword of the Old and New Testament against heretics, and exercise the power of Christ in excommunicating the wicked.
Our Saviour enables the saints to judge at the last day, Matthew 19:28.
This chiefly regards such as have left all things, like the apostles, and those who embrace a monastic life, Matthew 4:20., and 19:27., Acts 4:34., and 1 Corinthians 6:3.
They shall judge whether people have made good or bad use of their possessions.
All the blessed will approve of God's sentence; and their example in overcoming difficulties, will evince the baseness of the wicked, who have yielded to less temptations.

149:8.text: Iron. These poetical expressions denote victory. The Machabees made great conquests, and the Jews expect greater under the Messias. But this has been verified in the conversion of nations to Christ, Isaias 49:23., and 60:14.
The victories of the Machabees were of short duration.

149:9.text: This judicial power is part of the glory of the saints.
All shall judge the wicked who have imitated the disinterestedness of the apostles.

150:1.text: Alleluia. This invitation is addressed to the sacred ministers, as the people and all creatures had been already exhorted to praise God.
This psalm was sung when the first-fruits were brought to the temple.
All both in heaven and earth sound forth his praises.

150:2.text: Acts. Ye, for whom miracles have been wrought, praise him as much as you are able, though you cannot do it sufficiently.

150:3.text: Trumpet. This instrument was reserved for priests, Numbers 10:2. The nine others might be used by Levites.
The precise signification of them is not known.

150:4.text: Choir....Strings. Unity and mortification are requisite to make our praises acceptable, as strings are made of the bowels of beasts.

150:5.text: Spirit. Wind instrument, (Menochius) or man, who is bound to praise God, even more than the angels, as Christ has assumed his nature, and will bring him to sing with them Alleluia.
Let "all that breathes" praise, (Berthier) including every living creature, (Calmet) as well as the celestial spirits.

Alleluia is not in Septuagint, Syriac, etc. But it is in the Hebrew, and no word could answer better for a conclusion of the psalms, which are almost wholly employed in the divine praises. This ought to be our occupation both on earth, and in heaven. Amen.
Pope Damasus, A.D. 380, ordered the Glory, etc., to be added to each of the psalms, as it has been since observed.
An apocryphal psalm, concerning David encountering Goliath, occurs in many Greek and Latin copies; but it is of no authority.
